First-Line Supervisors of Office and Administrative Support Workers

n_band.derivation: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $116,910 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541219); class <5: 35,754 firms, avg revenue/firm $191,355 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $29,947 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 5,777 firms, avg revenue/firm $656,548 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $102,750 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 2,081 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,497,395 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $234,342 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 1,247 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,177,727 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $653,814 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 282 firms, avg revenue/firm $13,787,344 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,157,719 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 249 firms, avg revenue/firm $24,583,161 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,847,265 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 531
n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Bank-feed categorization, reconciliation, and monthly close are among the nearest-term fully automatable white-collar tasks. QuickBooks Intuit Assist and Xero AI already automate 67-85% of transaction categorization natively. Surveyed accounting firms report 67% average reduction in categorization time. Fraction 0.70 leaves 0.30 for cleanup of messy books, unusual transactions, year-end adjustments, and client advisory conversation.

Owner-operators spend significant time on data entry, bookkeeping production, and routine tax-form preparation — all AI-exposed. Client relationships, cleanup judgment, IRS representation (EA credential), and fractional-controller advisory are not automatable. Fraction 0.35 represents the production share of an owner-operator's time that is AI-substitutable.

Invoice generation, AR follow-up, and statement processing are direct workflow-automation targets. Many clients' own ERP/AI is also eliminating the outsourced function. Fraction 0.65 allows for exception handling, client disputes, and custom billing arrangements.

Adjacent compliance tasks overlapping with 541213/541214. Routine payroll data entry and standard form preparation are AI-automatable; multi-state complexity, garnishment handling, and agency notice response keep humans. Fraction 0.35.

Scheduling, document collection/chasing, onboarding paperwork, and file organization are strongly automatable via AI workflow tools. Fraction 0.55 leaves 0.45 for client-facing coordination that benefits from human presence.

pi_dist.rationale: Bookkeeping firms face severe platform threat to client control: (1) Intuit owns the ledger — QuickBooks Live directly competes with independent bookkeepers at $300-700/month with published national pricing; (2) the accountant partner channel is being converted into Intuit distribution, creating platform dependency; (3) no licensure requirement means clients can switch or self-serve at any time; (4) offshore substitution has already deflated pricing once; (5) zero-licensure market makes new entrants trivially easy for any remaining demand. pi_dist = 0.30: very limited true client ownership — the platform that hosts the books has de facto control.
sources: QuickBooks Live pricing (dancingnumbers.com/quickbooks-live-bookkeeping, 2026): $300-700/month
Intuit 10-K and investor materials documenting QuickBooks Live competitive positioning

pi_moat.rationale: Zero licensure for bookkeeping — anyone may practice. QuickBooks Live and Xero provide a national published price floor/ceiling that prevents margin retention when AI cuts costs. Switching cost is very low: a client's books live in QuickBooks/Xero and are trivially portable. Personal relationship with the owner-bookkeeper is the main moat, but this transfers poorly in an acquisition. The EA credential (enrolled agent) is a partial moat for IRS representation work, but this is the smaller sub-segment. pi_moat = 0.25.
sources: NACPB / AIPB: no licensure required for bookkeeping (any state)
QuickBooks Live published pricing as visible price ceiling

t50_years.derivation: Current adoption: ~55% of accounting/bookkeeping firms are materially using AI in automatable functions (QuickBooks Intuit Assist and Xero AI categorization are embedded by default in the platforms that host most bookkeepers' work — this is not a deliberate adoption choice but a platform feature rollout). Accounting Today 2026 survey and industry statistics consistently report 60-68% of small accounting firms (1-10 employees) using AI tools. Given 55%+ already past the threshold, t50 is effectively already crossed or 1-2 years away for the tail that hasn't yet engaged embedded AI features. Historical analog: QuickBooks Online itself reached ~50% of eligible US SMBs within ~5 years of meaningful push (2013 launch, ~50% by 2018). AI features embedded into existing subscriptions adopt much faster — closer to 2-3 years from rollout to 50%+ engagement. Central estimate: 2.0 years from now (2028) for the full industry tail to reach 50% material AI use, given most are already using it or have automatic access via their platform subscription.

current_adoption_pct.source: Multiple accounting technology surveys 2025-2026 (ReceiptsAI compilation; Accounting Today / Firm of the Future 2026 survey)
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: 60% of accounting firms now report using some form of AI in their practice; small firms (1-10 employees) 68% adoption rate (2026 accounting AI statistics compilation). 46% of accountants use AI daily (up from 18% in 2023). QuickBooks Intuit Assist and Xero AI categorization are embedded in platforms used by most bookkeeping firms. Calibrated to 55% material adoption for 541219 firms accounting for the smaller sole-prop tail.

historical_analogs.value: QuickBooks Online adoption: from ~5% (2013 launch) to ~50% of eligible US small businesses by ~2018 (~5 years). AI features embedded in existing subscriptions adopt faster (1-2 year rollout to 50%+ engagement). Cloud payroll analog: 12 years to ~65% enterprise.
historical_analogs.source: Intuit investor materials; Precedence Research cloud payroll market; Accounting Today surveys
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: 2026 Accountant Technology Survey (Firm of the Future): significant majority of accounting firms report AI tool use in 2026; QuickBooks AI and Xero reconciliation AI cited as primary adoption vectors.

owners_60plus_pct.source: Census Annual Business Survey 2022; Controllers Council / AICPA demographic reporting
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: Almost 75% of the CPA workforce met retirement age in 2020 (AICPA). Census ABS 2022: 52.3% of US business owners 55+; 22.8% 65+. Estimated 60+ fraction ~38% (half of 55-64 cohort at ~14.75% + all 65+ at 22.8%). Accounting/bookkeeping owner demographic is well-documented as aging — the Controllers Council cites 75% of CPAs near retirement as evidence of the aging principal class.

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: Controllers Council 2025: 75% of CPAs met retirement age by 2020, with succession as an acute documented shortage. Gallup 2024: 78% of small business owners lack a formal succession plan. Project Equity: 2.9M employer firms owned by 55+ owners facing succession pressure. The AICPA and NACPB member surveys consistently document the lack of next-generation succession in small accounting practices.

active_consolidators.source: PE accounting consolidator tracking; PrivSource and industry press 2024-2025
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: PE-backed accounting transactions hit 65 in 2024 and 104 in 2025, driven by platforms like Baker Tilly, Citrin Cooperman, Cherry Bekaert, Aprio, and Marcum — primarily targeting CPA firms (541211). Platforms specifically targeting 541219 bookkeeping/outsourced accounting: Bench (VC-backed), Pilot.com (VC-backed), FinancePal, Decimal (formerly AccountingDept.com, PE-backed), OutsourcePartners International. Counting 5 PE-backed or growth-equity platforms doing add-on acquisitions specifically in the bookkeeping/other-accounting segment (541219) in the last 24 months.

buy_mult.source: Accounting practice broker data (Naab Consulting, Poe Group Advisors, CT Acquisitions 2025-2026); Auxo Capital advisors
buy_mult.figure_quoted: Small accounting/bookkeeping practices sell at 0.7x-1.0x gross revenue or 1.8-3.2x SDE for owner-operated firms. Multi-partner CAS/outsourced accounting platforms: 4.5-8.0x normalized EBITDA. For the n_band (100-499 employee, avg EBITDA ~$2.16M): these are institutionalized outsourced accounting firms, not solo bookkeepers — trade closer to 3.0-5.0x EBITDA. Conservative buy_mult = 3.0x EBITDA after EBITDA normalization (adding back market-rate owner comp vs. SDE basis).

exit_mult.source: CT Acquisitions 2026 PE multiples report; Auxo Capital accounting firm M&A guide 2026
exit_mult.figure_quoted: PE platform accounting exits 2025: mid-tier CAS/outsourced accounting 6.5-8.0x EBITDA (Auxo); PE-backed platforms 8-12x EBITDA at exit; headline transactions (Citrin/Blackstone) at ~15x for top-tier CPA platforms. 541219 bookkeeping/outsourced platforms are below CPA firm tier (no licensure moat); exit_mult = 9.0x as mid-point of 6.5-12x range applicable to scalable outsourced accounting with recurring revenue.

pricing_structure.value: Monthly fixed fees ($200-1,500/month for SMB bookkeeping) and hourly rates ($25-100/hr) in a zero-licensure market. QuickBooks Live sets a national visible price ceiling at $300-700/month. Offshore substitution has compressed pricing. No cost-plus or percentage-of-value billing; fixed monthly fee is the standard, making AI efficiency savings directly competed away by platform pricing benchmarks.
pricing_structure.source: QuickBooks Live pricing (Dancing Numbers 2026); bookkeeping market surveys
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: QuickBooks Live Full-Service: $300/month (up to $10k monthly expenses), $500/month ($10k-$50k), $700/month (over $50k) plus QBO subscription. This is the effective national price ceiling for independent bookkeeping firms serving comparable SMB clients.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: V_raw = 0.44232, so AI could free 44.23pp of revenue. A = 5.0 (>=5). Uplift 44.23pp >> 3pp, so no tension. The combination of very high AI value (V=10, ceiling) and fast adoption (A=5) is consistent: bookkeeping is the industry where AI adoption is fastest and the value most concentrated, because the core product (monthly close/reconciliation) is already being natively automated inside accounting platforms. The high uplift and fast adoption are mutually reinforcing, not contradictory.

terminal_value.justification: The dominant sub-segment (bookkeeping/write-up) faces product-level replacement, not just cost reduction. Intuit (QuickBooks Live + Intuit Assist AI) and Xero are automating the monthly close, bank reconciliation, and transaction categorization natively inside the platforms that host the clients' books — the seller of the ledger is the automator. AI cuts the cost of producing the close but more importantly is deleting the close as a purchasable external service for standard SMBs. In 5-10 years, the residual demand for a paid human bookkeeper will be confined to: (1) cleanup of genuinely messy books (edge cases, M&A, reconstruction), (2) fractional-controller/advisory for clients needing judgment beyond data categorization, and (3) EA-credentialed IRS representation (a distinct sub-segment). A firm whose core product was the monthly close for standard SMBs cannot survive on those remnants alone. Classify MELTING: the product itself is being replaced, not merely delivered more cheaply.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V scored from labor-cost replacement analysis of specific bookkeeping functions (categorization, reconciliation, close, billing automation) — benchmarked against QuickBooks/Xero AI automation data and Symmetry/accounting-tech surveys. No reference to PE deal activity or consolidation trends. A scored from current_adoption_pct (accounting tech survey data) and historical analog (QuickBooks Online adoption timeline) — no reference to M&A or deal volume.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Bookkeeping is billed at monthly fixed fees ($200-1,500/mo) benchmarked against QuickBooks Live's published national price points ($300-700/mo). This is a visible deflation ceiling: when AI cuts the hours required to deliver a monthly close, competition from QuickBooks Live and offshore providers forces independent firms to pass savings to clients in lower fees rather than retain as margin. Zero licensure means no barrier prevents clients from switching to the platform directly. Modeled via pi_dist = 0.30 (Intuit/platform holds de facto client control through ledger ownership) and pi_moat = 0.25 (no licensure, trivial switching, visible national price ceiling). Both are depressed to reflect severe deflation pass-through.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A = min(10, 10/t50): at t50=1 yr A=10.0, S~5.4; at t50=4 yrs A=2.5, S~3.8. The 1-4 yr range shifts S from ~3.8 to ~5.4.
C = 10 × pi_dist × 0.25: at pi_dist=0.20 C=0.50, S~4.3; at pi_dist=0.45 C=1.125, S~5.1. Platform control is the central uncertainty.
M: at buy=2.0 M=clamp(4×(9-2)/2,0,10)=clamp(14,0,10)=10.0; at buy=5.0 M=clamp(4×4/5,0,10)=3.2. Large range due to sparse comp set for 541219-specific bookkeeping platforms on EBITDA basis.

evidence.reviewer_note: Catch-all code: 541219 covers (1) bookkeeping/write-up services (dominant — ~35,754 of 45,390 firms have <5 employees; these are overwhelmingly sole-prop bookkeepers), (2) billing and invoicing services (~10% of segment by revenue), (3) non-CPA accounting/enrolled agent services (~15%). Score reflects the dominant sub-segment (bookkeeping/write-up) per Core Rule 5. Role-mix warning: provided 4-digit OEWS is CPA-firm dominated (13-2011 Accountants 34.2%) and badly misrepresents this code; bookkeeping clerks and owner-operator bookkeepers dominate the actual wage bill. ai_replaceable_share built from prompt hypothesis structure (bookkeepers 45%, owner-operators 25%, billing 10%, payroll/tax 10%, admin 10%). n_band note: dataset methodology puts the n_band firms in 100-499 and 500+ employee classes — these are not local bookkeeping sole-props but institutionalized outsourced accounting/offshore delivery firms; the acquirable pool is actually much larger if solo practitioners with $1-10M EBITDA were counted (most fall below n_band because average revenue per very-small firm is ~$191K with EBITDA ~$30K). The reported n_band = 531 likely understates the real acquirable count of practices worth acquiring. buy_mult quality is MED given sparse 541219-specific EBITDA comp set; normalized from SDE-basis broker data. terminal_value = MELTING on primary sub-segment (bookkeeping); EA representation sub-segment would score PRESSURED independently. S reflects the combination of very high AI value (ceiling V=10), fast adoption (A=5), very weak capture (C=0.75 is low), good buy/sell spread (M=8), and a large though institutionalized n_band pool (B~7). The low C drags S to ~4.6 despite high V, A, and M — correct, as the fundamental problem is that AI efficiency cannot be retained as margin in a zero-licensure market with published national price competition from the platform that owns the ledger.

n_band.derivation: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $263,174 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541310); class <5: 13,996 firms, avg revenue/firm $377,518 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $59,082 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 3,719 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,387,500 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $217,144 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 1,928 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,115,513 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $487,578 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 1,361 firms, avg revenue/firm $10,632,600 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,664,002 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 228 firms, avg revenue/firm $44,196,899 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,916,815 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 62 firms, avg revenue/firm $111,625,710 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $17,469,424 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 1,589
n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Reconstructed for architecture-office mix (not 4-digit 5413 which is engineering-dominated; at the parent 5413 level, 17-1011 Architects are only 5.82% of wages vs. the ~40% that architecture firms actually employ). Licensed architects own stamp, code responsibility, client trust, and design judgment — none automatable. But construction-document production (CD phase, ~40% of fee) is the most automatable work in the firm: AI BIM/permit-set tooling directly targets this. Estimating: SD phase (~15% of hours) ~5% automatable, DD (~20%) ~20% automatable, CDs (~40%) ~65% automatable, CA (~25%) ~5% automatable. Weighted: 0.15*0.05 + 0.20*0.20 + 0.40*0.65 + 0.25*0.05 = 0.0075+0.04+0.26+0.0125 = 0.32; rounded down to 0.25 to be conservative given principal-level work dominance within this role label.

Reconstructed share: at 4-digit 5413, 17-3011 Architectural and Civil Drafters are 3.28% of wages; within a pure architecture office, drafter/junior designer staff plausibly ~18% (hypothesis per prompt). Construction-document production is the most directly automatable task in architecture: generative BIM and permit-set AI tools target this exactly. 70% automatable — 30% not automatable covers field observations, client-facing markups, and judgment calls that require licensed oversight.

At 4-digit 5413, 13-1082 Project Management Specialists are 6.46% of wages; architecture-only hypothesis ~10%. Consultant coordination and construction administration (site visits, submittals, RFIs) persist in the human/relationship half; the paperwork half (meeting minutes, RFI tracking, submittal logs, change order documentation) automates. 35% automatable.

At 4-digit 5413, 11-1021 General and Operations Managers 5.72% + 11-9041 Architectural and Engineering Managers 5.53% = ~11%; architecture-only hypothesis ~10%. Client relationships and firm leadership are not automatable. Internal reporting, scheduling, financial dashboarding, and HR administration automate at ~20% of this role's labor.

breakdown_by_role.role: Interior designers / spec writers / visualization staff
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Not in 5413 top-10 OEWS; estimated ~10% of architecture-office wages (hypothesis). Renderings and specification assembly are already AI-commoditized (Midjourney-class generative tools handle visualization; AI spec writers exist). Client taste-making, material selection judgment, and FF&E relationships are not automatable. 45% automatable.

Not in 5413 top-10; estimated ~12% of architecture-office wages (hypothesis per prompt 12-15%, using 12%). Proposals, qualifications packages, scheduling, invoicing, and bookkeeping are heavily automatable. 65% automatable — 35% not automatable reflects human client outreach, relationship management, and firm branding decisions.

pi_dist.rationale: Architecture firms typically hold the prime contract in commercial/institutional projects — client relationship is firm-owned but principal-dependent and erodes on leadership departure. Design-build growth (GC as prime) is shifting architecture increasingly to sub-consultant positions, especially in commercial. Hourly and fixed-fee billing (93% of firms use hourly, 92% fixed fee per AIA data) means AI efficiency savings are partially visible: hourly contracts automatically pass savings to clients (faster work = fewer hours billed); fixed-fee contracts capture margin short-term but are renegotiated downward at the next project. Historical CAD/BIM transitions show that eventually productivity gains reduce fee benchmarks. Residential and light-commercial segments (material share of small-firm revenue) have weak client-relationship hold because licensure is not required and clients can easily substitute. Moderate control: 0.55. Range: 0.45-0.65.
sources: AIA resource center: Charging for services (fee structure data) — aia.org/resource-center/charging-services (accessed 2026-07-20)
AIA ABI billings data — aia.org (accessed 2026-07-20)

pi_moat.rationale: State licensure for non-residential buildings requires a licensed architect in responsible charge in virtually all US jurisdictions — this is a real regulatory floor (practice act, not title act). However: (1) Residential and light-commercial exemptions are broad — the IRC's licensed-professional exemption covers the majority of single-family and small multi-family projects, which form material revenue for small firms; (2) AI tools (TestFit, Architype, Sidewalk Labs Delve class) are advancing toward code-compliant permit-set generation that could reduce the complexity gatekeeping; (3) design-build contractors increasingly commoditize the stamp-plus-CD product at a sub-fee rate; (4) switching costs are modest — clients can re-procure at project start. The licensure floor prevents full commoditization but the ceiling on what the monopoly commands is eroding. 0.50 moat reflects a real but contested barrier.
sources: NCARB By The Numbers 2025 (licensure demographics) — ncarb.org/sites/default/files/NBTN-2025.pdf (accessed 2026-07-20)
AIA practice resources on licensure scope — aia.org (accessed 2026-07-20)

t50_years.derivation: Current material adoption: AIA March 2025 survey (in collaboration with Deltek/ConstructConnect) shows only 8% of architecture firms have implemented AI into practice; Deltek Clarity 46th Annual A&E Study (May 2025) shows 53% leveraging AI broadly but only 38% reporting measurable returns — the 8% figure is the material-use baseline. Historical analog: BIM adoption in architecture. Large firms went from ~43% BIM use in 2005 to ~96% by 2016 (McGraw-Hill/NBS data), achieving ~50% adoption around 2008-2010 — roughly 5-6 years from 10% to 50%. However, BIM was firm-level infrastructure requiring significant investment; AI tools are subscription-based and lower barrier but face professional inertia, liability concerns, and small-firm resource constraints. Adjusting BIM analog (5-6 years from 10% to 50%) for AI starting lower (8% vs 10%), faster diffusion rate, but offsetting small-firm inertia — central estimate 7 years from 2026 (to 2033). Consistent with A&E sector AI adoption tripling YOY in Deltek 2025 data suggesting acceleration, but small architecture firms historically 3-5 years behind large firms.

current_adoption_pct.source: AIA / Deltek / ConstructConnect research on AI in architecture (March 2025)
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: 8% of firm leaders have integrated AI into their practices; 20% actively implementing; 6% of individual architects use AI regularly

historical_analogs.value: BIM adoption in US architecture: large firms from ~43% (2005) to ~96% (2016), reaching ~50% around 2008-2010 (~5-6 years from ~10% to 50%). Small firms lagged 3-5 years further. CAD similarly took ~10 years from ~1982 to domination by ~1992.
historical_analogs.source: McGraw-Hill Construction / NBS BIM studies; ASCE civil engineering source reporting AEC slow AI adoption (December 2025)
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: Large firms showed 96 percent fully engaged in BIM in 2016, more than double the percentage of only 43 percent in 2005 — suggesting 50% milestone was achieved approximately 2008-2010 for large firms

owners_60plus_pct.source: NCARB By the Numbers 2025; AIA member demographic reporting; secondary: archpaper.com architecture succession coverage (June 2026)
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: Architects over age 65 make up nearly 13% of the total architect pool (NCARB 2025); average AIA member age is 54; baby boomer retirement wave documented as demographic pressure. Estimating 60+ share at ~28% based on: 13% over 65, adding the 60-64 cohort approximately equal to the 65-69 cohort given typical cohort distribution, yielding ~28% ESTIMATE.

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: AIA 2024 Firm Survey: 84% of firms with 50+ employees have a transition plan, but a 'dramatic shortage of qualified and interested successors' is documented. Baby boomer retirement wave described as creating urgent succession pressure. Source: archpaper.com (June 2026) citing 'current firm owners and leaders must focus on the more urgent need for future leaders despite a dramatic shortage of qualified and interested successors.'

active_consolidators.source: Morrissey Goodale 2024 M&A Mid-Year Review; Zweig Group AEC M&A tracking; Capstone Partners AEC sector update
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: PE-backed buyers accounted for 42% of ~476 US AEC deals in 2024. Active AEC platforms include Verdantas (8 deals 2024), Salas O'Brien, IMEG, SLR, Bowman Consulting, Celnor Group (7 deals each). Signal Hill Equity Partners acquired MM Architects. Estimating 8 PE-backed platforms actively acquiring architecture firm add-ons in 24-month window based on proportion of architecture firms within AEC deal mix and named active platforms.

buy_mult.source: Zweig Group 2025 Valuation Report of AEC Firms; ValuAdder architecture valuation blog; ROG Partners A/E valuation study
buy_mult.figure_quoted: The value/EBITDA metric rose slightly from 4.23 to 4.28 in the 2025 Zweig report (industry-wide AEC average). Small architecture firms without exceptional characteristics see 2-3x EBITDA; founder-led firms with limited depth command 3.5-5.5x per Auxo Capital AEC guide. Entry multiple for small architecture firm estimated at 4.0x normalized EBITDA (after market-rate owner comp). Note: Zweig quotes these as value/EBITDA which includes owner compensation normalization per report methodology.

exit_mult.source: ROG Partners A/E Business Valuation and M&A Transaction Study; Morrissey Goodale AEC M&A data; Auxo Capital AEC valuation guide
exit_mult.figure_quoted: Engineering and architecture post-IIJA median EBITDA multiple 5.52x, upper quartile 7.50x (ROG Partners/Morrissey Goodale). Established lower middle-market AEC firms with depth command 5.5-7.5x EBITDA. Platform-level architecture+engineering firms (multi-office, $10M+ EBITDA) estimated at 6.5x — between the 5.52x median and 7.50x upper quartile, reflecting architecture's slightly lower multiple than engineering due to licensure-concentrated risk and thinner margins.

pricing_structure.value: 93% hourly/fixed-fee mix; 92% use stipulated sum (fixed fee); 28% use percentage of construction cost. AIA B101 now ties fees to owner's budget at contract signing. Construction administration budgeted at 25-30% of fee. Most small firms are hourly or lump-sum.
pricing_structure.source: AIA resource center on fee structures; AIA charging for services resource (citing 2024 AIA Firm Survey data)
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: 93% of firms use hourly rates (with or without agreed maximum) and 92% use stipulated sum (fixed fee); 42% use hourly regularly and 50% occasionally; 70% use stipulated sum regularly. Just 28% reported using percentage of construction cost.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: V_raw = 18.685% of revenue represents the theoretical maximum labor cost reduction if all automatable tasks are captured as margin. A = 1.43 (t50=7 years) is well below 5, so the cross-check tension flag does not trigger. No inconsistency: slow adoption (low A) is consistent with a large theoretical uplift — the uplift is real but slow to materialize.

terminal_value.justification: Licensure requirements (practice acts in most states for non-residential construction) preserve a regulatory floor — licensed architects in responsible charge cannot be eliminated by AI for building-permit submissions in the vast majority of jurisdictions. This prevents MELTING. However, the fee pool is being squeezed from multiple sides: (1) AI is dismantling the value of CD production (the largest fee phase) by automating permit-set generation — this reduces the cost of the output the firm sells without eliminating the requirement for it; (2) design-build growth shifts prime contract to GCs, reducing architect to sub-stamp-provider with fee-taker economics; (3) residential and light-commercial exemptions (material share of small firm revenue) leave significant segments outside the practice-act floor, where AI design-to-permit tools can bypass architects entirely. The decisive mechanism: AI cuts the firm's production cost but clients and design-builders reprice the stamped set downward once production cost visibly drops (BIM precedent). Demand shrinks in dollar terms but does not disappear — PRESSURED.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V was derived entirely from reconstructed role labor shares (rebuilt for architecture offices, not from deal data) multiplied by task-level automatable fractions based on phase analysis (SD/DD/CD/CA breakdown). A was derived from AIA/Deltek current adoption data and BIM historical timeline — no reference to PE deal volumes, consolidation waves, or M&A trends. Neither V nor A incorporated any deal-activity information.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Architecture billing is 93% hourly/fixed-fee with only 28% using percentage-of-construction-cost (AIA fee structure data). Hourly billing mechanically passes AI savings to clients: faster production = fewer hours billed at the same rate. Fixed-fee contracts provide short-term margin capture but reset downward at next procurement as AI-enabled competitors underbid historical fee benchmarks. BIM precedent confirms eventual deflation: CAD and BIM both led to downward fee pressure once productivity gains became visible and competitor pricing adjusted. This dynamic is reflected in pi_dist = 0.55 (moderate control with erosion risk) and pi_moat = 0.50 (licensure floor with contested ceiling).

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: At t50=5: A=2.00, S rises to ~3.86. At t50=10: A=1.00, S falls to ~3.07. The t50 range (5-10 years) moves S by approximately +/-0.4 around the central estimate of 3.39.

top_uncertain_inputs.plausible_range: 0.33-0.47 (varies with licensed-architect automatable fraction 0.20-0.30 and drafter fraction 0.60-0.80)

evidence.reviewer_note: Role-mix rebuild: The provided 4-digit 5413 OEWS role_mix is engineering-dominated (Civil Engineers 12.8% of wages; Architects only 5.82%). Within a pure architecture office (541310), architecture-specific occupations dominate — licensed architects + principals ~40%, drafters/junior designers ~18%, PMs ~10%, management ~10%, interior/spec/viz ~10%, admin ~12%. These are hypothesis fractions per prompt guidance, not census-verified. The rebuild materially raises ai_replaceable_share relative to a naive reading of the 4-digit mix. Buy/exit multiples estimated from Zweig AEC-wide average (4.28x) and ROG Partners/Morrissey Goodale post-IIJA median (5.52x) — architecture-specific multiples may differ (potentially lower due to thinner margins and ownership-restriction complexity). Owner-age estimate (28% over 60) is an ESTIMATE derived from NCARB's 13%-over-65 figure plus assumed cohort size for 60-64 — no direct primary source with 60+ cutoff found. PE-backed architecture consolidators (8) is also an estimate; architecture receives fewer dedicated PE platforms than engineering/environmental sub-sectors. Ownership restrictions: several states restrict architecture firm ownership to licensed professionals — this complicates pure PE buyout structures but does not preclude A&E consolidator bolt-ons (Bowman, Verdantas etc. use licensed principals as equity holders).

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Reconstructed for landscape architecture office (the provided 4-digit 5413 OEWS mix omits 17-1012 Landscape Architects from the top-10 entirely — it is engineering-dominated; the signature occupation sits in the unlisted tail). Within a pure LA office, licensed LAs plus principals are ~45% of wages (hypothesis per prompt guidance 40-50%). Stamp, grading/stormwater documents, public-realm approvals, agency coordination, and client relationships stay human. Planting plans, renderings, and specification production are AI-exposed. Automatable fraction ~25%: production drawing phases (~40% of time, ~60% of that automatable) yield ~24%; other phases ~5% → rounded to 25%.

At 4-digit 5413, 17-3011 Architectural and Civil Drafters are 3.28% of wages; within a pure LA office, design/drafter staff plausibly ~18% (hypothesis). Site-plan production, planting schedules, and renders are directly automatable — generative site-design tools target exactly this. 65% automatable; 35% not automatable covers field-dependent decisions, agency-specific requirements, and licensed-LA judgment overlays.

At 4-digit 5413, 13-1082 PMs are 6.46% of wages; LA-office hypothesis ~10%. Agency coordination, community meetings, approvals, and construction observation coordination persist as human work. Submittal logs, meeting minutes, change-order tracking, and RFI documentation are automatable. 30% automatable (slightly lower than architecture PMs given more field coordination in LA practice).

At 4-digit 5413, 47-4011 Construction Inspectors 2.05%; LA-office field staff hypothesis ~10%. Physical site presence for construction observation, plant-material approval, irrigation inspection, and punch lists requires human presence and cannot be substantially automated by current AI. Only ~5% automatable (post-visit reporting, punch-list documentation).

Not in 5413 top-10 OEWS; estimated ~17% of LA-office wages (hypothesis per prompt ~15%, adjusted up slightly to reach 100% total). RFP responses and proposal packages dominate — highly automatable with current AI writing tools. Administrative scheduling, invoicing, and routine correspondence also automatable. 65% automatable; 35% not automatable reflects client relationship outreach, firm branding, and BD strategy.

pi_dist.rationale: Sub-consultant economics are central to this code. A material share (estimated 40-50%) of LA firms work as sub-consultants to architects, engineers, or developer teams who hold the prime contract: when AI cuts design-production hours, the prime simply re-scopes or re-bids the LA sub-fee downward, passing AI savings to the client chain rather than letting the LA firm capture margin. Municipal and public-realm projects where the LA holds the prime offer stronger control, but these require firm size and reputation beyond most of the 5,041 firms in this code. Discretionary residential and commercial amenity design (a large share of small-firm revenue) has even weaker client relationship control — landscape contractors offer design-build competition that further commoditizes the LA fee. Score of 0.45 reflects: moderate control in the prime-held institutional segment, weak control in the sub-consultant and discretionary residential segments. Range: 0.35-0.55.
sources: ASLA 2024 Landscape Architecture Industry Report — firm structure and prime/sub economics
ASLA field resources on fee structure — asla.org (accessed 2026-07-20)

pi_moat.rationale: Most US states (46-47 of 50) operate practice acts requiring a licensed landscape architect for certain regulated work (grading, stormwater, public rights-of-way, entitlement drawings). Only ~3-4 states have title acts (Maine, Massachusetts, and disputed others). This creates a regulatory floor similar to architecture. However, the floor is weaker than architecture for several reasons: (1) Discretionary planting and amenity design — the dominant revenue source for many small LA firms — is broadly accessible to landscape contractors and designers without a license as long as they don't use the title 'landscape architect'; (2) landscape contractor design-build (NALP/561730) is the primary bypass channel, and PE consolidation in that segment (TruGreen, Davey Tree, etc.) is vigorous; (3) AI generative site-design tools can produce planting plans and visualization materials that substitute for the non-regulated design work; (4) LA firm brand is highly principal-dependent at the small-firm scale. The regulated core (stormwater, grading, public procurement) provides a real but limited moat. 0.45 moat reflects a contested and narrowing barrier. Range: 0.35-0.55.
sources: ASLA state licensure adoption resources — asla.org/resources/library/archives-blog/state-licensure-adoption (accessed 2026-07-20)
CLARB licensure requirements — clarb.org/licensure/ (accessed 2026-07-20)

t50_years.derivation: Current material adoption: ASLA 2024 Digital Technology Survey shows ~40% of respondents cite AI tools as used; IFLA/ASLA/CSLA 2025 global survey reports 43.4% using AI in practice. However, the dominant uses are background research, proposal drafting, and predesign work — not core design production. Material adoption in automatable production functions estimated at ~15% (a subset of the 43% broad adopters who have embedded AI in production workflows). Historical analog: BIM reached ~50% adoption in large AEC firms ~5-6 years from ~10% baseline; small LA firms lagged by 3-5 years further. LA firms are predominantly small (3,549 of 5,041 under 5 employees) with limited IT resources, creating higher inertia relative to architecture. Central estimate: 8 years from 2026 to 50% material adoption (to 2034), 1 year slower than architecture due to smaller firm size and more field-dominant workflow. Plausible range: 6-12 years.

current_adoption_pct.source: IFLA / ASLA / CSLA 2025 AI in Landscape Architecture Survey; ASLA 2024 Digital Technology Software Survey
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: 43.4% of respondents reported using AI in their practice (IFLA/ASLA/CSLA 2025 global survey); ASLA 2024 Digital Technology Survey: ~40% cite AI tools as currently used. Most common uses are background research (50%), proposal drafting (47%), predesign work (41%) — not core production design functions. Material adoption in automatable production functions estimated at 15% (a conservative estimate given that the 43% figure includes chatbot and research tools, not embedded production-workflow adoption).

historical_analogs.value: BIM adoption in AEC: large firms from ~43% (2005) to ~96% (2016), reaching ~50% around 2008-2010 (~5-6 years from ~10% to 50%). Small LA firms lag further — reaching 50% would take 3-5 additional years versus large firms. CAD took ~10 years to dominate in the 1980s-1990s.
historical_analogs.source: ASCE Civil Engineering Source — AEC sector slow AI adoption survey (December 2025); ASLA 2024 Digital Technology Survey context
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: Architecture, engineering, construction sector slow to adopt AI (ASCE December 2025); BIM adoption reference: large AEC firms 43% in 2005 to 96% in 2016, with 50% milestone circa 2008-2010

owners_60plus_pct.source: ASLA 2024 Landscape Architecture Industry Report; ASLA succession planning resources; AIA member age data (proxy for LA profession)
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: ASLA 2024 Landscape Architecture Industry Report: 'one in four firms undergo ownership transitions'; 72% of respondents say they do not have a succession plan; baby boomer retirement wave creating demographic pressure across AEC professions. Average AIA member age 54 (proxy). Estimating 30% of LA firm owners over 60 based on: baby boomer demographic wave, average AIA member age 54 implies significant share near or past 60, LA profession has historically older ownership given small-firm founder structures. ESTIMATE.

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: ASLA 2024 Landscape Architecture Industry Report documents that 72% of respondents do not have a succession plan, while one in four firms are actively in ownership transition — indicating an active succession shortage. ASLA offers 'What! Retire?' succession guidance courses, and the ASLA 2024 report case study on Mayer/Reed highlights the difficulty of finding and financially qualifying successors. This is a documented succession shortage more severe than architecture (where 84% of large firms have plans).

active_consolidators.source: Morrissey Goodale AEC M&A tracking; Zweig Group AEC M&A reports; businesswire.com Atwell/Terrascape acquisition (February 2025)
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: Atwell acquired Terrascape Consulting (landscape architecture and planning, Southwest, February 2025); Kimley-Horn acquired Gamble Design Collaborative (landscape architecture, Tennessee); Bowman acquired Project Design Consultants (civil engineering and landscape architecture). Dedicated landscape-architecture-only PE platforms are essentially absent — LA firms appear primarily as capability bolt-ons within engineering/AEC consolidators. Estimating 3 PE-backed platforms actively doing LA-firm add-ons in the 24-month window.

buy_mult.source: Zweig Group 2025 Valuation Report of AEC Firms; ROG Partners A/E Valuation Study; ValuAdder architecture firm valuation blog
buy_mult.figure_quoted: Zweig AEC-wide value/EBITDA average 4.28x (2025); small AEC firms without exceptional characteristics see 2-3x EBITDA (ValuAdder); founder-led firms with limited depth command 3.5-5.5x per AEC valuation guides. LA firms are typically smaller, more principal-dependent, and less institutionalized than engineering firms — entry multiple estimated at 3.5x normalized EBITDA (below AEC average of 4.28x). Normalized after market-rate owner/principal compensation.

exit_mult.source: ROG Partners A/E Business Valuation and M&A Transaction Study; Morrissey Goodale AEC M&A data
exit_mult.figure_quoted: Post-IIJA AEC median EBITDA multiple 5.52x (ROG Partners/Morrissey Goodale); upper quartile 7.50x. Landscape architecture platforms (multi-office, $10M+ EBITDA) are rare and typically exit as components of AEC consolidators rather than standalone. Exit multiple estimated at 5.5x — below the AEC median (5.52x) due to: smaller LA firm universe (n_band=46), limited dedicated PE platform competition for LA firms specifically, and lower EBITDA durability in discretionary design segments.

pricing_structure.value: Predominantly hourly and fixed-fee (lump sum), consistent with broader AEC professional services. Sub-consultant billing common when LA firm is not prime. Percentage of construction cost rarely used. Public/municipal projects often hourly not-to-exceed. Discretionary residential design frequently fixed or hourly.
pricing_structure.source: ASLA profession resources; AIA fee structure data (proxy for LA billing patterns); ASLA 2024 Landscape Architecture Industry Report
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: LA firms bill primarily hourly or fixed fee; sub-consultant arrangements dominate in multi-disciplinary projects; public procurement typically hourly not-to-exceed. No ASLA-specific fee-structure survey with % breakdowns found for 2024-2025; using AIA data as proxy (93% hourly, 92% fixed fee) with adjustment for LA's higher sub-consultant rate.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: V_raw = 18.33% of revenue represents the theoretical maximum labor cost reduction if all automatable tasks are captured as margin. A = 1.25 (t50=8 years) is well below 5, so the cross-check tension flag does not trigger. No inconsistency: slow adoption rate (low A) is consistent with a large theoretical uplift — the savings potential exists but materializes slowly given the small-firm, field-intensive character of landscape architecture.

terminal_value.justification: The regulated core of landscape architecture — grading, stormwater engineering, public-realm entitlement documents, and ADA compliance on public projects — requires a licensed LA in responsible charge in most practice-act states and will not be bypassed by AI alone. This prevents full MELTING. However: (1) The discretionary design product (planting plans, amenity design, visualization) — a large share of small-firm revenue — is highly AI-exposed and also accessible to unlicensed landscape contractors who serve most of the residential and commercial market; (2) Landscape contractor design-build (NALP/561730) is PE-consolidated and growing, directly displacing LA firms in the discretionary segment; (3) AI generative site-design tools can produce planting schedules and renders that substitute for the non-regulated design work; (4) Sub-consultant economics mean LA fee pools are compressed from above by architect/engineer primes. The deciding mechanism: in title-act states (3-4 states), the discretionary product is already unprotected; in practice-act states, the stamp protects only the regulated documents while AI erodes the rest of the fee. Demand for regulated LA services survives; demand for discretionary LA design shrinks materially — PRESSURED.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V was derived from reconstructed role labor shares (rebuilt for a landscape-architecture office, flagged as hypothesis) multiplied by task-level automatable fractions based on occupation-specific analysis (field staff treated as non-automatable; drafter/design roles treated as highly automatable). A was derived from IFLA/ASLA current adoption data and BIM historical timeline. Neither V nor A incorporated any reference to deal activity, PE platforms, or consolidation trends.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Landscape architecture firms bill primarily hourly or fixed-fee, with a significant sub-consultant tier where the prime (architect or engineer) controls fee levels. Hourly billing automatically passes AI production savings to clients (fewer hours billed). Fixed-fee sub-contracts are renegotiated downward by the prime at each project as AI-enabled competitors reduce their bids. Sub-consultant economics are the structural mechanism by which AI savings flow up the chain rather than being retained by the LA firm. pi_dist = 0.45 reflects this sub-consultant exposure; pi_moat = 0.45 reflects the practice-act regulatory floor offset by the large discretionary design segment that AI and landscape contractors threaten to bypass.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: At t50=6: A=1.667, S rises to ~3.14. At t50=12: A=0.833, S falls to ~2.70. The t50 range (6-12 years) moves S by approximately +/-0.22 around the central estimate of 2.90.

top_uncertain_inputs.plausible_range: 0.31-0.44 (varies with licensed LA automatable fraction 0.20-0.30 and drafter fraction 0.55-0.75)

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: At pi_dist=0.35: C=1.575, S~2.63. At pi_dist=0.55: C=2.475, S~3.07. Sub-consultant share is the biggest driver of uncertainty in pi_dist.

evidence.reviewer_note: Role-mix rebuild: The provided 4-digit 5413 OEWS role_mix omits 17-1012 Landscape Architects from the top-10 entirely (the parent 5413 is engineering-dominated). The rebuild for a pure LA office is entirely hypothesis-based (per prompt guidance). Five-role simplification is structurally different from architecture — field/construction observation staff included as a non-automatable role (no parallel in 541310) while interior designers/spec writers excluded. The n_band=46 is very small and the 500+ class shows anomalously lower revenue/firm ($9.1M) than the 100-499 class ($23.6M) — a SUSB data suppression/noise artifact flagged in prompt — this may understate true n_band but is used as provided. Buy and exit multiples are both ESTIMATE quality as no landscape-architecture-specific transaction database was accessible; multiples are set below the Zweig AEC average (4.28x buy, AEC median 5.52x exit) to reflect LA firms' smaller size, higher principal dependence, and limited PE platform competition specifically targeting pure-play LA firms. Active consolidators (3) is conservative estimate — LA firms appear as bolt-ons within AEC consolidators (Atwell, Kimley-Horn, Bowman) rather than targets of dedicated LA PE platforms. Practice-act vs. title-act: confirmed ~46-47 practice-act states with only ~3-4 title-act states remaining, which is stronger than the prompt suggested as a concern — the regulatory floor is broader than feared, but the discretionary design gap (unregulated amenity/planting work) remains the critical exposure.

n_band.derivation: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $182,559 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541340); class <5: 2,510 firms, avg revenue/firm $232,175 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $36,335 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 257 firms, avg revenue/firm $967,078 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $151,348 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 124 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,459,411 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $384,898 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 47 firms, avg revenue/firm $6,149,234 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $962,355 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 9 firms, avg revenue/firm $21,641,667 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,386,921 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 9 firms, avg revenue/firm $6,164,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $964,666 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 9
n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%

breakdown_by_role.role: CAD drafters / BIM technicians (hypothesis: ~60% of wage bill within 541340)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: 2D/3D production drafting is the product itself and the single most AI-automatable service in this set. AutoCAD 2025/2026 AI features (Object Detection, BCONVERT, DraftAid) target exactly this work. AI tools claim 30-90% reduction in drawing time for repetitive drafting tasks. The 15% non-automatable residual is client-specific judgment (standard interpretation, unusual conditions), which shrinks further as AI improves. ROLE-MIX WARNING: the provided role_mix is 4-digit 5413 (engineering-dominated; 17-3011 drafters show only 3.28% of wage bill). Within 541340 proper, drafters/BIM technicians are the majority of the wage bill (this is a hypothesis/rebuild per prompt instruction; flagged in reviewer_note).

breakdown_by_role.role: Owners / managers (hypothesis: ~17% of wage bill within 541340)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Client account management (A/E firms and manufacturers) and production oversight automate partially (~20%). Account management, client relationship touches, estimating, and quality judgment persist. Owner compensation is largely SDE outside wages for micro-firms; included in labor_share estimate.

breakdown_by_role.role: Checkers / QC and standards staff (hypothesis: ~10% of wage bill within 541340)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Drawing QA is increasingly automated by model-checking tools (rule-based clash detection, drawing standard compliance). ~40% automatable. However, liability-driven human review of stamped deliverables and client-specific standard interpretation persist because the client's professional stamps the work.

breakdown_by_role.role: Field capture technicians (hypothesis: ~5% of wage bill within 541340)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Physical site presence for laser scanning, as-built measurement, and scan-to-BIM field work is not automatable. AI/drone augmentation speeds processing but the site visit remains required. Only report upload and processing are automatable (~8%). This is the defensible niche in 541340.

breakdown_by_role.role: Administrative staff (hypothesis: ~8% of wage bill within 541340)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Job intake, file transmittal, invoicing, and scheduling are highly automatable (~70%). Micro-firm admin is often owner-handled; residual 30% requires customer judgment.

pi_dist.rationale: Near-complete pass-through. Piece-rate and per-hour pricing against clients (A/E firms and manufacturers) who benchmark against offshore bureaus (India $10-25/hr, Philippines $12-30/hr). The offshore price floor has already demonstrated that when labor costs fall, competitive markets immediately lower per-drawing rates. AI is a second, steeper deflation on the same mechanism. No cost-plus or audited overhead structure exists; no on-call relationship lock-in beyond current-project volume. Clients can insource with generative CAD tools (Autodesk Fusion, AutoCAD 2026 AI) backed by a single internal PE. The firm retains virtually no pricing power from AI efficiency gains.
sources: Offshore 24/7 / HiTech BIM Services: CAD drafting rates $10-25/hr (India), $12-30/hr (Philippines) - 2024 price cards
Apollo Technical: AI CAD job market analysis - traditional drafting skills dropped from 50% to 31% of job requirements 2023-2024
Autodesk AI in Architecture & Engineering - generative CAD tooling enabling client insourcing

pi_moat.rationale: Minimal moat. No licensure: work is performed under the client's professional's seal; firm needs no PE of its own. No proprietary data or switching costs beyond current-project familiarity. No physical-presence requirement in the dominant revenue line (production drafting). The scan-to-BIM niche (physical field capture) provides a small moat for ~5% of revenue. Client relationships are purely transactional: A/E firms and manufacturers will defect to internal generative CAD as tooling matures. AI tooling is equally or more available to the client than to the bureau. The plan-stamp bypass pattern (client runs AI, bureau provides nothing of value) is a near-term existential threat, not a long-term risk.
sources: Prompt guidance: no licensure moat of any kind; work done under clients' professionals' stamps
BIM Heroes / Apollo Technical: AI replacing repetitive drafting tasks 2024-2026
Autodesk generative CAD roadmap: neural CAD foundation models for Fusion and Forma announced AU 2025

t50_years.derivation: Current material adoption among US-based drafting service firms: ESTIMATE 20% (no primary survey found for 541340 specifically; ADDA data not accessible; energent.ai AutoCAD AI adoption data suggests ~8% firm-level integration of AI into practice; I use 20% as generous estimate acknowledging faster AI penetration in this most-directly-exposed code). Historical analog: BIM adoption in AEC ~7-8 years to 50% (2002-2010); CAD adoption in drafting shops was faster (~5-7 years to 50% in 1980s). AI adoption in 541340 may be slower than the broader A&E trend because these firms are smaller and resource-constrained, but also because the most advanced AI drafting tools are now embeded in client-facing Autodesk software. Many firms will exit rather than adopt. Survivor-only adoption could reach 50% in 3-4 years; accounting for the full current-firm population including late adopters and exiters, 4.5 years. t50 derivation: from ~20% today, need ~30pp more; at the current acceleration rate in A&E (~15pp/year in broader market but slower for this micro-firm segment) => ~3-5 years. Use 4.5 years.

current_adoption_pct.source: Energent.ai AutoCAD AI solutions market report 2026; Deltek Clarity 46th Annual A&E Study 2025 (demand-side proxy); Apollo Technical CAD job market analysis 2024
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: Only 8% of firms currently integrating AI into [AutoCAD] practices, even though 78% want to learn more (Energent.ai 2026). Broader A&E: 53% leveraging AI tools (Deltek 2025). Using 20% as generous estimate for material AI use (actually replacing production labor cost) among US 541340 firms, acknowledging that the 8% figure is for AI-into-practice integration and the 53% A&E figure is for the demand-side clients.

historical_analogs.value: CAD adoption by drafting firms: ~5-7 years to 50% in 1980s-1990s (drafting shops were early and fast adopters because CAD is their product). BIM adoption in AEC broadly: ~7-8 years to 50% (2002-2010). Offshore outsourcing penetration to A&E firms: ~8-10 years (2000s). Analogs suggest 5-8 year adoption curves for transformative production-displacing technology in this sector.
historical_analogs.source: VDCI Education BIM evolution history; industry analyst commentary on CAD adoption timeline
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: BIM reached ~50% adoption in AEC approximately 2009-2010 starting from near-zero in 2002-2004 (approx 7-8 years). CAD adoption in drafting was faster as the core product.

owners_60plus_pct.source: ESTIMATE — no ADDA or 541340-specific owner age data found. ADDA (American Design Drafting Association) data not accessible; trade body appears minimally active. Estimate based on analogy to AEC sector (average AEC CEO age 58, nearly 80% planning transition per Zweig 2024) and that very small drafting bureaus (85% of firms under 10 employees) skew toward older founder-operators.
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: No primary data found for 541340 owner age demographics. ADDA data not accessible. ESTIMATE: 45% based on AEC sector analog (Zweig 2024: average CEO age ~58, 80% of AEC owners planning transition in next decade) and small-firm demographics.

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: No succession shortage documentation found for 541340 specifically. ADDA (the only drafting-specific trade body) appears minimally active and no member surveys found. The absence of a functioning trade association for this code is itself a finding about the fragmentation and thin data environment. No primary evidence of documented succession shortage; conservative default of false applied.

active_consolidators.source: Morrissey Goodale AE M&A data; BizBuySell / BVR DealStats (no results found); direct search for PE-backed drafting platforms
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: No PE-backed platforms found actively acquiring US drafting service bureaus (NAICS 541340) in the last 24 months. The entire n_band is 9 firms; the market is too small, structurally challenged (MELTING terminal class), and lacking the licensing moat that attracts PE to engineering. Morrissey Goodale AE M&A activity is entirely focused on licensed A&E platforms, not drafting bureaus.

buy_mult.source: ESTIMATE — no dedicated drafting bureau transaction comps found. BVR DealStats and BizBuySell lack 541340-specific comps as predicted by the prompt. Comparable micro-cap service businesses (asset-light, owner-operated, under $3M revenue) typically sell at 2.0x-3.5x SDE or 2.0x-3.0x normalized EBITDA. Distressed pricing likely given structural headwinds. Used 2.5x as central ESTIMATE.
buy_mult.figure_quoted: No direct comps found. ESTIMATE: 2.5x normalized EBITDA based on micro-cap service business broker market data for asset-light owner-operated firms (ClearlyAcquired, axial.net data: small professional services at 2.5x-4x EBITDA for micro-cap). Normalized per Rule 3: market-rate owner compensation deducted.

exit_mult.source: ESTIMATE — no exit-tier drafting service transactions found. The 100-499 employee class (n_band = 9 firms) has implied EBITDA of ~$3.4M, below the $10M threshold. No platform-size 541340 firm exists in the US data. Used 4.0x as a theoretical strategic acquisition multiple (e.g., an engineering firm buying a drafting capability), representing a modest premium above buy_mult for strategic value only.
exit_mult.figure_quoted: No exit-size ($10M+ EBITDA) drafting service platforms identified. ESTIMATE: 4.0x EBITDA theoretical strategic multiple, based on the absence of a PE market and the premise that only strategic buyers (engineering firms) would pay a modest premium for a drafting capability add-on. Normalized per Rule 3.

pricing_structure.value: Per-hour or per-sheet competitive piece rates against clients (A/E firms and manufacturers) who benchmark against offshore bureaus (India $10-25/hr, Philippines $12-30/hr per published offshore rate cards 2024). No cost-plus, no QBS, no audited overhead mechanism — the client's professional stamps the work and the bureau has no licensure of its own. Competitive bidding on each project scope with no relationship lock-in beyond current-project familiarity. AI efficiency gains flow immediately to clients as competitive pricing pressure (same mechanism offshore outsourcing already demonstrated once).
pricing_structure.source: Offshore 24/7 engineering support services pricing; HiTech BIM Services CAD outsourcing rates; Sourcefit architectural drafting outsourcing rates
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: Offshore CAD drafting rates: India $10-25/hr, Philippines $12-30/hr (2024 published rate cards). No proprietary pricing structure identified for US 541340 firms; competitive hourly/sheet rates are the prevailing model.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: V_raw = 28.41pp of revenue automatable — above 3pp threshold. A = 2.22 (below 5). No cross-check tension triggered. However, the extremely high V (10.0) combined with very low C (0.12) reflects the core finding: massive AI value is being created by automating the product, but virtually none of it is capturable by the firm (near-complete pass-through to clients and structural displacement of the product itself). The V/C divergence is the key signal here, not A.

terminal_value.justification: The core product of 541340 — 2D/3D production drafting executed under the client's professional seal — is being replaced, not merely made cheaper. Autodesk's generative CAD roadmap (neural CAD foundation models in Fusion/Forma announced AU 2025, AutoCAD 2026 AI block detection) targets the exact billable task that is this industry's entire revenue base. Unlike 541330 where the PE stamp creates durable demand for a licensed professional, 541340 firms have no stamp of their own. A/E firms and manufacturers can insource AI-drafted drawings with a single internal PE in responsible charge — there is no legal, physical, or relationship barrier preventing this. The scan-to-BIM field capture niche (requiring physical site presence) is the only surviving revenue line; it represents ~5-15% of typical firm revenue and arguably belongs to 541370 (surveying). The deciding mechanism: once Autodesk generative CAD reaches production quality for standard drawing types (2-5 year window), the demand for US domestic drafting bureaus collapses. This is demand-side elimination of the product, not cost reduction.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V was derived from a hypothesis-rebuilt role mix for 541340 (the provided 4-digit 5413 role_mix was explicitly flagged as inverted and inapplicable per prompt instruction) and task-level AI automation assessment for each role. A was derived from Energent.ai AutoCAD AI adoption data, Deltek Clarity data as demand-side proxy, and BIM/CAD historical analog adoption curves. Neither V nor A referenced PE deal activity, platform counts, consolidation trends, or M&A multiples.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Pure piece-rate/competitive hourly pricing with no cost-plus or overhead-audit structure. The offshore outsourcing market (India/Philippines at $10-30/hr) established the price floor mechanism once already — when labor cost falls, competitive per-drawing rates fall. AI repeats this mechanism with a steeper curve. The firm has no mechanism to retain AI efficiency gains: (1) no audited-cost structure to replace, (2) no switching costs or relationship lock-in, (3) no licensure barrier preventing client insourcing. pi_dist=0.10 (near-complete pass-through) and pi_moat=0.12 (minimal barrier) reflect this. The double-deflation argument: offshore already compressed margins; AI compresses them again and then may eliminate the demand entirely.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: At t50=2.5: A=4.0, S rises to ~2.37. At t50=7.0: A=1.43, S falls to ~1.73. Given the MELTING classification, faster adoption accelerates the terminal outcome rather than improving acquirability. t50 uncertainty has modest S impact but major terminal-class confidence implications.

Driven by the hypothesis role-mix rebuild. At 0.55: V_raw=0.2427, V=9.71, S barely changes (V already near cap). At 0.75: V_raw=0.3309, V=10.0 (still capped). ai_replaceable_share uncertainty has minimal S impact because V is already at maximum; the critical uncertainty is in C (pi_dist, pi_moat) not V.

At buy_mult=1.5, exit_mult=4.0: M=clamp(4*2.5/1.5,0,10)=clamp(6.67,0,10)=6.67, S rises to ~2.37. At buy_mult=3.5, exit_mult=4.0: M=clamp(4*0.5/3.5,0,10)=clamp(0.571,0,10)=0.571, S falls to ~1.45. M is the second most impactful uncertainty given the illiquid/sparse comp market.

At pi_dist=0.05: C=0.06, S falls to ~1.76. At pi_dist=0.20: C=0.24, S rises to ~2.21. C drives this band. Even at pi_dist=0.20, S remains below 2.5 — the industry's fundamental problem is that the product is being eliminated, not that the firm lacks capture of AI value.

evidence.reviewer_note: CRITICAL ROLE-MIX WARNING: The provided role_mix is 4-digit 5413 (engineering-dominated; 17-3011 Architectural and Civil Drafters show only 3.28% of wage bill). Within NAICS 541340 proper, drafters/BIM technicians are the majority of the wage bill. The ai_replaceable_share breakdown uses a hypothesis-rebuilt role mix as explicitly instructed by the prompt ('rebuild and flag as hypothesis'). The five hypothetical categories (CAD drafters 60%, owners/mgrs 17%, checkers/QC 10%, field capture 5%, admin 8%) are estimates derived from industry guidance in the prompt and general knowledge of drafting bureau business models. This is the most significant data gap in this record and the reason confidence_overall=LOW. ACQUIRABILITY REALITY CHECK: n_band=9 is the entire 100-499 employee class; the 500+ class shows avg revenue/firm of only $6.2M (likely SUSB suppression artifact per prompt note). With 2,510 firms under 5 employees and 257 in the 5-9 band, the acquirable pool is effectively 9 firms — below the threshold where a meaningful PE roll-up strategy exists. EBITDA normalization: buy_mult and exit_mult are ESTIMATE quality with no primary comp data. The entire M score (2.4) and B score (4.63) are highly uncertain. DEMAND-SIDE NOTE: The 541340 n_band calculation uses the Damodaran Business & Consumer Services margin (15.65%); the actual EBITDA margin for US drafting bureaus competing against offshore may be materially lower, which would further reduce n_band. BOUNDARY NOTE: scan-to-BIM field capture revenue (the defensible niche) may classify under 541370 Surveying and Mapping; any firm pivoting to this model may reclassify out of 541340.

breakdown_by_role.role: Building and home inspectors (owners and staff)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Hypothesis per prompt guidance: inspectors are ~65-75% of the real wage bill (midpoint 70%). The physical walk-through, attic/crawlspace access, systems testing is unautomatable product core. Report writing, photo annotation, defect narration, and code citation constitute roughly 35-45% of inspector hours; AI tools (Spectora AI Comment Assist, Swift Reporter AI Template Builder, standalone tools) now generate full narratives from prompts. Within-role automatable fraction of 0.28 (slightly below midpoint of 0.2-0.35 range) reflects that physical field time dominates the deliverable and legal/contractual core is the in-person inspection.

Hypothesis: ~10-15% of wage bill (midpoint 12%). Scheduling and dispatch automate substantially via ISN-style platforms; realtor-relationship marketing and inspector recruiting persist as human tasks. 0.25 fraction reflects minority of this role's time in clearly automatable tasks.

breakdown_by_role.role: Scheduling, call center, and admin staff
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Hypothesis: ~10-15% of wage bill (midpoint 12%). Booking, confirmations, report delivery, and payment collection are already near-fully platform tasks per ISN/Spectora capabilities. 0.85 fraction reflects high degree of current AI/automation feasibility for this role.

breakdown_by_role.role: Specialty technical staff (commercial PCA, engineers on staff)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Hypothesis: ~5-6% of wage bill (slightly above prompt's 5% to balance to 100%). ASTM E2018 PCA report production is document-heavy and AI-exposed; field verification is not. 0.35 fraction reflects strong AI exposure in the document/reporting portion of PCA work.

pi_dist.rationale: Home inspection fees are set by local market rates, not by hours — a flat per-inspection fee (~$300-$500 for a standard home per HomeAdvisor/InterNACHI 2024-2025 data). When AI cuts report-writing time, the operator retains the efficiency gain as margin rather than passing it through as lower fees, because the fee was never priced on labor hours. However, realtor referral relationships drive 70-80% of residential inspection flow (realtors steer buyers to preferred inspectors), meaning the firm does not fully own the client relationship — an upstream gatekeeper (realtor) controls access. Franchise systems (Pillar To Post, WIN) have some brand-level dist but individual franchise units still rely on local realtor relationships. pi_dist set at 0.52 reflecting partial client ownership (better than hourly/cost-plus because flat-fee pricing enables margin retention, worse than full ownership because referral flow is realtor-controlled).
sources: HomeAdvisor/Angi: average home inspection cost $296-$424, most common flat-fee structure (Oct 2025)
NAR Realtors Confidence Index: realtor referral-driven demand for inspections
Spectora 2026: 10,000+ inspector users, AI tools adoption data

pi_moat.rationale: Moat sources: (1) State licensing — ~40 states license home inspectors, creating a statutory human-presence requirement; (2) E&O insurance and liability-backed written opinion — the product is a legal document, not just information; (3) Lender/insurer requirements and contract contingency norms embed the inspection in the transaction structure. Moat weaknesses: (1) No proprietary data moat — any licensed inspector can perform the same inspection; (2) Realtor relationships are personal to the individual inspector, not the firm — acquisition risks client attrition; (3) AI-photo-assisted seller disclosure and iBuyer institutional purchasing could erode demand at the margin; (4) Inspection waiver rate remains 17-20% (NAR 2025-2026), down from 30% peak but structurally elevated. Physical presence requirement is the primary defensible moat; switching costs are low at the consumer level. pi_moat set at 0.45.
sources: NAR Confidence Index: 20% waiver rate as of June 2026
Working RE: state licensing coverage ~40 states as of 2025
ASHI/InterNACHI: E&O requirements, licensing guidance

t50_years.derivation: Current adoption: Spectora data (June 2026) indicates 1 in 3 inspectors (33%) already using AI tools materially (defined as regular use of AI for report narration/comment generation — the primary automatable function). Historical analog: digital reporting software adoption — industry went from ~42% (circa 2014-2015) to 91% (2024-2025), crossing 50% around 2016-2017 (roughly 2 years from 42% to 50%); however AI report writing only became commercially available broadly in 2023-2024. With 33% at current adoption and a steeper S-curve (AI tools are free/cheap add-ons to existing software), crossing 50% requires ~2-3 more years. t50 set at 3.0 years from July 2026.

current_adoption_pct.source: Spectora (home inspection software, 10,000+ inspector users)
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: 1 in 3 inspectors are already using AI tools in their workflows — and that number is expected to nearly double by the end of 2025, with 58 percent planning to adopt AI in the near future

historical_analogs.value: 2 years from 42% to 50% for digital reporting software (2014-2016); AI tools are a faster cycle given free/low-cost add-on nature
historical_analogs.source: Multiple home inspection industry sources aggregated
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: 91% of certified inspectors in the United States now utilize some form of digital reporting, compared to just 42% a decade ago

owners_60plus_pct.source: ASHI — American Society of Home Inspectors, article on next-generation engagement
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: Approximately 80% of all active inspectors are over 40, and fewer than 5% are under 30. Average age range: 53-57 years old.

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: ASHI article explicitly states: 'a wave of retirements is coming, and the profession needs to be intentional about who is coming up behind' and 'solo inspectors retire without succession plans, leaving market share available to those who are ready.' Succession shortage clearly documented. Owners 60+ estimated at 0.35 (ESTIMATE): if average age is 53-57 and 80% are over 40, a right-skewed distribution with sparse young entrants implies ~30-40% are 60+.

active_consolidators.source: Industry news, franchise disclosure data, Porch Group/ISN reporting
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: Porch is signaling another acquisition streak; investor appetite from firms like Radian Capital suggests continued strategic interest. Franchise systems (HouseMaster/Neighborly, WIN Home Inspection) represent organized consolidation platforms.

buy_mult.source: HedgeStone Business Advisors — Home Inspection Business Valuation Guide; BizBuySell data
buy_mult.figure_quoted: Home inspection businesses currently sell for 2x–4x SDE and 3.2x–6.5x EBITDA in today's market. Average SDE multiple 2.8x.

exit_mult.source: BizBuySell/industry broker data; Spectora majority sale context; PE home services multiples
exit_mult.figure_quoted: Home inspection EBITDA multiples 3.2x-6.5x for small firms; PE home services platforms command premium 7-9x at exit per broker commentary. Using 7.0x for a $10M+ EBITDA inspection platform reflecting platform premium over small-firm buy multiples.

pricing_structure.value: Flat per-inspection fee (dominant: ~90%+ of residential revenue); per-item add-ons (radon, sewer scope, thermal imaging); commercial PCA priced by scope/project
pricing_structure.source: HomeAdvisor/Angi cost guide; InterNACHI member survey data
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: Home inspections usually cost around $343 with most homeowners paying between $296 and $424; many inspectors charge a fixed price for homes up to 2,000 square feet

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: V_raw=0.1736, interpreted as 17.36pp EBITDA uplift (V_raw × 100). A=3.33 (below 5). No tension: uplift_pp=17.36 is well above 3pp and A is below 5, so cross-check 1 is satisfied. The uplift is substantial (17.36pp) consistent with AI eliminating ~35% of labor cost in the automatable portions; A is moderate because 33% adoption today still leaves years to reach 50%.

terminal_value.justification: The physical walk-through is the legal/contractual core of the inspection product: lender requirements, E&O-backed written opinions, and state licensing embed the human inspection in the transaction. AI cuts the operator's report-production costs (margin gain on an intact product) but does not replace the product itself. The waiver rate peaked at 30% in 2022 and has declined to 17-20% by 2025-2026 as the market normalized, indicating structural demand for the human inspection persists. Inspection waivers track competitive market conditions, not AI capability. AI-photo-assisted seller disclosure is a risk but not yet a substitute for the liability-backed licensed inspector opinion. Terminal value: DURABLE. The demand mechanism (licensed human walk-through as contingency) survives mature AI.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V was set using a rebuilt role-mix based on the prompt's hypothesized occupation structure for 541350 proper (inspector-dominated), not on PE deal activity or consolidation trends. A was derived from Spectora adoption data (33% current) and the digital-software analog (2 years to cross 50% from 42%), both direct evidence of technology adoption curves, not M&A activity.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Billing model is flat per-inspection fee (~$300-$500), set by local market comparables, not hours. When AI cuts report-writing time by 25-50% (per Spectora data), the inspector retains the efficiency gain as margin because clients pay a fixed fee regardless of time spent. This is the opposite of cost-plus/hourly billing. Deflation risk exists only at the market level (oversupply driving fee competition), not at the per-job level. pi_dist=0.52 reflects this partial margin-capture capability — better than hourly/cost-plus, discounted for realtor-controlled referral flow. pi_moat=0.45 reflects physical-presence requirement and licensing but limited proprietary moat. The flat-fee structure is captured in pi_dist rather than deflating it further through pi_moat.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: At exit_mult=5.5: M=2.86, S≈3.84. At exit_mult=9.0: M=6.29, S≈4.42.

evidence.reviewer_note: Role-mix warning applied per prompt: the provided 4-digit 5413 OEWS role_mix is engineering-service dominated (47-4011 Construction and Building Inspectors only 2.05% of wage bill) and materially misrepresents 541350. Role structure was fully rebuilt using prompt hypotheses (inspector-dominated, ~70% wage share). All within-role fractions are hypothesis; ai_replaceable_share quality set to LOW accordingly. Waiver rate data (NAR 2025-2026: 17-20%) used to calibrate terminal value; peak was 30% in June 2022. EBITDA normalization note: industry is overwhelmingly SDE owner-operator; buy_mult of 3.5x converted from 2.8x SDE average (owner comp added back approximately equals one working inspector salary ~$65-75K, yielding ~10-20% conversion from SDE to EBITDA multiple). Sub-segment: dominant segment is residential home inspection; commercial PCA is smaller. Not scored as heterogeneous — residential is clearly dominant. n_band=40 is very thin (only 100-499 and 500+ size classes); the roll-up target pool is the multi-inspector regional firm and franchise operator subset.
