breakdown_by_role.role: Geoscientists and geophysicists (interpretation, QC, survey design)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Hypothesis per prompt: ~20-30% of wage bill (midpoint 27%). Seismic interpretation, velocity model building, anomaly picking, and report writing are heavily ML-assisted and AI-exposed per SEG/EAGE 2024-2025 literature. However, survey design judgment, client-facing interpretation calls, and professional sign-off are human-requiring. The within-role automatable fraction of 0.30 reflects that roughly a third of geoscientist hours are in tasks AI can substantially do today (data QC, narrative generation, parameter optimization), while the majority involves professional judgment and client interaction.
breakdown_by_role.plausible_range: 0.054 to 0.135 (0.20 to 0.50 within-role fraction × 0.27 wage share)

breakdown_by_role.role: Field acquisition crews and equipment operators
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Hypothesis: ~20-30% of wage bill (midpoint 25%). Physical data acquisition — land crews, marine streamers, airborne gravity/magnetics, drone magnetometry — cannot be automated by AI. Autonomous nodes and drones augment but crews, permitting, and logistics remain physical work. 0.05 within-role fraction reflects a small sliver of administrative/reporting tasks within field roles.

Hypothesis: ~10-15% of wage bill (midpoint 13%). Denoising, migration, stacking, and QC pipelines are already algorithmic. AI collapses the remaining manual parameter-tuning and QC labor per SEG/EAGE 2025 proceedings. This is the most directly automatable cost line. 0.80 within-role fraction reflects near-full automation feasibility for data processing tasks.

breakdown_by_role.role: Management layer (General & Operations Managers + A&E Managers)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Per role_mix: 11-1021 General and Operations Managers 5.72% + 11-9041 Architectural and Engineering Managers 5.53% = 11.25% wage share (rounded to 0.11). Client relationships with oil & gas/mining/infrastructure buyers and crew management persist as human tasks. Internal reporting, bidding paperwork, and resourcing automate. 0.20 within-role fraction reflects minority automation potential.

Per role_mix: 13-1082 Project Management Specialists 6.46% wage share (rounded to 0.06 for clean allocation). Permitting packages, HSE documentation, crew scheduling, and client reporting automate substantially per prompt guidance. Field coordination and claims judgment persist. 0.40 within-role fraction reflects significant but partial automation potential.

breakdown_by_role.role: Residual: 4-digit contamination engineering roles and admin/back office
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Residual 0.18 wage share (1.00 - 0.27 - 0.25 - 0.13 - 0.11 - 0.06) covers two components: (1) the 4-digit OEWS-contamination engineering roles (civil engineers, architects, mechanical/electrical engineers, architectural drafters, construction inspectors) which are artifacts of the 4-digit cell and have low real presence at 6-digit, estimated ~8-10% wage share with modest automation; (2) admin/back office (invoicing, data delivery logistics, proposal and contract admin) estimated ~8-10% wage share with high automation. Blended 0.25 within-role fraction reflects the mixed composition.

pi_dist.rationale: Geophysical work is sold via competitive project bids to sophisticated oil & gas, mining, and infrastructure procurement departments with professional procurement functions. When AI cuts processing and interpretation hours, buyers' competitive bid processes reprice the work quickly — sophisticated buyers observe falling unit costs and renegotiate future bids. The multi-client data-library model (TGS-style) could retain value via IP licensing, but small 541360 firms mostly lack library assets and compete on execution price. Senior geophysicists' credibility is personal and portable — client relationships do not reside at the firm level. Oil majors already run in-house ML tooling. pi_dist=0.30 reflects very limited client-relationship ownership and severe competitive bid pricing pressure that channels AI efficiency gains to clients.
sources: TGS-PGS merger materials 2024: competitive dynamics in seismic data markets
SEG/EAGE 2024-2025: AI tools proliferating in interpretation — client-facing commoditization

pi_moat.rationale: Physical acquisition work (crews, vessels, drones, permitting) creates a meaningful barrier: you need capital equipment and field logistics expertise AI cannot replicate. Some state geoscientist licensure (~40 states) provides modest regulatory gate for sign-off roles. However: (1) the interpretation layer (highest AI exposure) has no regulatory protection; (2) oil majors and miners can increasingly insource interpretation given AI tools; (3) no proprietary data library moat for small firms; (4) switching costs are low — clients re-bid projects regularly; (5) competitive bid environment. Physical-presence requirement for field acquisition is the primary moat; interpretation moat is eroding rapidly. pi_moat=0.35 balances the field-crew moat against the weak interpretation moat.
sources: EnerGeo Alliance (IAGC) industry context: capital equipment requirements for field acquisition
EAGE 86th Annual Conference 2025: seismic foundation models expanding AI interpretation capabilities

t50_years.derivation: Current adoption: Estimated 20% of small 541360 firms materially using AI in automatable functions (defined as ML-integrated processing/interpretation workflows). Evidence: SEG/EAGE 2024-2025 show AI seismic interpretation commercially deployed at large firms (TGS, Viridien, SLB) but small private firms lag due to compute infrastructure requirements and O&G capex conservatism. Historical analog: 3D seismic processing adoption in the 1990s-2000s took ~8-10 years for small firms to reach 50% after the technology was commercially available at large firms. AI/ML tools are faster (cloud-accessible, software-plugin) but small geophysical firms are O&G dependent and conservative adopters. Starting from 20% today, crossing 50% estimated 5-6 years from now; using 5.5 as central estimate.

current_adoption_pct.source: SEG (Society of Exploration Geophysicists) and EAGE publications; PatSnap AI seismic technology landscape
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: Machine-learning applications in seismic exploration are growing faster than applications in other industry fields, with ML algorithms being implemented for almost all steps involved in seismic processing and interpretation workflow — mainly for automation, processing time reduction, and efficiency

historical_analogs.value: 8-10 years for small geophysical firms to reach 50% on 3D seismic processing adoption (1990s-2000s); cloud-based AI tools are faster but energy-sector conservatism applies
historical_analogs.source: SEG/GeoScienceWorld: Machine learning for seismic exploration review
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: Machine-learning applications in seismic exploration are growing faster than applications in other industry fields — the 2024-2025 period marked by foundation-model adaptation pushing seismic-facies interpretation toward higher efficiency

owners_60plus_pct.source: SBA Office of Advocacy Business Ownership Demographics; geoscience workforce aging data (ESTIMATE — no 541360-specific survey found)
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: ESTIMATE: No 541360-specific owner age survey found. Applying SBA small-business analog (~35% of professional services firm owners 55-60+) adjusted for energy-services workforce aging (geophysicists trend older given 1990s-2000s industry peak employment). Estimated 35% of small geophysical firm owners are 60+.

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: No documented succession shortage survey found for NAICS 541360 specifically. Texas Board of Professional Geoscientists and SEG member demographics were suggested sources but no specific age data published. The geoscience workforce is aging (SEG membership data discussed informally) but no formal succession shortage study for small geophysical firms was located. Succession shortage not documented.

active_consolidators.source: Tracxn, industry news search, TGS/PGS merger documentation
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: No PE-backed platform identified as actively doing add-on acquisitions in NAICS 541360 specifically in the last 24 months. TGS-PGS merger (completed July 2024) is large-cap public company consolidation, not a PE roll-up of small firms. Environmental/geotechnical roll-ups (Terracon, etc.) target adjacent codes (541330, 541620).

buy_mult.source: Galata Acquisition Corp. merger proxy SEC filing FY2023; geophysical company EV/EBITDA comps
buy_mult.figure_quoted: For geophysical and seismic companies, EV/EBITDA multiples ranged from 2.9x to 7.3x (median 3.4x, mean 4.2x) for estimated 2023 EBITDA. Small private firm discount applied: using 3.0x for small-firm buy_mult.

exit_mult.source: Energy services M&A multiples; geophysical sector data; EBITDA multiple benchmarks 2024-2025
exit_mult.figure_quoted: Energy and Materials sector trades at lowest multiples (~7.4-8.9x broadly); geophysical/energy services specifically at 2.9-7.3x (median 3.4x) for public comps 2023. At platform scale ($10M+ EBITDA), strategic premium applies; using 5.5x for exit_mult reflecting a geophysical platform sale to oil-major or strategic acquirer.

pricing_structure.value: Competitive day-rate and project-bid pricing (~80-90% of revenue); some multi-client data licensing (minimal for small firms). Day-rate for field crews; project-bid for interpretation and processing assignments. No retainer or cost-plus model dominant.
pricing_structure.source: EnerGeo Alliance industry context; First Research industry profile for NAICS 541360
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: Geophysical Surveying and Mapping Services: competitive project bids to oil and gas, mining and infrastructure clients with professional procurement

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: V_raw=0.22552 → 22.55pp EBITDA uplift. A=1.82 (well below 5). No cross-check tension: uplift_pp=22.55 is well above 3pp and A is below 5, so the check is satisfied. Note the high labor_share (0.7817) drives a large V_raw, but C is very low (1.05), reflecting that clients capture most of that efficiency rather than the firm retaining it as margin.

terminal_value.justification: Field acquisition (crews, vessels, drones, permitting) is the physical-presence work that AI cannot replace and represents the DURABLE core of this code's service. However, the processing and interpretation layer — which constitutes meaningful revenue for most 541360 firms — is being replaced by AI embedded in client in-house tools and data-library platforms (TGS, Viridien). Oil majors and miners increasingly insource interpretation using ML tooling; the TGS-PGS merger creates a data library where interpretation is a software function. Small 541360 firms whose revenue skews toward interpretation face structural revenue erosion. The overall verdict is PRESSURED: demand for field acquisition persists (physical work cannot be automated), but the interpretation revenue component shrinks as clients bring it in-house. The demand for the service bundle survives but shifts in composition toward the field-acquisition portion, which has lower margins.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V was computed from a rebuilt role-mix based on actual 541360 occupational structure (geoscientists, field crews, data processors, management) per prompt guidance. The within-role automatable fractions were derived from SEG/EAGE AI adoption literature (technology capability evidence) and not from any deal activity or consolidation trend. A was derived from estimated current adoption (20%) and the 3D seismic historical adoption analog — both direct technology-diffusion evidence unrelated to M&A.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Billing is competitive day-rate and project-bid pricing (~80-90% of revenue). When AI cuts processing and interpretation hours, competitive bid repricing passes efficiency gains to clients — this is the opposite of margin retention. The mechanism: buyers re-bid projects at lower prices as the market learns that AI reduces labor inputs. Unlike flat-fee structures, day-rate/project-bid pricing directly links price to labor cost, and sophisticated oil & gas procurement enforces competitive discipline. This is fully reflected in pi_dist=0.30 (very limited client control) and pi_moat=0.35 (weak pricing moat). The small multi-client data library exception does not apply to most small 541360 firms.

top_uncertain_inputs.plausible_range: 0.20 to 0.38 (role_mix rebuild from 4-digit artifact introduces high uncertainty)

evidence.reviewer_note: Role-mix data gap applied per prompt: the 4-digit 5413 OEWS role_mix is dominated by civil engineers and architects — 4-digit artifacts with minimal real presence at the 541360 level. Role structure fully rebuilt around geoscientists, field crews, data processors, management, and project management per prompt guidance. All shares are hypothesis; ai_replaceable_share quality=LOW accordingly. Cyclicality warning: SUSB 2022 receipts are from a cyclical-recovery year post-2020 collapse; 2022 E&P capex was elevated. Labor_share of 0.7817 is high, potentially reflecting labor-heavy revenue in a recovery year; normalize caution noted but value used as given. Size-class oddity noted: 500+ employee class shows lower avg revenue per firm ($33.9M) than 100-499 class ($39.4M) — consistent with prompt warning about multinationals whose US-classified receipts are fraction of global operations. n_band=78 concentrated in 20-99 class (45 firms); 100-499 and 500+ classes add 33 more but are unusual. Buy_mult from geophysical public company comps (3.4x median 2023 EBITDA, reduced to 3.0x for small private firm discount). Exit_mult (5.5x) is speculative given lack of PE roll-up comps in this specific code — marked LOW quality. The TGS/PGS merger is not a useful small-firm comparable (multi-client library and ocean-bottom node business, not the small-firm acquisition market).

breakdown_by_role.rationale: PLS stamp on boundary surveys and legal descriptions is a statutory monopoly and physically non-automatable. But deed research, legal-description drafting, plat computation, and coordinate geometry are substantially AI-exposed. Estimated ~15% of PLS labor cost is in tasks AI can substantially do today. Role-mix 4-digit OEWS does not list 17-1022 Surveyors in top-10 (contamination from 541330); estimated 22% wage-bill share for licensed surveyors at 6-digit level per prompt guidance.

Physical occupation of the ground — monument recovery, instrument setup, construction staking, GNSS control — is not automatable by LLM-class AI. GNSS and robotic total stations already compressed 3-person to 1-2-person crews; marginal further compression from AI is small. Automatable slice is field data entry and report upload only. 17-3031 Surveying & Mapping Technicians absent from top-10 4-digit OEWS; estimated 30% wage share at 6-digit per prompt.

breakdown_by_role.role: Office processing (CAD, plat drafting, point-cloud, photogrammetry)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Point-cloud classification, feature extraction, plat drafting, deed plotting, and drawing production are the most directly automatable cost lines. AI tools (e.g., DeedPlotter AI endorsed by NSPS) are already addressing deed research and legal description generation. 17-3011 Architectural & Civil Drafters is 3.3% of 4-digit wage bill; adding cartographers and processing techs yields ~15% at 6-digit. 70% of this role's work is in AI-exposed tasks.

Client relationships with developers, title companies, DOTs persist. Proposal generation, scheduling, coordination paperwork, and progress reporting are automatable. 11-1021 Gen & Ops Mgrs (5.7%) + 11-9041 A&E Mgrs (5.5%) + 13-1082 PM Specialists (6.5%) sum to 17.7% at 4-digit; at 6-digit likely ~15% after removing engineer-firm contamination. Only ~20% of management labor is in tasks AI can substantially do.

Research requests, invoicing, client intake, records management, scheduling — near-fully automatable. Estimated 12% of wage bill from unlisted admin/clerical tail. 80% of this role is in AI-exposed tasks; the remaining 20% is judgment-intensive client contact.

Civil engineers (12.8%), Architects (5.8%), Mechanical engineers (3.8%), Electrical engineers (3.0%) dominate the 4-digit OEWS top-10 but are primarily contamination from 541330/541310 firms in the 4-digit cell. True share at 6-digit surveying firms is minimal (<8%). Assigned 6% residual wage share, treating as mixed technical support with low automatable fraction since survey firms employ few design engineers.

pi_dist.rationale: Boundary and ALTA surveys are sold as fixed fees per job — the PLS stamp and liability set the fee, not hours, so AI-driven compression of office hours flows to the firm as margin rather than price reduction in approximately 60% of revenue (developer/title/municipal repeat accounts). Construction staking and DOT work (~30%) are unit-price or low-bid markets where efficiency gains are competed away. Aerial/mapping work (~10%) is increasingly commoditized by drone operators and software. Weighted capture: ~0.60×0.90 + 0.30×0.30 + 0.10×0.20 = 0.54+0.09+0.02 = 0.55. Client relationships are firm-owned for developer/title repeat accounts but PLS-stamp portability risk exists for small founder-owned practices.
sources: cq-business-management-software.com: fixed-fee surveying project pricing with 25% profit margin line item — access 2026-07-20
ctacquisitions.com/how-to-sell-an-engineering-firm: surveying firm pricing and margin discussion — access 2026-07-20
NSPS prompt guidance on boundary survey pricing structure

pi_moat.rationale: Strong moat: State statutes reserve boundary determination and plat certification to a licensed PLS in responsible charge — this is a hard licensure gate that AI cannot circumvent. Physical monument recovery and ground occupation are required for boundary surveys; no AI bypass. Switching costs moderate: title companies and developers have preferred surveyors with county records knowledge. Weaker elements: aerial/mapping work (perhaps 20-30% of industry revenue) has no PLS licensure requirement in many uses and is rapidly commoditizing via drone operators and SaaS point-cloud processing. Local competitive markets for boundary surveys compress margins on repeat volume. The specific risk flagged in prompt — 'one PLS stamping AI-produced surveys at scale' — compresses per-firm revenue rather than eliminating demand. Overall moat is real but partial.
sources: NSPS prompt guidance on statutory PLS requirements for boundary surveys
projectgeospatial.org: GeoAI revolution noting mapping commoditization — access 2026-07-20
landsurveyorsunited.com: vanishing lines workforce article — access 2026-07-20

t50_years.derivation: Current adoption ~15% of firms materially using AI in office/processing functions (ESTIMATE basis: larger geospatial firms and aerial mapping firms are early adopters; boundary-survey-only small firms lag). Historical analogs: drone/UAV adoption in surveying went from ~5% (2014) to ~50% (2022) in ~8 years. GPS/GNSS adoption went from ~5% (1993) to ~50% (2005) in ~12 years. CAD adoption ~12 years. Average of drone-class analog (~8yr) and hardware-class analogs (~12yr) weighted toward software (easier than hardware): ~9-10 years. But two-thirds of the 9-year period has elapsed from initial AI tool availability (~2023), so from today (2026) with 15% base, remaining time to 50% ≈ 9.5 years (slow adoption among the large tail of aging small firms, license-workflow integration requirements). Plausible range: 7-13 years.

current_adoption_pct.source: Project Geospatial / MAPPS context; NSPS DeedPlotter AI endorsement; geoweeknews.com surveying 2025 trends article
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: 'dozens if not hundreds of firms across the ecosystem now embedding AI into their workflows' (Project Geospatial 2025); NSPS has endorsed DeedPlotter AI for deed research automation, indicating emerging but minority adoption; estimated 15% of ~6,927 firms materially using AI in office/processing functions

historical_analogs.value: 8-12 years to 50% adoption
historical_analogs.source: Land survey equipment market analysis; GPS World; geoweeknews.com; nottinghillsurveyors.com equipment market 2026
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: GPS/GNSS went from advanced to 'standard tools in every surveyor's arsenal' — adoption characterized as a 12-15 year transition for hardware tools. Drone/UAV adoption estimated 7-8 years to majority usage. Software/AI tools trend faster than hardware but integration with licensed workflows adds friction.

owners_60plus_pct.source: NSPS / state PLS boards via landsurveyorsunited.com; Zweig Group 2024 Valuation Report of AEC Firms
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: 'The average age of a licensed land surveyor in the U.S. hovers around 58'; 'for every new surveyor entering the field, two or three are preparing to retire' (NSPS/state boards cited in landsurveyorsunited.com, accessed 2026-07-20). Zweig Group 2024: 'nearly 80% of firm owners looking to transition in the next decade.' With average PLS age 58 and wide retirement wave, ~45% of survey firm owners estimated to be 60+.

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: NSPS and state licensing boards document: average PLS age ~58; for every new entrant 2-3 are retiring; Young Surveyors Network expanding to address pipeline gap; NCEES 2024 Squared report discusses licensure trends. Succession shortage is widely documented in the profession.

active_consolidators.source: Tracxn; PrivSource; BusinessWire; Bowman Consulting investor materials; SAM Companies press releases
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: Active consolidators in surveying/geospatial add-on acquisitions last 24 months: (1) SAM Companies (PE: New Mountain Capital since Jul 2024, previously Peak Rock Capital) — multiple 2024-2025 acquisitions including MTX Surveying, Doucet Survey, S. Nelson & Associates, Inland Geodetics; (2) Bowman Consulting (BWMN, public platform) — acquired MTX Surveying, High Mesa, Surdex, Smithlandsurvey; (3) NV5 Global (public platform) — acquired CRS Survey PLLC Mar 2025, GIS Solutions; (4) Woolpert (private, PE-backed) — acquired Dawood Jul 2025 including surveyors/geospatial. Count = 4 active platforms doing add-ons specifically in surveying/mapping last 24 months.

buy_mult.source: ctacquisitions.com engineering firm multiples 2026; Zweig Group 2024 Valuation Report; auxocapitaladvisors.com AEC M&A
buy_mult.figure_quoted: Single-office regional engineering/surveying firms ($1-3M EBITDA): '5x-7x EBITDA'; Zweig Group 2024: value/EBITDA ratio 4.23x for median AEC firm ($965k EBITDA, 38 FTE). Median buy multiple for small surveying firm add-ons ($1-5M EBITDA) estimated at 5.5x EBITDA after normalizing for owner compensation (EBITDA basis, market-rate PLS salary imputed). Note: Zweig's 4.23x reflects book-value-based internal transactions; external PE-backed add-ons typically 5x-6x.

exit_mult.source: ctacquisitions.com engineering firm multiples 2026; Bowman Consulting LTM EV/EBITDA per SEC filing/Seeking Alpha
exit_mult.figure_quoted: Mid-size multi-state engineering platforms ($8-25M EBITDA): '8x-11x EBITDA'. Bowman Consulting (BWMN) trades at 8.4x LTM EV/Adjusted EBITDA (as of May 2025, per search results citing SEC filing). For $10M+ EBITDA surveying/geospatial platforms with multi-state presence and DOT/infrastructure exposure: 8x-9x range, using 8.5x as midpoint.

pricing_structure.value: ~60% fixed-fee per project (boundary/ALTA surveys, developer accounts), ~30% unit-price or T&M (construction staking, DOT, low-bid public work), ~10% cost-plus or per-acre (large aerial/lidar mapping projects, government contracts)
pricing_structure.source: cq-business-management-software.com surveying pricing guide; ctacquisitions.com surveying firm discussion; NSPS prompt capture questions
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: Three primary methods: fixed project fee ('best for well-defined projects'), hourly rate ('best for smaller or variable projects'), per-acre/per-lot ('best for large land development projects'). Worked examples show fixed fees with 25% profit margin built in. Boundary surveys typically fixed fee; DOT/construction staking typically T&M or unit price.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: V_raw = 0.1463475, implying AI could free 14.63 percentage points of revenue as cost savings — well above the 3pp threshold. A = 1.053 (< 5), reflecting a slow 9.5-year adoption timeline for the majority of firms. No tension: high uplift is consistent with slow adoption — the value pool is large but the pace of realization is slow due to aging workforce, license-workflow integration requirements, and the small-firm tail. The combination of high V and low A is economically coherent: the opportunity is real but the industry moves slowly.

terminal_value.justification: Boundary surveying with PLS stamp survives mature AI because it is a statutory license plus a physical act (monument recovery, ground occupation). However, the office half of every boundary job (deed research, plat computation, legal description drafting) collapses in cost, and in fixed-fee markets this compression flows to firm margin rather than product elimination. The aerial/mapping half of the code (~20-30% of industry revenue) lacks the licensure lock and is already being commoditized by drone operators and SaaS point-cloud processing — this segment faces MELTING-style pressure. Aggregate terminal value is PRESSURED: demand per licensed PLS survives AI but revenue per firm shrinks as one PLS stamps more work, small firms consolidate into platforms, and the unlicensed mapping segment competes on software not labor. Demand does not collapse (regulation mandates the product) but per-firm economics compress materially.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V was computed from a rebuilt role structure using prompt guidance on the actual 6-digit occupation mix, with automatable fractions justified by the nature of each task (deed research, plat drafting, point-cloud processing vs. ground occupation, PLS judgment). No M&A deal activity or consolidation trends were referenced for V. A was derived from current adoption estimate (~15%) and historical technology analogs (GPS: 12-15yr, drone: 7-8yr) — no deal activity referenced.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Pricing mix: ~60% fixed-fee boundary/ALTA surveys (AI efficiency retained as firm margin — fee reflects PLS stamp and liability, not hours); ~30% unit-price or T&M construction staking and DOT work (efficiency competed away in bid markets); ~10% per-acre/cost-plus aerial mapping (efficiency largely passed to clients in competitive drone-operator market). Net effect: roughly 60% of revenue has potential for margin retention, 40% flows to clients. This is reflected in pi_dist = 0.55: slightly below the 60% fixed-fee share because even in fixed-fee markets, repeat-volume clients on developer accounts can renegotiate over time, and local competition still constrains pricing power.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: OW is already capped at 1.0 with succession bonus at 0.45; any value ≥ 0.45 yields OW=1.0 with bonus. Below 0.35 (OW=0.9+0.1=1.0 still at 0.35). Only if pct < ~0.25 (OW<0.9 without bonus) would B change materially. Given documented evidence, uncertainty in OW is low given the cap.

evidence.reviewer_note: Role-mix reconstruction was necessary: the 4-digit 5413 OEWS data is heavily contaminated by civil engineers, architects, and mechanical/electrical engineers from 541330/541310 firms that dominate the 4-digit cell. Actual 6-digit survey firms employ few design engineers. The six rebuilt roles follow prompt guidance directly. n_band=119 means the acquirable target universe is thin (76+43 firms in the 100-499 and 500+ size classes); the prompt notes these may already be AE-platform subsidiaries — this would reduce the effective independent-firm count further, overstating B somewhat. Aerial/mapping (non-boundary) segment likely represents ~20-30% of industry revenue; this segment lacks the licensure lock and is rapidly commoditizing, making terminal classification PRESSURED rather than DURABLE for the code as a whole. The drone/lidar pricing collapse analog (from the prompt) is already built into the low pi_dist and pi_moat for the mapping portion. buy_mult normalized to EBITDA after market-rate PLS owner compensation; the Zweig 4.23x figure reflects internal AEC firm transactions at book value, not arm's-length PE add-on acquisitions, so 5.5x is used as the arm's-length PE add-on estimate.

breakdown_by_role.role: Bench and field testing staff (physical testers, inspectors, samplers)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Physically running tests, collecting samples, operating instruments — core product is a physical act. 51-9061 Inspectors/Testers (wage_share 0.0919) + 19-4042 Env Science & Protection Technicians (0.0192) = 0.1111 from listed OEWS, plus estimated ~0.125 unlisted field/bench techs from the ~0.5997 unlisted remainder (couriers, materials techs, calibration techs, field samplers). Total estimated 0.236. Only data entry, field worksheets, and documentation (~8% of their work) is AI-exposed; physical test execution is not.

19-2031 Chemists (wage_share 0.0515) + 19-4031 Chemical Technicians (0.0433) = 0.0948. Method development, result review, sign-off, and anomaly interpretation persist (ISO 17025 requires qualified technical reviewer). Data reduction, QC flagging, trend analysis, and report narrative drafting automate substantially — estimated 35% of this role's labor.

17-2112 Industrial Engineers (0.0396) + 17-3029 Engineering Technologists & Technicians (0.0234) = 0.0630. Test-protocol design, certification engineering judgment, and client-specific specification interpretation persist. Calculation packages, compliance matrices, regulatory cross-references, and report assembly automate — estimated 30% of role.

11-1021 Gen & Ops Managers (0.0787) + estimated lab director/quality manager tail (~0.04 from unlisted remainder, per prompt 3-5% estimate). Total: 0.1187. Client relationships, ISO 17025 technical manager accountability (mandatory human role), and accreditation stewardship persist. Scheduling, capacity planning, internal reporting, and utilization dashboards automate — estimated 20%.

13-1082 PM Specialists (0.0263). Sample tracking, chain-of-custody status reporting to clients, and coordination paperwork automate; client-facing escalation and scope negotiation persist. Estimated 40% automatable.

43-6014 Secretaries/Admin (0.0135) + 43-9061 Office Clerks (0.0129) = 0.0264 listed; plus estimated ~0.10 unlisted admin/customer-service/data-entry tail from the remaining ~0.5997. Total: 0.1264. Sample login, chain-of-custody paperwork, test certificate generation, invoicing, LIMS data entry, and regulatory report formatting are the most directly automatable cost lines. Estimated 80% of this role's labor is in AI-exposed tasks.

breakdown_by_role.role: Remaining technical tail (bio techs, materials scientists, calibration techs, other field/bench)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Residual unlisted occupations: 1.0 minus all above role shares (1.0 - 0.236 - 0.0948 - 0.063 - 0.1187 - 0.0263 - 0.1264 = 0.3348). This catch-all includes bio/environmental techs, materials scientists, calibration technicians, couriers/drivers, and other lab-support staff. Per prompt guidance, allocate between physical-testing (~90%) and documentation patterns (~10%). Automatable fraction 10% for the mixed physical/documentation work.

pi_dist.rationale: Testing labs bill per-test off published fee schedules against accredited, standardized methods (ASTM, EPA, ISO). The fee reflects the accredited method and turnaround, not hours worked. In the short run, AI savings in reporting/admin are retained by the lab — clients do not observe the input cost change and do not renegotiate per-test fees immediately. Over time (~2-3yr bid cycles), large-volume environmental and construction materials contracts see lab-vs-lab competition compress per-test fees as costs fall. Estimated 70% of revenue is accreditation-gated per-test pricing where efficiency is retained; ~20% is project-bid or task-order competitive where savings pass through; ~10% is recurring monitoring where labs may retain margin. Weighted pi_dist = 0.70×0.80 + 0.20×0.25 + 0.10×0.55 = 0.56+0.05+0.055 = 0.665, adjusted down to 0.60 for the reality that even accreditation-gated tests face lab-vs-lab price competition in commodity segments (routine soil, water, asphalt testing) where the nearest cheap accredited lab wins.
sources: aventis-advisors.com TIC industry guide — per-test/accredited-method pricing discussion — access 2026-07-20
ENGEO 2024 lab fee schedule PDF (ASTM/EPA per-test pricing structure) — access 2026-07-20
EY TIC study 2025 (ey.com) — accreditation and pricing dynamics — access 2026-07-20

pi_moat.rationale: Strong moat drivers: (1) ISO/IEC 17025 accreditation and regulatory mandates (EPA, DOT, FDA for applicable tests) create mandatory demand for certified labs — clients cannot self-serve with generic AI for regulated compliance testing; (2) physical instruments, sample chain-of-custody, and qualified technical sign-off are legally required; (3) geographic proximity and turnaround time create switching friction for project-based clients; (4) lab-specific test scope accreditation (NELAP for environmental, A2LA/ANAB) means not all labs are interchangeable. Weaker: commodity environmental and routine materials tests (soil compaction, asphalt, water chemistry) have many competing accredited labs in each metro; large-volume contracts are heavily bid-competitive; global majors (Eurofins, SGS) can undercut on price for high-volume routine work. Clients cannot bring most regulated testing in-house with AI. Bypass risk is low for regulated compliance testing; higher for routine QC tests where in-house bench instruments are plausible. Overall: solid moat, stronger than average professional services.
sources: A2LA/ANAB ISO 17025 accreditation requirements (iso.org) — access 2026-07-20
aventis-advisors.com TIC complete guide — accreditation gate and switching costs — access 2026-07-20
mainsights.io TICC platform article — industry structure and barriers — access 2026-07-20

t50_years.derivation: Current adoption ~12% of US independent testing labs materially using AI in automatable functions (LIMS automation, AI data reduction, automated report generation). Historical analogs: LIMS adoption went from <10% to >50% in ~10-12 years (1995-2007). Digital QMS adoption similar timeline. Cisco 2024 AI Readiness Index shows <1/3 of organizations feel infrastructure is ready. Small independent labs face higher barriers: capital cost of LIMS upgrades, ISO 17025 validation requirements for automated systems, limited IT staff. Large labs (Eurofins, Element, UES) are already adopting; the 187-firm n_band pool of $1-10M EBITDA independents lags significantly. t50 from today: starting ~12% adoption in 2026, applying 10-12yr LIMS-class analog, the remaining gap (~38 percentage points) closes in roughly 10 years (2036). Use 10.0 years.

current_adoption_pct.source: Pistoia Alliance Lab of the Future 2024 Global Survey (via cloudlims.com); Cisco 2024 AI Readiness Index; TIC industry AI adoption context from marketsandmarkets.com and EY TIC study 2025
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: Pistoia Alliance Lab of Future 2024: 'AI use in laboratories increased by 14% over the previous year' (suggests still minority adoption, not majority). Cisco 2024 AI Readiness Index: 'less than a third of organizations believe their current data infrastructure is prepared for AI implementation.' EY TIC study 2025: 'Several (usually larger) TIC players have already started' on AI. TIC market reports note 'slower adoption rate of advanced technologies by small and medium-sized TIC companies.' Estimated 12% of US independent testing labs (~541380 code) materially using AI in reporting, LIMS automation, data reduction.

historical_analogs.value: 10-12 years to 50% adoption
historical_analogs.source: LIMS market analysis (grandviewresearch.com); industry context from revollims.com and intuitionlabs.ai LIMS guide 2025
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: LIMS adoption in testing labs progressed from early-adopter phase (mid-1990s) to >50% penetration in medium-large labs by ~2005-2008, roughly a 10-12 year adoption arc. Digital QMS systems followed a similar timeline. AI/ML for lab data reduction is a new layer on top of existing LIMS infrastructure, but ISO 17025 validation requirements add adoption friction beyond what LIMS adoption required.

owners_60plus_pct.source: Oberlo / Gallup small business owner age demographics 2024; ACIL member demographics not publicly available
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: 2024 small business owner demographics: Gen X (39-54) = 47%, Baby Boomers (55-73) = 37% of all small business owners. Of boomers (55-73 in 2024), those 60-73 represent roughly 55-60% of that cohort, implying ~20-22% of all small business owners are 60+. However, independent testing labs are capital-intensive, technically specialized, established businesses — owner base is older-skewing than typical small business. Estimated 35% of independent lab owners are 60+, reflecting the intersection of boomer demographics and the older-than-average ownership profile of technical services businesses.

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: ACIL (American Council of Independent Laboratories) does not publicly publish member age demographics or succession shortage documentation. No specific documented succession crisis found in lab sector equivalent to the PLS surveying shortage. General small business succession planning data (Gallup 2024: most small business owners lack a succession plan) applies, but this is not sector-specific documentation of a shortage. Succession shortage not confirmed as documented for this code.

active_consolidators.source: BusinessWire; PrivSource; PRNewswire; pehub.com; capstonepartners.com industrial services update
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: PE-backed consolidators actively doing testing lab add-ons in 541380-scope industries last 24 months: (1) UES / Universal Engineering Sciences (BDT Capital Partners) — construction materials, geotech testing; acquired Summit Engineering ~150 employees, CTE Inc. Jul 2025; (2) Alliance Technical Group (Morgan Stanley Capital Partners → Blackstone Jan 2026) — environmental/emissions testing; acquired CEMServices Nov 2024, DeNovo, Gas Ops, Environmental 360, ESC Spectrum 2025; (3) Pace Analytical Services (Leonard Green & Partners) — environmental/analytical; acquired 5 labs 2024 (Micron, Patriot, DCM, Quantem, Environmental Services Labs); (4) Element Materials Technology (PE-backed, global platform) — active US add-ons; acquired Analytical Lab Group (ALG); (5) Acuren Corp (post-SPAC, ex-American Securities 2024 @ 9.7x) — industrial inspection + testing; ~40 acquisitions since 2020; (6) Montrose Environmental Group (MEG, public/PE history) — environmental testing; acquired Paragon, Spirit Environmental, Origins Lab 2024. Count = 6.

buy_mult.source: aventis-advisors.com TIC valuations (public comps); mainsights.io TICC deal database; Capstone Partners industrial services update; search results citing 'small-cap TIC at 8x EBITDA'
buy_mult.figure_quoted: Small-cap TIC acquisitions: 'Transaction multiples for small-caps in the TIC sector have increased to 8x EBITDA' (search result citing Investec/sector report). mainsights.io: GBA Group (2021) at 10.7x but European context. Labs with fossil fuel exposure: '6x-8x'. Independent US testing labs ($1-5M EBITDA): estimated buy-side multiple of 7.5x EBITDA after market-rate owner/technical director compensation normalization. Basis: TIC sector is more technically specialized and defensible than generic services (Capstone: industrial/environmental services commands premium to general middle market 9.8x average). Small US lab add-ons estimated at 7x-8x; 7.5x used as median.

exit_mult.source: capstonepartners.com (Acuren/NV5 deal 10.9x EV/EBITDA); mainsights.io (Applied Technical Services/SGS at 11.2x); aventis-advisors.com (public TIC median 14.3x Nov 2025); Eurofins 10.5x, Intertek 9.4x
exit_mult.figure_quoted: Platform exits (>$10M EBITDA): Acuren acquisition by Admiral (2024) at 9.7x EV/EBITDA; NV5 Global acquired by Acuren at 10.9x (May 2025); Applied Technical Services acquired by SGS at 11.2x (2025). Public TIC comps: Eurofins 10.5x, Intertek 9.4x. For a US-focused testing lab platform at $10M+ EBITDA: 10x-12x range. Use 11.5x as midpoint, reflecting premium for multi-specialty, geographically distributed platforms with recurring regulatory-compliance revenue.

pricing_structure.value: ~70% per-test fixed fee off published schedules (accreditation-gated, ASTM/EPA/ISO methods), ~20% project-bid or task-order contract (large construction materials, environmental remediation testing), ~10% recurring/retainer (ongoing industrial emissions monitoring, food production QC)
pricing_structure.source: ENGEO 2024 lab fee schedule (PDF); Nelson Labs pricing page; Standard Labs coal testing price guide; EY TIC study 2025; aventis-advisors TIC guide
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: ENGEO 2024 lab fee schedule shows per-test pricing off published schedules (e.g., ASTM method-specific unit prices). Nelson Labs: 'testing performed under ISO/IEC 17025 often costs more than non-accredited testing' — confirming accreditation-gated pricing. Expediting fee = '100% of listed unit rate,' confirming unit-test base pricing. Market: TIC majors use per-test published schedules for most routine testing; large environmental contracts (DOT, EPA compliance) are competitively bid by task order.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: V_raw = 0.163725, implying AI could free 16.37 percentage points of revenue as cost savings. A = 1.000 (t50 = 10 years), indicating slow adoption. No tension: uplift is well above 3pp, and the slow A simply means the margin harvest takes a decade to fully materialize. The economic narrative is coherent: the automatable slice (primarily reporting, admin, QC data reduction) is meaningful in a high-labor-cost industry, but the physical testing mandate bounds the upside. Low A reflects rational expectation about small-lab adoption pace given LIMS validation costs and accreditation overhead.

terminal_value.justification: Regulation and accreditation mandate physical tests that AI cannot perform — ISO 17025 requires a qualified human technical reviewer to sign off results, and regulatory programs (EPA NELAP, USDA, DOT) mandate accredited lab testing. AI cuts the operator's admin/reporting costs (the ~24% of labor in automatable tasks) rather than replacing the operator's product. The sharp question from the prompt is whether the automation upside is bounded enough to limit the AI premium thesis — it is: with only ~24% of the wage bill in automatable tasks and labor_share 0.68, V_raw is 0.164, a meaningful but not exceptional uplift. Demand is highly durable because regulatory mandates, physical sample requirements, and accreditation gates mean AI cannot route around the lab's core value. This is one of the most demand-durable codes in the professional services sector.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V was computed from the role-structure breakdown using the actual OEWS 5-digit occupation shares plus estimated unlisted-tail allocations; automatable fractions were justified by the nature of each task (physical testing vs. LIMS data entry, report generation, QC flagging). No M&A deal counts or consolidation trends were referenced for V. A was derived from current adoption estimate (~12%) and historical LIMS/digital-QMS adoption analogs (~10-12 years). No deal activity referenced for A.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Pricing: ~70% per-test fixed fee off published accreditation-gated schedules; ~20% competitive-bid project contracts; ~10% recurring monitoring retainers. Per-test fixed-fee pricing means AI savings in the reporting/admin layer do not flow to clients in the short run — the client pays per accredited test method, not per hour. This is a favorable capture structure. However, in large-volume commodity testing (routine soil, water chemistry, asphalt), lab-vs-lab bid competition eventually compresses per-test fees over bid cycles as all labs reduce costs. This is reflected in pi_dist = 0.60: below the 70% fixed-fee share because even accreditation-gated per-test revenue faces volume discounting and bid pressure in competitive markets. pi_moat = 0.65 reflects the genuine barrier of accreditation but acknowledges that many labs hold the same accreditations in each metro.

evidence.reviewer_note: Sub-segment heterogeneity is material: environmental testing, construction materials/geotech, food testing, pharma/bioanalytical, electrical/product-safety, and calibration labs have different accreditation regimes (NELAP vs A2LA vs FDA-registered vs OSHA-recognized), margin profiles (pharma specialty commands premium, routine environmental is commoditized), and consolidation maturity. heterogeneous=true. The score reflects a weighted average tilted toward the dominant sub-segments in the $1-10M EBITDA band: construction materials testing and environmental labs likely dominate n_band=187 given their fragmented structure and proximity to regulated construction/remediation markets. The 20-99 employee class (749 firms, implied EBITDA $987k) sits just below the $1M floor, meaning the practical acquirable pool is larger than n_band alone suggests — sensitivity upward. EBITDA normalization: testing labs are more capex-intensive than other 54 codes (instruments, facilities); EBITDA multiples may flatter labs vs. maintenance-capex-adjusted basis; the buy_mult of 7.5x may be slightly generous if capex burden is high for the acquiree. Medical/diagnostic labs (621511) and R&D labs (5417) excluded from scope — deal comps were checked against scope. The high labor_share (0.6827) does NOT indicate high AI exposure — confirmed: the automatable slice is ~24% of wage bill, giving V_raw=0.164.

labor_share.method: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541410 ($4,053,951,989) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541410 ($18,324,956,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).
sources: BLS QCEW 2024 annual averages (US000, private)
BLS ECEC news release (raw/ecec.nr0.htm)
Census SUSB 2022 (receipts by NAICS)

occupations.title: Sales Representatives of Services, Except Advertising, Insurance, Financial Services, and Travel
Laborers and Freight, Stock, and Material Movers, Hand
Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists
Secretaries and Administrative Assistants, Except Legal, Medical, and Executive

n_band.derivation: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $342,107 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541410); class <5: 13,213 firms, avg revenue/firm $590,121 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $92,354 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 1,938 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,036,537 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $318,718 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 673 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,873,851 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $606,258 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 275 firms, avg revenue/firm $10,288,840 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,610,203 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 25 firms, avg revenue/firm $24,612,240 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,851,816 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 13 firms, avg revenue/firm $40,695,462 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,368,840 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 313
n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%

current_adoption_pct.source: 1stDibs Annual Designer Survey / Houzz 2025 State of AI in Construction & Design (722 US construction and design businesses, May 2025)
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: 31% of designers now use AI (Houzz May 2025); 1stDibs 2025 survey: 29% of designers adopting AI tools (up from 9% in 2023, 16% in 2024). Material use (automation/problem-solving/decision-making) estimated ~25-30% based on AEC cross-referencing (27% of AEC firms deploy AI for automation per Dan Cumberland Labs 2025).

historical_analogs.value: CAD adoption in design/AEC: AutoCAD launched 1982, majority of AEC firms using by early 1990s (~8-10 years to ~50%); BIM/Revit: launched 2000-2002, 52% of small AEC firms by 2021 (~19 years); AI adoption pace is 2.34x faster than classical technology benchmarks per research. At observed growth rate (9% in 2023 to 29% in 2025, +20pp in 2 years), extrapolating S-curve deceleration yields ~3-5 years from 2025 baseline to reach 50%.
historical_analogs.source: BIM adoption: PlanRadar/SmithGroup AEC survey 2021; AI pace multiplier: Dan Cumberland Labs AI Adoption Curve AEC 2025; 1stDibs/Houzz trajectory
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: AI adoption is diffusing 2.34x faster than classical technology benchmarks; only 27% of AEC firms deploy AI for automation, problem-solving, or decision-making (meaningful use); 63% stuck in pilot purgatory.

owners_60plus_pct.source: ESTIMATE — ASID succession planning research 2025 (no direct age breakdown available); US self-employed small business owner age distribution as cross-reference; interior design average practitioner age 40+ with owner/principal skew older
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: ASID 'Designing A Succession Plan: Insights for Firm Owners' (2025): only 12% of small firms (2-10 employees) have succession plans; less than 5% of sole practitioners have succession plans — implying large cohort approaching retirement without exit plans. No specific 60+ age fraction published.

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: ASID released 'Designing A Succession Plan: Insights for Firm Owners' (2025) documenting critical planning deficit: only 12% of small interior design firms (2-10 employees) have succession plans, under 5% of sole practitioners do, with ASID explicitly urging 'proactive succession planning for design firm longevity' (ASID press release, April 2025: https://builder.media/2025/04/asid-releases-new-research-exploring-succession-planning-for-interior-design-firms/).

active_consolidators.source: ESTIMATE — no documented PE-backed platform doing systematic add-ons in NAICS 541410 in last 24 months. Strategic acquirers (Havenly acquiring design brands; architecture firms buying interiors studios) are not PE roll-up platforms.
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: No PE roll-up platform specific to interior design services identified in 2024-2025 M&A coverage. Activity is strategic (Havenly, Arcturis, architecture firm bolt-ons) rather than PE buy-and-build in this NAICS code.

buy_mult.source: Peak Business Valuation — Valuation Multiples for Interior Design Businesses
buy_mult.figure_quoted: Average EBITDA Multiple Range: 3.05x – 3.75x for interior design businesses. Midpoint = 3.40x. SDE multiple range 2.27x–2.68x; revenue multiple 0.37x–0.81x. Used EBITDA midpoint per Rule 3 (EBITDA normalization after market-rate owner comp already assumed in this source's SDE-to-EBITDA range context).

exit_mult.source: ESTIMATE — no documented interior design platform exit. Cross-reference: A/E firm transactions (ROG Partners/Zweig 2025): median EBITDA multiple 5.30x, upper quartile 6.77x. Interior design platforms are less institutionalized than A/E, discount to ~5.0-6.0x midpoint.
exit_mult.figure_quoted: A/E firm transaction study (257 transactions, ROG Partners): median 5.30x, mean 5.91x, upper quartile 6.77x EBITDA. Interior design platforms estimated at lower end ~5.0-6.0x given weaker institutional buyer universe; midpoint 5.50x used.

pricing_structure.value: Mixed: approximately 40% flat/fixed fee, 30% hourly billing, 30% product markup (trade discount resale). Most firms use hybrid of at least two models. Residential skews toward markup+hourly; commercial/contract skews toward fixed fee or % of FF&E.
pricing_structure.source: Interior design industry trade coverage synthesis: InteriorDesignCommunity.com, DakotaDesignCompany.com, StudioDesigner.com blog; Business of Home coverage of design industry billing shifts 2024
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: Four primary billing models: flat fee, hourly billing, markup on goods/procurement, percentage of project cost; hybrid structures most common. No single authoritative revenue-split survey found; 40/30/30 allocation is ESTIMATE based on trade coverage.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Space planning drafts, 3D renderings, mood boards, FF&E schedules and spec documents are heavily AI-exposed (generative rendering already mainstream per 1stDibs/Houzz surveys). Non-automatable: client taste translation and advisory, site visits, showroom sourcing relationships, project oversight, code-compliance judgment. Interior designers are core fee earners with substantial creative-judgment non-automatable share; 45% automatable captures the production-drafting-documentation slice.

4-digit contamination from 541430 but still present at 541410 firms for visualization, presentation decks, marketing collateral. These are near-fully automatable production tasks (AI image generation, layout tools, social content). 75% automatable; 25% retained for brand direction judgment and complex client-facing presentations. Note: wage_share likely overstates true presence at 6-digit level per prompt guidance, but used as provided.

Principal-client relationship management and creative direction are the business in residential design — not automatable. Internal operations, purchasing admin oversight, reporting and financial monitoring automate moderately. 30% captures the ops/admin slice while preserving client-facing and leadership functions.

Lead generation content, proposal drafting, and pipeline tracking automate substantially. Showroom/trade-network relationship selling and referral cultivation persist — these rely on personal relationships and physical presence in the design trade ecosystem.

Order tracking, vendor follow-up, punch list management, budget reporting and schedule documentation are substantially automatable. Site coordination with contractors, physical inspections, and problem-solving during installation are physical/judgment tasks that persist.
General administrative duties, filing, correspondence, data entry — near-fully automatable by AI and process automation tools. Small residual (20%) for physical tasks and context-dependent judgment.

Physical receiving, inspection and installation logistics for furnishings — almost entirely physical, non-automatable by current AI. Tiny 5% for documentation/paperwork associated with receiving and inventory tracking.

breakdown_by_role.role: Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists (13-1161)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Market research analysis, social media content creation, email campaigns, and marketing collateral are substantially automatable. Strategic brand positioning, client-specific research, and trade relationship marketing have human judgment residual.
Scheduling, correspondence, document management, invoicing — near-fully automatable. Small residual for complex multi-party coordination requiring live judgment.

Mostly 541430 contamination in 4-digit data; where present at interior design firms they direct client presentation aesthetics and visual output. Production rendering/visualization automates; creative direction for high-touch residential clients less so. 45% mirrors interior designer fraction.

breakdown_by_role.role: Unallocated tail (all occupations not in top-10)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Residual 37.39% of wage bill (= 1.0 - 0.6261). Per prompt guidance: junior designers, CAD/Revit techs (automatable), bookkeepers (automatable), purchasing/receiving staff (physical, not automatable). Estimated 38% automatable — capturing the design-production and bookkeeping subset while excluding physical procurement/logistics roles. Range reflects uncertainty in exact occupational mix.

pi_dist.rationale: Mixed billing model means AI efficiency capture varies by revenue stream. Flat fee billers (~40% of revenue): operator keeps AI-driven efficiency as margin (high pi_dist for this slice). Hourly billers (~30%): AI cuts hours → revenue shrinks, efficiency transfers to clients as lower cost (low pi_dist). Markup billers (~30%): not directly affected by AI labor efficiency, but long-term erosion risk from client direct purchasing. Blended: 0.40×0.80 + 0.30×0.30 + 0.30×0.70 = 0.32 + 0.09 + 0.21 = 0.62. Discounted to 0.50 for key-person/client-relationship portability risk — in residential design the client book often walks with the principal designer, limiting firm-level capture of retained economics post-acquisition.
sources: InteriorDesignCommunity.com fee structures overview 2025
StudioDesigner.com pricing structures blog
DakotaDesignCompany.com billing models 2025

pi_moat.rationale: Barrier strength is weak-to-moderate. No federal licensure requirement (a minority of states require it; most allow practice without formal license), eliminating a regulatory moat. Trade-only showroom ecosystem (design trade discounts, trade-only access) provides a partial moat but is eroding as manufacturers expand direct-to-consumer and e-commerce bypass grows. Primary real barrier is personal brand/taste/network of the principal designer — this is fragile post-acquisition (key-person walks). Physical coordination of furnishings procurement and installation logistics (contractor interface) provides a modest durable function. Client bypass risk via generative design tools plus retail is real and growing but not yet dominant. No proprietary data or technology lock-in.
sources: BLS Occupational Outlook Handbook — Interior Designers (licensure discussion)
Business of Home trade coverage on design industry trends 2024

t50_years.derivation: Current material AI adoption at ~25-30% (1stDibs 2025: 29%; Houzz May 2025: 31% any use, ~27% meaningful use per AEC cross-reference). Growth trajectory: 9% (2023) → 16% (2024) → 29% (2025), approximately +10pp/year recently. S-curve deceleration expected as adoption reaches early-majority segment (more resistant, smaller firms). From ~30% (mid-2025) to 50% at decelerating ~5pp/year pace: ~4 years. From mid-2026 baseline: ~3.5-4.5 years. Using 4.0 years as central estimate (landing ~mid-2030).

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: Uplift = V_raw × 100 = 10.85pp. A = 2.5 (< 5). No tension: uplift is well above the 3pp threshold, and A < 5 means adoption is slow enough that the mismatch flag does not apply. The 10.85pp uplift reflects moderate labor-share and moderate AI replaceability across a mixed design-plus-goods-logistics workforce.

terminal_value.justification: Interior design demand survives mature AI but shrinks materially. AI can generate the core design artifacts (renderings, space plans, mood boards, FF&E schedules) that historically justified design fees, eroding the value of hourly design production. The product-markup revenue model (30% of revenue) faces long-run erosion from e-commerce direct purchasing. What survives durably: physical project coordination (procurement, receiving, contractor interface), bespoke client advisory for high-net-worth residential clients who value a human principal's taste and social access, and commercial code-compliance/FF&E project management. The industry is NOT melting because physical furnishings procurement and installation logistics require human presence; demand persists at a compressed margin. Classified PRESSURED: design-fee revenue shrinks, markup arbitrage erodes, but a project-coordination and advisory residue endures.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V was derived solely from the provided labor_share (dataset input) multiplied by ai_replaceable_share (judgment on task automability by occupation role, referencing occupational duties, not deal activity). A was derived from current_adoption_pct sourced from 1stDibs and Houzz designer surveys and historical technology adoption analogs (CAD/BIM timelines in design). Neither V nor A referenced PE deal counts, M&A consolidation trends, or active_consolidators.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Billing mix is approximately 40% flat fee / 30% hourly / 30% product markup. Hourly billers (30% of revenue) face direct deflation: AI reduces design hours, which directly reduces revenue under time-and-materials contracts — efficiency flows to clients, not operators. Flat fee billers (40%) capture AI savings as margin, but face market pressure to reprice downward at renewal or when competing on bids. Markup billers (30%) are insulated from AI efficiency risk but face long-run bypass threat. This mechanism is reflected in pi_dist = 0.50 (below 0.62 gross blended, discounted for residential key-person portability) and pi_moat = 0.40 (no durable structural barrier to repricing pressure or client bypass).

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: At t50=2.5: A=4.0; at t50=6.0: A=1.667. S ranges from ~3.58 (t50=2.5) to ~3.04 (t50=6.0). Moderate S impact (~0.54 spread) given A's 5th-root dampening.

top_uncertain_inputs.plausible_range: 0.35-0.57 (driven by unallocated tail fraction 0.30-0.50 and graphic designer contamination adjustment)
top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: At ars=0.35: V_raw=0.0826, V=3.30; at ars=0.57: V_raw=0.1344, V=5.38. S ranges from ~2.98 (ars=0.35) to ~3.47 (ars=0.57). Moderate impact on V and S.

top_uncertain_inputs.plausible_range: 0.22-0.40 (no direct data; ESTIMATE based on general small-business demographics and ASID succession research)
top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: OW ranges from 0.571 (at 0.30) pre-succession. At 0.22: OW_raw=0.343, +0.1=0.443, B=5.35. At 0.40: OW_raw=0.857 capped 0.9, +0.1 capped 1.0, B=7.91. S ranges from ~3.07 to ~3.43.

top_uncertain_inputs.plausible_range: 4.50-7.00x (no documented interior design platform; cross-referenced A/E data)
top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: At exit=4.50: M=1.294; at exit=7.00: M=4.235. S ranges from ~2.87 to ~3.72. Largest single uncertainty in S.

evidence.reviewer_note: Key issues: (1) Labor_share 0.2359 is low because receipts include resold furnishings at markup — the industry is partially a retail-margin-on-goods business, not pure service; V_raw of 10.85pp correctly reflects the blended basis. (2) Role_mix contamination: graphic designers (15.38% wage share) largely belong to 541430 at 4-digit level; applied 0.75 automatable fraction which appropriately treats the visualization/presentation work that does exist in interior design firms as highly automatable, without changing the provided labor_share_of_total. (3) No PE roll-up exists for this code; active_consolidators=0 drives CFD=0 and B=6.47 (high due to fragmentation and modest owner-aging). (4) Exit multiple is a significant ESTIMATE — no interior design platform transaction on record; A/E comps used as ceiling reference with discount applied. (5) pi_dist and pi_moat both LOW quality due to lack of primary survey data on billing mix and client-portability rates. (6) S=3.22 reflects moderate AI value potential held back by weak capture economics (low C=2.0 from mixed billing and no durable structural moat) and modest multiple arbitrage opportunity (M=2.47) — the industry is fragmented and aging (high B) but AI efficiency largely flows to clients or doesn't address the goods-logistics revenue base.
