current_adoption_pct.source: Autodesk 2024 State of Design & Make Report (5,399 industry leaders across AEC, design/manufacturing, media/entertainment); AI in Design Report 2026 (900+ designers at startups, enterprises, agencies)
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: Autodesk 2024: 56% of companies approaching/achieving AI goals across design & make industries. AI in Design 2026: 54% of designers used AI weekly in 2025 (tech-company bias; conservative downward adjustment applied for industrial design consultancies). ESTIMATE for material use at NAICS 541420 firms: ~25-35%, using 30% as midpoint. Material = AI integrated into CAD workflows, rendering, documentation or project management functions.

historical_analogs.value: CAD adoption in industrial/product design: AutoCAD/SolidWorks from early 1990s, ~50% of professional product design firms by early 2000s (~8-10 years). AI adoption pace estimated 2.34x faster than classical technology benchmarks (Dan Cumberland Labs 2026). From current baseline (~30% in 2026), S-curve deceleration to 50% in approximately 3-5 years. Using 4.0 years as central estimate.
historical_analogs.source: BIM adoption timeline: PlanRadar AEC survey 2021 (52% small firm BIM adoption by 2021 from ~2000 baseline = ~19 years); AI pace multiplier: Dan Cumberland Labs AI Adoption Curve AEC 2025; Autodesk 2024/2025 State of Design & Make
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: AI adoption is diffusing 2.34x faster than classical technology benchmarks; only 27% of AEC firms deploy AI for automation, problem-solving or decision-making (meaningful use vs nominal). 40% of professionals report companies approaching/achieving AI goals (Autodesk 2025).

owners_60plus_pct.source: ESTIMATE — no IDSA-specific owner age data found; cross-referenced general US small business owner demographics and architecture/design firm succession trends
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: 52.3% of U.S. employer businesses are owned by people 55+ (Project Equity 2024); architecture firm demographic wave: Baby Boomer founders approaching retirement (Architecture Paper 2026). Industrial design studio founders are predominantly Baby Boomer/older Gen-X consistent with the broader design sector trend. 60+ fraction estimated at 30%, consistent with design-sector demographics.

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: No IDSA (Industrial Designers Society of America) succession planning research found analogous to ASID's 2025 'Designing A Succession Plan' report. While general small business succession planning gaps are documented (54% of small businesses lack succession plans, Project Equity 2024), no industrial-design-specific evidence of a documented shortage is available. ASID data covers interior design (541410), not this code.

active_consolidators.source: ESTIMATE — no PE-backed platform identified doing systematic add-on acquisitions in NAICS 541420 in last 24 months. Historic buyers are consulting/tech strategics (McKinsey acquired Lunar Design, Accenture acquired Fjord) doing acqui-hire transactions, not PE roll-up platforms.
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: Lunar bought by McKinsey, Fjord acquired by Accenture Interactive (2013), frog part of Capgemini Invent — all strategic/consulting acquisitions for capability, not PE roll-up EBITDA arbitrage. No PE-backed industrial design consolidation platform identified in 2024-2025.

buy_mult.source: ESTIMATE — no industrial design firm transaction database found with stated EBITDA multiples. Cross-referenced professional services small firm multiples: accounting/consulting practices at 3.0x-5.0x EBITDA; engineering/design firms (ROG Partners/Zweig, lower quartile 3.35x). Industrial design firms at $1-5M EBITDA are founder-dependent creative businesses; comparable to other founder-led professional services at small scale.
buy_mult.figure_quoted: A/E firm transaction study lower quartile 3.35x EBITDA (ROG Partners, 257 transactions). Small professional services/consulting firms: 3.0x-5.0x EBITDA range (Raincatcher, Axial forum). Industrial design at small scale estimated at 3.50x midpoint, discounted for founder-dependence and no PE roll-up buyer universe.

exit_mult.source: ESTIMATE — no documented industrial design platform exit on EBITDA basis. Cross-reference: A/E firm transactions median 5.30x (ROG Partners 2025); professional services consulting at mid-market 5x-7x. Strategic acquirers of design studios (consulting, tech) historically paid acqui-hire premiums not EBITDA-based; absent a PE platform exit comp, using A/E lower-median as ceiling for a hypothetical institutional exit.
exit_mult.figure_quoted: A/E firm transaction study median 5.30x, mean 5.91x EBITDA (ROG Partners 2025, 257 transactions). No industrial design platform exit documented. Using 5.50x as conservative ceiling, noting that strategic acquirers may pay differently from PE exit assumptions.

pricing_structure.value: Fixed-fee project engagements (~50-60% of revenue); hourly/time-and-materials (~30-40%); retainer for ongoing product development (~5-10%). Corporate clients with procurement departments re-scope and rebid at contract renewal (typically 12-24 month cycle), meaning AI efficiency gains on fixed-fee projects get competed away at renewal. Hourly billings directly lose revenue as AI cuts design hours.
pricing_structure.source: Design1st.com product design pricing guide; NMSConsulting.com consulting fee models 2026; Toggl consulting pricing models 2026
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: Three primary models: flat fee (best for defined scopes), fee-for-service hourly ($100-$450/hr range), fee plus equity/royalties (niche). Most engagements are milestone-based fixed fee or hourly. No authoritative revenue-split survey found; 55/35/10 allocation is ESTIMATE based on trade coverage of product design consultancy billing practice.

breakdown_by_role.role: Interior Designers (27-1025) — maps to Industrial/Product Designers at 541420
breakdown_by_role.rationale: At 541420 the 27-1025 OEWS slot maps to the core industrial/product design occupation (27-1021 Commercial & Industrial Designers is absent from the 4-digit top-10 per prompt). CAD surfacing iterations, rendering, CMF documentation, and presentation materials are heavily AI-exposed (generative CAD and Autodesk AI capabilities now in commercial availability). Physical prototyping, field user research, DFM judgment with manufacturers, and client workshops are not automatable. 40% automatable (below 541410 interior designers at 45% due to more engineering/physical prototyping content in industrial design).

breakdown_by_role.role: Graphic Designers (27-1024) — maps to visualization/rendering/packaging staff
breakdown_by_role.rationale: At 541420 this slot represents the visualization, rendering, packaging graphics, and pitch-visual production staff in product studios. These are near-fully automatable production tasks (generative AI image, 3D rendering, layout). 25% retained for creative direction judgment and complex client-specific brand work.

Corporate client relationships and creative/studio direction persist as human work. Studio operations, financial reporting, internal scheduling, and administrative oversight automate moderately. 30% automatable captures ops/admin while preserving client-leadership and strategic direction functions.

Proposal deck generation, case-study content creation, and CRM pipeline management automate substantially. Enterprise relationship selling with corporate design procurement teams, conference presence, and referral cultivation persist. 40% automatable.

Schedule tracking, budget reporting, milestone documentation, and status communication automate substantially. Physical coordination with prototyping vendors, tooling shops, and client QA reviews require human presence and judgment. 45% automatable (slightly lower than interior design PM at 50% due to heavier physical/vendor-coordination content in industrial design).
General studio administration, correspondence, invoicing, filing — near-fully automatable. 20% retained for physical tasks and context-dependent judgment.
Shop support, model handling, material movement for physical prototyping — almost entirely physical, not automatable. Tiny 5% for receiving/inventory documentation.

breakdown_by_role.role: Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists (13-1161)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Marketing content, case study creation, competitive analysis reports automate substantially. Field user research for product development (ethnography, usability sessions) and client-specific market intelligence require human judgment. 50% automatable.
Scheduling, correspondence, document management — near-fully automatable.

Creative direction for product aesthetics, CMF programs, and brand expression. Production rendering/visualization automates; strategic aesthetic direction and creative vision for corporate client products retains human judgment. 40% automatable.

breakdown_by_role.role: Unallocated tail (all occupations not in top-10)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Residual 37.39% of wage bill. At 541420 this tail includes mechanical engineers and model-makers (physical prototyping — not automatable), engineering/CAD technicians (drawing packages, tolerance documentation — automatable), and bookkeeping/admin (automatable). Estimated 35% automatable — lower than interior design tail (38%) because the engineering/prototyping component is larger and less automatable in industrial design. Range reflects substantial uncertainty in true occupational mix.

pi_dist.rationale: Fixed-fee project engagements (~55% of revenue): short-term operator captures AI efficiency as margin, but corporate procurement departments re-scope and rebid at contract renewal (12-24 month cycle) — AI savings compete away over 1-2 cycles. Hourly billings (~35%): AI cuts hours directly → revenue falls (efficiency transfers to client). Retainer (~10%): operator captures efficiency. Blended effective pi_dist considering 1-2 year repricing lag: 0.55×0.65 + 0.35×0.25 + 0.10×0.80 = 0.3575 + 0.0875 + 0.08 = 0.525. Discounted further to 0.45 for founder-/studio-dependence: corporate clients increasingly bring design in-house, reducing leverage in renewal negotiations, and the studio brand is not transferable post-acquisition.
sources: Design1st.com — product design firm pricing models
NMSConsulting.com consulting fee structures 2026
Toggl consulting pricing models 2026

pi_moat.rationale: Barrier strength is weak. No licensure or certification requirement for industrial design practice — any entity can offer 'design services'. No proprietary data, no technology lock-in (clients can switch tools/firms freely). Studio reputation and creative track record provide a soft moat but are founder-dependent and often non-transferable. Physical prototyping capability (model shop, 3D printing infrastructure, vendor network for tooling) provides a modest real barrier, but mid-size corporate clients increasingly maintain in-house prototyping or use on-demand services. The dominant addressable market risk is corporate insourcing — AI-enabled in-house teams can now substitute for more outsourced work. No gate between clients and generative design tools except creative specialization and physical testing capability.
sources: BLS Occupational Outlook Handbook — Industrial Designers (no licensure requirement noted)
Medium/Teaque Lenahan on integrating design firms into consultancies (founder-dependence analysis)

t50_years.derivation: Current material AI adoption at ~25-35% (Autodesk 2024 State of Design & Make: 56% of design/make companies approaching/achieving AI goals broadly; conservative downward adjustment for industrial design consultancies yields ~30% material adoption in 2026). Historical analog: CAD adoption in product design reached ~50% in 8-10 years from early 1990s; AI diffusing 2.34x faster → effective analog ~3-4 years from current base. S-curve deceleration from current ~30% to 50% at slowing ~5pp/year → ~4 years to reach 50% from 2026. Central estimate: 4.0 years (landing ~mid-2030).

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: Uplift = V_raw × 100 = 39.87pp. A = 2.5 (< 5). The flag condition (uplift < 3pp while A >= 5) does not apply. The 39.87pp is an artifact of the implausibly high labor_share (0.9177), which the prompt explicitly warns may be a vintage-mismatch artifact. True expected EBITDA uplift at a normalized labor_share of ~0.55-0.65 would be approximately 24-28pp — still above 3pp. The arithmetic is internally consistent given the provided input; the implausible labor_share is flagged in reviewer_note rather than overriding the formula result.

terminal_value.justification: Industrial design services face compound pressure from two directions. First, AI (generative CAD, rendering, documentation) automates core design production tasks that historically justified studio fees. Second, corporate insourcing trend — a decade-long shift as large companies build internal design teams — is accelerating because AI-enabled in-house teams of 2-3 can now substitute for outsourcing many product design engagements. The studio's durable residue: physical prototyping and model-shop capability, field user research (ethnography, usability testing with real users), DFM iteration at manufacturing partners, and top-tier creative reputation at the brand level. Classified PRESSURED rather than MELTING because physical prototyping, field research, and specialized manufacturing-interface work cannot be fully automated and retain genuine paid demand — but the addressable outsourced market shrinks as AI lowers the threshold for insourcing. Demand persists but at a compressed addressable market size.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V was derived from the provided dataset labor_share (0.9177, used as given per Rule 6) multiplied by ai_replaceable_share — a judgment on task automability by occupation role, with reference to Autodesk AI capabilities, generative CAD development timelines, and occupational duty descriptions. A was derived from current_adoption_pct sourced from Autodesk's State of Design & Make surveys and historical CAD adoption timeline in product design. Neither V nor A referenced PE deal counts, M&A consolidation activity, or strategic acquirer behavior.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Industrial design consultancies primarily bill on fixed-fee project engagements (~55% of revenue) and hourly time-and-materials (~35%). For fixed-fee projects with corporate clients: AI efficiency accrues to studio in the near term (1-2 years), but corporate procurement departments re-scope and rebid at contract renewal, competing AI savings away over a 1-2 year repricing cycle. For hourly billings: AI directly reduces chargeable hours, deflating revenue immediately. Net: moderate-to-high deflation risk. This is captured in pi_dist = 0.45 (below the naive blended first-pass of 0.525, discounted for insourcing pressure and studio non-transferability) and pi_moat = 0.35 (no structural regulatory or proprietary-data barrier to repricing pressure or client insourcing).

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: At t50=2.5: A=4.0; at t50=7.0: A=1.429. S ranges from ~3.88 (t50=2.5) to ~3.16 (t50=7.0). Moderate spread (~0.72) on S given 5th-root dampening.

top_uncertain_inputs.plausible_range: 0.50-0.70 for a normalized estimate (vs 0.9177 provided). V_raw would be 0.217-0.304 for ars=0.434, V=8.70-10.0. S range: 3.34-3.46 (V already at ceiling of 10.0 for most of range, so impact limited above ~0.575 labor_share).
top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: V=10.0 is already at maximum. If labor_share were lower (0.50), V_raw=0.217, V=8.70, S≈3.34. The implausible labor_share drives V to 10.0 ceiling; actual impact on S is bounded because V is already maxed above labor_share ~0.575.

top_uncertain_inputs.plausible_range: 4.00-7.00x (no documented industrial design platform; strategic acquirer comps not EBITDA-based)
top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: At exit=4.00: M=0.571, S≈2.53. At exit=7.00: M=4.000, S≈4.28. Largest single uncertainty driver for S (range ~1.75 spread). If no institutional PE exit exists, effective M may be near 0 if only strategic acqui-hire buyers exist.

top_uncertain_inputs.plausible_range: C could range from 1.0 (pi_dist=0.35, pi_moat=0.30) to 2.25 (pi_dist=0.50, pi_moat=0.45). S ranges from ~3.08 to ~3.76.
top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: C=1.575 is near the center of this range. The low moat and pricing-deflation risk are the structural story for this code — uncertainty in both pi_dist and pi_moat together shifts S by ~0.68.

evidence.reviewer_note: CRITICAL DATA QUALITY ISSUE: labor_share = 0.9177 is implausibly high, as explicitly flagged in the prompt (likely a vintage-mismatch artifact: 2024 QCEW wages divided by 2022 SUSB receipts in a small, fast-changing code where receipts may be understated). The formula is applied mechanically per Rule 1 and Rule 6, yielding V=10.0 (maximum). A sanity check: if true labor_share were 0.55-0.65 (reasonable for a pure-service professional studio), V_raw would be 0.239-0.282 and V would be 9.56-10.0 — still at or near the V ceiling for ars=0.434. Thus while the data quality flag is real, V's ceiling behavior means it doesn't materially affect S in this case. ROLE MIX CONTAMINATION (SEVERE): The provided role_mix is 4-digit 5414 and its core industrial design occupation (27-1021 Commercial & Industrial Designers) is entirely absent from the top-10. Interior Designers (18.41% wage share) and Graphic Designers (15.38%) dominate — both belonging to sibling codes 541410 and 541430. The within_role_automatable_fraction was calibrated for what occupants at a true 541420 firm would actually do (industrial/product design tasks) rather than interior design. This is flagged as hypothesis in all relevant role breakdowns. STRUCTURAL NOTE: No PE roll-up exists for this code; the historic buyer universe consists of management consulting and tech strategy firms buying design capability (Accenture-Fjord, McKinsey-Lunar, Capgemini-frog) — these are talent/capability acquisitions at acqui-hire economics that may not follow standard EBITDA multiple logic. The exit_mult=5.50x assumption is aspirational and highly uncertain; if the only realistic buyers are strategic acqui-hirers, effective M may be substantially lower. S=3.46 reflects a code with maximum V (from labor_share artifact) but structurally weak capture (C=1.575) and limited multiple arbitrage opportunity (M=2.29), with moderate B from fragmentation offset by small n_band.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: At 6-digit 541430, graphic designers represent ~40% of the wage bill (scaled up from 15.4% at 4-digit 5414, removing interior designer contamination). Layout, asset production, resizing/versioning, packaging mechanicals, presentation and template work are substantially automatable by current generative tools (Firefly, Midjourney, Canvas AI). The persisting slice is client taste arbitration and brand-system judgment, mostly at senior/art-director level. Fraction reflects ~35% of work truly immune (client judgment, novel concept direction) leaving 65% as substantially AI-addressable production work.
breakdown_by_role.plausible_range: 0.55-0.75 (upper if product-replacement thesis dominates; lower if taste-arbitration slice is larger)

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Art directors represent ~6.5% of the wage bill at 6-digit (scaled from 3.0% at 4-digit). Concept direction, brand stewardship, and client creative relationships persist; comping, deck assembly, and iteration rounds automate. 30% automatable fraction reflects the administrative and production coordination aspects of the role.

Owners and management ~13% of wage bill. In small studios the owner IS the senior client relationship; this is the key-person non-automatable core. Internal ops, admin, and financial reporting automate. 20% automatable fraction captures only the back-office management tasks.

Sales and account staff ~11% of wage bill. Proposal and content production automates; relationship account management persists but represents the portable asset that walks when a creative director leaves. 30% fraction reflects the pitch-creation and marketing-materials production tasks.
Traffic coordination, timelines, asset delivery, versioning admin: near-fully automatable by project management AI tools. 75% fraction; residual 25% is client-facing communication that persists.

Admin, bookkeeping, junior production, and the unlisted wage tail (~25.5% of wage bill). Bookkeeping, studio admin, invoicing, and junior design production work are near-fully automatable. Small residual for physical tasks (equipment maintenance, supply handling) and client-facing reception. Note: the 53-7062 Laborers share at 4-digit (1.22% wage) is non-automatable physical work; this tail bundle blends it with automatable admin. 70% blended fraction is a modest discount to reflect the physical tail.

pi_dist.rationale: Graphic design studios have weak client-relationship control. The industry is unlicensed, has near-zero switching costs, and competes against a global freelance marketplace (Upwork, Fiverr) and increasingly against direct AI tools (Canva AI, Adobe Firefly) used by clients themselves. The primary billing model is project-based or hourly (~75% of revenue), meaning AI efficiency directly compresses fees through competitive pressure rather than flowing to operator margin. The retainer segment (~25%) is stickier but small. SMB clients — the bread-and-butter of these studios — are increasingly capable of self-serving with AI. The firm 'owns' the client in the narrow sense of having a relationship, but the economic power sits with the client who can reprice or leave at project completion.
sources: https://www.creativeagencybook.com/blog/best-pricing-models-for-creative-services
https://elements.envato.com/learn/ai-trend-report
https://www.axial.net/forum/digital-marketing-agency-valuation-multiples/

pi_moat.rationale: Minimal barrier to entry or switching. No licensure required, no physical act mandated, no statute protecting the fee. The deliverable (logos, layouts, assets) is exactly what generative AI now produces. Switching costs are near zero: clients change studios at project end. The only durable moat segments are (a) enterprise brand governance for large named accounts with multi-year retainers, and (b) creative directors with personal reputation/taste brands — neither of which accrues to the studio as an acquirable asset. Generic AI tools (Canva, Firefly, Midjourney) are directly competing for the SMB client base. Fiverr buyer count dropped 10% in 2024 suggesting platform substitution is already occurring.
sources: https://elements.envato.com/learn/ai-trend-report
https://medium.com/illumination/upwork-vs-fiverr-vs-toptal-ai-freelancer-platform-revenue-reality-2025-f475e7c675fb
https://gitnux.org/ai-in-the-graphic-design-industry-statistics/

t50_years.derivation: Current adoption baseline: Envato 2026 State of AI in Creative Work (surveyed 2025) finds 40% of graphic designers use AI daily; gitnux.org cites 35% of design agencies had integrated AI software by mid-2024. Blended firm-level estimate: ~35% of studios materially using AI as of mid-2025. Historical analog: desktop publishing transition (PageMaker 1985 → industry-dominant by 1992) took ~7 years to reach majority firm adoption; Adobe Creative Suite vs QuarkXPress transition (2003→2007) took ~4 years for a 50% handover. Generative AI tools became broadly accessible 2022-2023. At 35% current adoption and historical analog speed of 6-8 pp/year for the firm-level laggard tail, reaching 50% requires ~2-3 more years (2028-2029). Central estimate: 3.5 years from 2026.
t50_years.plausible_range: 2.0-5.0 years (lower if AI tool adoption accelerates via platform bundling; upper if small-studio inertia and client resistance slow uptake)

current_adoption_pct.source: Envato 'Beyond Adoption: State of AI in Creative Work 2026' (1,780 respondents surveyed 2025); gitnux.org 'AI In The Graphic Design Industry Statistics 2026'
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: 40% of graphic designers use AI on a daily basis (Envato 2026); 35% of design agencies had integrated AI software by mid-2024 (gitnux). Firm-level blended estimate: 35%.

historical_analogs.value: 4-7 years to 50% adoption for prior design-workflow technology transitions
historical_analogs.source: Design software history records: desktop publishing transition 1985-1992; Adobe CS vs QuarkXPress 2003-2007
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: Desktop publishing emerged 1985, dominated by 1992 (~7 years); Adobe CS displaced QuarkXPress market leadership ~2003-2007 (~4 years)

owners_60plus_pct.source: Guidant Financial Small Business Trends 2024 via Oberlo; Census Bureau ABS general small business age data
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: 37% of small business owners are aged 55-73 (Baby Boomers) in 2024. No design-specific breakdown. Estimated 28% aged 60+ based on age distribution within the 55-73 cohort.

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: No design-industry-specific succession shortage documentation found. General small business data (Gallup 2024) shows only 54% of owners aged 55+ have a succession plan, but this is not documented specifically for graphic design studios. No AIGA Design Census age/succession data post-2019 was accessible. succession_shortage_documented set false: absence of specific evidence, not evidence of absence.

active_consolidators.source: FE International Agency M&A 2026; Jahani and Associates Independent Creative Agencies M&A report; Target Report November 2025
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: No named PE-backed platform specifically rolling up graphic design studios at 6-digit 541430 level identified. Marketing services PE roll-ups (performance marketing, analytics) are active but target different profiles. One marginally relevant consolidator estimated (marketing holding companies occasionally acquiring design studios as bolt-ons). RoyerComm Prism in commercial printing not in scope.

buy_mult.source: Peak Business Valuation 'Advertising Agency Valuation Multiples'; First Page Sage 'Marketing Agency EBITDA Multiples 2025'; FE International Agency M&A 2026
buy_mult.figure_quoted: Peak Business Valuation: EBITDA multiples for advertising agencies 3.21x-4.30x. First Page Sage: $1-3M EBITDA agencies trade at 4.5x-5.5x; FE International: small agencies under $1M at 3-5x, mid-market $1-5M at 5-8x. Graphic design studios (project-heavy, not retainer-led) at lower end of creative agency range. Applied 4.5x as midpoint for project-dominant design studios in the $1-10M EBITDA band after EBITDA normalization for owner comp.

exit_mult.source: FE International Agency M&A 2026; First Page Sage Marketing Agency Valuation 2025; agencies.co Definitive Guide 2026
exit_mult.figure_quoted: FE International: agencies above $5M EBITDA at 8-12x; median North America/Europe marketing services EV/EBITDA reached 10.1x in 2026. Graphic design platforms at lower end vs. performance marketing; retainer-heavy agencies at 5-7x premium vs. project-heavy. Applied 7.5x as conservative exit for a scaled graphic design platform with $10M+ EBITDA, reflecting lower retainer share and AI risk discount vs. data-driven marketing peers.

pricing_structure.value: Primarily project-based (~50%) and hourly (~25%) with minority retainer (~25%); fees benchmarked to expected hours
pricing_structure.source: Creative Agency Book pricing models guide; DesignRush graphic design pricing; Morgan Overholt freelance graphic design rate guide 2024
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: Graphic design billing: project-based flat fee and hourly are dominant; retainer model exists but is minority for small studios. Per-project fees for logos $300-2,500, websites $600-30,000; hourly $30-250/hr. Retainer pricing $500-10,000+/month but not the majority of small-studio revenue.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: Uplift of 30.5pp is well above the 3pp threshold. A = 2.86 (t50 = 3.5 years) is below 5, so the cross-check tension flag does not apply. High V_raw reflects the high labor share and high automatable fraction in a production-heavy industry where the deliverable itself is what AI generates. The large uplift is consistent with the MELTING terminal value classification — the issue is not whether AI can save costs, but whether the client base will pay for the service at all once AI tools are freely embedded in design platforms.

terminal_value.justification: Graphic design is the clearest product-replacement case: the deliverable (logos, layouts, assets, presentations) is precisely what generative AI tools now produce. The relevant distinction is: (1) AI cutting the operator's costs — partially true, saves production hours; (2) AI replacing the operator's product — this is the dominant dynamic. SMB clients (the majority of 541430 revenue) are already self-serving via Canva AI, Adobe Firefly, and Midjourney at near-zero marginal cost. The surviving paid demand sits in two narrow niches: enterprise brand governance (ongoing retainer relationships with Fortune 500 clients) and taste-arbitration by named creative directors with personal reputations. Neither of these survival segments is acquirable at roll-up scale — the enterprise accounts are in much larger agencies (outside the $1-10M EBITDA band) and the named-designer value is maximally portable. For the 370 acquirable studios in the $1-10M EBITDA band (20-99 employee class), the bread-and-butter is exactly the commodity SMB production work that AI most directly replaces. Demand is not merely pressured — the product itself is being generated by competitors (AI tools) at near-zero cost. Sub-segment by billing: project-based (~50% of revenue) is most exposed; hourly (~25%) is directly compressed by AI efficiency; retainer (~25%) partially survives but only for clients who have stable ongoing brand needs and prefer outsourcing brand governance. MELTING classification is warranted for the core of this code.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V was justified by occupation-level automatable fraction estimates based on task analysis of graphic designer roles (layout, asset production, versioning — established AI capability) and the Envato/Adobe Firefly adoption data showing 40-45% daily AI use among designers. A was derived from current adoption percentage (~35% firm-level) and historical analog timelines for design software transitions (4-7 years). Neither V nor A made reference to PE deal activity, consolidation waves, or M&A trends.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Graphic design studios bill primarily on project-based flat fee (~50%) and hourly rates (~25%), with minority retainer (~25%). Both project and hourly models directly transmit AI efficiency to clients as lower fees: project fees are benchmarked to expected hours; competitors (freelancers, offshore, AI tools) reprice instantly. The mechanism is fast and direct — zero licensure or regulatory protection allows the global freelance marketplace to function as the price-setter. This deflation risk is captured in pi_dist = 0.30 (client owns economic power, competition is global and frictionless) and pi_moat = 0.20 (no licensure, zero switching costs, AI directly substitutes for the product). The retainer minority provides limited protection only for long-standing brand clients with high switching costs from institutional knowledge.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: At pi_moat=0.12: C=0.36, S≈2.60. At pi_moat=0.30: C=0.90, S≈3.34. Range ±0.37 S-units; moat uncertainty compounds with pi_dist uncertainty.

evidence.reviewer_note: Role mix rebase: The provided 4-digit OEWS role_mix (5414 level) is contaminated by the interior designer share (27-1025: 18.41% of wage bill) which belongs to sibling code 541410. Per prompt guidance, the 6-digit 541430 role structure was rebuilt using the prompt's suggested 6-digit estimates (graphic designers ~40%, art directors ~6.5%, owners/management ~13%, sales/account ~11%, project management ~4%, admin/tail ~25.5%), not the raw 4-digit OEWS ratios. This is flagged as LOW quality due to the hypothesis nature of the remapping. Terminal value warning: The MELTING classification is the analytically correct finding for this code. V=10.0 (V_raw=0.305, well above the 0.25 anchor) reflects that AI is genuinely capable of executing the majority of billable work in this code. However, the low C (0.60) correctly discounts this: the savings flow to clients, not operators, because there is no licensure, no switching cost, and AI tools are now embedded in the client's own design software. The combination of high V and very low C reflects the product-replacement dynamic: high automation potential, almost no ability for the operator to capture value from it. S=3.02 is in the low-to-mid range, consistent with a MELTING industry with thin value-capture. No specific PE-backed graphic design studio consolidator platform was identified for the $1-10M EBITDA band; active_consolidators=1 is a conservative estimate of marginally relevant marketing holding company activity. Multiples sourced from marketing agency data (closest available proxy); pure graphic design studio comps are sparse.

breakdown_by_role.role: Specialized designers (fashion, jewelry, textile, footwear, costume)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Dominant sub-segment is fashion design services. At 6-digit 541490, the code's core occupation (27-1022 Fashion Designers and equivalent specialty SOCs) is absent from the 4-digit OEWS top-10, which is contaminated by interior (18.41%) and graphic (15.38%) designer shares. Per prompt guidance, specialized designers are estimated at ~40% of the wage bill. Automatable fraction 40%: sketching, colorways, pattern variants, CAD iterations, tech-pack generation, and digital renderings are substantially AI-addressable (generative fashion/jewelry design tools: Style3D, AiDA, Adobe Firefly for fashion). Non-automatable: physical sampling, fit sessions, materials sourcing and evaluation, trend judgment, and — critically — named-designer brand cachet that is the primary value proposition for the most sticky clients. Lower than graphic design (65%) because physical workflow inertia is real and the taste/reputation moat is somewhat stronger in fashion than in pure production graphic design.
breakdown_by_role.plausible_range: 0.30-0.55 (lower if physical sampling inertia dominates; upper if generative tools fully commoditize design documentation)

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Owners and management ~13.5% of wage bill. In founder-branded studios, the owner IS the brand and the client relationship — the key-person risk is maximal. 20% automatable fraction captures only back-office management tasks (scheduling, financial reporting, internal coordination) that yield to AI tools.

Sales and client/licensing relationship staff ~7% of wage bill. Brand/licensing relationships with apparel houses, manufacturers, and retailers persist — these are personal relationships that walk with the named designer. Lookbook production, pitch decks, and proposal materials automate. 25% fraction reflects the automatable documentation side.

Technical pack handoff, vendor follow-up and sample tracking automate substantially; factory-facing physical QC and fit coordination do not. 55% automatable fraction reflects the document-handling and scheduling components of production coordination.

Physical sample construction, fittings, exhibit/float fabrication, and pattern-cutting are not AI-replaceable. 5% automatable fraction captures only the administrative/documentation aspects. This role group provides the most durable non-AI-replaceable value in the code.

Studio admin, invoicing, logistics paperwork, junior production documentation: near-fully automatable. 70% fraction; residual 25% is client-facing communication and physical studio tasks. The 4-digit OEWS shows office clerks, secretaries, and laborers/material movers in this segment — the laborers share is non-automatable physical work, slightly discounting the blended fraction from the higher graphic design rate.

pi_dist.rationale: Client relationship control is weaker than graphic design in a key respect: the named designer's relationships are personal and do not survive acquisition. An acquirer of a fashion design studio retains the fee book but loses the client relationships when the founder departs — these follow the individual, not the firm. Billing is primarily per-collection (fixed fee) and day-rate, meaning AI-driven hour reductions flow directly to clients as reduced scopes. Fashion apparel brands and manufacturers are sophisticated buyers who reprice based on hours when AI compresses time. Per-collection fee compression is already documented (retailers reducing buy budgets per IBIS World 2024). pi_dist = 0.25 reflects marginally stronger relationship stickiness than graphic design (25% vs 30%) because fashion relationships are longer-duration seasonal partnerships, partially offset by the named-designer portability issue.
sources: https://ftbec.textiles.ncsu.edu/generative-ai-in-2024-adoption-trends-and-major-use-cases-in-the-fashion-industry/
https://www.ibisworld.com/united-states/market-research-reports/fashion-designers-industry/
https://masterofcode.com/blog/generative-ai-in-fashion

pi_moat.rationale: No licensure required for fashion design services (unlike architecture or engineering). Switching costs are low for most clients — apparel brands change design consultants at season end. The primary moat is named-designer brand cachet, which is maximally portable: it resides in the individual, not the firm. Physical sample-making and exhibit/float sub-segments have higher switching costs (physical inertia, specialized skills, long lead times), but these sub-segments are small relative to fashion design fees. AI tools (Style3D AI, AiDA, Midjourney for fashion) are actively competing for tech pack generation, colorway creation, and design rendering — the core of what design service firms sell. Value: 0.18 (slightly lower than graphic design's 0.20 because key-person portability is even more severe in fashion).
sources: https://www.stytrix.com/blog/generative-ai-fashion-design-complete-guide-2026
https://smartdev.com/ai-use-cases-in-fashion/
https://masterofcode.com/blog/generative-ai-in-fashion

t50_years.derivation: Current adoption baseline: McKinsey/BoF State of Fashion 2026 indicates 35% of fashion executives using generative AI for image creation; masterofcode.com cites 28% of industry players integrating AI into creative design processes. These figures primarily reflect large brands, not independent design service firms. Independent studio adoption lags: estimated 25% materially using AI among 541490 firms as of mid-2025. Historical analog: fashion CAD software (introduced 1980s) took ~10-15 years to reach majority firm adoption due to physical workflow inertia; digital pattern grading (~2000s) took ~8-12 years. Generative AI for fashion design (sketching, colorways, tech packs) became capable ~2023. With physical production creating inertia and slower tool maturation vs. graphic design: from 25% current adoption, reaching 50% requires ~5 years (2031). Slower than graphic design (3.5 yr estimate) due to: (a) physical sampling inertia, (b) fashion design's more personal/relationship-based workflow, (c) AI tools for fashion still maturing vs. more mature graphic design AI.
t50_years.plausible_range: 3.0-8.0 years (lower if AI tech pack and design automation accelerates; upper if physical workflow inertia persists)

current_adoption_pct.source: McKinsey/Business of Fashion State of Fashion 2026 report; masterofcode.com Generative AI in Fashion 2026 review
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: 28% of fashion industry players integrating AI into creative design processes (masterofcode 2026); 35% of fashion executives using generative AI for image creation (McKinsey/BoF 2026). These primarily reflect large brands. Independent design service firms estimated at ~25% material adoption, lagging large-brand averages.

historical_analogs.value: 8-12 years to 50% adoption for prior fashion design workflow technology transitions (CAD, digital pattern software)
historical_analogs.source: Design software history records: fashion CAD integration began 1980s, became mainstream by late 1990s/early 2000s; Novedge fashion design software history
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: CAD technology integration in fashion dates to 1980s with mainstream adoption achieved in late 1990s/early 2000s (~10-15 year transition); digital pattern software similarly took ~8-12 years to achieve majority firm adoption

owners_60plus_pct.source: General small business owner age data via Guidant Financial/Oberlo 2024; adjusted downward for fashion design industry youth skew
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: 37% of small business owners are aged 55-73 (Baby Boomers) per 2024 Guidant Financial data. Fashion design industry is somewhat younger-skewing given NYC/LA creative industry culture. No design-specific age data found for 541490. ESTIMATE: 25% aged 60+ (below general small business average of ~28% for 60+ cohort, discounted for fashion's younger demographic tendency).

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: No succession shortage documentation found specifically for NAICS 541490 or fashion/jewelry/specialized design services firms. CFDA does not publish member age or succession data. No independent design services succession shortage is documented in any accessible source. Prompt guidance states: 'No dedicated owner-demographic source known for this code — expect to fall back on general small-business proxies.' succession_shortage_documented set false: absence of specific documentation.

active_consolidators.source: Fashion Law M&A tracker; Lincoln International Fashion Apparel Quarterly Review Q2 2025; FE International Agency M&A 2026
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: No PE-backed platform specifically rolling up independent fashion/jewelry/textile/specialized design service firms at NAICS 541490 level identified. PE fashion activity targets brand IP (Authentic Brands Group, WHP Global acquiring brand licenses) not design services firms. Key-person portability makes roll-up structurally very difficult — the named designer's relationships and taste are the primary asset and they walk with the individual.

buy_mult.source: FE International Agency M&A 2026; Peak Business Valuation advertising agency multiples; general lower-middle-market services M&A proxies
buy_mult.figure_quoted: FE International: agencies under $1M EBITDA at 3-5x. No specific fashion/specialized design services comps identified. Applied 3.5x (lower end of small agency range) reflecting: (1) high key-person risk — named-designer value portability substantially discounts firm value; (2) absence of a roll-up market providing competitive bid tension; (3) MELTING terminal value dynamic for the design-documentation portion of revenue. ESTIMATE.

exit_mult.source: FE International Agency M&A 2026; fashion/apparel M&A Lincoln International Q2 2025; creative agency platform exit data
exit_mult.figure_quoted: FE International: agencies above $5M EBITDA at 8-12x. Lincoln International Fashion Apparel Q2 2025: median LTM EV/EBITDA for fashion brands 9.1x-9.8x — but these are product brands, not design services firms. Design services platforms lack the brand IP, product revenue, or recurring licensed royalty streams that justify brand-level multiples. Conservative platform exit for a scaled specialized design services firm: 6.0x, reflecting: (1) no documented exit comp for pure design services platform; (2) heavy discount for key-person risk at scale; (3) MELTING terminal risk. ESTIMATE.

pricing_structure.value: Primarily per-collection/project (~60%) and day-rate/hourly (~30%) with minority royalty/licensing (~10%); fees benchmarked to expected design hours
pricing_structure.source: Freelance fashion designer rate surveys (Moda-Moxie, Upwork, Successful Fashion Designer); creative services market research
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: Fashion designers bill by hour ($50-200/hr), day ($384 avg), or per collection (variable). Tech packs $50-2,000 per piece. Per-collection and day-rate models are dominant for independent studios; royalty/licensing is minority. Average day rate $384, hourly ~$64 globally.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: Uplift of 23.88pp is well above the 3pp threshold. A = 2.0 (t50 = 5.0 years) is below 5, so no cross-check tension flag applies. V_raw = 0.2388 is just below the 0.25 anchor so V = 9.55 (not max-out). The high V reflects substantial labor share (58.9%) and meaningful automatable fraction (40.55%), primarily driven by the admin tail (15.75pp) and the specialized designer automation slice (16pp). The moderate uplift is consistent with the MELTING classification for the design-documentation revenue stream, partially offset by the physical sample-making component that cannot be automated and anchors some durable demand.

terminal_value.justification: Sub-segment analysis required (catch-all code): (1) Fashion design services (dominant ~60% of 541490 revenue): The deliverable — sketches, colorways, tech packs, CAD patterns, digital renderings — is exactly what generative AI tools now produce. McKinsey estimates AI can reduce design iteration time by 40-60%; tools like Style3D AI, AiDA, and Adobe Firefly are directly competing for the billable design documentation that constitutes ~60% of fashion design service fees. The surviving paid demand is the named-designer taste brand and physical sampling expertise. For the 82 acquirable firms in the $1-10M EBITDA band, most are not named-designer anchor firms — they are mid-sized design service operations whose commodity tech-pack and colorway work is being directly replaced. MELTING for this sub-segment. (2) Jewelry design services (secondary ~15%): Similar product-replacement dynamic. AI tools (generative jewelry design, 3D rendering) are commoditizing the digital design deliverable. Physical samples persist but constitute a small fraction of fee revenue. MELTING for digital design; PRESSURED for physical prototype work. (3) Exhibit/float design (niche ~10%): The product is a physical object — float fabrication, exhibit construction. AI cuts design documentation but not the fabrication. PRESSURED or DURABLE for this sub-segment. (4) Textile/print design (~15%): Print patterns and colorway generation are among the most AI-automatable design tasks (demonstrated by firms like Stitch Fix AI, Pattern AI). MELTING. Blended verdict: MELTING dominates the code's revenue base. The physical sample-making and exhibit/float fabrication niches survive but are insufficient to pull the overall verdict to PRESSURED, given the dominant fashion design sub-segment is clearly in product-replacement territory.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V was justified by occupation-level automatable fraction estimates based on task analysis of fashion/specialized designer roles (tech packs, colorways, digital renderings — established AI capability per Style3D, AiDA, McKinsey research) and the fashion industry AI adoption data from McKinsey/BoF and masterofcode. A was derived from current adoption percentage (~25% firm-level) and historical analog timelines for fashion CAD software (8-12 years). Neither V nor A made reference to PE deal activity, consolidation waves, or M&A trends.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Specialized design services firms bill primarily per-collection/project (~60%) and day-rate/hourly (~30%) with a minority royalty share (~10%). Both per-collection and day-rate models directly transmit AI efficiency to clients: when AI reduces hours to generate tech packs, colorways, and digital renderings, apparel brand clients reduce the scope of commissioned work (fewer paid design days per season, or reduced per-piece tech pack fees as competition from AI tools intensifies). The mechanism is documented: apparel brands in 2024 'trimmed buy budgets and delayed designer collaborations' per IBIS World fashion designers market report. This deflation channel is reflected in pi_dist = 0.25 (clients capture efficiency gains through scope reduction) and pi_moat = 0.18 (no licensure, key-person value is not retained by the firm, AI directly substitutes for design documentation).

top_uncertain_inputs.plausible_range: 2.5-5.0 (very thin comps for pure design services firms)

evidence.reviewer_note: CATCH-ALL CODE — heterogeneous: true. Top-3 revenue sub-segments scored against dominant sub-segment (fashion design services): (1) Fashion design services — dominant by revenue (NYC/LA concentration), MELTING terminal value; (2) Jewelry design services — secondary, similar MELTING dynamic for digital design work; (3) Textile/print design services — third, also MELTING. The exhibit/float fabrication niche (physical deliverable, PRESSURED/DURABLE) is too small to shift the dominant sub-segment verdict. Role mix rebase required: the 4-digit OEWS role_mix is contaminated by interior designer (18.41%) and graphic designer (15.38%) shares belonging to sibling codes 541410/541430. The code's actual core occupations (27-1022 Fashion Designers, jewelry/textile specialty SOCs) are absent from the OEWS top-10. Role shares rebuilt from prompt guidance as hypotheses — flagged LOW quality throughout. No specific PE-backed consolidator targeting 541490 firms was identified; active_consolidators=0 is the research finding, not an assumption. buy_mult and exit_mult are ESTIMATE quality due to complete absence of transaction comps specifically for independent fashion/specialized design services firms — the only available analogues are marketing agency data and fashion brand (product company) M&A, neither of which directly applies. M score is particularly uncertain. confidence_overall = LOW, reflecting: (1) role_mix is a hypothesis rebuilt from contaminated 4-digit data; (2) no direct M&A comps; (3) no design-specific owner age data; (4) catch-all code heterogeneity. S = 2.57 is near the low range of the scoring distribution, consistent with a MELTING terminal value, near-zero consolidation activity, and weak value-capture despite high V.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: AI coding assistants (GitHub Copilot 46% of code generated per GitHub 2025 data; SO Survey 2025: 68% of developers use AI daily/weekly) heavily automate boilerplate, tests, migrations, documentation, and maintenance. Requirements elicitation, architecture judgment, legacy-system spelunking, and accountability for production systems persist. For dev-shop context where per-developer tasks skew toward delivery implementation, within-role fraction 0.40 is supported by current tool evidence and reflects AI writing ~40-46% of code while humans retain architectural and client-context work.

Tier-1 support, internal helpdesk, password resets, onboarding/offboarding are among the most AI-automatable tasks. AI service-desk tools (Zendesk AI, ServiceNow AI) already handle majority of tier-1 tickets. Physical desk-side support persists. 0.70 reflects strong automation evidence.

Status reporting, estimation, sprint mechanics, resource scheduling, and administrative paperwork automate substantially. Client escalation, delivery accountability, hiring decisions, and senior client relationship management persist. AI also compresses the manager-to-developer ratio. 0.25 reflects partial automation of managerial overhead.

Requirements documentation, specifications, process mapping, gap analyses automate substantially with AI. Stakeholder elicitation, organizational navigation, and vendor selection judgment involving political and relational context persist. 0.35 reflects that documentation-heavy work is the majority of analyst hours.

Proposal and SOW production, CRM data entry, pipeline reporting automate. Relationship selling to enterprise IT buyers and procurement navigation persists. The revenue moat in dev shops is largely relationship-driven. 0.20 captures the administrative fraction of sales work.

Internal operations (scheduling, reporting, invoicing, compliance tracking) automate. Executive client relationship and strategic hiring decisions persist. Owner-operators hold critical client relationships in sub-$10M firms. 0.20 reflects internal ops share of manager time.

Heterogeneous category including additional delivery engineers, DevOps/infrastructure roles, and other technical staff. Similar automation exposure to software developers for delivery tasks; configuration, scripting, and infrastructure automation are AI-exposed. 0.40 matches developer analog.

Sprint mechanics, burn-rate tracking, resource allocation spreadsheets, status report generation, and estimation automate substantially. Client escalation, scope negotiation, and delivery accountability are the non-automatable residue. 0.35 reflects that administrative PM work is the larger fraction.

Process mapping, requirements documentation, and analytical report generation automate substantially. Stakeholder interviews and organizational judgment persist. 0.35 consistent with analyst-facing automation evidence.

Automated testing, test case generation, regression testing, and documentation are among the most directly AI-automatable QA tasks. GitHub Copilot and AI tools generate tests as a primary use case. Exploratory testing of novel user scenarios and security edge cases persist. 0.60 reflects that scripted/routine testing dominates QA hours.

breakdown_by_role.role: Admin HR Finance and Unallocated Tail
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Top-10 occupations sum to 0.6059 of wages; tail = 0.3941. Tail includes recruiting/HR, bench management, finance/invoicing, and additional billable engineers not in top-10. Recruiting and back-office automate heavily; physical site work and senior bench-management judgment persist. Some tail is additional delivery engineers (treat as 0.40 automatable) and some is pure admin (0.60 automatable). Blended 0.30 reflects conservatism given unknown composition of tail and that bench/utilization management is operationally critical in dev shops.

pi_dist.rationale: Custom programming is billed predominantly T&M (time and materials) and staff-augmentation FTE rates. Everest Group Q1 2024: ~60-70% of new agile development contracts use capacity-based/T&M models. When AI cuts hours per deliverable 30-50%, T&M revenue falls mechanically unless repricing succeeds. Globant launched outcome-based AI Pods in mid-2025 (subscription not hourly), and EPAM is pursuing modest price increases — evidence that large-cap firms are attempting repricing, but the $1-10M EBITDA target tier has no pricing power to impose outcome-based terms on enterprise clients. Firms do own the client relationship via MSAs and multi-year support agreements, and client-specific codebase knowledge and documentation confer some lock-in. But code is transferable by design, switching costs are moderate, and AI directly relieves the engineering-scarcity moat. pi_dist = 0.35 reflects firm-owns-relationship (not an upstream gatekeeper) but with explicit T&M deflation mechanism: AI savings flow to clients as fewer billed hours in ~65% of revenue.
sources: Everest Group Q1 2024 agile contract analysis (capacity-based model ~60-70% of new agile contracts)
Globant AI Pods announcement mid-2025 (ainvest.com coverage, June 2025)
EPAM Systems Q4 2025 earnings commentary on price increases

pi_moat.rationale: Switching costs for custom dev shops are moderate but not high: codebase is documented and transferable by design (the product's portability is a selling point). MSAs and multi-year support agreements provide contractual lock-in for maintenance streams but not for new development. No licensure requirement. The historic scarcity moat — engineering talent — is directly eroded by AI making clients' own small teams more productive. Offshore competition (India, Eastern Europe) already prices marginal dev capacity competitively; AI erases the remaining onshore premium around communication and context bandwidth. The moat that survives is accountability for production systems — a client cannot easily dismiss the firm mid-project without stranding work — and domain expertise accumulated in specific client environments. pi_moat = 0.30 reflects these real but modest barriers against the direct AI-bypass risk.
sources: Custom software development outsourcing trends (Freshcode IT, 2026 statistics)
Aventis Advisors IT Services Valuation Multiples report (size-discount data implies limited moat premium at small scale)
Prompt guidance on sibling-boundary switching cost analysis

t50_years.derivation: Current individual developer AI tool adoption: 68% daily/weekly per Stack Overflow Developer Survey 2025 (50.6% daily + 17.4% weekly). At the firm level, material AI integration (AI coding assistants used by majority of staff, shaping delivery methodology) is estimated at ~55% of firms in the $1-10M EBITDA tier, based on the SO developer-level data discounted for firm-level organizational lag (smaller firms adopt slower than large-firm Fortune 100 data would imply). Historical analog: cloud computing reached 50% of professional services firms in ~5-6 years (2010-2016); agile methodology in ~7 years from broad introduction. However, AI coding tool adoption has been dramatically faster — SO 2023 showed <50% using AI, SO 2025 shows 68% using AI daily/weekly — two years from minority to supermajority among individual developers. Given we are at or very near the 50% firm-level threshold now (2026), t50 is approximately 2 years accounting for the organizational lag between individual developer adoption and firm-level integration into delivery methodology and pricing model.

current_adoption_pct.source: Stack Overflow Developer Survey 2025 (primary, ~65,000 developer respondents); firm-level estimate derived from individual-developer data
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: 68% of professional developers use AI tools daily or weekly (50.6% daily, 17.4% weekly); 84% currently using or planning to use AI tools; discounted to ~55% at firm-level for target $1-10M EBITDA tier given organizational lag vs. enterprise adoption

historical_analogs.value: Cloud computing (2010-2016): ~6 years to 50% firm adoption in professional services; agile methodology: ~7 years to majority adoption; mobile-first development: ~5 years — but AI coding tool adoption has been dramatically faster, with SO survey showing jump from <50% to 68% individual usage in approximately 2 years (2023-2025)
historical_analogs.source: Stack Overflow Developer Survey historical series; industry technology adoption research
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: AI tool usage jumped from ~65% in 2024 to 84% currently using or planning to use in 2025; fastest technology adoption in developer tooling history per SO survey commentary

owners_60plus_pct.source: Census Annual Business Survey 2022; Harvard Business Review founder age research; general small business demographics (Gallup, Project Equity 2024)
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: Average tech company founder age at founding ~41.9 years (Azoulay et al.); SaaS founders average 35 at founding; general small business 52% owned by 55+ (Gallup 2024). Tech sector founders materially younger than sector-54 norms. For established dev shops (20-99 employee tier, ~10-15 years old on average), applying 42+12=54 median current-owner age implies ~30% at 60+, materially below sector-54 norm of ~35-40%.

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: No succession shortage specifically documented for custom programming dev shops. Prompt notes founder demographics skew younger than sector-54 norms, weakening succession pressure. No dedicated owner-age survey found for this 6-digit code. General small-business surveys (Gallup 2024: 52% of employer businesses owned by 55+ owners) are not sector-specific and overstate aging in tech.

active_consolidators.source: PE roll-up market research; Aventis Advisors IT Services M&A report; general PE TMT activity survey 2024-2025
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: PE represented 60% of mid-market TMT deal volume in 2025. Custom programming (541511) is a fragmented target; strategic acquirers (Accenture, Globant, EPAM) dominate large deals while PE buys smaller dev shops. Approximately 8-12 active PE-backed platforms doing dev-shop add-ons in the $1-10M EBITDA tier identified in current market, smaller wave than the 541512 MSP consolidation; 10 used as central estimate.

buy_mult.source: Aventis Advisors IT Services Valuation Multiples report (361 transactions, 2015-2026)
buy_mult.figure_quoted: Deals <$5M: median EV/EBITDA 5.9x; deals $5-20M: median EV/EBITDA 7.9x. For $1-5M EBITDA dev shops (deal size roughly $6-33M at 6-7x), median ~6.5x interpolated between the two size buckets. Note: EBITDA basis after market-rate compensation per rule 3; source is transaction-level not SDE-based.

exit_mult.source: Aventis Advisors IT Services Valuation Multiples report; IT Consulting public market data (Mar 2026: 10.2x EV/EBITDA)
exit_mult.figure_quoted: Deals $20-50M: median 10.5x EV/EBITDA; deals $50-100M: 11.5x; IT Consulting public market Mar 2026: 10.2x EV/EBITDA. Platform exits for $10M+ EBITDA dev-services firms range 10.5-11.5x; central estimate 11.0x. EBITDA basis post market-rate owner compensation.

pricing_structure.value: ~65% T&M/staff-augmentation hourly or FTE-rate billing, ~20% fixed-price, ~10% hybrid/retainer, ~5% other. T&M dominates the target $1-10M EBITDA tier.
pricing_structure.source: Everest Group Q1 2024 agile contract analysis; Scio Diamond Digital State of Nearshore Report 2023; industry practitioner commentary (baytechconsulting.com, forecast.app, scnsoft.com 2025-2026)
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: Everest Group Q1 2024: approximately 60-70% of new agile development contracts use capacity-based/T&M model; Scio 2023: 55% dedicated team, 30% T&M, 15% fixed-price; industry commentary consistently places T&M at majority of custom dev shop revenue

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: Uplift = V_raw x 100 = 22.79 pp, well above the 3 pp threshold while A = 5.0. Consistent — no tension. AI frees a significant fraction of labor cost (~23 pp of revenue), and adoption is fast (t50 = 2 years). The high V is moderated by low C (T&M billing deflates capture), producing a mid-range S.

terminal_value.justification: Custom programming demand is not being replaced wholesale — the custom software development market is forecast to grow at 20%+ CAGR (GMInsights, SNS Insider 2025 reports, $51.8B in 2025). Enterprise outsourcing is not declining: Deloitte 2024 Global Outsourcing Survey shows access to talent (42%) now ranks as primary driver, and GenAI integration in outsourcing contracts grew 40% (2023-2025). However, demand is PRESSURED not DURABLE because: (1) AI relieves the scarcity moat that drove outsourcing — clients can now build more with smaller internal teams; (2) T&M billing means AI productivity reduces billable hours mechanically, shrinking engagement size at the same scope; (3) offshore competition erases the remaining onshore communication premium. The distinguishing factor from MELTING is accountability: enterprises still outsource because they want someone else to hold liability for production systems, security, and maintenance — this accountability role persists even as AI handles code generation. The product (working production software, delivered with accountability) survives; the revenue per deliverable shrinks as AI compresses hours. PRESSURED reflects shrinking per-engagement revenue offset by broader addressable market.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V (ai_replaceable_share = 0.338405) was derived solely from occupation-by-occupation within-role automatable fractions grounded in: (1) GitHub Copilot data showing 46% of code is AI-generated; (2) Stack Overflow Developer Survey 2025 showing 68% daily/weekly AI tool use; (3) automation evidence per specific occupation type (QA test automation, support desk AI tools, documentation generation). No reference to M&A deal activity or consolidation trends. A (t50 = 2.0 years) was derived from the SO survey adoption trajectory (2023-to-2025 jump from minority to 68% daily/weekly use) and technology adoption analogs (cloud ~6 years; but AI tools adopted faster), adjusted to firm-level organizational lag. No deal activity referenced.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Billing mix: ~65% T&M/staff-aug (hourly or FTE-rate), ~20% fixed-price, ~10% hybrid/retainer, ~5% other. T&M dominates the target $1-10M EBITDA tier. Mechanism: When AI cuts developer hours per deliverable by 30-50% (GitHub Copilot evidence: developers complete tasks 55% faster; median code AI-generated 46%), T&M revenue falls mechanically unless firms reprice to fixed-price or outcome-based. Large-cap firms (Globant, EPAM) are piloting outcome/subscription models, but the target sub-$10M dev shop has no pricing power to impose outcome-based terms on enterprise procurement. The pricing deflation is reflected in pi_dist = 0.35 (below 0.5, indicating that T&M billing structure substantially hands AI savings to clients as reduced hours billed rather than retained as operator margin) and pi_moat = 0.30 (limited ability to prevent clients from insourcing or switching as AI democratizes development capacity).

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A ranges from min(10,10/1.5)=6.67 to min(10,10/4.0)=2.50; S would range from ~4.19 (t50=1.5) to ~3.32 (t50=4.0), a ~0.87 swing in S
C ranges from 10x0.25x0.30=0.75 to 10x0.45x0.30=1.35; S would range from ~3.63 (pi_dist=0.25) to ~4.05 (pi_dist=0.45), a ~0.42 swing in S
OW ranges from 0.429 (0.25) to 0.857 (0.40; capped at 0.9 at higher values); B ranges from ~5.93 to ~7.74; S would range from ~3.73 to ~3.98, a ~0.25 swing in S

evidence.reviewer_note: 541511 is a first-order pricing-deflation candidate. V is high (9.12) because labor-intensity is high and AI-automates large fractions of delivery, QA, and support work. However, C is low (1.05) because T&M billing mechanically passes AI productivity gains to clients as fewer billed hours, not operator margin. This combination (high V, low C) is the defining tension for this industry. S = 3.84 reflects the fundamental problem: a very automatable labor pool (V=9.1) coupled with a billing model that prevents the operator from capturing AI savings (C=1.05). The terminal-value classification is PRESSURED not MELTING because accountability for production systems persists as a durable outsourcing driver even as AI code generation matures — clients still want someone to call at 3am when the system goes down. The n_band of 5,767 is the largest in sector 54 (TD=0.940), boosting B. M is modest (2.77) reflecting a real but not extraordinary arbitrage between buy (6.5x) and exit (11.0x) multiples. Key uncertainty is t50: if AI coding tool adoption at firm-level has essentially already reached 50% (supported by SO Survey data), t50 could be 1-2 years, pushing A to 5-6.67 and S to 3.8-4.2. The 4-digit OEWS role_mix is used per dataset specification; for 541511 specifically, the developer share is likely higher than the 4-digit average (prompt notes 35-40% plausible vs. 23.9% in OEWS), but the dataset inputs are used as provided per core rule 6. This understates V slightly. Active consolidators count (10) is an estimate; the 541511 roll-up wave is less concentrated and documented than 541512 MSP consolidation.

Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).
