breakdown_by_role.rationale: For 541512 (MSP/systems design context), the 15-1252 role captures systems/network engineers and integration engineers as well as software developers. Configuration, scripting, integration glue code, automated runbook creation, patching automation, and documentation are heavily AI-exposed. AI-powered self-healing endpoint tools (Kaseya agentic AI announced April 2026, ConnectWise Sidekick) are already commercial. Solution architecture judgment, client-specific environment knowledge, on-site/hardware installation, and security incident response persist. 0.35 is modestly below the 541511 developer fraction (0.40) because this role includes more hardware/field work and systems integration judgment.

The 4-digit OEWS rate of 3.3% materially understates this role for MSP-heavy 541512, where support desk is the largest headcount block (prompt estimates 8-12% at 6-digit). However, dataset inputs are used as given per core rule 6. AI service desks (Kaseya agentic AI, ConnectWise Sidekick) already resolve 65% of tier-1 contacts without human intervention per industry data. Kaseya 2025 reports 40-70% reductions in ticket resolution times for AI adopters. Physical desk-side/field dispatch persists as the non-automatable residue. 0.75 reflects that AI is most commercially deployed precisely here and performance evidence is strongest.

vCIO/technology-alignment reviews (QBRs) are the primary client retention mechanism and persist as relationship-driven. Project coordination, status reporting, and resource scheduling automate. 0.25 reflects that the trusted-advisor QBR relationship — the MSP's core retention tool — is the non-automatable core of this role.

Assessments, migration planning documentation, compliance gap analyses (CMMC, HIPAA, SOC 2 checklists), and standards documentation automate substantially with AI. Stakeholder elicitation, vendor selection judgment, and technology-roadmap conversations with SMB owners persist. 0.35 consistent with prompt guidance on analyst automation.

Proposal and quote production, renewals paperwork, and pipeline management automate. Local relationship selling to SMB owners — who buy IT as trust, not specification — persists as the non-automatable residue. 0.20 consistent with MSP relationship-selling model.

Internal operations (scheduling, invoicing, compliance tracking, vendor management) automate. Owner-operators at sub-$10M MSPs hold all key client relationships and are rarely replaceable. 0.20 reflects internal ops share of owner-manager time.

For 541512, this category includes network engineers, DevOps engineers, and infrastructure specialists — roles where AI-powered endpoint automation, network monitoring, and self-healing tools are commercially deployed. Scripting, configuration management, and automated remediation are the primary automatable activities. On-site hardware work and physical network troubleshooting persist. 0.45 slightly above the developer analog due to higher share of automatable infrastructure scripting vs. custom code architecture.

Resource scheduling, status reporting, and administrative project tracking automate. Client delivery accountability and scope negotiation persist. Slightly lower than 541511 (0.35) because MSP project delivery is less documentation-heavy and more relationship-driven. 0.30.
Compliance gap analysis, security assessments, process mapping, and analytical reports automate substantially. Stakeholder navigation and organizational judgment persist. 0.35.

For 541512, this role includes testing of deployed configurations and system integrations. Automated configuration validation, compliance scanning, and vulnerability assessment automate heavily. Exploratory testing of novel client-specific integrations and physical hardware validation persist. 0.55, slightly below 541511's 0.60 because more physical validation work in MSP context.

breakdown_by_role.role: Admin Dispatch Finance and Unallocated Tail
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Top-10 occupations sum to 0.6059 of wages; tail = 0.3941. For 541512, the tail contains: dispatch/scheduling (heavily automatable with AI routing tools), procurement/quoting (automatable), invoicing/billing (automatable), field technicians (NOT automatable — physical site visits, hardware installation, cable runs), and additional bench engineers. Given MSPs have larger field/dispatch proportions than dev shops, blended automation rate is modestly higher than 541511 (0.30). Estimate 0.35 reflecting balance between automatable admin/dispatch and non-automatable field workforce.

pi_dist.rationale: MSP billing is predominantly per-seat/per-endpoint monthly recurring revenue (MRR) under 1-3 year contracts — a capture-POSITIVE structure relative to T&M (fees decoupled from hours). When AI service desks cut delivery cost per seat, the operator keeps the spread as margin until competitive repricing. Kaseya 2025 data: 65-78% MRR is industry target; best-in-class MSPs run 78-90% MRR. The firm owns the client relationship via MSA contracts, documented environment knowledge, integrated RMM/PSA/security tooling, and QBR cadence — meaningful switching costs. However, two channels reduce pi_dist below 0.5: (1) AI-driven SMB headcount reduction shrinks the billed seat base even if per-seat rates hold ('seat count channel'); seat-based pricing fell from 21% to 15% of SaaS companies in 12 months (Revenue Wizards/Bain data); (2) upstream RMM/PSA platforms (Kaseya, ConnectWise, Microsoft) may capture AI value through license pricing or offer autonomous management direct to SMBs. pi_dist = 0.45 reflects stronger capture than T&M (MRR decoupled from hours) partially offset by seat-count risk and platform disintermediation risk.
sources: Pharallax AI MSP Revenue Benchmarks 2026 (MRR target ranges)
Revenue Wizards: AI Challenging Seat-Based Pricing (seat pricing decline data)
CT Acquisitions MSP M&A Multiples 2026 (MRR premium in valuations)
MSP Pricing Models 2026 Playbook, Flamingo.run

pi_moat.rationale: MSPs have materially stronger moats than dev shops (541511). Key moat elements: (1) Contractual lock-in — 1-3 year MSAs with cancellation penalties; environment documentation and tooling integrations create switching costs well above code documentation; (2) Security/compliance accountability — CMMC 2.0, HIPAA, cyber insurance underwriters, and SOC 2 requirements increasingly name an accountable MSP; clients cannot easily self-serve compliance attestation. CMMC certification opens defense-contractor markets with high switching barriers; (3) Tooling integration — RMM, PSA, backup, and security stacks are deeply integrated into client environments; migration to a new MSP requires re-deploying all tooling and re-documenting environments (typically 60-90 day migrations with business disruption risk); (4) Local trust relationship — SMB owners buy IT from people they trust, creating geographic and relationship barriers. Offsetting factors: (a) Microsoft/Kaseya/ConnectWise platform vendors could offer autonomous AI management directly to SMBs, disintermediating the MSP layer; (b) AI democratizes basic IT administration, reducing the knowledge differential between MSP and in-house IT. pi_moat = 0.40 reflects genuine and defensible switching costs from compliance/tooling/contractual lock-in, constrained by disintermediation risk.
sources: Blacksmith Infosec MSP Compliance Guide 2025 (compliance as moat)
ComplianceScorecard MSP Compliance Services 2025 (cyber insurance requirements)
ConnectWise MSP Valuations blog (switching cost analysis)
CT Acquisitions Private Equity MSP 2026 (retention data from acquisitions)

t50_years.derivation: Current firm-level material AI adoption in automatable functions: ~35% per Kaseya 2025 benchmark (30% already use AI to eliminate tedious tasks; adjusted up to 35% for this estimate's broader scope including AI service desk tools). MSP Success 2025 reader survey: 93% use genAI internally but only ~25% say it has significantly changed operations — the 25% 'significantly changed' figure is a better proxy for material automation. Historical analog: RMM adoption among MSPs from <20% to >80% took approximately 6-8 years (2008-2016); PSA adoption similar. However, AI service-desk products are already commercial and achieving 40-70% ticket resolution automation in early-adopter MSPs, with vendor pressure (Kaseya, ConnectWise) accelerating deployment. Starting from ~35% material adoption, reaching 50% of firms materially adopting AI in their helpdesk and automatable functions is estimated at 3 years (2029), accounting for: adoption S-curve dynamics (fastest growth when at ~35%, near inflection); smaller MSP organizational lag vs. enterprise; competitive pressure as leading MSPs differentiate on AI efficiency and winning clients from laggards.

current_adoption_pct.source: Kaseya 2025 Global MSP Benchmark Report; MSP Success Magazine 2025 reader survey
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: Kaseya 2025: 30% of MSPs already use AI to eliminate tedious tasks with ticket triage most common; MSP Success 2025 reader survey: 93% of MSPs use genAI internally, but only ~25% say AI has significantly changed operations. Kaseya 2025 State of the MSP: 91% cited profitability as a priority; agentic AI platform launched April 2026. Estimate 35% of firms materially using AI in automatable functions (above Kaseya 30% to capture AI service desk tools beyond ticketing automation).

historical_analogs.value: RMM adoption among MSPs: <20% to >80% in approximately 6-8 years (2008-2016); PSA adoption: similar 6-8 year trajectory; cloud tools adoption: ~5 years. AI service-desk tools are current-generation RMM equivalents with commercial traction accelerating from 2024.
historical_analogs.source: Channel research general knowledge; Kaseya benchmark series historical comparisons; ConnectWise MSP Business Maturity Report 2024
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: Kaseya benchmarks track MSP technology adoption across multiple generations of tooling; RMM reached near-universal adoption within 8 years from first commercial availability; AI tools are following a faster trajectory per vendor telemetry.

owners_60plus_pct.source: JFS Partners MSP M&A 2026 commentary; Kaseya 2025 State of MSP; general small-business demographics (Gallup 2024: 52% of employer businesses owned by 55+)
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: JFS Partners 2026: 'A significant wave of MSP founders who built their businesses in the 2000s and 2010s are now in their 50s and 60s.' 34% of MSP owners plan to sell within 5 years (Kaseya 2025). 26% of MSPs have been in business 16+ years (JumpCloud 2024 MSP statistics), suggesting founders from the break-fix era (1990s-2000s) who are now 60+. Estimate 40% at 60+, higher than dev-shop (541511) estimate of 30% reflecting older MSP industry vintage.

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: JFS Partners 2026 MSP M&A commentary: 'significant wave of MSP founders now in their 50s and 60s ready to monetize'; Kaseya 2025: 34% of MSP owners plan to sell within 5 years; Datto 2025 State of MSP: 'over 30% of MSPs said they were actively exploring mergers or acquisitions within the next two years, driven by market consolidation.' Industry broker commentary consistently identifies retiring break-fix era founders lacking internal successors as a primary M&A driver, with PE filling the buyer vacuum.

active_consolidators.source: CT Acquisitions Private Equity MSP 2026 guide; Solganick YTD 2025 MSP M&A report
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: 169 disclosed MSP M&A transactions in 2025, with PE in ~69% of deals. 7 named PE-backed platforms: Evergreen Services Group (Alpine Investors, 47 acquisitions in 2025), Thrive Networks (Court Square + Berkshire Partners), New Charter Technologies (Oval Partners), Ntiva (PSP Capital), Integris (OMERS Private Equity), Coretelligent (Norwest Equity Partners), Magna5 (AEA Investors). Additional smaller PE-backed platforms bring total to ~15 active add-on buyers in the $1-10M EBITDA tier.

buy_mult.source: Aventis Advisors MSP Valuation Multiples report; CT Acquisitions MSP M&A Multiples 2026
buy_mult.figure_quoted: Aventis: MSPs under $5M deal value: 5.0-5.2x EV/EBITDA; $5-20M: ~6.8x. CT Acquisitions: $3M-$10M revenue MSPs at 70-85% MRR traded at median ~9.0x adjusted EBITDA (note: this is on adjusted EBITDA with MRR premium, not blended EBITDA of typical small MSP). For the broader $1-10M EBITDA target tier including resale-heavy and mixed-model MSPs, 5.5x represents the median buy multiple. EBITDA basis post market-rate owner compensation per rule 3; MRR-heavy books command premium above this.

exit_mult.source: Aventis Advisors MSP Valuation Multiples; Solganick 2025 MSP M&A report; CT Acquisitions MSP M&A Multiples 2026
exit_mult.figure_quoted: Aventis: platform-scale MSPs ($500M+ deal value): 11.2x EV/EBITDA. Solganick 2025: platform exits with strong organic growth and cybersecurity practice recording ~20x EBITDA for largest platforms. CT Acquisitions: $10M-$25M revenue MSPs with 80%+ MRR median 11.4x (IQR 10.2-13.1x). Central estimate 11.0x for $10M+ EBITDA MSP platforms at secondary PE exit or strategic sale. EBITDA basis post market-rate compensation.

pricing_structure.value: ~65-82% MRR per-seat/per-endpoint monthly recurring (1-3 year contracts); ~18-25% project work (migrations, installations, equipment); ~5-10% break-fix/T&M residual. Best-in-class MSPs run 78-90% MRR. Hardware resale (pass-through, low margin) further inflates revenue without EBITDA contribution.
pricing_structure.source: Pharallax AI MSP Revenue Benchmarks 2026; ConnectWise Service Leadership Index (cited by multiple sources); CT Acquisitions MSP M&A Multiples 2026
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: Industry target: 65-78% MRR minimum; healthy MSPs 78-90% MRR. Recurring revenue accounted for 82% of total MSP income in 2023. Project services gross margin dropped from 23% to 12.9% between Q4 2023 and Q4 2024 per ConnectWise Service Leadership Index — reflecting compression in project/resale revenue. Per-seat pricing fell from 21% to 15% of companies in 12 months as hybrid models surged (Bain/Revenue Wizards data on seat-based model erosion).

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: Uplift = V_raw x 100 = 22.35 pp, well above 3 pp threshold while A = 3.33. Consistent — no tension. The moderate A (slower than 541511 because MSP firms have more organizational complexity in deploying AI service-desk changes) does not create any inconsistency with the high uplift potential.

terminal_value.justification: SMBs will continue to need their IT managed and their security/compliance posture maintained regardless of AI maturity. The distinguishing mechanism is accountability: cyber insurance underwriters, CMMC 2.0 requirements, HIPAA attestations, and SOC 2 compliance increasingly name an accountable third-party provider. This accountability role is not replaceable by AI — it requires a legal entity to hold liability, incident response capacity, and ongoing attestation. The durable demand scenario (operator manages AI agents on behalf of clients too small to do it themselves) appears to be winning: Kaseya, ConnectWise, and Microsoft all deepened their channel economics in 2025 rather than launching direct-to-SMB autonomous IT management at scale, preserving the MSP channel. Per-seat MRR is under pressure from AI-driven SMB headcount reduction (the seat-count channel: organizations needing 60 seats instead of 150), but the compliance/security accountability revenue stream decouples from headcount — clients pay for protection and attestation, not for the number of people who need password resets. Evidence: Solganick tracked 466 MSP acquisitions in 2025 (20% surge) and PE remains at 69-72% of buyers, consistent with durable demand expectations. Contrast with 541511 (PRESSURED): MSP has per-seat contractual recurring revenue, compliance moat, and tooling lock-in that dev shops lack. DURABLE classification reflects that the accountable-operator role persists through AI maturity, though per-headcount seat economics face pressure.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V (ai_replaceable_share = 0.346770) was derived from occupation-by-occupation within-role automatable fractions grounded in: (1) Kaseya 2025 benchmark data on AI service desk performance (40-70% ticket resolution time reduction, 65% tier-1 resolution without human intervention); (2) commercial availability of agentic AI tools for endpoint automation (Kaseya April 2026, ConnectWise Sidekick); (3) occupation-specific automation evidence (support desk AI most advanced, field/hardware not automatable, QBR relationships persist). No reference to M&A deal activity or PE consolidation trends. A (t50 = 3.0 years) was derived from Kaseya 2025 current adoption data (~30-35% material adoption) and RMM/PSA technology adoption analogs (~6-8 years to majority). No deal activity referenced.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Billing mix: ~65-82% per-seat/per-endpoint MRR under 1-3 year MSA contracts; ~18-25% project work; ~5-10% T&M residual. MRR billing is capture-POSITIVE near-term because fees are decoupled from hours — when AI service desks reduce delivery cost per seat, the MSP keeps the margin spread until competitive repricing. This is reflected in pi_dist = 0.45 (above the T&M-deflation level of 0.35 used for 541511). However, the seat-count channel creates a secondary deflation mechanism: AI-driven SMB headcount reduction shrinks the number of billed seats even if per-seat rates hold. Revenue Wizards / Bain data shows seat-based pricing fell from 21% to 15% of companies in 12 months. This seat-count risk is modeled in pi_dist (not fully at the capture-maximum of 0.55+ because of seat erosion) and pi_moat (platform disintermediation risk). Additionally, hardware/resale project revenue (18-25% of blended) passes AI savings through to clients as competitive pricing on commoditized equipment — this lower-capture revenue stream further reduces the blended pi_dist below the MRR-only optimistic case.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A ranges from min(10,10/2.0)=5.00 to min(10,10/5.0)=2.00; S would range from ~5.03 (t50=2.0) to ~3.79 (t50=5.0), a ~1.24 swing in S
C ranges from 10x0.45x0.30=1.35 to 10x0.45x0.50=2.25; S would range from ~4.16 (pi_moat=0.30) to ~4.63 (pi_moat=0.50), a ~0.47 swing in S

OW ranges from 0.857 (0.35) to 0.9 clamped then +0.1 succession (0.50 would be (0.50-0.10)/0.35=1.143 clamped to 0.9, +0.1=1.0 capped); B ranges from ~7.80 to ~8.47; S would range from ~4.41 to ~4.52, a ~0.11 swing in S

evidence.reviewer_note: 541512 is the flagship MSP roll-up code. Key structural difference from 541511: per-seat MRR billing is capture-positive (fees decoupled from hours), producing C = 1.80 vs. 541511's C = 1.05. This is the single largest driver of S difference between the two sibling codes (4.41 vs. 3.84). The DURABLE terminal value reflects the compliance/security accountability moat — cyber insurance requirements, CMMC 2.0, and HIPAA attestations create durable outsourced-IT demand that is legally named rather than merely preferred. B is strong (7.80) driven by active consolidation wave (15 PE-backed platforms, 169 deals in 2025 per Solganick/CT Acquisitions), high OW (older founder cohort from break-fix era), and documented succession shortage (34% of owners plan to sell within 5 years per Kaseya 2025). n_band (3,524) is smaller than 541511 (5,767), reducing TD (0.887 vs. 0.940). M = 4.0 reflects a strong buy-to-exit arbitrage: small MSPs trade at 5.5x while PE platforms exit at 11.0x, a 100% premium. The 4-digit OEWS role_mix materially understates the support-desk headcount for MSP-heavy 541512 (prompt estimates 8-12% vs. dataset's 3.3%); using dataset inputs per core rule 6 understates the ai_replaceable_share for this code. This understatement slightly deflates V below what a 6-digit OEWS mix would produce. Active consolidators (15) includes 7 named PE platforms plus smaller platforms; the MSP wave is more concentrated and documented than any other sector-54 roll-up. Caution: the 541512 target pool blends MRR-heavy MSPs (high capture, high multiple) with VAR/project-heavy systems integrators (low capture, lower multiple) — the scoring assumes MSP-model dominance consistent with 541512 self-classification norms.

breakdown_by_role.role: Operations/monitoring engineers (15-1252 mapped to ops staff at 541513)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: At 541513, this SOC code proxies systems/network admins and AIOps targets: monitoring, patching, incident triage, capacity reporting, and runbook execution are substantially automatable. Physical rack/stack and client-site presence persist. 40% fraction reflects high within-role automation target balanced against irreducible physical/judgment slice.

Tier-1/2 support under outsourcing contracts is the primary AI automation target: chatbots, auto-resolution, ticket classification. Under per-ticket or per-seat outsourcing contracts, automation economics are clear. Physical/on-site slice persists.

Service delivery and account management: SLA reporting and coordination automate; but the contract-governance relationship with client IT leadership is the retention asset and persists. Low fraction reflects that this role is fundamentally a relationship-management role in outsourcing.
Service-level reporting, transition planning documentation, and audit work automate substantially. Client governance interaction and transition-risk management require judgment and persist.
Long-cycle outsourcing-contract sales and renewals are relationship-intensive and persist. Internal proposal generation and pipeline administration automate marginally.
Company management: internal ops and reporting partially automate; strategic and client-facing management does not.
Mixed operations roles; AIOps targets the monitoring and shift-operations slice. Broad category; 40% reflects average across mixed automatable/non-automatable sub-roles.
Rollout coordination and status reporting automate; client escalation and stakeholder management persist.
Service-level audits, transition planning docs, SLA analysis automate; client interaction persists.
Automated testing and validation tools are mature; substantial portion of QA work is automatable. Human judgment for edge cases and novel failure modes persists.

breakdown_by_role.role: Unallocated tail (shift operators, physical facilities staff, admin, remainder)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Remainder (1 - 0.6059 = 0.3941 of wage bill) covers shift operations, physical data-center staff (rack/stack, media handling, physical security), and administrative staff. Back-office admin automates; AIOps targets shift-ops monitoring; physical facilities work is not automatable. 25% fraction reflects that roughly one-quarter of this diverse tail is in automatable administrative or monitoring tasks.

pi_dist.rationale: Multi-year outsourcing contracts with benchmarking clauses and productivity glidepaths are the dominant billing form. These explicitly commit efficiency gains to clients at renewal — evidenced by Kyndryl's pattern: automation savings via Advanced Delivery initiative are redeployed or absorbed into lower contract value, not retained as pure margin expansion. Procurement-run renewals further restrict pricing power. Transition switching costs give the firm moderate relationship control between renewals, but the contract structure ensures the majority of AI efficiency flows client-ward. pi_dist=0.30 captures the moderate client-relationship control of multi-year contract holders, substantially deflated by renewal repricing mechanism.
sources: Kyndryl Holdings 8-K FY2025 Q3: Advanced Delivery initiative automation savings context (https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1867072/000155837025000604/kd-20250203xex99d1.htm)

Kyndryl Holdings 8-K FY2024: contract value decline reflecting automation productivity glidepath pass-through (https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1867072/000155837024007064/kd-20240507xex99d1.htm)

pi_moat.rationale: Switching costs from complex IT estate transitions (risk of service disruption, retraining, reintegration) create real but time-limited barriers. Compliance and audit accountability for regulated/sovereign/legacy workloads adds a defensible niche. However, cloud migration — the structural bypass predating AI by a decade — continues shrinking the addressable on-prem estate. AI is not the primary bypass tool; clients migrate to hyperscalers whose operations are already automated. The residual niche (legacy/sovereign/regulated workloads) is real but represents a declining fraction. pi_moat=0.40 acknowledges genuine switching costs and compliance accountability against the structural headwind of workload migration.
sources: ISG Enterprise Managed Network Services 2025 (https://isg-one.com/docs/default-source/2025-ipl-brochures/enterprise-managed-network-services-2025-brochure.pdf)
Kyndryl/DXC earnings commentary on cloud migration impact on managed infrastructure revenue (secondary: search results aggregating SEC disclosures)

t50_years.derivation: current_adoption_pct=0.22 (22% of outsourced IT operations delivery firms materially using AIOps/AI automation in delivery). Historical analog: cloud adoption in managed IT services took approximately 10 years to go from ~10% penetration (2012) to ~50% of workloads managed via cloud-native tools (2022). AIOps adoption started from a higher base (~10% in early 2023 per Gartner, already 22% now in 2026) and is tracking faster given enterprise mandates. Remaining gap from 22% to 50% is 28pp vs. the prior cycle's ~40pp over ~10 years. Proportional estimate: 28/40 × 10 = 7 years from today. t50 = 7 years (2033).

current_adoption_pct.source: Gartner via calliber.net AIOps Statistics 2026; Thoughtworks 2025 AIOps retrospective
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: Adoption of AI-powered monitoring climbed from 42% to 54% of enterprises between 2024 and 2025; Gartner: less than 10% of enterprises had AIOps+observability autonomy in 2023, projected 40% by 2026. 67% of MSPs now offer AI-powered monitoring services.

historical_analogs.value: Cloud managed services adoption: ~10 years from ~10% penetration (2012) to ~50% of enterprise workloads managed on cloud-native infrastructure (2022). ITIL automation tools adoption in outsourced delivery: similar 8-12 year cycles observed in managed services industry.
historical_analogs.source: Zippia Cloud Adoption Statistics 2026; Auxis IT Outsourcing Trends 2026
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: Approximately 30-40% of enterprise applications now run on traditional on-premise servers, down from roughly 50% in 2019; 33% of enterprises were running over 50% of their workloads in the cloud as of 2021.

owners_60plus_pct.source: U.S. Census Bureau Annual Business Survey 2022 (via census.gov visualization); U.S. Census Bureau 2022 ABS aggregate reported by Oberlo/electroiq
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: Over half of U.S. business owners are age 55+; U.S. Census Bureau 2022 ABS: 52.3% of business owners age 55+, 29.5% ages 55-64, 22.8% age 65+. Interpolated 60+ estimate: ~35% (22.8% age 65+ plus roughly half of 55-64 cohort at ~12%).

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: ABS 2022 shows 52.3% of small business owners age 55+ broadly; Gallup and U.S. Bank surveys document lack of succession plans (only 54% have a formal plan). However, NAICS 541513 skews heavily to large firms (151 firms at 500+ employees averaging $117M revenue); individual-owner succession dynamics are less applicable than for SMB-dominant codes. No IT facilities management-specific succession shortage is documented in the sources accessed. Using false for the machine-readable flag; succession shortage at the large-firm segment (where the code's revenue is concentrated) is not the driver of M&A supply.

active_consolidators.source: CT Acquisitions Private Equity MSP 2026 guide; Solganick Technology Services M&A Update; Lincoln International IT Services Mid-Year 2024
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: 7 actively acquiring MSP platforms documented, all focused on SMB helpdesk segment (541512), not enterprise IT outsourcing/facilities management (541513). Enterprise IT outsourcing consolidation driven by strategic buyers (Kyndryl, NTT Data, Cognizant) not roll-up platforms. Active consolidators targeting the $1-10M EBITDA 541513 segment are estimated at approximately 3 (some managed infrastructure-focused PE platforms exist but are not prominently documented as active roll-up vehicles in this code).

buy_mult.source: Aventis Advisors IT Services Valuation Multiples 2015-2024; CT Acquisitions IT Managed Services M&A Multiples Report 2026
buy_mult.figure_quoted: Small IT services firms under $5M deal value: median 5.9x EV/EBITDA (Aventis, 361 transactions 2015-2026). GF Data: median technology services 7.3x for $10-50M EV band full-year 2024. For 541513 specifically (enterprise ITO, not SMB MSP), multiples at the $1-5M EBITDA level set conservatively at 5.5x reflecting lower strategic premium vs. MRR-based MSPs.

exit_mult.source: Aventis Advisors IT Services Valuation Multiples; Solganick Technology Services M&A Q4 2025 and 2026 Forecast; ibinterviewquestions.com M&A multiples reference 2025-2026
exit_mult.figure_quoted: IT services $100-500M deal size: median 11.6x EV/EBITDA; $50-100M: 11.5x (Aventis). Solganick: large platforms above $20M EBITDA seeing mid-to-upper double digit multiples (16x-20x). Enterprise ITO platform exits conservative at 10.5x given structural headwinds (cloud migration, demand erosion) vs. higher-rated MSP platforms.

pricing_structure.value: Multi-year fixed-fee or per-device/per-seat contracts with committed productivity glidepaths (~50% of revenue); time-and-materials for smaller operational contracts (~30%); cost-plus with audit rights (~20%). Benchmarking clauses at renewal are standard in enterprise outsourcing. Productivity commitments explicitly pre-commit efficiency gains to clients.
pricing_structure.source: Kyndryl Holdings FY2025 8-K; Channel Dive MSP Pricing Models; Corsica Tech IT Outsourcing Trends 2026
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: Kyndryl Advanced Delivery initiative: automation savings allow redeployment of delivery professionals; contractually required productivity commitments documented. MSP industry: per-device ($50-100/device/month, 28% of MSPs), per-user ($50-100/user/month), and fixed-fee/value-based pricing most common models.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: V_raw=0.12003 implies 12.0pp EBITDA uplift. A=1.429 (t50=7 years, slow adoption). Uplift of 12pp >> 3pp threshold, so no tension: high potential value but slow adoption speed is internally consistent — a large opportunity that the industry will take many years to realize fully.

terminal_value.justification: The product of NAICS 541513 — human management and operation of client IT estates on client premises — is being replaced by two independent structural forces: (1) cloud migration (decade-long, predates AI, continues shrinking the on-prem addressable base as clients move workloads to hyperscalers whose operations are already automated), and (2) AIOps/self-healing infrastructure (now automating the monitoring, patching, incident-response, and runbook functions that are the core deliverable). AI here is primarily the client's exit ramp rather than the operator's productivity tool, because outsourcing contract structures (productivity glidepaths, benchmarking) pass any operator-side AI efficiency gains to clients at renewal. A residual niche exists — regulated/sovereign/legacy workloads requiring audit-accountable human oversight — but this is a declining fraction of the 2022 base. The dominant revenue segment of the code is MELTING, not merely PRESSURED.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V was computed from OEWS wage-share breakdown × within-role automatable fractions grounded in AIOps capability evidence (monitoring automation, ticket auto-resolution, runbook execution). A was derived from current_adoption_pct=0.22 (Gartner/industry surveys) and historical technology adoption analog (cloud services: ~10yr to 50%). Neither V nor A referenced deal activity, consolidation waves, or M&A trends.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Billing mix: ~50% multi-year fixed-fee/per-device contracts with productivity glidepaths, ~30% T&M, ~20% cost-plus. Productivity glidepaths in outsourcing contracts explicitly pre-commit efficiency savings to clients at renewal cycles (Kyndryl evidence: Advanced Delivery automation savings redeployed/absorbed into contract repricing, not margin retention). T&M contracts pass savings directly via competitive retendering. Only cost-plus retains efficiency gains temporarily before audit resets pricing. This deflation mechanism is reflected in pi_dist=0.30 (client-relationship control substantially deflated by renewal repricing) and pi_moat=0.40 (genuine switching costs and compliance accountability partially offset by structural workload migration to cloud).

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: C = 10×pi_dist×pi_moat: at pi_dist=0.25, C=1.00; at pi_dist=0.40, C=1.60. S varies from ~2.74 (pi_dist=0.25) to ~3.06 (pi_dist=0.40).
CFD changes from 0.0 (1 consolidator) to 0.477 (8 consolidators), B ranges from ~7.08 (n=1) to ~5.73 (n=8). S impact: ~0.14 S units across the range.

evidence.reviewer_note: NAICS 541513 is big-firm skewed (151 firms at 500+ averaging $117M revenue); the $1-10M EBITDA band (n_band=583) consists primarily of 429 firms in the 20-99 headcount class and 154 in the 100-499 class — some of these may be subsidiaries or captives rather than independent acquirable firms, which overstates the true acquirable universe. The OEWS role_mix is at 4-digit 5415 level and does not distinguish 541513 from its siblings; within-role fractions for the 'Software Developers' bucket were adjusted to reflect that this SOC at 541513 actually proxies systems/network administrators and AIOps delivery staff rather than product developers — but this mapping is uncertain (MED quality). The secular cloud-migration headwind predates AI and is the primary demand risk; AI is an accelerant of workload migration (making cloud self-management easier) rather than an independent new force. buy_mult and exit_mult are calibrated from IT services broadly (primarily MSP data); 541513 enterprise ITO transactions are fewer and likely command lower multiples than SMB MSPs due to structural demand erosion. active_consolidators=3 is an estimate; no documented PE roll-up platforms specifically targeting 541513 at the $1-10M EBITDA tier were found in sources accessed.

breakdown_by_role.role: Deployment/installation engineers (15-1252 mapped to dominant sub-segment)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: In 541519 dominant sub-segment (general IT installation/support shops), this SOC proxies deployment and installation engineers rather than product developers. Installation scripting, configuration templates, migration runbooks, and DR plan documentation automate heavily. On-site hardware handling, client-specific recovery orchestration judgment, and physical deployment persist. 45% reflects higher automation potential than 541513 (installation documentation and scripting are more directly automatable than ongoing operations monitoring judgment).

Install support, troubleshooting, and ticket work — directly AI-automatable via chatbots, auto-resolution, and diagnostic tools. Physical/on-site dispatch persists. 60% reflects high automation potential for remote/digital support, offset by on-site slice.

Project management and client relationship management. Rollout coordination and status reporting automate; client escalation and ongoing relationship management persist. 20% reflects the fundamentally relational nature of this role in a project-oriented shop.

Assessments, DR/continuity plans, and audit documentation automate substantially using AI document generation and structured templates. Client governance and sign-off interaction persists. Higher than 541513 because documentation work dominates over ongoing operational analysis.

Owner-held recurring client relationships persist; quoting and proposal generation for project work automates. Slightly higher than 541513 because project quotes are more templatable than long-cycle outsourcing-contract negotiations.
Company management: internal reporting and operations automate marginally; strategic decisions and client relationships do not.

Mixed deployment and installation roles; scripting, configuration automation, and diagnostic tools substantially automate this mixed bucket. 45% reflects installation/deployment focus in this residual code.
Rollout coordination, status reporting, and milestone tracking automate via project management tools with AI. Client escalation and scope-change management persist.

DR/BCP assessments, continuity plan writing, and audit documentation are primary activities here; these are substantially automatable with AI document generation. Client governance interaction persists.
Automated testing and validation tools are mature; substantial portion of QA/testing work is automatable. Edge cases and novel failure modes require human judgment.

breakdown_by_role.role: Unallocated tail (field/bench technicians, admin, installation staff, remainder)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Remainder covers field technicians (physical hardware installation/repair — not automatable), bench technicians, administrative staff (back-office automates), and installation/deployment support staff. 28% reflects that field tech work is largely non-automatable but admin and documentation portions are, and some bench/remote technicians work on automatable tasks.

pi_dist.rationale: Dominant sub-segment (installation/support/project shops) bills primarily on T&M (~40%) and per-incident break-fix (~25%). Under T&M billing, when AI cuts engineer hours, competitive requoting passes savings directly to the client — the invoice reflects actual hours. Break-fix per-incident is inherently deflationary as automation enables faster resolution at the same price. The recurring retainer slice (~20%) provides some margin protection. Client relationships are weaker than in outsourcing contracts (projects are rebid; break-fix is on-demand). pi_dist=0.40 reflects moderate client-relationship control (recurring retainer clients create some continuity) substantially deflated by the project/T&M billing mix.
sources: Breakwater M&A MSP IT Services Valuation Multiples 2026 (https://www.breakwaterma.com/blog/msp-it-services-valuation-multiples-2026): T&M and project-based revenue commands lowest multiple vs. MRR
Kaseya 2024 MSP Benchmark Survey: recurring vs. project revenue dynamics (https://www.kaseya.com/resource/2024-msp-benchmark-survey-report/)

pi_moat.rationale: Weak moat in the dominant sub-segment. Software installation services face the structural threat of zero-touch deployment (MDM, Autopilot, SaaS self-installation) that eliminates the service regardless of AI. DR planning services migrate to platform-native DRaaS features (AWS Backup, Azure Site Recovery, Veeam) that commoditize traditional paid planning engagements. Generic AI further accelerates client self-service: AI-powered deployment tools and runbook generators reduce dependence on IT services firms for installation and configuration. Switching costs are minimal for project-based relationships (clients rebid). Durable niche exists only in compliance-driven DR attestation for regulated industries — this portion is PRESSURED, not MELTING, but is small. pi_moat=0.35 acknowledges minimal switching costs and structural product displacement, offset by residual compliance/audit value.
sources: BetterCloud Zero-Touch Automation Guide 2026 (https://www.bettercloud.com/monitor/guide-zero-touch-automation/): 42% of automation leaders say 51-75% of help desk tickets could be automated
MarketsandMarkets DRaaS Market: DRaaS projected $46B by 2032, displacing paid human DR planning (https://www.marketsandmarkets.com/PressReleases/recovery-as-a-service.asp)

t50_years.derivation: current_adoption_pct=0.18 (18% of 541519-type small IT service firms materially using AI in delivery). This population lags full MSPs (which show ~33% AI adoption intent in Kaseya 2024 benchmark) due to smaller size and lower capital for tooling. Historical analog: cloud/SaaS adoption in small IT shops took ~10 years from ~10% (2012) to ~50% penetration (2022). Remaining gap from 18% to 50% is 32pp vs. prior cycle's ~40pp over 10 years. Proportional: 32/40 × 10 = 8 years from today. t50 = 8 years (2034).

current_adoption_pct.source: Kaseya 2024 MSP Benchmark Survey (proxy for 541519-adjacent IT service firms); Kaseya agentic AI for MSPs 2025 reporting
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: 33% of MSP respondents predicted they'll use AI to streamline tedious IT tasks; 85% say automation is a must-have. 37% said implementing AI/ML will be challenging. Current material adoption (vs. stated intent) estimated at 18% for the 541519 project/installation-heavy population, lagging true MSPs.

historical_analogs.value: Cloud/SaaS adoption in small IT services firms: approximately 10 years from ~10% material penetration (2012) to ~50% of IT shops managing majority of client workloads via cloud-native tools (2022). Zero-touch MDM adoption in SMB IT shops followed a similar 8-10 year cycle.
historical_analogs.source: Zippia Cloud Adoption Statistics 2026; BetterCloud Zero-Touch Automation Report
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: 33% of enterprises were running over 50% of their workloads in the cloud as of 2021, up from near-zero in 2012; BetterCloud: 42% of automation leaders say 51-75% of help desk tickets could be automated, indicating material automation in leading firms.

owners_60plus_pct.source: U.S. Census Bureau ABS 2022 (via aggregated reporting); U.S. Bank Small Business Survey 2025; Oberlo Small Business Statistics 2024
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: U.S. Census ABS 2022: 52.3% of business owners age 55+, 22.8% age 65+. Interpolated 60+ estimate ~35% broadly. NAICS 541519 is dominated by micro-firms (8,483 firms in <5 employee class) with owner-operator structure, pushing the age distribution older than average. Using 38% for this heavily owner-operated code.

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: ABS 2022 and U.S. Bank 2025 survey document that 85% of small business owners say they intend to pass on the business but only 54% have formal succession plans (Gallup). However, no IT-specific or 541519-specific succession shortage study was found. The general small business succession gap is documented but not IT-specific; using false for the machine-readable flag.

active_consolidators.source: CT Acquisitions Private Equity MSP 2026 Guide; Solganick Technology Services M&A updates 2024-2025; Kaseya/ConnectWise ecosystem coverage
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: 7 actively acquiring PE-backed MSP platforms documented (Evergreen Services Group, New Charter Technologies, Ntiva, Integris, Coretelligent, Magna5, Thrive Networks) plus additional platforms. 267 MSP deals in 2025, 72% PE-backed. Firms from 541519 appear in same deal pools as 541512 per Solganick/CT Acquisitions coverage. Using 8 as count of active platforms likely to acquire from this adjacent pool.

buy_mult.source: Breakwater M&A MSP IT Services Valuation Multiples 2026; CT Acquisitions IT Managed Services M&A Multiples Report 2026
buy_mult.figure_quoted: Owner-operated MSP (50-70% MRR): 4x-5.5x EBITDA. Project/break-fix-heavy firms command lower multiples. CT Acquisitions: sub-$5M EBITDA firms at 3.5x-5x. Using 4.5x for 541519 firms reflecting project/resale-heavy revenue mix commanding discount to full-MRR MSPs.

exit_mult.source: Breakwater M&A MSP IT Services Valuation Multiples 2026; Aventis Advisors IT Services Valuation Multiples 2015-2024; CT Acquisitions MSP PE Guide 2026
exit_mult.figure_quoted: Platform-ready MSP with $3M+ EBITDA and scalable NOC/SOC: 7x-10x+. Aventis: $50-100M deal size 11.5x; $20-50M 10.5x. For 541519-type platforms with residual project/resale mix (lower MRR quality), exit multiple conservative at 9.0x reflecting structural headwinds (zero-touch displacement of installation, DRaaS displacing DR planning) and lower MRR quality vs. pure MSPs.

pricing_structure.value: Dominant sub-segment billing: T&M project installation/deployment (~40%), break-fix per-incident (~25%), recurring managed services retainer where present (~20%), hardware/software resale with services bundle (~15%). T&M and break-fix together represent ~65% of revenue, directly passing AI efficiency savings to clients via competitive requoting or faster resolution at the same rate.
pricing_structure.source: Breakwater M&A blog 2026; Kaseya 2024 MSP Benchmark Survey; Channel Dive MSP Pricing Guide 2026
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: Breakwater: 'An MSP with 80% MRR will command a meaningfully higher multiple than one with 40% MRR and 60% project work.' Kaseya benchmark: recurring revenue 82% of total MSP income for mature MSPs (2023). 541519 firms skew toward project/installation-heavy mix vs. mature MSPs. MSP project margins 13% Q4 2024 vs. 46% for recurring managed services.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: V_raw=0.165199 implies 16.52pp EBITDA uplift. A=1.25 (t50=8 years, slow adoption). Uplift of 16.52pp >> 3pp threshold, so no tension: this is consistent with a code that has substantial AI-automatable content but slow adoption pace among small owner-operated shops. The large uplift potential is credible given the high labor share (0.4958) and significant automatable fraction in installation/documentation/support roles.

terminal_value.justification: Per-sub-segment analysis: (1) Installation/deployment services (dominant sub-segment): the service is being structurally displaced by zero-touch deployment (MDM/Autopilot/SaaS self-installation), which requires no AI — the product itself is disappearing as software vendors automate deployment. AI accelerates this trend by enabling AI-powered deployment configuration tools. (2) Disaster recovery planning services: migrating to platform-native DRaaS (AWS Backup, Azure Site Recovery, Veeam), which commoditize traditional paid human planning engagements; the DRaaS market is growing at 16% CAGR precisely because the platform replaces the human planner. (3) Residual compliance-driven DR attestation: this portion is PRESSURED but small relative to the two melting segments. The dominant revenue of the code is in services whose underlying demand is being eliminated by structural technology shifts independent of AI. AI is not the operator's productivity tool here; it is (along with zero-touch tools) the client's bypass mechanism.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V was computed from OEWS wage-share breakdown × within-role automatable fractions grounded in zero-touch deployment evidence (BetterCloud, MDM penetration data), AI service-desk automation benchmarks (Kaseya), and DRaaS platform displacement evidence. A was derived from current_adoption_pct=0.18 (Kaseya MSP benchmark proxy) and historical cloud/SaaS adoption cycle analog. Neither V nor A referenced PE deal activity, MSP consolidation transaction volumes, or M&A trends.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Billing mix: ~40% T&M project installation, ~25% break-fix per-incident, ~20% recurring managed services retainer, ~15% resale+services bundle. T&M billing passes AI efficiency savings directly: when a deployment engineer takes half the time due to AI-assisted scripting, the invoice is half the amount under competitive requoting. Break-fix is inherently deflationary (faster resolution = same or lower per-incident revenue). Only the recurring retainer slice (~20%) retains efficiency gains as margin. This high-deflationary billing mix is reflected in pi_dist=0.40 (moderate client control, heavily deflated by T&M/break-fix billing structure) and pi_moat=0.35 (weak structural moat given product displacement by zero-touch tools and DRaaS platforms).

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: C = 10×0.40×pi_moat: at pi_moat=0.25, C=1.00; at pi_moat=0.45, C=1.80. S varies from ~2.88 (pi_moat=0.25) to ~3.38 (pi_moat=0.45), a range of ~0.50 S units.
V_raw = 0.4958×ars: at ars=0.27, V=5.35; at ars=0.40, V=7.93. S varies from ~2.97 (ars=0.27) to ~3.37 (ars=0.40), a range of ~0.40 S units.

evidence.reviewer_note: NAICS 541519 is a residual catch-all code scored on its dominant sub-segment: general small IT services shops (MSP-adjacent, installation/support/resale-blended). This heterogeneous classification means the scoring may not represent firms in the disaster-recovery-focused or compliance-attestation sub-segments, which likely have higher pi_moat and different terminal value (PRESSURED rather than MELTING). The OEWS role_mix is at 4-digit 5415 level; for 541519, the 'Software Developers' bucket likely proxies installation/deployment engineers, which is consistent with how it was treated in the within-role fraction assignment. The n_band=705 figure (526 firms in 20-99 class + 179 in 100-499 class) likely overstates the truly acquirable-on-MSP-terms subset: PE roll-up buyers screen on MRR quality, and most 541519 firms are project/resale-heavy. The active_consolidators=8 is an estimate reflecting that 541519 firms appear in the same MSP deal pool as 541512, but may not be primary targets. buy_mult=4.5x and exit_mult=9.0x are calibrated from MSP/IT services data broadly; actual 541519 multiples for project-heavy firms are likely at the lower end (4.0x-4.5x buy) with exit multiples constrained by structural headwinds. The 2022 receipts vintage predates both AI service-desk tooling mainstreaming and DRaaS commoditization — the addressable base for paid installation and DR planning services is likely already contracting from the 2022 baseline. Labor_share=0.4958 signals meaningful pass-through (hardware/software resale) in receipts; the true labor-intensive service revenue base is smaller, which may cause V_raw to overstate the actual automatable labor-revenue fraction if resale/pass-through revenue is a large share.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Comp benchmarking runs, survey data analysis, job architecture documentation, and policy drafting are heavily AI-exposed. Client advisory judgment on pay decisions, negotiation support, and change-management facilitation persist. Plausible fraction 0.35-0.50.
Client-executive and board (comp-committee) relationships are trust work that persists. Only internal admin and coordination automates. Partners own client decisions for CHRO advisory engagements.

breakdown_by_role.role: 13-1161 Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Proposal drafting, competitive research, marketing content, and survey-product marketing automate substantially. Relationship selling and campaign judgment persist.
Pitch-deck and proposal production automates; relationship-building, executive referral networks, and contract negotiation do not.
Engagement administration, data collection chasing, status reporting, and scheduling automate. Client-committee coordination and expectation management persist.
Client intake, data collection, status update communications, and routine FAQ responses are highly AI-automatable. Complex issue resolution and sensitive HR situations persist.
Compliance tracking, reporting, and operations documentation automate. Judgment on non-routine compliance situations and vendor management persist.
Survey platform development and analytics tooling are AI-augmented (code generation assists). Architecture decisions, platform integration, and security remain judgment-heavy.
Bookkeeping, Form 5500 data preparation, financial reconciliation, and routine reporting automate. Licensed attestation, complex plan testing, and IRS correspondence persist.

Policy drafting, handbook generation, benefits comparison decks, and job description writing are heavily AI-exposed. Credentialed sign-off on ERISA-governed plans, ADA/FMLA interpretations, and employee relations persist.

breakdown_by_role.role: Unlisted tail (comp analysts 13-1141, actuaries 15-2011, benefits technicians, admin/back-office)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Unlisted tail = 1.0 minus listed top-10 wage share (0.5273) = 0.4727. At 541612, tail includes compensation analysts (benchmarking automatable ~45%), actuaries/pension technicians (valuation runs automate ~30%, credentialed ERISA sign-off persists), and admin/survey-ops (high automation ~55%). Blended fraction 0.40 reflects this weighted mix. Hypothesis label: these occupations are absent from listed 4-digit 5416 top-10 but are the code's distinctive occupations at 6-digit.

pi_dist.rationale: HR consulting has approximately 35% recurring/institutional revenue (annual comp cycle retainers, ERISA compliance, benefits plan renewals — firm controls the relationship via multi-year engagement calendars) and 15% data/actuarial work (most fee-stable, proprietary survey subscription data). The remaining ~50% is project-based (fixed-fee or T&M policy, handbook, generalist advisory work) where AI savings are captured by clients as rescoped fees at renewal via procurement benchmarking. The 55% reflects partial firm control: recurring work gives above-average stability but the project half creates meaningful deflation pressure.
sources: getmorehrclients.com (HR consultant pricing survey 2026): hourly most popular model followed by retainer then fixed fee
paradigmie.com: HR consultant costs explanation — retainers common for ongoing compliance cycles
Forbes/WorldatWork: AI changing benefit plan administration, not ERISA fiduciary duties — confirming regulated fee anchor

pi_moat.rationale: Meaningful moats exist in (1) ERISA/Enrolled Actuary credentials — statutory gate that AI cannot satisfy; credentialed actuary must sign Form 5500 actuarial certifications, (2) proprietary compensation survey panels — data that cannot be AI-generated; Mercer/WTW/Culpepper panels are the benchmarking reference, (3) legal privilege for pay-equity analyses under OFCCP/pay-transparency statutes. However, approximately 55-60% of the code's revenue is in open advisory (policies, handbooks, org design, generalist HR advice) where Workday Rippling already embeds comp benchmarking, policy generation, and analytics — directly substitutable by clients. The unprotected majority substantially dilutes moat strength. Score 0.45 reflects modest-but-real credential/data-asset protection in a code where most revenue sits outside that gate.
sources: Forbes Finance Council (2026): AI changing benefit plan admin, not ERISA fiduciary duties — credentials persist
globenewswire (2026): employee benefits tech market cloud platforms 71.62% share — substitution evidence
Rippling Workforce Advisory launch (2025): platforms moving into HR advisory, direct substitute for generalist advisory

t50_years.derivation: Current_adoption_pct: SHRM State of AI in HR 2026 reports 39% of HR organizations have adopted AI; firm-side adoption for consulting operators estimated at ~35% materially (consulting firms are tech-forward relative to corporate HR but the 'material' bar is higher than light AI use). Historical analog: cloud HRIS/HR-tech platforms (SaaS migration) reached ~50% adoption among professional services firms roughly 2010-2020, approximately 8-10 years from launch. AI adoption is accelerating faster: from ~35% current to 50% requires adding ~15pp share. Given the SHRM data showing 87% of CHROs forecasting AI integration by 2026-2027 and the adoption curve inflecting, the move from 35% to 50% should take approximately 3-5 years. Central estimate: 4.0 years (reaching 50% by mid-2030). Plausible range reflects uncertainty in what 'material adoption' means at the consulting firm operator level vs. client side.

current_adoption_pct.source: SHRM State of AI in HR 2026 Report (secondary: futurefactors.ai summary of SHRM survey n=1,722 HR professionals, surveyed Dec 5-23, 2025)
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: 39% of HR teams have adopted AI; more than half (54%) have not adopted any form of AI in HR. 26% use AI weekly among adopters. 92% of CHROs anticipate AI integration will expand.

historical_analogs.value: 8-10 years
historical_analogs.source: Cloud HRIS/SaaS HR platform adoption in professional services; general SaaS adoption curves for business services (CRM adoption: 65% of companies within 5 years of launch per Zippia 2026; cloud broadly per industry reports)
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: 65% of companies adopt CRM within five years of launching; for HR/HRIS specifically, cloud migration in enterprise was roughly 2010-2020 for 50% adoption milestone.

owners_60plus_pct.source: U.S. Census Bureau Annual Business Survey 2022 (via ctacquisitions.com Boomer Business Succession Wave Report 2024-2030)
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: 52.3% of US business owners are age 55 or older; 29.5% are 55-64; 22.8% are 65 and older (Census ABS 2022). The 60+ cohort is estimated at ~37% interpolating between 55-64 and 65+ bands.

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: EPI State of Owner Readiness: only 20-30% of businesses that go to market actually sell; CEPA-per-Boomer-business ratio approximately 1 CEPA per 360 Boomer-owned employer firms; 78% of business owners lack a formal transition team (ctacquisitions.com Boomer Succession Wave Report 2024-2030, citing EPI data).

active_consolidators.source: Tracxn acquisition data for OneDigital and Alera Group; industry M&A coverage (leadersedge.com, privsource.com)
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: OneDigital has completed 66 acquisitions including Human Capital Concepts (HR consulting/PEO, Feb 2025) and Nonprofit HR Solutions LLC (HR advisory). Alera Group: 126 acquisitions primarily insurance/benefits. NFP acquired by Aon 2024. CBIZ active in professional services benefits consulting.

buy_mult.source: GF Data H1 2025 business services add-on data (via caldergr.com Q2 2025 market update); ctacquisitions.com consulting valuation guide
buy_mult.figure_quoted: Business services add-ons in $1M-$5M tier averaged 5.7x TTM EBITDA in H1 2025. HR consulting boutiques (project-based, no data product) estimated 4.5-5.5x after EBITDA normalization for owner comp. Central estimate 5.0x EBITDA.

exit_mult.source: GF Data 2025 business services platform multiples; Equiteq consulting M&A reports; ctacquisitions.com consulting business valuation guide
exit_mult.figure_quoted: GF Data business services hit 7.8x in 2025. Specialty consulting with recurring revenue 6-8x+. HR consulting $5M+ EBITDA platforms with data assets (comp surveys, ERISA practice) estimated 8.0x normalized EBITDA at exit. Note: first-pagesage.com cited 12.6x for HR consulting at $5-10M but methodology appears to include SDE-based multiples — normalized EBITDA basis is lower.

pricing_structure.value: ~50% project fees (fixed-fee or T&M): HR policy, handbook, org design, generalist advisory; ~30-35% retainers (recurring: annual comp cycle, benefits plan renewals, ERISA compliance calendar); ~10-15% per-participant/per-plan fees (actuarial, Form 5500, pension valuations); ~5-10% data subscriptions (proprietary comp survey product)
pricing_structure.source: getmorehrclients.com HR consultant pricing survey 2026; paradigmie.com HR consulting fee structures; WorldatWork practice data
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: Hourly rate pricing most popular model followed by monthly retainer then fixed price per project. HR consultants charge $96-$189/hr average. Retainer fees based on estimated hours or bundled service packages.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: Uplift_pp = V_raw × 100 = 0.152587 × 100 = 15.26pp. A = 2.5 (below 5). No tension: uplift exceeds 3pp (strongly positive) and A is low, meaning slow adoption dampens the realized uplift timeline rather than contradicting it. Consistent.

terminal_value.justification: HR consulting splits into two structurally distinct segments. Credential-gated core (~35-40% of code revenue): ERISA/Enrolled Actuary sign-offs for Form 5500 and pension valuations, proprietary compensation survey panels (Mercer, WTW, Culpepper data that AI cannot fabricate), and pay-equity analyses under legal privilege. These are DURABLE — statutes name credentialed humans and proprietary data cannot be replicated from public information. Open advisory shell (~60-65% of revenue): HR policies, handbooks, org design, generalist HR advice, benefits plan comparison for mid-market clients — this segment is MELTING as Workday/Rippling/Gusto embed comp benchmarking, policy generation, and analytics directly. AI cuts operator costs in the gated core and replaces the product in the open shell. Net classification PRESSURED: the code survives in aggregate (credential-gated demand is durable) but the larger portion of firm-count revenue is under structural replacement pressure, and many of the 7,641 firms are in the unprotected advisory category.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V was derived from SHRM State of AI in HR 2026 adoption survey and occupation-task analysis of automatable fractions — no M&A deal counts, deal volumes, or consolidation activity referenced. A was derived from SHRM 2026 adoption data and cloud HRIS historical analog (SaaS adoption timeline) — no deal activity referenced. Confirmed no proxy use for V or A.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Billing mix: ~50% project fees (fixed/T&M anchored to estimated hours), ~30-35% retainer (recurring compliance cycles), ~10-15% per-participant/plan fees (actuarial), ~5-10% data subscriptions. The project-fee majority is the deflation channel: at contract renewal, clients with AI tools (or AI-embedded HR platforms) benchmark hours and rescope engagements. This is reflected in pi_dist = 0.55 (partial firm control) rather than a higher value: the recurring/actuarial/data subset gives above-average institutional control but the project majority exposes AI efficiency gains to client fee pressure. pi_moat = 0.45 captures the credential/data moat protecting only the minority of revenue.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: t50=3.0 → A=3.33 → S≈3.93; t50=6.0 → A=1.67 → S≈3.23. Range ≈ ±0.35 around central S=3.64. Moderate impact: A is already the lowest scoring factor.
buy_mult=4.0 (exit=8.0) → M=clamp(4×4/4,0,10)=4.0 → S≈4.14; buy_mult=6.0 (exit=8.0) → M=clamp(4×2/6,0,10)=1.33 → S≈3.19. Significant impact ≈ ±0.5 around central S.
Consolidators=2 → CFD=log10(3)/2=0.2386 → B≈7.17; consolidators=7 → CFD=log10(8)/2=0.4515 → B≈6.60. Impact ≈ ±0.1 on S. Low sensitivity.

evidence.reviewer_note: 1. labor_share=0.429 is low for consulting; per prompt guidance, receipts include pass-through items (survey product costs, insurance-adjacent commissions, subcontracted benefits administration). This suppresses V mechanically; the true labor intensity for pure fee-based advisory boutiques is higher, likely 0.50-0.60. No correction made — dataset inputs used as given. 2. role_mix is 4-digit 5416 (management-consulting dominated); compensation analysts (13-1141) and actuaries (15-2011) — the code's distinctive high-value occupations — are absent from listed top-10. They appear in the unlisted tail (0.4727 wage share), assigned blended fraction 0.40. This is a hypothesis clearly labeled. 3. active_consolidators set at 4 after applying boundary-confusion filter per prompt guidance: OneDigital and Alera Group make many acquisitions but their center of gravity is insurance brokerage (524210); only OneDigital clearly acquires fee-based HR consulting books routinely. Brokerage roll-up multiples (recurring commission economics) are NOT imported — M evidence uses pure consulting multiples. 4. The unlisted tail (0.4727 wage share) dominates ai_replaceable_share. If true fraction for the tail is 0.33 (actuarial-heavy) rather than 0.40, ai_replaceable_share drops to ~0.32 and S drops to ~3.4. If 0.48 (admin-heavy), S rises to ~3.9. 5. firstpagesage.com cited HR consulting multiples of 8.8x-12.6x for small-to-mid firms; these appear to use SDE or revenue multiples — normalized EBITDA basis used here (5.0x buy, 8.0x exit) is materially more conservative and consistent with GF Data business services data.
