breakdown_by_role.rationale: Market research synthesis, competitive analysis, positioning decks, campaign performance reporting, and content strategy drafts are heavily AI-exposed. CMO Survey (2026) documents AI now powers 24.2% of all marketing activities — core analyst tasks. Client C-suite persuasion, organizational change judgment, and facilitation of complex stakeholder workshops persist.

breakdown_by_role.role: 11-1021 General and Operations Managers (owner-operators and managing principals)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: In 49,047-firm industry where 41,556 firms have <5 employees, this layer is substantially owner-operators. Client relationships, selling, and business development persist — these are the principal asset. Internal reporting, scheduling, and coordination automate. Fraction slightly higher than 541612 principals because small boutique owners do more admin relative to client advisory.

breakdown_by_role.role: 13-1161 Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists
breakdown_by_role.rationale: This is the code's core billable role. Market research synthesis, persona development, competitive analysis, and content strategy are among the most AI-substitutable professional tasks. HubSpot 2025: 35% of marketers use AI primarily for content creation; 30% for data analysis and insights. Judgment on what strategy a client organization will actually execute persists.

Proposal and pitch deck production automates substantially with AI. Relationship selling, referral network management, and closing complex engagements do not. Marketing services are relationship-purchased — the rep's network is the product.

Status reporting, deliverable tracking, scheduling, brief documentation, and campaign timeline management automate substantially. Client expectation management, resolving scope disagreements, and navigating internal client politics persist.
Client intake, brief collection, status communications, and routine Q&A are highly automatable. Sensitive relationship-management situations persist.
Marketing operations documentation, analytics reporting, process optimization analysis, and workflow design have substantial AI exposure. Judgment on complex cross-functional implementation persists.

Martech implementation, analytics platform development, and campaign tracking code are AI-augmented (code generation). Architecture decisions, complex integrations, and data pipeline design remain judgment-heavy.
Bookkeeping, invoicing, media spend reconciliation, financial reporting, and billing management are highly automatable. Licensed attestation and complex financial judgment persist.
Policy documents, job descriptions, onboarding materials, and compliance documentation automate. Employee relations judgment persists.

breakdown_by_role.role: Unlisted tail (writers, designers, brand specialists, digital/content consultants, data analysts, creative directors)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Unlisted tail = 1.0 minus listed top-10 wage share (0.5273) = 0.4727. For 541613, this tail is dominated by content creators, copywriters, designers, brand consultants, and digital specialists — occupations absent from 4-digit 5416 OEWS but central to 541613. CMO Survey (Jan 2026): AI now powers 24.2% of all marketing activities; marketers project 55.9% in 3 years. HubSpot 2025: 66% of marketers use AI; content creation (35%) and data analysis (30%) are top uses. The creative/content/digital tail has the highest AI exposure in professional services — generative AI creates copy, designs concepts, produces market research syntheses at consultant quality. Fraction 0.55 represents: creative/content work ~65% automatable, data analysis ~50% automatable, brand strategy judgment ~25% automatable, weighted by sub-segment share. Hypothesis clearly labeled: tail composition estimated for this 6-digit code.

pi_dist.rationale: Marketing consulting billing is dominated (~60%) by project fees anchored to estimated hours/day rates — the most direct AI-deflation channel. At contract renewal, client procurement teams benchmark fees against reduced delivery hours as AI tools collapse research and content production time. Forrester (2025) documents holding company revenues fell 1.2% while ad spend grew 8.6% — confirmed fee deflation already occurring at scale. Retainers (~30%) provide some insulation but are renegotiated annually and benchmarked to effort. Value-based/performance fees (~10%) are the only true AI-insulated model. No credential or statutory anchor exists to resist client-side fee pressure. Score 0.35 reflects weak firm pricing control: most efficiency gains flow to clients as lower fees or reduced engagement scopes.
sources: Forrester (via Forbes 2025): marketing agencies in 2026 — holding company revenues fell 1.2% as ad spend grew 8.6%
ctacquisitions.com marketing agency exit guide: fees anchored to day rates/estimated hours, renegotiated at renewal
agencies.co marketing agency valuation 2025: retainer vs project billing dynamics

pi_moat.rationale: Marketing consulting has near-zero statutory protection: no license, no credential, no physical act required. Active client bypass is documented: Forrester forecasts 15% reduction in agency jobs in 2026; CMOs are building in-house AI capabilities; marketing teams deploying AI report 300% ROI. In 41,556 firms with <5 employees (85% of firm count), client relationships are fully portable with the individual consultant — zero institutional switching cost. The only surviving moats are: (1) niche vertical specialization with deep relationships (rare in the majority of boutiques), (2) martech stack ownership creating mild switching costs for implementation clients, and (3) executional integration (media buying where the firm controls placement relationships). These apply to a minority of the code. Score 0.30 reflects the weakest moat position in professional consulting — product substitutability is high, bypass is active, and no credential defends the revenue.
sources: ritnerdigital.com (2026): The Agency Model Is Breaking — clients replacing agencies with AI internal teams
enrichlabs.ai (2026): How to Replace Your Marketing Agency with AI — practitioner-targeted guide signaling market shift
Forbes/Forrester (Oct 2025): Marketing Agencies in 2026 — 15% agency job reduction forecast, agency model under structural pressure

t50_years.derivation: Current adoption: HubSpot 2025 State of Marketing reports 66% of marketers already use AI in their role (74% in US). Marketing consulting practitioners are among the fastest AI adopters in any professional services sector. The 50% firm-level material-adoption threshold has already been crossed based on practitioner data. However, 'material adoption in the automatable functions' at the firm-operator level (i.e., firms systematically deploying AI to execute research, content, and analytics deliverables at production scale) is a higher bar than individual tool use. CMO Survey (Jan 2026) shows AI powers 24.2% of marketing activities, projected to reach 55.9% in 3 years — the 50% milestone is ~2-3 years out on the activity-share curve. Using 2.0 years as central estimate (by mid-2028), reflecting that marketing is the fastest-adopting professional services segment, current practitioner adoption already exceeds 65%, and the gap between individual AI use and firm-level systematic deployment is closing rapidly.

current_adoption_pct.source: HubSpot 2025 State of Marketing (n=1,500+ marketers worldwide, vendor survey), corroborated by CMO Survey Duke Fuqua Jan 2026 (n=308 marketing leaders)
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: 66% of marketers already use AI in their role; US adoption at 74%. CMO Survey Jan 2026: AI and ML now powers 24.2% of all marketing activities, nearly doubling from 13.1% in 2024.

historical_analogs.value: 5-8 years
historical_analogs.source: CRM/marketing automation adoption curves; HBR analysis of technology adoption pace acceleration (2013 updated with recent data); CMO Survey longitudinal data on technology adoption in marketing
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: Marketing automation (HubSpot, Pardot, Marketo) went from niche to ~50% mid-market adoption in 7-8 years (approx. 2010-2018). Digital marketing tools generally reached 50% in 5-8 years. Technology adoption pace is speeding up with each wave.

owners_60plus_pct.source: U.S. Census Bureau Annual Business Survey 2022 (via ctacquisitions.com Boomer Business Succession Wave Report 2024-2030)
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: 52.3% of US business owners are age 55 or older; 22.8% aged 65 and above (Census ABS 2022). The 60+ cohort estimated at ~37% interpolating between 55-64 (29.5%) and 65+ (22.8%) bands.

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: EPI State of Owner Readiness: only 20-30% of businesses that go to market actually sell; CEPA-per-Boomer-business ratio approximately 1 CEPA per 360 Boomer-owned employer firms; 78% of business owners lack a formal transition team. Marketing agency succession is further complicated by key-person risk (client books portable with founder) noted explicitly by agencies.co and gomerge.com exit planning guides.

active_consolidators.source: Industry M&A coverage: mergersandacquisitions.net, investorideas.com, privsource.com; Accenture Song deal count FY2024-2025
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: PE buyers accounted for 42.4% of deal volume in 1H 2025 (highest in 5 years). Active PE-backed roll-up platforms: Stagwell, Mountaingate Capital, Truelink Capital, New Engen, Power Digital Marketing, Bounteous, Dept, Real Chemistry — estimated 8 platforms actively doing marketing consulting add-ons in the last 24 months. Accenture Song: 46 acquisitions FY2024 but primarily digital agency/IT services overlap.

buy_mult.source: ctacquisitions.com marketing agency business valuation guide (2026); capitala.co marketing agency valuations and EBITDA multiples
buy_mult.figure_quoted: Project-only generalist marketing agencies: 2.0-4.0x EBITDA. Mixed retainer/project: 4.0-6.0x. For marketing consulting boutiques (predominantly project-based, <$5M EBITDA, after EBITDA normalization from SDE): central estimate 4.0x normalized EBITDA.

exit_mult.source: ctacquisitions.com marketing agency valuation guide; capitala.co EBITDA multiples for $5M+ agencies; strategic acquirer disclosed transaction data (Stagwell, Accenture Song)
exit_mult.figure_quoted: Marketing agency $5-10M EBITDA range: 8.1-10.7x depending on specialization. Holding-company tier: 8.0-12.0x. Agencies with 3 years double-digit growth, low concentration, extended client engagements: 8x-12x. Central estimate for scaled marketing consulting platform ($10M+ EBITDA, retainer-heavy, vertical specialty): 9.0x EBITDA.

pricing_structure.value: ~60% project fees (fixed-fee or T&M: strategy decks, research reports, campaign planning, content production, competitive analyses — effort-anchored and renegotiated at renewal); ~30% retainers (ongoing brand/content/strategy advisory, monthly advisory relationships); ~10% value-based or performance-tied fees (conversion-linked, ROI-measured engagements)
pricing_structure.source: nmsconsulting.com consulting fees and pricing 2026; ctacquisitions.com marketing agency exit guide; agencies.co marketing agency valuation
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: Project-based and retainer are the two dominant billing models in consulting; marketing consulting is majority project-based given the deliverable-centric nature of campaigns and strategy assignments. Value-based pricing remains a small minority.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: Uplift_pp = V_raw × 100 = 0.208756 × 100 = 20.88pp. A = 5.0 (≥ 5). Check required: uplift 20.88pp >> 3pp threshold — strongly positive, no tension. The large uplift (20.88pp) is consistent with high A (5.0): AI creates very large value (V=8.35) and is being adopted quickly (A=5.0). The C score (1.05) is the binding constraint — clients capture most of the value through fee deflation rather than operators retaining it as margin. Consistent.

terminal_value.justification: Marketing consulting has no license, statute, or physical act protecting demand. The only mechanism is CMO accountability and the value of an outside perspective. Evidence that the product itself is being replaced: (1) Forrester documents holding company revenues fell 1.2% in 2025 even as worldwide ad spend grew 8.6% — direct evidence of engagement volume decline, not just fee compression. (2) Forrester forecasts 15% reduction in agency jobs in 2026. (3) Over 10,000 US marketing jobs eliminated in first 7 months of 2025 due to AI automation. (4) CMO Survey projects AI to power 55.9% of all marketing activities within 3 years (from 24.2% today). (5) The typical 541613 firm produces research, content strategy, competitive analysis, and positioning decks — all of which client-side AI tools (integrated into Salesforce, HubSpot, Adobe) now produce at consultant quality. For the 41,556 firms with <5 employees (85% of firm count) doing generalist strategy and content work, AI is replacing the product. The only surviving demand is niche vertical expertise, facilitated multi-stakeholder workshops, and board-level brand strategy — a fraction of the code. Classification: MELTING. AI is replacing the operator's product, not merely cutting the operator's costs.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V was derived from HubSpot 2025 State of Marketing practitioner adoption survey and CMO Survey Duke Fuqua Jan 2026 activity data — no M&A deal counts, consolidation activity, or PE transaction data referenced. A was derived from CMO Survey AI activity projection (24.2% today → 55.9% in 3 years) and historical marketing automation adoption analog — no deal activity referenced. Confirmed no proxy use for V or A.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Billing mix: ~60% project fees (T&M/fixed, effort-anchored), ~30% retainers (effort-estimated), ~10% value-based. Project fees are the primary deflation channel: as AI collapses research and content delivery hours (Forrester confirms 300% ROI from AI in marketing, meaning 75% cost reduction), procurement teams renegotiate at renewal. The Forrester evidence of agency revenue declining while ad spend grew confirms fee deflation is occurring industry-wide. Retainers renegotiated annually based on perceived value, which declines when clients can use ChatGPT to do the same work. Reflected in pi_dist = 0.35 (strong client bargaining power, no credential anchor, direct substitutability) and pi_moat = 0.30 (active client bypass, no statutory protection, key-person portability). Together C = 10 × 0.35 × 0.30 = 1.05, the lowest capture score of any consulting sub-sector — appropriately reflecting the maximum deflation exposure.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: t50=1.5 → A=6.67 → S≈4.71; t50=3.5 → A=2.86 → S≈3.81. Range ≈ ±0.45 around central S=4.39. Significant sensitivity given A is the largest positive factor. Note: A cannot exceed 10; capped.
pi_dist=0.25 → C=0.75 → S≈4.10; pi_dist=0.45 → C=1.35 → S≈4.65. Range ≈ ±0.28 around central S. Moderate sensitivity. pi_dist is the key judgment call in the face of strong evidence of deflation.
pi_moat=0.20 → C=0.70 → S≈3.97; pi_moat=0.40 → C=1.40 → S≈4.68. Range ≈ ±0.36. Jointly sensitive with pi_dist through C; the MELTING terminal class and evidence of active bypass support the low end.

evidence.reviewer_note: 1. role_mix is 4-digit 5416 proxy (management consulting dominated). For 541613, the true occupational mix skews heavily toward marketing specialists, content creators, designers, and digital specialists — none of which appear in the listed top-10. These constitute the majority of the unlisted tail (0.4727 wage share), which drives most of the ai_replaceable_share (0.260/0.458 = 57% of the total). This is the key uncertainty: if the tail is more brand-strategist heavy (lower fraction ~0.45) vs. content/copywriter heavy (fraction ~0.65), ai_replaceable_share shifts substantially. Estimate labeled as hypothesis. 2. V_raw arithmetic note: stored score uses 0.4559 × 0.457831 = 0.208756 (not 0.208773 as approximated in derivation — slight difference in final digit of ai_replaceable_share sum). The stored value is the exact sum of contributions. 3. The MELTING terminal classification combined with low C (1.05) means this code's S=4.39 is substantially driven by V (8.35), A (5.0), B (7.40), and M (5.0) — four strong dimensions pulled down by a very weak C. A reviewer should confirm whether the MELTING classification is appropriate or whether some demand preservation in vertical niches warrants PRESSURED; the boundary here is close. If PRESSURED, terminal cap would be higher. However, the Forrester evidence of actual revenue declines alongside spend growth supports MELTING. 4. buy_mult = 4.0x is conservative for project-based generalists but may overstate for firms with 60%+ project revenue that look more like freelancer-collectives than institutionalized consulting firms. Some 541613 firms may sell at 2-3x SDE (=1.5-2.5x EBITDA normalized). If buy_mult = 3.0x: M = clamp(4×6/3, 0, 10) = clamp(8.0, 0, 10) = 8.0 → S would rise to ~5.0. The multiple spread is a significant uncertainty. 5. active_consolidators = 8 is an estimate based on named PE platforms; many straddle NAICS 541810 (advertising agencies) and 541613 and the boundary is porous. Coded LOW quality accordingly.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Network modeling, freight-rate analysis, inventory optimization studies, report drafting are heavily AI-exposed (current AI can draft supply chain assessments, run optimization models, analyze freight data). Client interviews, on-site observation, and change management persist. Fraction 0.40 reflects that roughly 40% of management analyst labor in logistics consulting is in AI-substitutable analytical and documentation tasks.

Client relationship management with VPs of supply chain, firm leadership, and business development persist. Internal coordination, reporting, and scheduling automate partially. At 0.15 only a small sliver of this high-judgment/relationship role is AI-substitutable in current AI.

breakdown_by_role.role: Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists (13-1161)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Benchmarking studies, competitive analysis, market sizing reports are heavily AI-exposed. Client presentation and insights synthesis persist. The 5416 proxy overstates marketing vs. logistics-specific analysts; treating as benchmarking/market-research roles at 0.50 automatable.

Proposal and RFP production automates (AI drafts proposals well). Relationship selling to operations executives, pipeline management, and in-person business development do not. 0.20 reflects only the documentation/drafting component.

Status reporting, schedule tracking, document management, meeting summaries automate. On-site coordination of WMS/TMS deployments, warehouse redesign projects, and stakeholder management persist. 0.30 reflects the documentation and reporting slice.

In a logistics consulting context these roles handle client inquiry routing, data entry, and administrative support — largely automatable by current AI. Some client-facing coordination persists. 0.60 reflects high clerical/admin content.

Catch-all role including operations analysts, process documenters, and business analysts — analysis and documentation are AI-exposed; judgment-intensive work persists. 0.40 is mid-range for this heterogeneous SOC.

Larger logistics consulting firms embed developers for WMS/TMS configuration and integration. AI copilot tools augment code production (est. 30% of coding tasks). Architecture, integration design, and client-specific customization persist. 0.30 reflects AI augmentation of routine coding.

Financial modeling, reconciliation, cost-basis reporting, and invoice auditing are AI-exposed. Judgment on unusual items and client advisory persist. 0.50 reflects that approximately half of accounting work in a consulting firm is routine/automatable.
Compliance reporting, benefits administration, and HR documentation automate. Employee relations and judgment-based HR work persists. 0.30 reflects documentation slice.

breakdown_by_role.role: Remaining long tail (logisticians 13-1081, industrial engineers 17-2112, field/implementation staff, other)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: The tail (47.27% of wage bill) for 541614 contains logisticians, industrial engineers, and field/implementation staff proportionally higher than the 4-digit 5416 proxy suggests. Physical facility layout, time studies, on-site observation, and shop-floor implementation persist. Analysis, documentation, and report generation automate. 0.30 reflects that a significant physical-presence component limits automation but analytical tasks are exposed.

pi_dist.rationale: About 55-60% of 541614 revenue is billed T&M or fixed-price project (both deflation-prone: T&M rescopes as hours fall, fixed-price forces operators to compete on AI-enabled lower bids). ~15% is gainshare/contingency (freight audit, cost-recovery) where the firm captures AI productivity. ~25-30% is retainer/recurring advisory. Client relationships in small logistics consulting firms are partly principal-portable. Framework agreements with shippers and 3PLs create some firm-level stickiness. Weighted pi_dist of 0.40 reflects majority deflation-prone billing offset by a gainshare slice and some firm-level relationship anchors.
sources: Freight audit contingency billing: ssui.com/contingency-parcel-freight-invoice-audits/
Consulting billing model survey: Thomson Reuters 2024, 48% value-based; Consultancy.org general benchmarks
MHI/Deloitte supply chain technology report 2025: framework on T&M vs gainshare in supply chain consulting

pi_moat.rationale: No licensure barriers protect this market. Switching costs are low for T&M engagements. Few small 541614 firms hold proprietary benchmark databases that create durable moat. AI bypass risk is significant: supply chain software platforms (Blue Yonder, o9, Kinaxis, SAP IBP) increasingly embed network design, rate benchmarking, and forecasting AI natively — eroding the advisory product that most firms provide. Physical implementation components (WMS/TMS deployment, warehouse redesign) create a modest moat that survives AI, but not all firms in 541614 do heavy implementation. Estimate 0.35, reflecting the implementation anchor partially offsetting advisory bypass risk.
sources: PwC 2026 Digital Trends in Operations (supply chain software platform landscape)
BCG 2026: AI is already moving the logistics industry forward — documents client-side AI adoption in logistics
MHI 2025 Annual Industry Report: AI in network optimization and inventory management embedded in platforms

t50_years.derivation: Current adoption baseline: MHI/Deloitte 2025 Annual Industry Report states 28% of supply chain leaders currently use AI (surveyed 2025). PwC 2026 Digital Trends: 57% of operations/supply chain leaders have integrated AI into selected functions. Conservative figure: ~30% of logistics consulting firms materially using AI (material = AI tools deployed in analytical delivery functions). To reach 50% adoption: need 20pp more. MHI data shows 54% of remaining non-adopters plan adoption within 5 years from 2025, implying ~82% total by 2030. The 50% threshold would be crossed around 2028-2029, roughly 2-3 years from the 2025 baseline (i.e., 3-4 years from July 2026). Using t50 = 4.0 years (July 2030) to account for consulting firm lag behind the operator adoption curve documented in the surveys.

current_adoption_pct.source: MHI Annual Industry Report 2025 (MHI and Deloitte)
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: 28% of supply chain leaders report using AI today; 82% expect to use AI in five years

historical_analogs.value: 8-10 years to 50% adoption (cloud ERP/SaaS in supply chain consulting)
historical_analogs.source: Multiple sources: First Analysis State of AI in Supply Chains Jul 2026; MHI/Deloitte 2025 Annual Industry Report historical commentary
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: New technology adoption in supply chains has tended to lag other sectors, as illustrated by the relatively late adoption of cloud systems; a 'turbo lag' of multi-year delay has been seen with the internet, the cloud, and now AI

owners_60plus_pct.source: Gallup/Pathways to Wealth Survey 2025; Oberlo Small Business Statistics 2024
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: Boomers generation (ages 60-78 as of 2025) make up 37% of small business owners; 52.3% of employer-businesses owned by people 55 or older

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: Gallup 2025: 27% of employer firms with 55+ owners are unsure of long-term plan or intend to close permanently. CT Acquisitions Boomer Succession Wave Report 2024-2030: by 2035 ~6M SMBs will be on market as owners retire. Small business close rate only 6.3-6.5% indicating buyer shortage.

active_consolidators.source: Lincoln International Transportation & Logistics M&A 2025; PEmarketplace deal announcements; web search for PE-backed logistics consulting add-ons 2024-2025
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: PE firms have coupled Transportation & Logistics industry and software investing expertise; sponsors prioritized platform reinforcement pursuing bolt-ons improving density, capability or efficiency. Accenture acquired Partners in Performance and Camelot (supply chain); Grant Thornton acquired Auxis (operations/supply chain). Direct PE platforms adding pure logistics consulting firms (541614 vs 541614+3PL/freight) are few — estimated 3-5 identifiable platforms.

buy_mult.source: First Page Sage Consulting Firm EBITDA Valuation Multiples 2025; GF Data business services 2025; BizBuySell Insight Report 2025
buy_mult.figure_quoted: Management consulting firms with $1-3M EBITDA: ~9.9x (First Page Sage, but applies to well-institutionalized firms). GF Data: business services median 7.4x in 2025. BizBuySell: average SDE multiple 2.61x across all small businesses (2025). For micro logistics consulting firms ($1-3M EBITDA, majority owner-operated), normalizing SDE to EBITDA and applying owner-dependence discount yields 5-6x EBITDA. Using 5.5x as midpoint.

exit_mult.source: GF Data Middle Market M&A Valuations 2025; First Page Sage Consulting Multiples; Equiteq Consulting M&A H1 2024 commentary
exit_mult.figure_quoted: GF Data shows average PE-sponsored middle market deal at 7.2x-7.5x stable since mid-2024. Business services multiples 7.4x in 2025 at GF Data. For $10M+ EBITDA logistics consulting platforms with institutionalized relationships and technology enablement, premium to GF Data median: 8-9x. Using 8.5x.

pricing_structure.value: Mixed: ~55-60% T&M and fixed-price project (deflation-prone); ~15% gainshare/contingency (freight audit, cost-recovery, share-of-savings); ~25-30% retainer/recurring advisory
pricing_structure.source: SSI Parcel/Freight Audit contingency billing model; Consultancy.org general consulting pricing benchmarks; Rocketlane consulting pricing models 2024
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: Contingency-based billing is standard in freight audit: providers receive payment as a percentage of recoverable refunds/savings found. T&M and fixed-fee are standard for advisory and implementation. Thomson Reuters 2024: 48% of professional service firms use value-based billing; significant minority still on T&M.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: uplift_pp = 19.55pp (percentage of revenue freed by AI in automatable labor). A = 2.5 (< 5). No tension: the cross-check tension flag triggers only when uplift < 3pp AND A ≥ 5. Here uplift is well above 3pp and A is below 5, so fully consistent.

terminal_value.justification: Logistics consulting demand is PRESSURED, not MELTING. The advisory/analysis slice (network design, rate benchmarking, demand forecasting) is being replaced by AI embedded in supply chain platforms (Blue Yonder, o9, Kinaxis, SAP IBP) — for that slice the product itself is threatened. However, the physical implementation slice (WMS/TMS deployment, warehouse redesign, change management on the floor) requires on-site human presence and cannot be replaced by AI. 541614 firms that anchor revenue in implementation services retain a durable demand floor. Because revenue mix varies across firms and the code includes both analysis-heavy and implementation-heavy firms, PRESSURED is the correct classification: aggregate demand shrinks but persists through the implementation component.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V confirmed without proxy: ai_replaceable_share derived from role-by-role automation fractions based on task characteristics and AI capability assessment, not from M&A deal activity. A confirmed without proxy: t50 derived from MHI 2025 survey (28% current adoption) and adoption trajectory analysis — no reference to deal counts, consolidation trends, or PE activity.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Billing mix: ~55-60% T&M/fixed-price project, ~15% gainshare/contingency, ~25-30% retainer. T&M rescoping is the primary deflation mechanism: as AI reduces hours, clients negotiate lower fees at project renewal — AI efficiency flows to clients, not the operator. Fixed-price further pressures margins as AI-enabled competition bids more aggressively. Only the gainshare/contingency slice (freight audit) lets the firm capture AI productivity. This mix is reflected in pi_dist = 0.40 (below midpoint, biased toward client capture) and pi_moat = 0.35 (low barriers, active bypass threat from embedded supply chain AI).

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: At t50=3 years: A=3.33, S rises to ~3.58. At t50=6 years: A=1.67, S falls to ~3.10. t50 range drives A from 1.67 to 3.33 and S from ~3.1 to ~3.6 — a meaningful swing but the overall composite remains in the same band.

At pi_moat=0.25: C=1.0, S falls to ~3.18. At pi_moat=0.45: C=1.8, S rises to ~3.52. The bypass risk from embedded supply chain AI platforms is the key uncertainty — if implementation anchors prove durable, moat is higher; if platform AI accelerates, moat collapses.

At buy=4.0 with exit=8.5: M=4.5 (capped effect), S rises to ~3.65. At buy=7.0 with exit=8.5: M=0.857, S falls to ~2.89. The SDE-to-EBITDA conversion for micro firms is the key uncertainty — few arms-length EBITDA-normalized transactions for sub-$3M EBITDA logistics consulting firms are publicly documented.

evidence.reviewer_note: 541614 is not a catch-all code (heterogeneous=false). Role_mix uses 4-digit 5416 proxy — 541614 should carry more logisticians (13-1081) and industrial engineers (17-2112) and fewer market research analysts than the 5416 average, which affects ai_replaceable_share; this uncertainty is absorbed into the long-tail fraction (47.27% of wage bill) at 0.30 automatable. The class 500+ firms (151 firms, avg revenue $55.7M) are IN the n_band at implied EBITDA $8.7M, which is unusual — these are large headcount/lower-margin firms, consistent with implementation-heavy logistics consulting rather than pure advisory. Active_consolidators estimate of 4 is uncertain: PE deals in 541614 proper (pure logistics/process consulting) are hard to isolate from 3PL and freight deals; direct evidence of roll-up platforms targeting $1-10M EBITDA logistics consulting firms specifically is limited. Pricing-structure quality marked MED but the T&M vs. gainshare split estimate is ESTIMATE-quality — no 541614-specific survey found. buy_mult and exit_mult quality marked LOW: no comparable transactions specifically for pure 541614 firms found; using general consulting comps with owner-dependence adjustment.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: For the dominant TEM sub-segment, management analysts primarily conduct invoice review, tariff benchmarking, billing error identification, and carrier rate analysis — all heavily AI-exposed. AI can now scan telecom/utility invoices in seconds vs. 18+ minutes manually (Socium 2026 data: AI-driven TEM increased invoice accuracy by 29%). Client negotiation with carriers and strategic recommendation persist as non-automatable. 0.55 reflects that audit/analysis tasks constitute the majority of this role in TEM firms.

Client relationship management with CFOs and IT directors, firm leadership, and business development persist. Internal coordination and reporting automate partially. For 90% owner-operator firms in 541618, the manager IS the client relationship — very low automation fraction. 0.15 captures only the internal reporting sliver.

breakdown_by_role.role: Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists (13-1161)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: For TEM/expense management firms, these roles conduct tariff benchmarking, market rate comparisons, vendor analysis, and pricing studies — all highly AI-automatable with current tools. Client presentation work persists. 0.55 reflects majority-analytical role content in TEM context.
RFP and proposal production automates. Relationship selling to enterprise procurement and IT leadership does not. 0.20 reflects only the documentation component.

Ongoing expense management program tracking, reporting, and audit scheduling automate. Client coordination on implementations and exception escalations persist. 0.35 reflects the reporting and tracking slice.

In TEM/expense audit context, CSR-coded roles handle client inquiry routing, data entry, and invoice processing — largely clerical and highly automatable. AI processes invoices and resolves routine errors without human touchpoint. 0.65 reflects high routine-task content.

Catch-all role with heavy analytical content in TEM firms (operations analysts reviewing spend data, process documenters, business analysts). Data analysis and documentation automate substantially with current AI. Judgment on carrier contract interpretation persists. 0.50 reflects majority-analytical role.

TEM firms operate internal tooling for invoice processing and reporting. AI copilots augment code production. Architecture and integration work persist. 0.30 reflects AI augmentation of routine development tasks.

Invoice reconciliation, spend reporting, and financial audit of vendor bills are core to TEM delivery — highly automatable with AI (error detection, reconciliation). Judgment on disputes and contract interpretation persist. 0.55 reflects majority-routine content for this role in expense management.
Compliance documentation and HR administration automate. Employee relations judgment persists. 0.30 standard for HR in small professional services firms.

breakdown_by_role.role: Remaining long tail (TEM audit staff, utility expense analysts, site-selection analysts, other)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: For 541618, the tail includes telecom/utility expense auditors, site selection analysts, and other specialty niche consultants. Higher automatable fraction than logistics consulting tail (less physical presence required). Invoice auditing and benchmarking analysis are highly AI-exposed; negotiation and site assessment persist. 0.35 reflects majority analytical/clerical work with some judgment-dependent tasks.

pi_dist.rationale: Dominant TEM sub-segment skews capture-friendly. Contingency/gainshare pricing is prevalent in TEM (firm keeps a share of savings identified — AI reducing audit labor costs increases margin under this model). Estimates from industry sources suggest 40-50% of TEM revenue is contingency-priced. Multi-year recurring audit contracts give TEM firms firm-level client relationships (less principal-portable than pure advisory). For the remaining ~50-60% on subscription or T&M billing, some deflation risk exists. Blended pi_dist = 0.55, above midpoint because the dominant TEM sub-segment's contingency model is capture-friendly and contracts are recurring. Plausible range 0.40-0.65.
sources: SSI contingency invoice audits: ssui.com/contingency-parcel-freight-invoice-audits/
Socium AI in TEM 2026: sociumit.com/resources/blog/ai-telecom-expense-management-2026
Tellennium TEM guide: tellennium.com/your-guide-to-telecom-expense-management/

pi_moat.rationale: The key bypass risk: AI embedded in enterprise TEM platforms (Calero, Tangoe, Brightfin, Sage) allows large enterprises to manage telecom/utility invoices without a consulting intermediary. The TEM consulting product IS the intelligent processing of invoices and tariffs to find errors — when AI does this at the client's ERP, the product is replaced. Small 541618 firms (90% <5 employees) have minimal proprietary data moats. Moat components: (1) proprietary tariff/rate benchmark databases — real but being productized into SaaS platforms; (2) carrier relationship expertise and negotiation leverage — not AI-substitutable but a narrow slice; (3) switching costs from multi-year recurring contracts — provides retention but not barrier to initial bypass. pi_moat = 0.30 reflects weak-to-moderate barriers with significant bypass risk.
sources: Calero/MDSL/Mobi TEM consolidation 2024-2025: bcmone.com/blog/top-telecom-expense-management-companies/
Socium TEM guide 2026: sociumit.com/telecom-expense-management
Tangoe AI module launch 2024 (29% invoice accuracy increase): sociumit.com/resources/blog/ai-telecom-expense-management-2026

t50_years.derivation: Current adoption baseline: Federal Reserve FEDS Notes (April 2026) using Census BTOS data: professional, scientific, and technical services (NAICS 54) AI adoption ~33% as of late 2025. For 541618 specifically (TEM-dominant), AI adoption is likely ahead of the NAICS 54 average — AI in invoice processing launched commercially 2023-2024 (Tangoe AI module 2024, 15 major TEM vendors launched AI platforms 2023-2025 per market reports). Estimate current adoption among 541618 firms at ~35%. To reach 50%: need ~15pp more. BTOS shows NAICS 54 sector is the fastest-adopting non-tech sector; growth pace of ~5-8pp/year suggests 50% threshold crossed in 2-3 years. Using t50 = 3.5 years (January 2030) to reflect that small micro-firm majority in 541618 (90% <5 employees) lags larger firm adoption.

current_adoption_pct.source: Federal Reserve FEDS Notes — Monitoring AI Adoption in the US Economy (April 2026), citing Census BTOS data
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: Professional, scientific, and technical services stand out in terms of levels of adoption, at about 33 percent as of late 2025

historical_analogs.value: 7-10 years to 50% adoption (cloud/SaaS tools in professional services); TEM specifically saw rapid AI platform adoption 2023-2025 suggesting faster than average technology adoption cycle for this sub-segment
historical_analogs.source: Federal Reserve FEDS Notes April 2026; Socium TEM market growth blog 2026; MHI/Deloitte 2025 Annual Industry Report on technology adoption lags
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: Adoption jumped 47 percent (10.6 percentage points) between survey methodologies in professional services; AI-driven TEM adoption increased by 36% in 2024 alone

owners_60plus_pct.source: Gallup Pathways to Wealth Survey 2025; McKinsey Great Ownership Transfer 2026; Oberlo Small Business Statistics 2024
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: Boomers (ages 60-78 as of 2025) make up 37% of small business owners; more than half of all small-business owners are over 55, up from roughly 30 percent in 2002; one in four small-business owners is 65 or older

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: Gallup 2025: 27% of employer firms with 55+ owners unsure of long-term plan or intend to close permanently. McKinsey 2026: approximately 6M small businesses will exit ownership over the next decade. Chase 2026 Local Snapshot survey: most small business owners are not prepared for succession, lack fully developed plans. This is acute for 541618 where 90% of firms have <5 employees and the entire firm value may reside in the owner's carrier relationships and expertise.

active_consolidators.source: Calero Software M&A activity 2024-2025; TEM market consolidation reports; PEmarketplace deal announcements; CT Acquisitions consulting firm roll-up data 2026
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: Calero Software acquired MDSL (April 2024) and Mobi Wireless Management (2025); TEM market has seen a wave of mergers and acquisitions. However, these are primarily software/platform consolidations rather than PE roll-ups of small consulting firms. Estimated 2-4 PE-backed platforms actively adding small 541618 consulting firms as add-ons in the last 24 months.

buy_mult.source: BizBuySell Insight Report 2025; First Page Sage Consulting Multiples 2025; GF Data Middle Market Valuations 2025; CT Acquisitions consulting firm exit guide 2026
buy_mult.figure_quoted: BizBuySell 2025: average SDE multiple 2.61x across all small businesses. For micro consulting firms at $1-3M EBITDA after owner-comp normalization, more general management consulting trades lower than specialty; applying owner-dependence discount and normalization yields 4-5x EBITDA. CT Acquisitions: below $2M EBITDA is the add-on entry band at 3-5x. Using 4.5x EBITDA as midpoint for small 541618 firms.

exit_mult.source: GF Data Middle Market M&A Valuations 2025; First Page Sage Consulting Multiples 2025; CT Acquisitions PE roll-up exit data 2026
exit_mult.figure_quoted: GF Data business services median 7.4x in 2025. PE roll-up exit from $5-10M EBITDA platform: CT Acquisitions documents EBITDA multiple expansion from 5-7x at entry to 10-12x at exit; using 8.0x for a specialized management consulting platform with recurring expense-management revenue, reflecting GF Data median plus modest recurring-revenue premium but discounting for TEM's AI disruption risk.

pricing_structure.value: TEM dominant sub-segment: ~40-50% contingency/gainshare (share of savings identified); ~30-40% subscription/managed-service (recurring flat fee or per-invoice); ~15-20% T&M/project advisory. Other sub-segments (site selection, utility management): primarily project-fee or retainer.
pricing_structure.source: SSI contingency audit model; Socium TEM market guide 2026; Tellennium TEM guide; TEM market intelligence report GlobeNewswire 2025
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: Managed services account for 42.6% of TEM market share in 2025; contingency-based billing standard in expense audit (providers receive percentage of recoverable savings); cloud-native SaaS delivery models account for over 65% of implementations in 2024

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: uplift_pp = 51.38pp is an artifact of the labor_share anomaly (1.3991 > 1.0 is economically impossible). True economically-implied uplift using a plausible labor_share of ~0.55 (sibling codes average) would be: 0.55 × 0.367235 × 100 = 20.2pp. A = 2.86 (< 5). No tension flag triggered (A < 5 regardless of uplift figure). The stored uplift_pp of 51.38 is flagged as anomalous in reviewer_note — it is not an economic estimate but the mechanical product of the formula applied to a flagged input.

terminal_value.justification: For the dominant TEM sub-segment: the product IS the intelligent processing of telecom/utility/SaaS invoices to identify billing errors and optimization opportunities — precisely what AI does natively. As AI becomes embedded in enterprise ERP and TEM platforms (Calero, Tangoe, Brightfin, Sage), large enterprises self-serve the savings identification that TEM consultants previously provided. The mechanism that kept paid demand alive was the information-processing labor clients did not want to spend; AI eliminates that reason to hire a consultant. Carrier negotiation persists as a human-agency component, but it is a small fraction of TEM revenue and is being productized into procurement platforms. The product itself is being replaced, not just the operator's cost of delivery. MELTING classification justified. (For the site selection and utility management sub-segments, PRESSURED would apply — those sub-segments have judgment and relationship components; but MELTING applies to the dominant TEM segment.)

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V confirmed without proxy: ai_replaceable_share derived from role-by-role task automation assessment based on AI capabilities in invoice auditing, benchmarking, and analysis — no reference to deal activity or M&A trends. A confirmed without proxy: t50 derived from BTOS adoption data (33% professional services adoption per Federal Reserve analysis) and TEM-specific AI platform launch data — no reference to consolidation or PE activity.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: For TEM dominant sub-segment: contingency/gainshare model (~40-50% of revenue) is capture-FRIENDLY — AI reducing audit labor cost expands margins because the fee is a percentage of savings found, not hours spent. This is the opposite of deflation and is reflected in pi_dist = 0.55 (above midpoint). However, subscription/managed-service billing (~30-40%) faces bypass risk as enterprise AI eliminates the need for third-party TEM vendors entirely — this is not fee deflation but product substitution, reflected in pi_moat = 0.30. The mixed model means AI creates both a near-term margin opportunity (contingency slice) and a long-term bypass threat (full product replacement by embedded AI).

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: At t50=2.5: A=4.0, S rises to ~4.31. At t50=5.0: A=2.0, S falls to ~3.67. t50 drives A from 2.0 to 4.0 across the plausible range, moving S by ~0.64 — the largest single-input swing for well-identified inputs.

V is capped at 10.0 due to the anomaly — any labor_share that yields V_raw > 0.25 produces V=10.0 (which any plausible labor_share × ai_replaceable_share would too at 0.55-0.63 range × 0.37 = 0.20-0.23, borderline). If true labor_share were 0.46 (minimum sibling): V_raw = 0.46 × 0.367235 = 0.169, V = 6.76, S drops to ~3.60. The anomaly causes V to be overstated vs. plausible economics; S is meaningfully sensitive to this. Reported with flag: ESTIMATE-HEAVY risk from anomaly.

At pi_moat=0.20: C=1.10, S falls to ~3.64. At pi_moat=0.45: C=2.475, S rises to ~4.34. TEM bypass trajectory is the key uncertainty — if enterprise AI platform adoption accelerates, pi_moat collapses to 0.15-0.20; if carrier complexity keeps specialists employed, pi_moat holds at 0.35-0.40.

evidence.reviewer_note: 541618 IS a catch-all code (heterogeneous=true). Top-3 sub-segments: (1) DOMINANT — Telecom/Technology Expense Management (TEM): contingency-fee invoice audit and ongoing cost optimization for enterprise telecom, SaaS, and cloud spend; firms include Tangoe, Calero, Tellennium and thousands of small TEM consultancies. (2) Utility expense management/optimization consulting: analogous to TEM for energy and utility bills. (3) Site selection and other specialty niches: site-selection advisors (incentive negotiation with municipalities), government-relations management consulting, and other residual niche advisory services excluded from 541611-541614. Scoring is performed for the DOMINANT TEM sub-segment. FLAGGED ANOMALY: labor_share=1.3991 is economically impossible (wages exceed receipts by 40%) and signals a QCEW/SUSB classification mismatch on this residual code. QCEW wages ($14.9B) appear to capture employment from a broader swath of management consulting than SUSB receipts ($11.4B) recorded in 2022; the 2024 vs 2022 vintage mismatch amplifies this — significant reclassification of firms between 541618 and siblings (541611, 541619) likely occurred. Rule 6 requires using the provided value as given; the formula mechanically produces V=10 (capped) regardless of ai_replaceable_share, because V_raw=1.3991×0.367235=0.514 exceeds the 0.25 anchor. If the true labor_share is ~0.55 (sibling average), V_raw≈0.202, V≈8.07, S≈3.78 — still a meaningful number but not 10.0. This V=10 is an artifact, not an economic signal. The confidence_overall is set to LOW primarily due to this anomaly and the catch-all code heterogeneity. Role_mix uses 4-digit 5416 proxy; 541618's true dominant TEM role mix likely has more auditors and clerical invoice processors than the 5416 average, which would increase ai_replaceable_share if measured directly. The terminal value of MELTING for the dominant TEM segment reflects the genuine risk that AI embedded in enterprise platforms replaces the consulting product — the reviewer should weigh this against the PRESSURED classification that might apply if the site selection and utility management sub-segments are larger than estimated.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Phase I ESA report writing, regulatory database searches, permit application drafting and monitoring-report production are heavily AI-exposed (estimated 38% of task time); site reconnaissance, sampling oversight, agency negotiation and professional sign-off (PG/PE where required by ASTM E1527-21 / AAI rule) are not automatable. This role dominates real 541620 firms but does not appear in the 4-digit OEWS proxy top-10.

Physical presence for drilling oversight, sampling, monitoring well work, and site walks cannot be automated. Sensor and drone augmentation assists but does not replace. Only admin/log-filing slice (5%) is automatable.

Client and regulator relationships persist; expert judgment and accountability required. Internal reporting, scheduling, and back-office oversight (~15%) automate. Substantial owner-operator comp in the 5,655 firms with <5 employees.

Deliverable tracking, subcontractor coordination paperwork, and budget reporting (~30%) automate via PM software and AI; agency and client coordination, schedule negotiations, and field-team oversight persist.

Data reduction, mapping production (standard layers, report figures), and compliance-report assembly are directly automatable with AI/GIS tools. Residual 25% involves custom interpretation, client-specific judgment, and regulatory agency negotiation.

T&M billing admin (time entry, invoice generation, markup tracking), proposal production, and document control are directly automatable. Residual 30% involves judgment calls on disputed charges, subcontractor relations, and client service.

The 4-digit 5416 proxy shows Management Analysts at 15.5% wage share — largely an artifact of sibling 5416 codes. Only ~5% of 541620 wage bill represents real ESG/compliance advisory staff at larger firms. Report drafting and disclosure writing automate at 35%; bespoke advisory and regulatory strategy persist.
Proposal production and RFP response drafting (~20%) automate; expert-referral selling, relationship maintenance with lenders and developers, and MSA negotiations do not.
Internal tooling, field data app maintenance. AI-assisted coding for routine tasks (~20%); custom environmental data platform work persists.

Standard accounting (payroll, AP/AR), benefits administration, and onboarding paperwork are substantially automatable (~50%). Complex multi-project cost accounting with subcontractor cost-plus tracking requires judgment.

pi_dist.rationale: Three billing mechanisms create different capture dynamics: (1) T&M (~45% of revenue): when AI cuts report-writing or analysis hours, clients can rescope the engagement or the next bid competition reflects lower hours — savings flow to clients; (2) Fixed-fee Phase I ESA (~35%): commodity work priced at $2,500-$5,500 with competitive lender vendor lists — AI savings go to clients via lower competitive bids, not to operators; (3) Cost-plus federal/public contracts (~20%): audited overhead clawback passes AI-driven labor savings to contracting agencies. The firm-level MSA for lender Phase I programs creates some switching-cost stickiness (vendor lists), but pricing is still shopped. Net pi_dist = 0.38 reflects majority of billing flowing client-wards under AI.
sources: ASTM E1527-21 Phase I ESA standard (fixed-fee commodity framing)
ctacquisitions.com environmental consulting M&A overview
PricingLink.com Phase I ESA pricing structure
lightboxre.com environmental consulting AI commentary

pi_moat.rationale: Moat sources: (1) Licensed credential requirement — ASTM E1527-21 / 40 CFR 312 AAI rule requires a qualified 'environmental professional' (defined credential) to sign Phase I ESAs; AI cannot sign, but a single solo EP can (plan-stamp bypass is realistic, not theoretical); (2) Physical field presence for sampling, reconnaissance, and monitoring well work creates irreducible human requirement; (3) Liability chain — firm holds E&O insurance and is the named defendant in CERCLA litigation, creating switching costs and brand stickiness; (4) Partially offsetting: most Phase I work is lender-driven commodity at competitive pricing with little proprietary data moat; federal deregulatory push (2025-2026 NEPA enforcement rollbacks) threatens some statutory demand base; clients cannot fully bypass but can direct-hire a single EP. Net: meaningful but not high moat.
sources: EPA All Appropriate Inquiries rule 40 CFR 312
ASTM E1527-21 standard (environmental professional definition)
SCS Engineers blog on Phase I ESA liability
lightboxre.com commentary on AI and environmental consulting

t50_years.derivation: current_adoption_pct = 0.18 (professional/technical services ~33% overall per Census BTOS Nov 2025-Jan 2026, discounted to ~18% for environmental consulting given small-firm dominance — 68% of firms have <10 employees — and regulatory/liability friction on AI-assisted work products). Gap to 50% = 32 percentage points. Historical analog: GIS adoption in environmental/AE consulting took ~10-12 years from early-adopter to majority (~50%) usage across the industry (late 1990s to ~2008-2010). AI diffusion is faster due to lower capital cost and broader tool availability. Environmental-specific friction (regulatory acceptance of AI-assisted deliverables, liability chain for EP sign-off, small-firm inertia) moderates pace. Estimated adoption pace: ~4 pp/year from current base. 32 pp / 4 pp/yr = 8 years. Plausible range: if friction low and AI tools accepted for regulatory deliverables, 5-6 years; if regulatory friction high and small-firm inertia dominates, 10-12 years.

current_adoption_pct.source: Census Bureau Business Trends and Outlook Survey (BTOS) + Census library story May 2026
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: Professional, scientific, and technical services sector: ~33% AI use as of late 2025-early 2026 (any business function). Small firms (<20 employees): below 20%. Environmental consulting discount applied to ~18% given 68% of 541620 firms have <10 employees and regulatory friction on AI-assisted deliverables.

historical_analogs.value: 10 years (GIS adoption in AEC/environmental consulting, late 1990s to ~2008-2010)
historical_analogs.source: Esri — How GIS Became the Enterprise Technology It Is Today; EMI AEC Technology Adoption Podcast
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: GIS evolved from specialized technical tool to widespread enterprise use across AEC and environmental consulting over approximately 10-12 years; by 2000s was used across engineering, urban planning, and environmental functions broadly.

owners_60plus_pct.source: Zweig Group 2025 AEC Leadership survey summary; Russell Reynolds Associates engineering services succession analysis 2024
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: A little more than 30% of all firms have a managing principal age 60 or older (Zweig Group data cited in search results). Russell Reynolds analysis of 7 major global engineering services firms (n=242 executives): ~35% of ExCo members and ~30% of EVPs aged 60+.

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: Russell Reynolds (2024): only 39% of construction/engineering/infrastructure leaders describe their organizations as 'proactive' in succession planning; 67% of firms cite talent acquisition and retention as top concern (2025 AEC Inspire Report). Zweig Group: majority of principals prefer internal transition but internal successor shortfall is documented across AEC.

active_consolidators.source: Capstone Partners EHS Market Update Sept 2025; Auxo Capital AEC M&A activity; Privsource PE environmental services; Morrissey Goodale M&A commentary
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: Active PE-backed environmental consulting platforms doing add-ons in last 24 months: Verdantas (Sterling, 14+ acquisitions since May 2024), ERM (KKR, multiple 2024-2025 acquisitions including NewFields 2025), Montrose Environmental (public, 6 acquisitions in 2024), Kleinfelder (Lindsay Goldberg, Veenstra & Kimm 2025), True Environmental (Halle Capital, Sundance acquisition), Geosyntec (Blackstone), RSK Group (Ares, 200+ portfolio), Aventia (Bernhard Capital, Habitat Management 2025). Count = 8.

buy_mult.source: CT Acquisitions — How to Sell an Engineering Firm: 2026 Multiples; Morrissey Goodale AE M&A database commentary
buy_mult.figure_quoted: Single-office regional environmental/engineering firms ($1-3M EBITDA): 5x-7x EBITDA. Small multi-office diversified ($3-8M EBITDA): 6x-9x EBITDA. Median for target $1-10M EBITDA band: approximately 6x EBITDA.

exit_mult.source: CT Acquisitions — How to Sell an Engineering Firm: 2026 Multiples; Morrissey Goodale median EBITDA multiple commentary
exit_mult.figure_quoted: Mid-size multi-state environmental/engineering platforms ($8-25M EBITDA): 8x-11x EBITDA. Morrissey Goodale: MEP and environmental sector median ~7x with top-tier reaching 7.5x for all AE deals; premium environmental platforms (water, infrastructure alignment) command 8-11x. Platform exit at $10M+ EBITDA: midpoint 9x used.

pricing_structure.value: ~35% fixed-fee Phase I ESA (commodity, $2,500-$5,500 per engagement); ~45% T&M monitoring/remediation/engineering oversight (hourly rate schedules, rescoped when hours drop); ~20% cost-plus federal/public (EPA, DoD, DOT environmental contracts with audited overhead)
pricing_structure.source: PricingLink.com Phase I ESA pricing; GeoForward Phase I ESA cost; SCS Engineers blog; CT Acquisitions environmental services overview
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: Most Phase I ESA projects priced between $4,200 and $5,500 on a fixed-fee basis. Phase I ESA scope under ASTM E1527-21 is well-defined and ideally suited for fixed-fee. T&M dominates monitoring, remediation oversight, and longer-duration regulatory compliance work. Federal EPA/DoD/DOT environmental work runs on cost-plus contracts with audited overhead.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: V_raw = 15.93pp EBITDA uplift potential. A = 1.25 (slow adoption, t50=8 years). Uplift is well above 3pp so no tension with A; the low A score reflects real friction (regulatory acceptance, small-firm inertia, EP sign-off requirements) that also reduces near-term revenue capture even though the underlying labor substitution potential is substantial. Consistent.

terminal_value.justification: Demand is substantially statutory: CERCLA liability defenses require AAI/Phase I ESAs by a qualified environmental professional (40 CFR 312), permits and consent decrees mandate monitoring and reporting, and sampling is a physical act. AI cuts the operator's cost of producing Phase I reports and monitoring documentation but does not eliminate the statutory requirement for an EP sign-off — the regulatory mechanism persists. However: (1) AI enables the plan-stamp pattern (solo EP + AI replaces a multi-person firm for commodity Phase I work), compressing the market toward sole practitioners and away from mid-size firms; (2) The 2025-2026 federal deregulatory push (NEPA enforcement rollbacks, reduced EPA enforcement activity) shrinks the discretionary advisory portion of demand (ESG reporting, voluntary assessments) and may narrow statutory demand over 5-10 years; (3) CRE transaction volumes (Phase I ESA demand driver) remain depressed vs. 2021-2022 peaks. Demand PRESSURED: statutory core survives AI but competitive structure shifts toward AI-augmented sole practitioners, and deregulation creates demand headwinds in the advisory slice.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V justified from a rebuilt role-mix based on actual 541620 workforce (environmental scientists/engineers, field techs, GIS staff) — not from the 4-digit 5416 OEWS proxy which is dominated by management analysts (an artifact of sibling codes). A was derived from Census BTOS professional services adoption data (discounted for small-firm dominance in 541620) and GIS-adoption historical analog — no reference to PE deal activity or consolidation trends.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Billing mix: ~35% fixed-fee Phase I ESA (commodity, competitive lender vendor lists — AI savings go to clients via lower competitive bids), ~45% T&M monitoring/remediation (hourly rate schedules — rescoping passes AI hour-savings to clients), ~20% cost-plus federal (audited overhead clawback). All three mechanisms pass majority of AI efficiency gains to clients rather than to operators as margin. This is reflected in pi_dist = 0.38 (below midpoint) — the firm owns the client relationship via liability chain and EP credentials but cannot retain AI-generated margin under prevailing billing structures. pi_moat = 0.45 captures the credential/physical-presence barrier without overstating price retention.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: At t50=5: A=2.0, S≈3.11; at t50=8: A=1.25, S≈2.78; at t50=12: A=0.833, S≈2.46. Range: approximately ±0.33 on S, significant for borderline verdicts.
At ars=0.25 (V=4.56): S≈2.56; at ars=0.42 (V=7.65): S≈2.96. Range: ~±0.20 on S. Depends heavily on within-role fraction for environmental scientists which is the largest role and most contested.

evidence.reviewer_note: Role-mix caveat is severe and intentionally corrected: the provided dataset role_mix is the 4-digit 5416 OEWS proxy dominated by Management Analysts (15.5% wage share) — this is an artifact of sibling codes (541611, 541618, 541690) and badly misrepresents real 541620 firms. The V breakdown was rebuilt from scratch using actual 541620 occupational structure (environmental scientists/geologists/engineers as the dominant professional core, field techs as second-largest labor block, GIS/data and back-office as automatable tail). The Management Analyst proxy wage share (15.5%) was redistributed across the real workforce categories with only a small slice (~5%) attributed to real ESG/compliance advisory staff at larger firms. Pricing-model deflation is the dominant C constraint: all three billing mechanisms (fixed-fee Phase I, T&M, cost-plus federal) pass AI-generated efficiency gains to clients rather than operators, keeping C low despite a meaningful moat from EP credential requirements and liability chain. The plan-stamp risk (solo EP + AI bypassing mid-size firms on commodity Phase I work) is real and was incorporated in pi_moat. Federal deregulatory tailwinds (NEPA/EPA rollbacks in 2025-2026) reduce the discretionary advisory slice of demand; captured in PRESSURED terminal class. Multiples sourced from ctacquisitions.com 2026 data which covers the relevant size range; M=2.0 reflects a real but modest arbitrage in a consolidating market (buy at 6x, exit at 9x).
