breakdown_by_role.role: Technical consultants and analysts (energy engineers, economists, scientists)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Dominant sub-segment is energy consulting. Load forecasting, energy audit report writing, regulatory filing preparation, benchmarking analysis, and compliance documentation are heavily AI-exposed (45% of task time). Expert testimony, field site inspection, utility negotiation, and client-specific project economics persist. The 4-digit proxy understates this role (Management Analysts capture only a fraction); real 541690 firms are staffed by energy engineers, economists, agronomists, safety professionals. Within-role fraction of 0.45 reflects the analytical/documentation majority of energy consulting work.

21,931 of 26,288 firms have <5 employees — substantially owner-operator practices where expert reputation IS the product. Client relationships, project origination, and regulatory/legal testimony cannot be automated. Internal scheduling, reporting, and administrative oversight (~15%) automate.
Deliverable tracking, schedule reporting, and budget paperwork (~30%) automate; client/utility agency coordination and scope negotiation persist.
Proposal production and RFP response drafting (~20%) automate; expert-referral selling to utilities, industrial clients, and government agencies does not.

Energy modeling (EnergyPlus, eQUEST, load forecasting tools) is AI-augmented but requires domain configuration; standard tooling maintenance (~35%) automates. Custom model development for proprietary data platforms persists.
Invoicing, scheduling, document production, and standard HR are directly automatable (~70%). Complex project cost tracking with shared-savings calculation requires judgment.

breakdown_by_role.role: Field-based specialists (site inspectors, safety auditors, crop scouts)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Physical presence required for site energy audits, safety inspections, and field scouting. Sensor and drone augmentation assists but does not replace. Only log-filing and basic reporting (~5%) automate.
Market analysis, competitive benchmarking, and standardized proposal templates (~40%) are directly AI-automatable. Sector-specific client intelligence and relationship management persist.
Standard accounting, payroll, and benefits administration (~50%) are substantially automatable. Complex project cost allocation for shared-savings and performance contracts requires oversight.

pi_dist.rationale: For the dominant sub-segment (energy consulting), billing mechanisms include: (1) Shared-savings/performance contracts (~30%): firm retains a negotiated share of documented savings — AI that increases savings-identification accuracy actually expands the capture base, partially moat-friendly; (2) Project fees for audits and assessments (~35%): hourly or fixed-fee, where AI cutting analysis hours creates rescoping risk and competitive bid pressure; (3) Proprietary data subscription/retainer services (~20%): sticky recurring revenue where AI enhances the product; (4) Utility program cost-plus or incentive contracts (~15%): savings pass to utility clients under program rules. Net pi_dist=0.45 is slightly higher than environmental consulting because the shared-savings mechanism and proprietary data subscriptions create genuine capture pathways. Key person risk is high (owner IS the expert), meaning relationships survive firm acquisition imperfectly.
sources: ctacquisitions.com consulting firm exit guide 2026
Privsource PE-backed consulting acquirers data
Capstone Partners EHS/AEC market update

pi_moat.rationale: Moat sources: (1) CEM (Certified Energy Manager) and PE credentials required for some utility program work; (2) Proprietary load models and historical utility billing data create switching costs for recurring clients; (3) Physical field presence for site audits and safety inspections; (4) Expert reputation in litigation/regulatory testimony contexts (economic consulting sub-segment). Countervailing forces: generic AI can perform energy audits from utility data increasingly well without a dedicated consulting firm; the plan-stamp pattern (solo credentialed expert + AI tooling) is a real bypass risk; economic consulting is extremely key-person, making acquisition value capture difficult (PI moat is the individual, not the firm); agricultural consulting has minimal formal licensure. Overall moat is moderate — meaningful for established recurring-client energy advisory but modest for project-based analytical work where AI substitution risk is high.
sources: Association of Energy Engineers CEM certification requirements
ctacquisitions.com energy consulting M&A overview
IBISWorld energy utility consulting market commentary

t50_years.derivation: current_adoption_pct = 0.22 (professional/scientific/technical services ~33% overall per Census BTOS Nov 2025-Jan 2026; energy consulting discount to ~22% reflecting 83% of 541690 firms with <5 employees, but energy consulting is more data-intensive and tech-forward than environmental, so smaller discount than 541620). Gap to 50% = 28 percentage points. Energy consulting has more AI-native workflows (load forecasting software, energy modeling tools already widely used), tech-forward utility clients, and no regulatory sign-off barrier equivalent to environmental EP requirement. Historical analog: energy modeling software (EnergyPlus, eQUEST) reached widespread use in ~8-10 years from introduction (early 2000s to ~2010-2012); AI diffusion is faster. Estimated adoption pace: ~4-5 pp/year from current base. At 4pp/yr: 28/4 = 7 years. Plausible range: if AI tools for energy analysis are quickly accepted by utilities (5-6 years); if small-firm inertia and expert-reputation models dominate (9-12 years).

current_adoption_pct.source: Census Bureau Business Trends and Outlook Survey (BTOS) May 2026; SBA Research Spotlight AI in Business: Small Firms Closing In (September 2025)
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: Professional, scientific, and technical services sector ~33% AI use as of late 2025-early 2026 (any business function). Small firms (<20 employees): below 20% per census data. Energy consulting is more data-forward than average for the sector, so 22% estimated (above small-firm average of ~17-20% but below large-firm sector rate) to reflect the analytical/modeling nature of work.

historical_analogs.value: 8-10 years (energy modeling software EnergyPlus/eQUEST to widespread professional adoption, early 2000s to ~2010-2012)
historical_analogs.source: Association of Energy Engineers; ESRI GIS enterprise technology evolution; Engineering Management Institute AEC technology adoption
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: Energy consulting/AEC sector technology adoption historically spans 8-12 years from early-adopter to majority use. Energy modeling tools introduced in late 1990s-early 2000s reached widespread professional use by approximately 2010-2012. AI diffusion is faster due to lower capital cost and SaaS delivery.

owners_60plus_pct.source: McKinsey Institute for Economic Mobility — The Great Ownership Transfer; Gallup Pathways to Wealth Survey 2025; small business ownership demographics
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: Boomers (ages 60-78) make up 37% of small business owners (multiple sources). More than half of small business owners are already 55 or older. The Gallup 2025 survey found 27% of employer firms with owners aged 55+ are unsure of long-term plan or intend to close. Applied to 541690 where 83% of 26,288 firms have <5 employees, consistent with Boomer-owner demographic.

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: Gallup Pathways to Wealth Survey 2025: 27% of employer firms with owners 55+ are unsure of plan or will close. U.S. Bank survey 2025: 62% of small-business owners find succession planning overwhelming. Exit Planning Institute: only 54% of small business owners have a formal succession plan despite 85% wanting to create transferable value. Documented succession shortage for small professional services firms consistent with 541690's 83% sub-5-employee composition.

active_consolidators.source: Privsource PE-backed consulting acquirers; CT Acquisitions consulting firm PE buyer data; Capstone Partners EHS/AEC Market Update
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: Active PE-backed platforms doing add-ons in energy/scientific/technical consulting in last 24 months: Legence (Blackstone, 23+ acquisitions including LORD Green Strategies 2025, Bowers Group 2025), SLR Consulting (Ares Management), Arcadia (acquired ENGIE Impact 2026, RPD Energy 2025), E Source (Align Capital Partners, acquired ILLUME Advising 2025), Guidehouse (Bain Capital, energy utility advisory), Berkeley Research Group (TowerBrook Capital 2025, economic/technical consulting). Count = 6. Note: many 541690 sub-segments (agricultural consulting, safety consulting) have less active PE consolidation than energy/economic advisory.

buy_mult.source: CT Acquisitions consulting firm exit guide 2026; ClearlyAcquired valuation multiples for professional services
buy_mult.figure_quoted: Specialty/technical consulting firms ($1M-$3M EBITDA): 7x-9x EBITDA. Firms in $3M-$5M EBITDA range: 8x-11x. Energy consulting commands premium over generalist management consulting (7x generalist vs 8x+ specialty). Median buy multiple for 541690-type firms in $1-10M EBITDA band: approximately 8x. EBITDA normalization note: small firms in this code are predominantly SDE — owner compensation (often $150-300k for solo practitioner) must be added back and replaced with market-rate operator salary before applying multiple.

exit_mult.source: CT Acquisitions consulting firm exit guide 2026; disclosed transaction data from Capstone Partners
exit_mult.figure_quoted: Specialty/technical consulting platforms ($5M-$10M+ EBITDA): 9x-13x EBITDA. WSP/Power Engineers: ~11x. Platform exits for energy advisory firms with recurring utility contracts and proprietary data platforms: 9-12x range, midpoint 10x. Add-on acquisitions into platforms trade 1-3x lower than platform multiples per CT Acquisitions framework. Bain Capital/Guidehouse at $5B in 2023 implies ~15x+ at platform tier; smaller exits cluster 9-11x.

pricing_structure.value: ~30% shared-savings/performance contracts (firm retains negotiated fraction of documented energy savings); ~35% project fees for energy audits and assessments (hourly or fixed-fee); ~20% subscription/retainer data and advisory services (proprietary load forecasting, utility program tracking); ~15% cost-plus utility program contracts
pricing_structure.source: IBISWorld Energy & Utility Consulting Services US analysis; CT Acquisitions energy consulting overview; Guidehouse/Legence service line descriptions
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: Energy consulting billing mix: shared-savings (where AI that improves identification benefits operator), project fees (rescoping risk), and recurring data services (sticky). IBISWorld: Energy & Utility Consulting US market $7.1B in 2024 with 4,419 businesses. Dominant revenue model varies by sub-segment: energy auditors are often fixed-fee/performance; utility program evaluators are often cost-plus; economic consultants are hourly retainer.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: V_raw = 23.43pp EBITDA uplift potential. A = 1.43 (slow-moderate adoption, t50=7 years). Uplift is well above 3pp with no tension. High V reflects labor_share=0.615 (well above average, consistent with this being a knowledge-intensive services industry) and substantial ai_replaceable_share=0.381 in analytical/documentation-heavy energy consulting. A is constrained by small-firm inertia (83% of firms <5 employees) despite energy consulting's tech-forward character. Consistent.

terminal_value.justification: For the dominant sub-segment (energy consulting): the mechanism keeping paid demand alive under mature AI is a combination of (1) regulatory/utility program requirements for certified energy auditors (CEM), some state building code compliance certifications, and utility incentive program administrator credentials — partial statutory anchor; (2) physical field presence for site audits, equipment inspection, and sensor installation; (3) proprietary client relationship data and utility account access. However: the analytical core of energy consulting — load forecasting, energy modeling, savings calculations, compliance reporting — is directly substitutable by AI given client-supplied utility data. The plan-stamp risk is high: a solo CEM with AI tooling can replace a 5-person energy audit firm for most commercial audit engagements. The broader 541690 code spans sub-segments with varying durability: economic consulting faces MELTING risk for routine analysis (AI replaces the analytical product, not just the cost) while retaining expert-testimony demand; agricultural consulting retains field-scouting demand; safety consulting retains physical inspection demand. Scored PRESSURED overall: regulatory and physical-presence anchors prevent full melting, but the analytical majority of the dominant sub-segment (energy consulting) faces significant AI substitution of the product itself, not just production efficiency.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V justified from a rebuilt role-mix based on the dominant sub-segment (energy consulting) workforce, not the 4-digit 5416 OEWS proxy which is dominated by Management Analysts (a sibling-code artifact). The ai_replaceable_share breakdown was constructed from the actual energy consulting role structure: energy engineers/economists as the core technical layer, field specialists as the physical-presence anchor, and back office as the highest-automatable-fraction layer. A was derived from Census BTOS professional services adoption data (adjusted for small-firm dominance) and energy modeling software historical adoption timeline — no reference to PE deal activity or consolidation trends.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Billing mix for energy consulting (dominant sub-segment): ~30% shared-savings/performance (AI improving savings identification partially benefits the operator); ~35% project fees (hourly/fixed — rescoping risk where AI savings go to clients via lower competitive bids); ~20% subscription/retainer data services (sticky, AI enhances the product value); ~15% utility program cost-plus (savings flow to utility/program administrator). Net: pi_dist=0.45 reflects the mixed billing structure — better capture than environmental consulting (where all three mechanisms push savings to clients) because shared-savings and proprietary data subscriptions create genuine retention pathways. pi_moat=0.40 reflects moderate credential/physical-presence barriers, discounted by the high analytical substitutability risk from generic AI for energy audits.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: At ars=0.28 (V=6.89): S≈2.52; at ars=0.46 (V≥10, capped): S≈2.97. Range: ~±0.35 on S. The dominant sub-segment choice (energy consulting vs. economic consulting) materially changes the within-role automatable fraction: economic consulting would have higher automatable fraction but different capture dynamics.

evidence.reviewer_note: CATCH-ALL CODE: 541690 is 'Other Scientific and Technical Consulting' — a residual code spanning energy consulting, economic consulting, agricultural/agronomic consulting, safety consulting, and miscellaneous scientific advisory. heterogeneous=true is set per core rule 5. Top-3 revenue sub-segments by hypothesis (no definitive Census breakdown at 6-digit level): (1) Energy consulting — IBISWorld tracks 'Energy & Utility Consulting' at $7.1B in the US in 2024, 4,419 firms; (2) Economic/quantitative consulting — spans litigation support, regulatory economics, and macro advisory (firms like Analysis Group, NERA, Charles River partially in this code); (3) Safety consulting (EHS) — a growing segment with significant PE interest (multiple active consolidators). Scoring was performed on energy consulting as the dominant single identifiable sub-segment by revenue and firm count within 541690. The 4-digit OEWS proxy (dominated by Management Analysts at 15.5% wage share) was discarded in favor of an energy-consulting-specific role reconstruction, per prompt instruction. Role-mix caveat: the 83% sub-5-employee composition means the code is substantially owner-operator expert practitioners — key-person risk is extreme and multiples embed earn-outs that are not captured in this record's nominal buy_mult/exit_mult. If the record were scored on economic consulting (higher analytical substitutability but smaller statutory demand) the terminal class might shift to MELTING. The labor_share of 0.615 is notably high — consistent with the knowledge-intensive, human-capital-intensive nature of this catch-all; revenue per employee of $280,662 (from dataset derivation) is high, suggesting the labor_share figure reflects both employee compensation and imputed owner compensation for the many sole practitioners. EBITDA normalization: for firms in the $1-10M band, the provided Damodaran-basis margin of 15.65% is applied; real owner-operator firms likely show higher pre-owner-comp margins but true EBITDA after market-rate replacement is closer to the stated margin.

n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%

current_adoption_pct.source: Federal Reserve FEDS Notes — Monitoring AI Adoption in the US Economy (April 2026); US Census BTOS AI Supplement May-June 2026
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: Professional, scientific and technical services sector ~33% AI adoption overall (Federal Reserve April 2026); BTOS overall firm adoption 18% at end-2025. Material use defined as AI deployed in production workflows affecting automatable R&D functions (literature review, data analysis, report writing, grant drafting). Nanotech-specific estimate set at 0.20 — below PST average (33%) due to firm-size effect (BTOS shows large-firm adoption 50-60% vs small-firm substantially lower) and SBIR/grant-funded firm culture with slower tooling adoption.

historical_analogs.value: 8 years from ~0% to 50% (ERP in professional services mid-1990s to mid-2000s; cloud computing in HPC R&D labs ~2010-2018); nanotech R&D adjusted to 8 years given small-firm, grant-funded, cleanroom-oriented character slower than PST average
historical_analogs.source: InfoQ Technology Adoption Curve analysis; Cloud Adoption driving HPC Digital R&D (Big Compute); Census/Fed BTOS working papers
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: Cloud computing crossed 50% HPC user adoption ~8 years after commercial launch (~2010-2018). ERP in professional services ~8-year analog. AI for knowledge-intensive R&D labs expected similar timeline given complexity of integration with physical lab workflows.

owners_60plus_pct.source: Digital Web Solutions (DWS) Average Age of Small Business Owners 2026; Forbes small business owner demographics 2024
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: 50.9% of small business owners are between 50 and 88; 37% of owners are between 55-73 (2024 data). Capital-intensive/technical sectors (financial/insurance average owner age 54) tend older than average. SBIR nanotech firm founders skew older (academic spinouts, serial SBIR awardees). Estimated 30% of principals age 60+ based on PST sector analog.

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: No succession shortage documented specifically for NAICS 541713 nanotech R&D firms. General 'silver tsunami' trend applies (McKinsey: 52.3% of US employer-businesses owned by people 55+; Gallup: 60% of small business owners lack succession plan) but no nanotech-specific survey or report explicitly documents succession shortage for this code.

active_consolidators.source: KippsDeSanto Aerospace/Defense/Government Technology MarketView 2025; web search for PE-backed nanotech R&D consolidators
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: No PE-backed platform identified as focused exclusively on nanotechnology R&D services in 2023-2025. Defense-adjacent government services platforms (tracked by KippsDeSanto) occasionally absorb SBIR-funded nanotech labs as add-ons to broader government technology platforms. Estimate 1 active consolidator with meaningful nanotech R&D add-on activity.

buy_mult.source: CT Acquisitions defense contractor EBITDA multiple analysis 2026; ctacquisitions.com
buy_mult.figure_quoted: Defense contractors with ITAR registration and past performance typically sell for 7-10x EBITDA. SBIR-funded nanotech R&D at lower end (7-8x) due to grant dependency, thin EBITDA margins, and PI-portable customer relationships. Buy multiple estimated 7.5x for small firm ($1-10M EBITDA) in government-funded nanotech R&D.

exit_mult.source: PCE Investment Bankers Aerospace/Defense/Government Q2 2025; KippsDeSanto Marketview 2025
exit_mult.figure_quoted: Aerospace/Defense/Government sector median 18.96x TEV/EBITDA Q2 2026 (PCE); CACI acquired Azure Summit at 11.6x ($1.275B / $110M EBITDA); KippsDeSanto market median 16.4x across full sector. R&D-only services platforms (no defense hardware) trade at discount to sector median; estimate 12.5x for a scaled nanotech/government-R&D platform at $10M+ EBITDA.

pricing_structure.value: SBIR Phase I: firm-fixed-price (~35% of federal contract value); SBIR Phase II: cost-plus-fixed-fee (CPFF) with 7-10% fee cap (~45% of federal contract value); commercial NRE/FTE agreements (~10%); other grants/cooperative agreements (~10%). Under CPFF, AI-driven labor hour reductions flow to the government sponsor (reduced billable hours, not retained as margin). Under FFP and commercial NRE, firm captures efficiency gains. Net: approximately 55% of revenue sits under cost-plus/grant structures where AI savings accrue to sponsor.
pricing_structure.source: SBIR Basics — Accounting System Differences for Fixed-Price and Cost-Plus SBIR Awards (2025); SBIR.gov tutorials on CPFF fee limits
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: Phase II awards often use CPFF model; fixed fee typically 7-10% of estimated costs; CPFF contracts reimburse actual allowable costs plus fixed fee, shifting financial risk to government. 46% of SBIR Phase II companies receive up to 50% of company revenue from SBIR awards.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Literature review, experiment design drafts, simulation setup, data analysis and grant/report writing are heavily AI-exposed (estimated 28% of this role's labor time). Physical synthesis, cleanroom/characterization runs and instrument operation are not automatable. The 5-digit OEWS proxy overstates biotech (Medical Scientists 6.1%) and understates materials/chemistry/physics roles dominant in nanotech; the guidance estimates real nanotech firms skew to 19-2032 Materials Scientists, 19-2031 Chemists, 19-2012 Physicists. Plausible fraction 0.22-0.35; 0.28 is mid-range.

Design drafting, CAD documentation and technical report writing automate substantially. Fab/cleanroom process engineering, equipment integration and physical prototyping are not automatable. OEWS lists 17-2141 Mechanical Engineers at 1.9% of wage bill with significant unlisted tail; total engineering est. ~14% of wage bill for nanotech vs biotech-biased proxy. Plausible fraction 0.20-0.35.

Hands-on sample prep, instrument operation, cleanroom work and physical characterization are not automatable by current AI. Small fraction (5%) captures administrative-adjacent tasks such as data entry, sample logging and protocol documentation that AI can assist. OEWS lists Biological Technicians (proxy artifact) at 1.5%; real nanotech has chemical/engineering technicians in unlisted tail est. ~9% total.

Natural Sciences Managers (7.3% wage share) and Managers All Other (3.1%) combined ~10% of wage bill. Sponsor/customer relationship management, scientific direction and judgment persist. Internal reporting, proposal assembly, budget tracking and status presentations are AI-automatable (~20% of this role's time).

General and Operations Managers (5.15%), Project Management Specialists (2.57%), Business Operations Specialists (1.56%) combined ~9% of wage bill. Grant/contract administration, compliance paperwork, project tracking and scheduling are heavily AI-automatable (~30%). Sponsor-facing leadership and risk management persist.

Software Developers at 6.58% of wage bill; AI-code-generation tools (GitHub Copilot etc.) augment ~25% of output (boilerplate, documentation, data pipeline scripts). Simulation design, instrument control and novel algorithm work persist as human-led. AI-augmented rather than replaced.

breakdown_by_role.role: Admin, facilities, EHS and remaining tail
breakdown_by_role.rationale: The unlisted 62% tail of the 5-digit OEWS (only top-10 cover ~38% of wage bill) is reallocated here together with admin/facilities/EHS staff. Admin and document work (invoicing, scheduling, HR forms, compliance documentation) automates heavily; cleanroom EHS staff, facilities maintenance and animal/lab facility operations do not. Blended automation fraction ~15% for this diverse tail.

pi_dist.rationale: Client relationship control is significantly constrained: (1) ~55% of revenue is cost-reimbursable (CPFF SBIR Phase II and government grants) where the government sponsor owns the IP and the relationship is to the program office/contracting officer, not the firm; AI savings flow to sponsors as reduced billable hours. (2) Individual principal investigators are highly portable — their grants and agency relationships often leave with them. (3) ~35% fixed-price SBIR and commercial NRE revenue is more capture-friendly, but these are shorter-duration projects. Net: firm controls ~30% of relationship value; sponsor dependency and PI-portability dominate.
sources: SBIR.gov pricing structure documentation
sbirbasics.com CPFF vs FFP accounting system analysis 2025

pi_moat.rationale: Moat factors: (1) Physical: specialized cleanroom equipment, characterization instruments (SEM, TEM, XRD) and controlled-environment labs represent a real barrier — agencies cannot quickly replicate this; (2) Regulatory: past performance ratings, facility clearances and DCSA/sponsor quals are sticky; (3) Proprietary IP: materials synthesis protocols and characterized nano-systems create switching costs for specific program work. Counterfactors: (1) The analytical/simulation portion of billable work (30-40% of revenue) has thin moat — agencies can increasingly access AI tools directly; (2) Large enterprise receipts ($56.9B vs $3.6B wages implies many receipts are from product/IP firms classified here, not services — service sub-segment moat is weaker); (3) Client concentration risk is high in government-funded R&D. Net moat: moderate, driven by physical/facility barriers but constrained by analytical-work substitution risk. 0.40 reflects mid-moat, range 0.30-0.55.
sources: NNI FY25 Budget Supplement — structure of federally funded nanotech R&D
ctacquisitions.com defense contractor value drivers (clearances, facilities, past performance)

t50_years.derivation: Starting point: current_adoption_pct = 0.20 (20% of firms materially using AI). Historical analog: cloud computing reached ~50% HPC adoption in ~8 years from ~0% (2010-2018); ERP in professional services ~8 years (mid-1990s to mid-2000s). Nanotech R&D is at ~20% after AI tools became available (~2022-2023 for LLMs), so approximately 2-3 years into the adoption cycle. If 0%→50% takes 8 years and we are at roughly year 2-3 with 20% penetration, remaining time to 50% is roughly 5-6 years. However, nanotech R&D firms face structural delays: predominantly small (<20 employees), SBIR-funded with government compliance constraints, lab-bench-centered culture with slower technology tooling adoption, and physical-lab operations that limit AI integration to analytical/writing functions only. Adding 1-2 year structural delay: t50 = 7 years from 2026. Range: 5-10 years.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: V_raw = 0.01410136, implying 1.41 pp of revenue freed — well below the 3 pp threshold. A = 1.43 (< 5), so the cross-check tension flag does not trigger. The low uplift is mechanically driven by the implausibly low labor_share of 0.0676 (flagged dataset anomaly: $3.6B wages vs $56.9B receipts, likely classification mismatch with large-enterprise product revenues booked at this code). For the fee-for-service service sub-segment the true labor share is estimated closer to 0.50 per siblings (541715: 0.49), which would yield V_raw ~0.10 and uplift ~10 pp — but the dataset value is used as given per rule 6. The combination of low A (slow adoption) and mechanically suppressed V (dataset artifact) is arithmetically consistent.

terminal_value.justification: The dominant sub-segment — materials/semiconductor-adjacent contract R&D and nanomaterials development — sits at the intersection of physical lab services and analytical services. Physical synthesis, cleanroom fabrication and characterization (SEM/TEM/XRD etc.) are DURABLE: AI cannot run these instruments or execute physical deposition. However, the analytical/design services (simulation, literature review, study design, grant/report writing) that are currently billable hours are AI-replaceable — representing a shrinking revenue segment. The key risk: as agencies assume AI-accelerated research productivity, they may reduce funding allocations per research objective ('same science, fewer dollars'), compressing fee-for-service revenue even if physical work persists. Government budget volatility (NNI $2.2B FY2025 request but subject to appropriations) adds demand-side risk. Net: PRESSURED — physical-lab demand survives mature AI, but analytical-hour revenue shrinks and government pricing efficiency expectations further compress the billable base.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V was set from labor_share (Census dataset) and ai_replaceable_share (OEWS role mix plus judgment fractions derived from task-level AI capability assessment). A was derived from current AI adoption rate (Census BTOS/Federal Reserve data) and historical technology adoption analog (cloud/ERP). Neither V nor A referenced any M&A deal activity, PE consolidation trends, or market multiple data.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Pricing mix: ~45% CPFF SBIR Phase II (AI labor savings flow to government sponsor via reduced billable hours or program scope expansion — firm keeps fixed fee only, typically 7-10% of costs); ~35% firm-fixed-price SBIR Phase I and commercial NRE (firm retains AI efficiency gain); ~10% grants/cooperative agreements (sponsor keeps savings); ~10% commercial/licensing. Net: approximately 55% of revenue sits under cost-reimbursable structures where AI efficiency accrues to sponsors, not the firm. This is reflected in pi_dist = 0.30, substantially discounting the theoretical capture rate to account for the dominant CPFF billing model. The SBIR commercialization data (19% of Phase II companies get >50% revenue from SBIR awards) confirms heavy government dependence.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: At t50=5: A=2.0, S≈1.90; at t50=10: A=1.0, S≈1.57. Range of S ≈ 1.57-1.90. Moderate sensitivity given A is not the binding constraint — V and C are both low.

top_uncertain_inputs.plausible_range: Dataset value 0.0676 (used per rule 6); true service sub-segment labor share estimated 0.45-0.55 (analogous to 541715 at 0.49). If true labor share 0.50: V_raw=0.104, V=4.16 — dramatically higher than recorded 0.56. This is the single largest source of scoring error for 541713.
top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: If corrected labor_share 0.50: V≈4.16, S≈(4.16×1.2×1.43×5.69×2.67)^0.2≈3.16. Dataset artifact suppresses V by ~7x; true service-segment score would be meaningfully higher.
At pi_dist=0.20: C=0.80, S≈1.55; at pi_dist=0.40: C=1.60, S≈1.87. Moderate range.
At 0 consolidators: CFD=0, B=5.95, S≈1.74; at 3 consolidators: CFD=0.301, B=5.23, S≈1.67. B is not highly sensitive to consolidators in this range.

evidence.reviewer_note: HETEROGENEOUS CODE: 541713 spans (1) nanomaterials and materials-science contract R&D shops (dominant EBITDA-generating sub-segment — hypothesis), (2) semiconductor-adjacent cleanroom/characterization services firms, and (3) venture/grant-funded nanotech ventures with no EBITDA. Dominant sub-segment scored: materials/semiconductor-adjacent contract R&D and nanomaterials development services for government and commercial sponsors. DATA ANOMALY: labor_share = 0.0676 is implausibly low for an R&D services code. QCEW wages of $3.6B vs SUSB receipts of $56.9B implies a ratio inconsistent with service-firm economics — likely driven by 232 large-enterprise firms (500+ employees) booking product/IP revenues at this NAICS code without matching QCEW wage records at this level. Siblings: 541714 labor_share=0.90 (pre-revenue biotech artifact), 541715 labor_share=0.49 (likely more representative). The dataset value is used per rule 6 but mechanically suppresses V by ~7x vs what the service sub-segment would show at labor_share ~0.50. V score of 0.56 likely understates AI value for the acquirable sub-segment. PRICING TRAP: Cost-plus CPFF structure (~45% of revenue) passes AI hour-savings to government sponsors; this is correctly reflected in pi_dist=0.30. ACQUISITION POPULATION: n_band of 301 uses generic 15.65% margin applied to SUSB size classes — meaningless for the grant/VC-funded majority of firms; the genuinely EBITDA-positive service sub-segment is likely 50-100 firms, overstating the roll-up target universe.

Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

BLS ECEC news release (raw/ecec.nr0.htm)
Census SUSB 2022 (receipts by NAICS)

n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%

current_adoption_pct.source: IntuitionLabs AI Adoption in Pharma and Biotech: 2026 Industry Benchmarks; ICON/GlobalData 2024 CRO survey; Benchling 2026 Biotech AI Report
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: 49% of pharma/biotech firms use AI/Big Data in R&D programs (ICON/GlobalData 2024); 92% report some AI use but only 13-30% have fully implemented programs (ZoomRx 2024/Define Ventures 2025). Most widely adopted: literature review (76%), protein structure prediction (71%), scientific reporting (66%). Material use defined as AI deployed in production workflows for automatable R&D functions. Estimate set at 0.35 for the fee-for-service CRO sub-segment — below the 49% headline (which skews to large pharma) and above the 13-30% fully-implemented rate, reflecting that most mid-sized preclinical CROs now use AI tools in at least some production workflows (medical writing, data analysis) but not comprehensively.

historical_analogs.value: 7 years from ~0% to 50% (bioinformatics/NGS tool adoption in biotech labs ~2010-2017; LIMS adoption in research organizations ~8 years mid-2000s to mid-2010s); AI adoption in drug discovery accelerating faster than LIMS due to vendor/academic push (AlphaFold 2020, GPT-era 2022+); analog set at 7 years
historical_analogs.source: Drug Discovery News — The 2026 AI Power Shift; Benchling 2026 Biotech AI Report; IntuitionLabs Pharma AI Vendor Landscape 2026
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: AI in drug discovery crossing 50% adoption threshold rapidly; 2026 expected to see AI-first discovery as standard. Benchling 2026 report shows 50% of adopters already report faster time-to-target. Bioinformatics/NGS tools reached ~50% adoption in research labs by 2015-2017 (~6-8 years after commercial availability circa 2008-2010).

owners_60plus_pct.source: Digital Web Solutions Average Age of Small Business Owners 2026; Forbes small business owner demographics 2024; general small business demographics
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: 50.9% of small business owners are between 50 and 88 (DWS 2026); 37% of owners are 55-73 (2024 data). Biotech/CRO founders trend slightly younger than average small business (VC-backed firms with academic spinout culture; some serial entrepreneurs in their 40s-50s). Fee-for-service preclinical CRO founders who built businesses over 10-20 years are more likely to be 60+. Estimated 28% of CRO sub-segment principals are 60+ (slight discount to PST average of ~30% given founder age profile).

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: No succession shortage documented specifically for NAICS 541714 fee-for-service biotech/CRO firms. BioSpace article on succession planning in life sciences discusses challenges but does not document a documented shortage for small CRO owners. General 'silver tsunami' applies (52.3% of employer-business owners are 55+; Gallup 60% lack succession plan) but no code-specific survey confirms shortage.

active_consolidators.source: IntuitionLabs CRO Consolidation analysis 2025; bioxconomy.com CRO M&A 2024 review; Delancey Street Partners Pharma Services 2025 Year in Review
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: PE sponsors responsible for more than half of pharma services platform transactions in 2022-2023. Active PE-backed platforms doing add-ons 2023-2025 include: Arsenal/Lumanity, Kohlberg/Trinity, Permira/Valeris, Worldwide Clinical Trials (announced $500M Catalyst acquisition Jan 2026), Audax Private Equity (Avantor Clinical Services, $650M Aug 2024). Inotiv (14 acquisitions 2018-2022) entered Chapter 11 restructuring, reducing consolidator count. Estimate 5 active PE-backed platforms pursuing add-on acquisitions specifically in preclinical/biotech CRO space in 24-month window through July 2026.

buy_mult.source: Objective Investment Banking and Valuation — Valuation of Clinical Research Sites (2025); Aventis Advisors TIC Company Valuations (Nov 2025)
buy_mult.figure_quoted: Smaller standalone sites with EBITDA below $5M valued in 4x-6x range; mid-sized operators ($5-10M EBITDA) may justify 7x-9x; mid-market operators generally transact in 6x-12x EBITDA range. For $1-10M EBITDA preclinical/biotech CRO, median buy multiple estimated 7x — reflecting lower end of the mid-sized range, adjusted down slightly for 2024 capacity oversupply and Inotiv restructuring reducing competition for add-ons.

exit_mult.source: Objective Investment Banking — CRO valuation report 2025; Aventis Advisors TIC valuations Nov 2025
exit_mult.figure_quoted: Public CRO comparables trading at median 17.8x EBITDA; larger networks with $10M+ EBITDA consistently achieve low-teens multiples (12x-14x); mid-market operators at 6x-12x. Pharma services overall median 19.8x (Delancey Street Q1-Q4 2025 sample) but this skews to large clinical CROs. For scaled preclinical CRO platform at $10M+ EBITDA: 13x estimated as mid-teens discount to public comps, reflecting private-market illiquidity and service-only (no proprietary assets) profile.

pricing_structure.value: FTE-based agreements (~50% of revenue for large sponsor relationships): AI productivity gains cut FTE-hours billed, passing efficiency to sponsors unless firm maintains headcount; FFS/unit pricing (~35%): per-study or per-assay billing allows firm to retain AI efficiency gains until competitive repricing; milestone/deliverable contracts (~15%): mixed capture. FTE rate stagnation documented (real-term decline over 60% since 1990s per ChemistryWorld analysis). Net: approximately 50% of revenue under FTE/cost-plus structures where AI savings initially accrue to sponsors via reduced unit demand.
pricing_structure.source: Contract Pharma — Full-Time Equivalent Agreements; Chemistry World — CRO FTE pricing analysis; biopharmatrend.com CRO industry in flux 2024
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: FTE model offers dedicated continuous support billed per scientist; FFS ties payments to deliverables. Full-time equivalent FTE rates have declined over 60% in real terms since early 1990s. Traditional FTE and FFS models increasingly being replaced by hybrid results-driven collaborations.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Literature review, study design, data analysis, regulatory writing and manuscript/report production are heavily AI-exposed (estimated 30% of this role's time). Wet-lab experimentation, assay execution, cell culture and in-vivo work are not automatable. OEWS proxy (19-1042 Medical Scientists 6.1%, 19-1021 Biochemists 2.2%) likely closer to real biotech composition than for nanotech; additional unlisted tail of life science researchers expands to ~25% of wage bill. AI adoption in literature review (76%) and scientific reporting (66%) per Benchling/IntuitionLabs data grounds the 30% estimate.

Hands-on bench work — cell culture, sample prep, assay execution, animal handling — is not automatable by AI. Robotics (liquid handlers, automated imaging) is the substitution channel, not LLMs. Small 5% fraction captures data entry, sample documentation and protocol logging. OEWS lists Biological Technicians at 1.5% (proxy for larger unlisted research associate population in biotech); total estimated ~11.5%.

Natural Sciences Managers (7.3% wage share) and Managers All Other (3.1%) combined ~10% of wage bill. Sponsor relationship management, scientific judgment and program direction persist as non-automatable. Internal reporting, resourcing analyses and proposal assembly are AI-automatable (~20%).

General and Operations Managers (5.15%), Project Management Specialists (2.57%), Business Operations Specialists (1.56%) totaling ~9% of wage bill. Study coordination, contract administration and compliance paperwork automate heavily (~30%). Sponsor-facing project leadership, milestone negotiation and escalation management persist.

breakdown_by_role.role: Software developers and computational biology staff
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Software Developers (6.58% wage share) plus estimated ~2% tail of data scientists and bioinformaticians in unlisted occupations totaling ~8.5%. AI-code-generation tools automate ~25% of output (boilerplate, pipeline scripts, documentation). Bioinformatics pipeline design, novel algorithm development and instrument software integration persist as human-led.

QA/RA occupations in unlisted tail estimated ~6.5% of wage bill. GLP/GMP documentation, audit trail maintenance and regulatory submission assembly are heavily AI-exposed (~25% of role time). Accountable sign-off, FDA inspection response and sponsor audit management persist as judgment-dependent. AI document drafting and template population are already widely deployed in pharma regulatory affairs.

The unlisted tail of 5-digit OEWS (62% of wage bill uncovered by top-10) plus admin, vivarium, facilities and EHS staff. Admin and document work (scheduling, invoicing, HR, compliance tracking) automates; vivarium caretakers, animal facility technicians and facilities/EHS staff for BSL-rated labs are physical and not AI-replaceable. Blended automation fraction ~15% for this diverse tail.

pi_dist.rationale: Client relationship control for fee-for-service preclinical CROs: (1) Master Service Agreements (MSAs) and Preferred Service Agreements (PSAs) with pharma sponsors are firm-level contracts — not PI-portable as in academic/grant-funded SBIR shops; (2) Approved vendor status in pharma procurement systems creates firm-level stickiness; (3) However, FTE pricing (~50% of revenue) means AI productivity translates to fewer billable scientist-units — revenue shrinks with efficiency even if the relationship persists; (4) Competitive market: 2024-2025 capacity oversupply in preclinical CRO market (Inotiv restructuring, China CRO competition) erodes pricing power. Net: firm controls ~40% of the economic relationship value. Better than SBIR-funded nanotech (no PI-portability risk, MSA-level relationships) but significantly deflated by FTE billing model and competitive dynamics. Range 0.30-0.50.
sources: Contract Pharma FTE agreement analysis
biopharmatrend.com CRO industry pricing evolution 2024
IntuitionLabs CRO consolidation analysis 2025

pi_moat.rationale: Moat factors: (1) Physical/regulatory: GLP-compliant animal facilities (AAALAC accreditation), BSL-rated labs, FDA-audited bioanalytical platforms — genuine barriers requiring significant capital and 2-3 year audit history to establish; (2) Accreditation switching costs: pharma sponsors re-qualify CRO vendors which takes 6-18 months per program — creating sticky relationships for ongoing programs; (3) Proprietary biological models: unique transgenic mouse lines, proprietary cell lines and validated assay platforms create program-specific lock-in. Counterfactors: (1) AI bypasses analytical/computational billing which represents 20-30% of CRO revenue (in-house AI increasingly handles literature review, data analysis, report writing); (2) China CROs (WuXi, Pharmaron) offer significant price competition, eroding commodity preclinical services moat; (3) Large pharma insourcing trend for early discovery. Net moat: moderate-to-good, driven by physical/GLP/accreditation barriers which are genuine. Slightly higher than nanotech (better regulatory moat, firm-level relationships). Range 0.35-0.60.
sources: Objective IBV CRO valuation factors 2025
Proclinical — Top 10 CROs to Watch 2025
IntuitionLabs CRO consolidation dynamics

t50_years.derivation: Starting point: current_adoption_pct = 0.35 (35% of fee-for-service biotech CRO firms materially using AI in automatable functions). Historical analog: bioinformatics/NGS tool adoption in research labs reached ~50% in ~7 years (2010-2017). LIMS adoption in research organizations ~8 years (mid-2000s to mid-2010s). AI adoption in drug discovery is accelerating faster than LIMS: vendor push (AlphaFold, LLMs), competitive pressure and published proof-of-concept (literature review 76%, scientific reporting 66% adoption per Benchling). Starting at 35% and targeting 50%, with a 7-year total S-curve, we are approximately at year 4 of the adoption cycle; remaining time ≈ 3 years. However, GLP-regulated validation requirements for AI in formal study documentation, FDA/EMA regulatory uncertainty about AI-generated submissions, and sponsor qualification of AI outputs slow deployment in the most-regulated functions. Adding 2-year regulatory-delay buffer: t50 = 5 years from 2026. Range 3-8 years.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: V_raw = 0.18867570, implying 18.87 pp of revenue freed as labor cost savings. A = 2.0 (< 5). No cross-check tension: uplift is well above 3 pp and A is below 5. The high uplift is driven by the mechanically elevated labor_share of 0.9006 (flagged dataset artifact: pre-revenue biotech firms inflate the wage-to-receipts ratio since equity and grant funding are not receipts). For the fee-for-service CRO sub-segment actually generating EBITDA, the true labor share is approximately 0.50-0.65 (typical for services firms), which would yield V_raw ≈ 0.10-0.14 and uplift ≈ 10-14 pp — still substantial and consistent with a high-AI-impact industry. The dataset-inflated V = 7.55 likely overstates V for the acquirable sub-segment, though the direction of AI impact is real.

terminal_value.justification: The dominant sub-segment — fee-for-service preclinical contract research (in vitro assays, in vivo pharmacology, bioanalytical, toxicology) — sits at the intersection of physical/regulated services and AI-replaceable analytical services. Physical wet-lab demand (GLP animal studies, cell-based assays, ADME/Tox studies with conducted experiments required for FDA submissions) is DURABLE: AI cannot conduct in-vivo studies and FDA requires conducted experiments, not AI predictions, for IND/NDA submissions. However, the analytical/computational fraction of CRO billing (bioinformatics, literature reviews, data interpretation reports, regulatory writing) is being AI-replaced or in-sourced — this represents 20-30% of CRO revenue. More importantly, as AI-first discovery compresses the number of wet experiments per drug program (better target selection, fewer false leads to synthesize), the total volume of outsourced wet-lab studies per drug discovery program may shrink. The competitive threat from AI-enabled in-house capabilities and China CROs (WuXi, Pharmaron) further compresses pricing. Net: PRESSURED — physical GLP demand survives AI, but the total billable universe per drug program shrinks and analytical-hour revenue declines.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V was set from labor_share (Census dataset) and ai_replaceable_share (OEWS role mix plus task-level judgment fractions). Benchling 2026 Biotech AI Report and IntuitionLabs pharma AI benchmarks were used to ground the current adoption rate (A input) and the within-role automatable fractions. A was derived from current AI adoption data and historical bioinformatics/LIMS adoption analog. Neither V nor A referenced M&A deal activity, PE consolidation trends, or market multiple data.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Pricing mix: ~50% FTE-based agreements (AI productivity passes to sponsors as reduced FTE unit demand; firm keeps FTE rate but sells fewer scientist-equivalents as AI compresses hours); ~35% fee-for-service/unit pricing (firm retains efficiency gains until competitive repricing); ~15% milestone/deliverable contracts (mixed). FTE rate stagnation documented (real-term decline 60% since 1990s). Sponsor procurement increasingly demanding FTE-rate transparency and productivity benchmarking. This deflation is reflected in pi_dist = 0.40 — firm captures 40% of the economic relationship value, substantially discounted from 1.0 by the dominant FTE billing model and the competitive pricing environment (China CRO pressure, Inotiv capacity overhang). The combination of pi_dist=0.40 and pi_moat=0.45 yields C=1.80, reflecting that physical/GLP moat is real but pricing model captures only a portion of AI efficiency gains for the operator.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: At t50=3: A=3.33, S≈4.08; at t50=8: A=1.25, S≈2.93. Range of S ≈ 2.93-4.08. Significant sensitivity. t50 is the primary uncertainty driver for A given the fast-moving AI adoption curve in pharma/biotech.

top_uncertain_inputs.plausible_range: Dataset value 0.9006 (used per rule 6); true fee-for-service CRO sub-segment labor share estimated 0.50-0.65 (pre-revenue biotech ventures inflate the ratio). If true labor share 0.55: V_raw=0.115, V=4.62 — significantly below the dataset-derived V=7.55 but still a high-V industry. Direction unchanged but magnitude overstated by dataset artifact.
top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: If corrected labor_share 0.55: V≈4.62, S≈(4.62×1.8×2.0×5.35×3.43)^0.2≈(305)^0.2≈3.12. Dataset artifact inflates V by ~1.6x; true service-segment S would be somewhat lower but still in the 3.0+ range.
At 3: CFD=0.301, B=5.78, S≈3.57; at 7: CFD=0.452, B=5.06, S≈3.37. Moderate sensitivity — higher consolidator count modestly reduces B due to CFD deflation.
At pi_moat=0.35: C=1.40, S≈3.21; at pi_moat=0.60: C=2.40, S≈3.80. Notable range driven by uncertainty about China CRO competitive threat and AI insourcing speed.

evidence.reviewer_note: HETEROGENEOUS CODE: 541714 mixes (a) venture/grant-funded biotech ventures with no EBITDA (majority of firm count — estimated 70-80% of the 4,937 firms have no acquirable EBITDA), (b) fee-for-service contract research / preclinical CROs (dominant EBITDA-generating sub-segment — hypothesis, approximately 400-600 genuinely cash-flowing firms), and (c) hybrid platform companies with partial product revenue. Dominant sub-segment scored: fee-for-service preclinical services / contract research labs for pharmaceutical sponsors. DATA ANOMALY: labor_share = 0.9006 is a pre-revenue biotech artifact. QCEW wages $65.7B vs SUSB receipts $77.8B — when equity and grant funding finance wages but are not counted as receipts, the wage-to-receipt ratio inflates beyond 1.0 before the IPS harmonization factor is applied. The IPS ÷1.3407 brings it to 0.9006 but this still overstates true service-firm labor share by ~1.6x. This mechanically inflates V_raw to 0.189 and V to 7.55 — likely 1.5-2x the true CRO sub-segment value. Per rule 6, dataset value used and conflict noted. n_band of 599 (using 15.65% margin on receipts) is essentially meaningless for pre-revenue ventures; genuinely acquirable cash-flowing targets likely 200-400 firms. CONSOLIDATION NOTE (B/M use only): Inotiv's 2021-2022 acquisition run (14 deals) collapsed in Chapter 11 restructuring — active consolidators excludes Inotiv as an ongoing buyer. Remaining active platforms (Arsenal, Kohlberg, Permira, Worldwide, Audax) are genuine 2024-2025 add-on buyers. China CRO competitive pressure (WuXi AppTec, Pharmaron) materially affects moat estimates for commodity preclinical services.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Analysis, literature review, and scientific writing automate; physical lab experimentation and instrument work do not. In 541715 context this biotech-proxy role is partially representative; 30% automatable fraction reflects writing/analysis slice.
Simulation, analysis, and embedded code writing are AI-augmented; classified defense SW and hardware-integration work are less automatable due to CMMC/security constraints and system-level judgment.
Proposal assembly, status reporting, and resource scheduling automate; program direction, sponsor relationship management, and technical judgment on novel R&D do not.
FAR Part 31 compliance documentation, cost reporting, and internal coordination automate; BD strategy, government contract negotiations, and program oversight do not.
Physical lab and instrument work; AI augments data logging and analysis but cannot replace hands-on specimen/sample handling.
Schedule/cost tracking, CDRLs, and status reporting automate heavily; government CO/COR interface and risk management judgment persist.
Data analysis, literature synthesis, and report writing automate; bench experimentation and instrument design do not. (Proxy artifact from 541714 weight; 541715 parallel = physicists and chemists.)
Simulation setup, technical reports, and drawing review automate; hardware prototyping, test execution, and on-site classified work do not.
Administrative oversight and reporting automate; program and personnel decisions requiring security-cleared context do not.
FAR/DFARS compliance paperwork, subcontract administration, and indirect rate documentation are heavily document-automatable tasks.

The 54171 proxy severely under-weights aerospace/electrical/materials/systems engineers who dominate 541715 revenue. Estimated ~20% of wage bill per prompt guidance (17-2141 listed at only 1.94%; actual 541715 reality is ~18-25%). AI automates simulation, technical writing, and drawing review; hardware and test execution persist. 30% automatable is the mid of 25-45% stated plausible range.

breakdown_by_role.role: Physical scientists unlisted tail (physicists, chemists, materials scientists)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Analysis and report writing automate; experimental and laboratory bench work does not. 8% of wage bill is low estimate for physical scientists in defense/energy R&D.

breakdown_by_role.role: Technicians and test/lab staff unlisted (engineering techs, machinists, field testers)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Physical prototype fabrication, test rig operation, instrumentation, and field trials are not automatable. AI assists in data logging and anomaly detection (small fraction).

breakdown_by_role.role: Admin, security, contracts, and remaining tail
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Document production, travel/expense administration, and contract tracking automate; facility security clearance personnel and classified-facility compliance staff (mandated human presence) do not. 13.15% is residual after all other tail allocations sum to 0.90 of total. 30% automatable fraction reflects partial-automation of mixed role.

pi_dist.rationale: Dominant sub-segment is federally funded applied R&D (~60-65% of revenue under CPFF/CPAF contracts with DCAA-audited indirect rates). Under cost-plus, AI efficiency reduces direct labor hours; DCAA-negotiated indirect rates must be rebased downward as AI becomes the audited norm; billings shrink, savings flow to government sponsor. Firm controls ~15% of pricing on CPFF work. Fixed-price SBIR Phase I/II and commercial NRE (~35-40% of revenue): contractor retains savings but client owns the intellectual property deliverable and contract vehicles are government-held. Weighted pi_dist: 0.60 x 0.15 + 0.40 x 0.55 = 0.09 + 0.22 = 0.31, rounded to 0.30. SBIR affiliation loss on acquisition further depresses the firm's pricing control.
sources: FAR Part 31 / DCAA audit guidance (cost-reimbursable contracting)
SBIR.gov eligibility guide (affiliation rules on acquisition)
CPFF cap guidance: 15% of estimated cost for R&D (10 USC 3322)

pi_moat.rationale: Structural barriers: facility security clearances (SECRET/TS/SCI) require 12-24 month investigation and cannot be transferred quickly; classified facilities (SCIFs) represent real capital barriers; IDIQ/SEAPORT contract vehicle incumbency creates recompete advantage; past-performance record is institutionally held and survives modest roll-ups. Erosion forces: SBIR/small-business set-aside revenue evaporates on large-firm acquisition (SBA affiliation rules — an acquired firm loses small-business eligibility); government sponsors can insource analysis/simulation work using AI directly; FAR cost-plus environment removes price moat by design. Net: moat is real but narrow and structurally threatened by acquisition mechanics.
sources: SBIR.gov affiliation and size standard documentation
SBA 13 CFR 121.702 size and eligibility for SBIR/STTR programs
FAR Subpart 9.4 debarment and suspension / clearance requirements

t50_years.derivation: Current adoption anchor: OECD (2025) reports >25% AI adoption in professional/scientific/technical activities; Census BTOS (narrower production definition) ~10%; BCG 2025 reports two-thirds of A&D AI efforts still in proof-of-concept, implying ~25-33% materially using AI in automatable functions for broader professional services but defense R&D firms trailing due to classified systems barriers, CMMC requirements, and conservative acquisition culture. Estimated current adoption: ~22%. Historical analogs: CAD adoption in engineering firms from commercial availability (~1982) to >50% penetration by mid-1990s = ~12-13 years; ERP in professional services from late 1990s to ~2010 = ~10-12 years. AI diffuses faster (SaaS, no hardware) but defense R&D has unique brakes (ITAR-controlled environments, classified networks, CMMC compliance, CUI handling requirements). Pace estimate: OECD measured 13pp gain 2021-2024 (3 years) = ~4.3pp/yr. From 22% to 50% = 28pp remaining at 4.3pp/yr = ~6.5 years. Blending pace-based (~6.5 yr) and analog-based (~10 yr midpoint after removing time already elapsed): t50 = 8.0 years from today (2026). Plausible range: 6-12 years.

current_adoption_pct.source: OECD AI Adoption by SMEs report 2025 + Census BTOS AI supplement Nov 2025 (professional/scientific sector) + BCG Three Truths About AI in Aerospace and Defense 2025
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: OECD 2025: AI use reached more than 25% for professional, scientific and technical activities sector; BCG 2025: two-thirds of A&D AI efforts still in proof-of-concept phase, implying ~33% at early deployment. Defense R&D specific adoption estimated below sector average due to classified systems constraints.

historical_analogs.value: CAD adoption in engineering firms: ~1982 commercial availability to mid-1990s >50% penetration = ~12-13 years. ERP in professional services: late 1990s rollout to ~50% penetration ~2010 = ~10-12 years.
historical_analogs.source: CAD adoption history: Scan2CAD historical overview; ERP adoption: industry knowledge base
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: AutoCAD released 1982; solid modeling widespread by mid-1990s. ERP professional services adoption reached critical mass ~2008-2012.

owners_60plus_pct.source: General small business owner demographics: McKinsey Boomers and Business Baton chart 2025; SBA FAQ 2024; Federal Reserve Small Business Credit Survey 2025 reference
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: More than half of U.S. small-business owners are over age 55; one in four (25%) being 65 or older. In 2025, record 4.18 million Americans turned 65. Baby Boomers make up ~40% of small business owners. 62% of owners have seen retirement timelines accelerate. NAICS 54 professional services firms lack sector-specific owner age data; 40% at 60+ is a conservative cross-sector estimate.

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: SBA/McKinsey baby boomer wave: 6 million SMBs expected to transfer by 2035; less than one-third have succession plans. Defense R&D sector-specific: security-cleared technical principals create acute succession bottleneck — clearances are personal, not transferable, and the cleared workforce pipeline is constrained. Less than a third of small business owners have a formal succession plan (McKinsey 2025).

active_consolidators.source: Janes Capital Top 5 Most Active A&D PE Buyers 2022-2024; PCE Companies A&D Q2 2025; Enlightenment Capital portfolio tracking
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: 137 PE-backed Government Services platforms active (PCE Q2 2025); 17 new platforms formed in H2 2024; top 5 A&D PE buyers did 10-17 add-ons each 2022-2024 (Arcline: 17, Arlington: 13, AE Industrial: 12, CORE: 12, Enlightenment: 10). Subset active specifically in engineering/applied R&D services (541715-type): estimated 8 platforms (Enlightenment Capital, Arlington Capital, Arcline, AE Industrial, CORE Industrial, Blu Epsilon, Veritas Capital, additional family office/growth equity).

buy_mult.source: First Page Sage Aerospace EBITDA Valuation Multiples 2025; ctacquisitions.com defense contractor sale guide 2026; Azure Summit acquisition comp (11.6x at $110M EBITDA scale)
buy_mult.figure_quoted: Military/Defense $1-3M EBITDA: 7.7x; IDIQ holders: 7-8.5x per ctacquisitions. SBIR-dependent small R&D firms receive discount vs. prime incumbents due to affiliation risk on acquisition (size-standard loss) and cost-plus revenue structure. Estimated buy multiple for SBIR/cost-plus R&D shop with $1-10M EBITDA: 6.5x (normalized post-owner-comp EBITDA basis).

exit_mult.source: First Page Sage Aerospace EBITDA Valuation Multiples 2025; Objective IBV A&D Q3 2025 report; PCE Companies A&D Q2 2025
exit_mult.figure_quoted: Military/Defense $5-15M EBITDA: 14.7x per First Page Sage; government services public-market TEV/EBITDA 11.4-13.6x (Objective IBV Q3 2025); PCE Q2 2025 median EV/EBITDA 18.96x (broad sector, includes product cos). Defense R&D services platforms (services-heavy, cost-plus mix) trade at discount to hardware/product peers; 12.0x is MED estimate for $10M+ EBITDA government R&D services platform.

pricing_structure.value: Dominant: cost-plus-fixed-fee (CPFF) federal contracts ~60-65% of revenue; fixed-price SBIR Phase I/II ~15-20%; commercial and firm-fixed-price NRE ~15-20%. Cost-plus: DCAA audits indirect and direct rates; AI efficiency reduces billable hours and must be reflected in forward-pricing rate proposals, passing savings to government. SBIR fixed-price: contractor retains efficiency gains but awards are capped (~$275K Phase I, ~$1.8M Phase II) and non-scalable. Commercial NRE: contractor retains margin but small share of total.
pricing_structure.source: FAR Part 31 CPFF structure; SBIR.gov award amounts; DCAA indirect rate audit guidance; GovDash CPFF contracts guide
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: CPFF statutory cap: 15% of estimated cost for R&D per 10 USC 3322. SBIR Phase I cap ~$275K, Phase II ~$1.8M per SBA. DCAA FY24: examined $599.8B in contract costs.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: V_raw = 0.11226 → EBITDA uplift = 11.23 pp. A = 1.25 (t50 = 8 years, slow adoption). Uplift of 11.23 pp is well above the 3 pp threshold and is consistent with A < 5 — the tension check does not apply here since A is below 5. No inconsistency: the modest A reflects slow adoption in the classified defense R&D environment; the moderate V reflects real but partially captured AI automation opportunity.

terminal_value.justification: The dominant sub-segment (federally funded applied R&D, especially defense/energy/space) faces two distinct mechanisms: (1) Physical test infrastructure, security clearances, classified facilities, and SBIR set-asides protect slices of demand that AI cannot replace directly — these are DURABLE. (2) However, analysis, simulation, documentation, and report-writing hours — which make up the largest share of cost-plus billings — are being automated, and under CPFF contracts DCAA-audited rates must reflect declining AI-assisted labor productivity, shrinking the cost base and thus billable revenue even without losing any contracts. AI cuts the operator's costs, but under cost-plus, cost-cutting IS revenue shrinkage. Additionally, government sponsors could insource analytical/simulation work using AI, leaving only physical test and classified-facility work outsourced. Overall: demand survives but shrinks materially in the analysis/simulation slice — PRESSURED.
terminal_value.class_note: PRESSURED rather than MELTING because physical test, clearance-dependent work, and statutory set-asides create a durable demand floor; the product is not fully replaceable.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V was derived from role-by-role automatable fraction analysis grounded in the nature of tasks (physical vs. analytical, classified vs. open), not from any deal activity or consolidation trends. A was derived from current AI adoption data (Census BTOS, OECD) and historical technology diffusion analogs (CAD, ERP), with no reference to M&A activity.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Billing mix: ~60-65% CPFF federal contracts, ~15-20% fixed-price SBIR, ~15-20% commercial/FFP NRE. Under CPFF, AI efficiency flows directly to government sponsor: reduced direct labor hours lower DCAA-allowable costs, which lowers billings under the cost-reimbursable model. Forward pricing rate proposals must reflect AI-enabled productivity; contractors who resist lose audit challenges. This is modeled in pi_dist = 0.30 (below 0.5 midpoint): the firm retains pricing control only on the ~35-40% of fixed-price work. pi_moat = 0.45 reflects that clearances and contract vehicles create switching-cost barriers but the cost-plus mechanism itself is the primary moat-eroder for the dominant revenue slice.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: At t50=6: A=1.667, S≈3.14; at t50=12: A=0.833, S≈2.58. Range of ~0.56 in S depending on adoption speed. Key uncertainty is whether CMMC/classified-systems constraints delay adoption significantly beyond the professional-services average.
At pi_dist=0.20: C=0.90, S≈2.62; at pi_dist=0.40: C=1.80, S≈3.13. Hinges on actual share of cost-plus vs. fixed-price revenue and whether the DCAA deflation mechanism is as complete as modeled.
At buy=5.0: M=5.60, S≈3.36; at buy=8.0: M=2.00, S≈2.47. Small-firm SBIR-discount magnitude is estimated; affiliation-risk discount may be larger than modeled, pushing buy_mult lower (and M higher).
At 0.30: OW_raw=0.571, OW_with_succession=0.671, B≈7.07, S≈2.79; at 0.55: OW capped at 0.9+0.1=1.0, B≈7.70, S≈2.88. Modest sensitivity because OW is already near cap with succession bonus.

evidence.reviewer_note: HETEROGENEOUS CODE: 541715 spans (1) federally funded applied R&D — defense/energy/space/intelligence, including SBIR-dependent shops (dominant by revenue, estimated 55-65%); (2) commercial contract product-development and engineering-research firms serving industry (est. 20-25%); (3) agricultural, materials, and grant-funded ventures (est. 10-15%). This run scores the DOMINANT sub-segment: federally funded applied R&D. ROLE-MIX CAVEAT: The provided 54171 OEWS proxy severely over-weights biotech occupations (medical scientists, biological technicians, biochemists) which are 541714 artifacts. True 541715 role mix is dominated by aerospace, electrical, mechanical, and systems engineers in the unlisted tail. The breakdown_by_role includes four explicit unlisted-tail segments (engineers ~20%, physical scientists ~8%, technicians ~11%, admin/other ~13.15%) to correct this. Total labeled roles account for 90% of wage bill (10% unaccounted — assumed distributed across existing unlisted categories). SBIR TRAP: A roll-up acquiring five SBIR-dependent shops triggers SBA affiliation recertification; combined firm likely exceeds 500-employee threshold, destroying SBIR eligibility on all acquired revenue. buy_mult reflects this with a discount vs. non-SBIR defense contractors. PRICING DEFLATION: Cost-plus structure is the central value-capture trap — modeled explicitly in pi_dist = 0.30.

labor_share.method: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541720 ($6,516,505,720) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541720 ($9,647,627,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).
sources: BLS QCEW 2024 annual averages (US000, private)
BLS ECEC news release (raw/ecec.nr0.htm)
Census SUSB 2022 (receipts by NAICS)
