n_band.derivation: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $201,227 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541720); class <5: 1,062 firms, avg revenue/firm $375,004 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $58,688 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 244 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,139,684 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $178,361 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 184 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,368,761 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $370,711 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 196 firms, avg revenue/firm $7,014,362 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,097,748 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 62 firms, avg revenue/firm $35,454,306 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,548,599 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 39 firms, avg revenue/firm $127,242,462 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $19,913,445 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 258
n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Literature reviews, survey instrument drafting, qualitative coding, statistical analysis, and report writing are heavily AI-exposed — AI tools directly replace the core tasks. Study design judgment, IRB navigation, and sponsor-facing interpretation persist. 40% automatable is mid of 30-50% range.

Contract administration, compliance reporting, and internal coordination automate; federal CO/COR interface, proposal leadership, and program direction do not. 11.6% wage share is unusually high — this is the dominant management layer in both federal evaluation and CRM firms.

Data entry, document production, filing, scheduling — directly and substantially automatable. This clerical-heavy mix (unusual vs. other R&D codes per prompt) is especially exposed. 65% is conservative given full-automation potential of most clerical tasks.
Statistical analysis, data synthesis, and research writing automate substantially; original study design, policy interpretation, and human-subjects expertise do not.

breakdown_by_role.role: Mental Health and Substance Abuse Social Workers (21-1023)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Human-delivered relational services; documentation tasks (case notes, compliance reporting) marginally automate but the core service is not automatable.
Schedule tracking, status reporting, deliverable tracking automate; sponsor interface and risk management judgment persist.

Section 106 NHPA field surveys, shovel testing, and excavation are statutory physical fieldwork — not automatable. Report writing (Phase I/II reports) automates partially. 15% captures only the documentation slice.
Grant/contract compliance, NICRA rate submissions, budget tracking automate substantially.
Scheduling, correspondence, document management — largely automatable.

breakdown_by_role.role: Behavioral Disorder and Mental Health Counselors (21-1018)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Program delivery is relational; documentation marginally automates. These roles signal research orgs that also deliver services, as noted in prompt.

breakdown_by_role.role: Statisticians and data scientists unlisted tail
breakdown_by_role.rationale: A substantial unlisted tail of statisticians, data analysts, and quantitative researchers. Model specification, quality assurance, and novel methodological design persist; data wrangling, standard analysis pipelines, and report generation automate heavily. Estimated 45% automatable.

breakdown_by_role.role: Survey interviewers and field staff unlisted tail
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Phone and web-based interviewing automates substantially (AI-driven survey tools, synthetic respondent pilots active 2024-2025); in-person interviewing, ethnographic fieldwork, and enumeration require physical presence and persist. 25% reflects partial automation of the mix.

breakdown_by_role.role: IT and facilities staff unlisted tail
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Routine IT administration automates; specialized secure-data handling and facilities management persist.

Heterogeneous residual: includes outreach/engagement staff, translation, lab/data collection support, and mixed professional functions. 25% automatable is a conservative blended estimate across this diverse group.

pi_dist.rationale: Dominant sub-segment is federally funded evaluation/survey research (~50-55% of revenue). Federal cost-reimbursable contracts (T&M and cost-plus with NICRA-audited indirect rates): AI-driven hour reductions lower billable direct labor, which lowers billings under NICRA-based pricing — savings flow to government sponsor. Federal clients (USAID, HHS, ED, NSF) also control contract vehicles, IDIQs, and task order awards; firm relationship is institutional but gated by agency-maintained vehicles. CRM archaeology (~25-30% of revenue): fixed-price per project, firm retains efficiency; but agency-driven demand (Section 106) and low switching costs reduce pricing power. Weighted: 0.52 x 0.15 (federal cost-reimb slice; firm controls almost none of pricing) + 0.28 x 0.45 (CRM fixed-price; firm captures savings) + 0.20 x 0.30 (behavioral health programs; grant-funded, limited leverage) = 0.078 + 0.126 + 0.060 = 0.264, rounded to 0.25.
sources: NICRA indirect cost rate negotiation mechanics (OMB 2 CFR Part 200, Subpart E)
AAPOR 2025 conference coverage on AI in survey research
PSC federal market outlooks on cost-reimbursable evaluation contracts

pi_moat.rationale: Barriers: federal contract vehicles (IDIQs, task orders) create incumbency advantage; CRM Section 106 requires Secretary of Interior-qualified archaeologists (credential barrier); federal past-performance record is institutionally sticky. Erosion forces: AI can automate the core analytical product (literature synthesis, data analysis, report writing — which is what sponsors are paying for in evaluation contracts); synthetic respondents and AI-driven survey tools threaten the data-collection product line; 2025 federal contract terminations (DOGE cuts $881M in IES contracts, $695M in HHS de-obligations) demonstrate non-stickiness of demand; government sponsors have insourced evaluation with AI directly. CRM has stronger moat (physical fieldwork mandate) but smaller revenue share. Net: moderate structural barriers partially offset by high product-replaceability risk.
sources: DOGE/IES contract termination news (Chalkbeat, EdWeek, Feb-Apr 2025)
AAPOR 2025 on synthetic respondents and AI survey tools
Section 106 NHPA qualification requirements (Secretary of Interior Professional Qualification Standards)

t50_years.derivation: Current adoption anchor: AAPOR/survey research community shows rapid uptake — LLM use in research methodology papers jumped from 1.6% (2022) to 59% (2024). For firm-level material AI use in automatable functions: broader professional/scientific sector is ~25-34% (OECD 2025). Social science researchers are arguably ahead of the sector average given text-heavy work. Estimated current adoption: ~25%. Historical analogs: CAPI (computer-assisted personal interviewing) adoption in survey research: early 1990s to ~2000 for majority adoption = ~10 years; statistical software (SPSS/Stata) in social science: ~12 years from commercial release (late 1970s) to 50%+ penetration (~1990). AI is diffusing faster (SaaS, no hardware, API access). From 25% to 50% at ~5pp/yr pace (faster than defense due to no classified constraints): ~5 years. Blending analog (~10 year total, ~7 remaining) and pace-based (~5 years remaining): t50 = 7.0 years. Note: federal evaluation demand shock (DOGE cuts 2025) creates near-term pressure that could accelerate AI adoption as firms seek productivity to compete on lower-cost contracts.

current_adoption_pct.source: AAPOR Responsible AI Integration in Survey Research (2026 report); OECD AI Adoption by SMEs 2025; Lumivero State of AI in Qualitative Research 2025
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: AAPOR 2025 conference and 2026 report document active adoption of AI tools in survey research. OECD 2025: >25% of professional/scientific activities sector uses AI. Lumivero 2025: LLM adoption in qualitative research papers from 1.6% (2022) to 59% (2024). Material firm-level use in automatable functions estimated at ~25%.

historical_analogs.value: CAPI adoption in survey research: early 1990s introduction to ~50%+ by ~2000 = ~10 years. SPSS/Stata in social science: commercial release ~1968-1975 to majority adoption ~1988-1990 = ~12-15 years. AI diffuses faster.
historical_analogs.source: General survey methodology history and statistical software adoption; academic literature on CAPI adoption curves
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: CAPI became standard in major federal surveys (NLSY, CPS) by late 1990s; SPSS adoption in social sciences was widespread by late 1980s. AI adoption expected to be significantly faster given SaaS model and no hardware barrier.

owners_60plus_pct.source: General small business demographics: McKinsey Boomers and Business Baton 2025; SBA Office of Advocacy 2024 FAQ; silver tsunami research. CRM/archaeology-specific: ACRA member firm demographics (no published survey found — estimate based on academic-origin ownership patterns).
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: More than half of US small business owners are over age 55; 25% are 65+. CRM archaeology firms are disproportionately founded by academic-trained principals (PhDs in archaeology/anthropology) who tend to found firms in mid-career; firms founded 1990s-2000s now have founders in 60s-70s. Estimate 42% at 60+ slightly above broad average.

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: Baby boomer succession wave broadly documented (McKinsey 2025: <1/3 of SB owners have succession plans; 6M SMBs to transfer by 2035). CRM-specific: ACRA member firms are largely founder-owned small businesses with specialist principal investigators; archaeology PhD pipeline is narrow; PaleoWest/Riverside roll-up (2021 onward) documents active demand from PE for exactly this succession gap. No sector-specific ACRA survey found in public sources — succession shortage inferred from PE roll-up activity and academic-origin ownership patterns.

active_consolidators.source: Riverside Company / PaleoWest CRM platform (confirmed 2021 platform + 2 add-ons: Commonwealth Heritage Group 2022, SEAS 2022-2023); broader social science research sector has minimal PE consolidation due to nonprofit dominance (RTI, Westat, NORC, Mathematica all employee-owned or nonprofit).
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: Riverside invested in PaleoWest (end of 2021) and made two add-on acquisitions (Commonwealth Heritage Group Sept 2022; SEAS). 2025 DOGE federal contract cuts have cooled interest in federal evaluation platforms. Estimated 2 active consolidators: Riverside/PaleoWest (CRM) plus one additional environmental/consulting platform doing CRM add-ons.

buy_mult.source: Environmental services small firm multiples (RL Hulett Q4 2025, ctacquisitions.com 2026); CRM firm acquisitions not publicly disclosed with multiples; blended estimate for social science/CRM small firm add-ons.
buy_mult.figure_quoted: Environmental services small firm (project remediation-heavy): 5-7x EBITDA. Federal evaluation small for-profit firms: similar range, likely 5-6x due to cost-reimbursable pricing structure and 2025 demand shock from DOGE cuts. Blended buy multiple estimated 5.5x on post-owner-comp normalized EBITDA basis.

exit_mult.source: Environmental services strategic deal multiples (median 20.9x for permit-holding platforms in Q4 2025; services-heavy firms lower); professional services consulting firm exit comps; government services platform exits.
exit_mult.figure_quoted: Environmental services EBITDA 5-13x for sub-$10M, 8-13x for $20M+ per ctacquisitions 2026; strategic deal median 20.9x (permit-heavy TSDF/hazmat — not comparable to CRM/social science). Professional services consulting exit multiples: 7-12x. For a $10M+ EBITDA social science research or CRM platform: estimated 10x (below environmental premium, above basic consulting, reflecting contract vehicle incumbency and statutory demand).

pricing_structure.value: Dominant sub-segment (federal evaluation/survey, ~50-55% of revenue): T&M and cost-reimbursable with NICRA-audited indirect rates — AI efficiency reduces direct labor hours and NICRA rates must be renegotiated downward, passing savings to government. CRM archaeology (~25-30%): fixed-price per project (Phase I/II survey, Phase III excavation) based on scope/acreage — firm retains efficiency gains. Behavioral-health research/programs (~15-20%): grant-funded T&M or fixed-price, largely pass-through efficiency.
pricing_structure.source: NICRA mechanics (OMB 2 CFR Part 200 Subpart E); CRM industry billing practice (ACRA, standard project-based pricing); federal evaluation contract structure (PSC, AAPOR commentary).
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: NICRA rate proposal submitted annually; indirect rates adjusted based on actual costs. Fixed-price CRM projects priced per archaeological unit/acre with profit margin captured by firm if AI reduces field and lab time.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: V_raw = 0.21764 → EBITDA uplift = 21.76 pp. A = 1.429 (t50 = 7 years). Uplift of 21.76 pp with A < 5 — no cross-check tension (tension check fires only when uplift < 3 pp while A ≥ 5; this has high uplift and low A, which is consistent: the high labor share of 72% means substantial EBITDA leverage even from partial automation, but slow adoption means the value capture is deferred). The combination of high V and low A is coherent for a labor-intensive sector with structural adoption barriers.

terminal_value.justification: Two distinct mechanisms govern terminal value and must not be blurred: (1) Statutory fieldwork mechanism (CRM archaeology): NHPA Section 106 and NEPA compliance require physical archaeological survey by Secretary of Interior-qualified professionals — AI cannot replace the shovel-testing, field documentation, and artifact analysis requiring physical presence and credentialed judgment. This demand is DURABLE as long as Section 106 regulations hold (deregulation risk exists but not imminent). (2) Sponsored research mechanism (federal/foundation evaluation and survey): AI can automate literature synthesis, data analysis, and report writing — which is precisely what federal sponsors are buying. Synthetic respondents, AI-driven survey tools, and automated evaluation pipelines threaten the product itself. The 2025 DOGE wave (IES: $881M terminated; HHS: $695M de-obligated) has already demonstrated that the demand base is neither sticky nor growing — and AI insourcing by sponsor agencies could accelerate this. Overall classification: PRESSURED. The statutory CRM slice creates a durable floor; the dominant evaluation/survey slice faces demand compression as both AI product-replacement and political/budgetary demand reduction operate simultaneously. Not MELTING because the CRM statutory demand floor is real and large relative to n_band; not DURABLE because the evaluation/survey product is directly replaceable and the 2025 demand shock is empirical, not hypothetical.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V was derived from task-by-task analysis of role automatable fractions: clerical tasks (Office Clerks, Secretaries), data analysis and coding (Social Science Research Assistants, Statisticians), and document production are the drivers — none of this analysis relied on M&A activity or deal trends. A was derived from current AI adoption data (AAPOR, OECD, Lumivero) and historical technology diffusion analogs (CAPI, statistical software), with zero reference to consolidation activity.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Federal evaluation/survey (~52% of revenue): T&M and cost-reimbursable with NICRA-audited indirect rates. AI efficiency reduces direct labor hours; NICRA rates are renegotiated annually and must reflect actual cost experience — AI-enabled productivity gains reduce the indirect rate base, lowering billings. The contractor cannot hold AI savings under this structure: they flow to the government sponsor through rate reduction. Modeled in pi_dist = 0.25: far below the no-deflation default, reflecting that the dominant revenue slice (federal cost-reimbursable) has near-complete AI savings deflation, partially offset by CRM fixed-price work where firm captures gains. pi_moat = 0.40 reflects structural demand protection (Section 106 statutory fieldwork, federal IDIQ incumbency) against the deflation and product-replacement pressures.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: At t50=5: A=2.00, S≈3.47; at t50=10: A=1.00, S≈2.82. Range of ~0.65 in S. Key uncertainty: whether the DOGE-driven demand shock accelerates adoption (firms cut costs via AI to compete on lower-price contracts) or depresses it (firms exit market, reducing active adopters).

At ars=0.24: V_raw=0.1728, V=6.91, S≈2.90; at ars=0.37: V_raw=0.2664, V=10.0, S≈3.36. The statisticians/data scientists unlisted tail (0.15 wage share × 0.45 fraction) and miscellaneous remaining tail (0.2952 × 0.25) are the largest uncertain components.

At pi_dist=0.18: C=0.72, S≈2.88; at pi_dist=0.35: C=1.40, S≈3.30. Hinges on actual share of cost-reimbursable vs. fixed-price revenue and severity of NICRA deflation mechanism for the federal evaluation slice.

At buy=4.5: M=4.89, S≈3.44; at buy=7.0: M=1.71, S≈2.76. The CRM-specific and federal evaluation small-firm buy multiples are not well-documented in public sources; the 2025 demand shock may have depressed valuations below pre-2025 levels.

evidence.reviewer_note: HETEROGENEOUS CODE: 541720 spans three distinct sub-segments: (1) Federal/foundation survey and program-evaluation contractors (~50-55% of revenue) — the largest segment by revenue, but dominated by nonprofits/employee-owned organizations (RTI, NORC, Westat, Mathematica) that are not acquirable; the acquirable for-profit tail is small and was hit hard by the 2025 DOGE federal contract terminations ($881M in IES contracts, $695M in HHS de-obligations). (2) CRM archaeology firms (~25-30% of revenue) — the most roll-up-shaped sub-segment; Section 106 demand is statutory and durable; Riverside/PaleoWest is the active platform. (3) Research organizations delivering behavioral-health programs (~15-20%) — relational service delivery, minimal acquisition activity. This run scores the DOMINANT sub-segment (federal evaluation/survey research) while noting that CRM archaeology would produce higher scores (stronger moat via statutory demand, fixed-price billing, active PE consolidator). NONPROFIT/EMPLOYEE-OWNED CONTAMINATION: n_band = 258 understates the distortion; the $1-10M EBITDA acquirable universe is dominated by for-profit tail firms, not the large nonprofits that shape industry revenue statistics. DEMAND SHOCK: 2025 DOGE cuts are empirical and material; pre-2025 federal evaluation revenue baselines overstate sustainable demand for the dominant sub-segment. PRICING DEFLATION: NICRA indirect rates are the primary mechanism; explicitly modeled in pi_dist = 0.25. AI PRODUCT-REPLACEMENT RISK: For federal evaluation, AI does not just cut operator costs — it can replace the deliverable (automated literature synthesis, AI-driven program evaluation, synthetic respondents). This is the terminal-value mechanism distinguishing this code from pure cost-reduction scenarios.

labor_share.method: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) direct: labor compensation (L02) $33,005.0M / sectoral output (T30) $68,557.8M, year 2022
sources: BLS Industry Productivity and Costs flat files (ip.data.1.AllData), measures L02 and T30

occupations.title: Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists
Sales Representatives of Services, Except Advertising, Insurance, Financial Services, and Travel

n_band.derivation: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $303,923 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541810); class <5: 9,620 firms, avg revenue/firm $521,704 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $81,647 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 1,690 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,849,936 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $289,515 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 1,101 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,849,742 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $602,485 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 977 firms, avg revenue/firm $12,049,911 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,885,811 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 198 firms, avg revenue/firm $48,263,556 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $7,553,246 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 96 firms, avg revenue/firm $267,062,250 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $41,795,242 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 1,175
n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%

breakdown_by_role.role: Creative staff (design, art direction, copy, production)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Production design, asset versioning, copy drafting and image generation are directly generative-AI-exposed. Creative direction, brand judgment and client sell-through persist. Prompt guidance uses 0.4-0.6 range for production tiers; 0.50 is central estimate. Excludes contaminant OEWS occupations (41-9011 Demonstrators, 27-1026 Merchandise Displayers); uses adjusted agency structure per prompt guidance.

Status decks, briefs, contact reports and coordination automate substantially. Client relationship ownership is the core agency asset and does not automate. Fraction 0.35 reflects that the majority of account management hours are relationship-facing or judgment-laden.

Research synthesis, media planning models, campaign reporting and optimization are heavily AI-exposed. Platforms automate the buying side natively (Google PMax, Meta Advantage+). Senior strategist judgment and media negotiation persist. 0.45 reflects that analytical execution is more automatable than the senior strategy and negotiation layer.

Client and pitch leadership persists; internal reporting, resourcing and revenue management automate. Management's primary value is client trust and firm direction, which is not automatable. Low fraction 0.20 reflects that most management hours are client-facing or involve judgment calls.

Pitch decks, RFP responses and capability presentations automate heavily. Rainmaking and relationship-driven new business do not. Adjusted for contamination from sibling codes (media rep sales agents) as noted in prompt guidance; true agency new-business staff is estimated at 6-8% of wage bill.

Content production, press materials and social drafts automate; media relationships and strategic communications judgment persist. Adjusted for contamination: OEWS shows 27-3031 PR Specialists at 8.3% wage share but this reflects sibling code 541820 contamination; within true integrated agencies this function is estimated at 4-6% of wage bill.

breakdown_by_role.role: Back office, admin and remaining tail
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Billing reconciliation (client-cost reconciliation is notoriously manual), traffic management, timesheet administration and production admin are among the most automatable cost lines. 0.60 reflects high task routinization. The 4-digit OEWS top-10 covers only ~51% of the wage bill; remaining occupations are predominantly administrative and back-office.

pi_dist.rationale: Agency revenue is ~75% fixed-fee/retainer with labor-hour scoping (4A's/ANA 2024). Client procurement departments audit staffing plans and rate cards; when AI cuts hours behind a scope, procurement-driven renegotiations historically convert efficiency into lower fees rather than agency margin — the industry already experienced this with the procurement-compression wave of the 2010s. ANA (2023) shows 82% of client organizations now have in-house agencies handling 61% of work, structurally limiting the small independent's pricing power. Small agencies lack AOR contract stability; account movement data shows high concentration risk at one or two anchor clients. Pi_dist is set low at 0.30 to reflect that the firm captures only a minority of AI productivity gains — most flows to clients via scope renegotiation or is competed away in pitches.
sources: 4A's/ANA 'Decoding Compensation Models' report, March 2024 — 75% of plans are fee-based
ANA In-House Agency Report 2023 — 82% of brands have in-house agencies, handling 61% of work on average

pi_moat.rationale: No licensure protects advertising. Switching costs are low for SMB clients — most small agencies have month-to-month or annual retainers. Proprietary data moats are available only at holding-company scale. The realistic bypass threat is real: clients can combine AI creative tools (Midjourney, Adobe Firefly, ChatGPT) with Google/Meta self-serve platforms and an in-house coordinator. At the independent agency tier serving the roll-up universe ($1-10M EBITDA), the moat is primarily personal relationships and execution quality — both portable and fragile. Platform disintermediation of media buying (PMax, Advantage+) directly competes with a core agency service. Moat is modestly above floor (0.30 vs 0.10) only because small-agency client relationships, integrated campaign management and the operator's local market knowledge provide some stickiness.
sources: ANA In-House Agency Study 2023 — structural in-housing trend at 82%
StackAdapt/Ascend2 2025 — AI integration accelerating among agencies

t50_years.derivation: Current adoption: ~39% of agencies have significantly integrated AI into day-to-day workflows (StackAdapt/Ascend2 2025); 91% use AI in some form but material integration is defined as AI embedded in core deliverable workflows (copy, creative, analytics), which StackAdapt estimates at 39%. Historical analog: programmatic advertising adoption went from ~4% to ~68% of display spending in roughly 10 years (2010-2020), implying ~6-7 years to reach 50%; however AI tool accessibility (no infrastructure buildout required) and competitive pressure are accelerating this cycle. From 39% today (mid-2026) to 50% requires closing an 11-percentage-point gap. Given the trajectory is accelerating (AI tools became widely accessible in 2023-2024 and adoption surged rapidly to 39%), and given competitive pressure means agencies adopting AI outcompete those that don't, the gap closes in approximately 2-3 years. Central estimate: 2.5 years (reaching 50% material adoption by end of 2028).

current_adoption_pct.source: StackAdapt and Ascend2 State of AI in Advertising Agencies 2025
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: only 39% of agencies have significantly integrated AI into their day-to-day workflows

historical_analogs.value: 6-10 years to reach 50% on comparable prior workflow technologies
historical_analogs.source: Multiple: programmatic advertising IAB/eMarketer data; cloud SaaS agency adoption
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: Programmatic advertising grew from ~4% of display to ~68% over roughly a decade (2010-2020); 68% of agency budgets being allocated programmatically by 2024

owners_60plus_pct.source: US Census Bureau Business Owner Characteristics data (2025 release); SBA small business profiles; Oberlo analysis of SBO survey
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: Baby Boomers (currently 55-73) form 37% of small business owners; share of self-employed age 65+ increased from 13.0% to 16.3% over 2013-2023. Age 60+ estimated at approximately 25% of small business owners (interpolated: 60+ is between the 55+ Boomer cohort at 37% and the 65+ self-employed cohort at 16.3%).

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: No advertising-agency-specific succession shortage has been formally documented by 4A's, ANA, or an industry trade source. The general small-business succession gap (Exit Planning Institute 2025: only 13% of Baby Boomer owners have a written exit plan) applies but is not advertising-specific. Marking false: no trade-association citation specific to 541810.

active_consolidators.source: Ad Age, AdWeek, InvestorIdeas.com, PE Hub — named platform companies actively making add-on acquisitions in advertising agencies in the 24 months through mid-2026
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: Named PE-backed platforms with documented advertising-agency add-ons 2024-2026: Mountaingate Capital/Ignite Visibility, Truelink Capital/R/GA, Gemspring Capital/ROR Partners, Shamrock Capital/Penta Group, New Mountain Capital/W2O, plus multiple others documented by JEGI CLARITY as active; estimated 7 active PE-backed consolidator platforms with documented add-on activity in the 24-month window.

buy_mult.source: Agencies.co 'Definitive Guide to Marketing Agency Valuation 2026'; First Page Sage Marketing Agency EBITDA Multiples 2025
buy_mult.figure_quoted: Small agencies under $3M EBITDA: 4.5x-5.5x range, average 5.0x. First Page Sage: advertising agencies $1-5M EBITDA at 5.5x-7.6x (higher end includes digital-first specialty shops); median for plain-vanilla small independent closer to 5.0x after EBITDA normalization for owner comp.

exit_mult.source: Agencies.co 'Definitive Guide to Marketing Agency Valuation 2026'; First Page Sage Marketing Agency EBITDA Multiples 2025
exit_mult.figure_quoted: Large agencies $10M+ EBITDA: 8.1x-10.6x range, average 9.3x. JEGI CLARITY noted median of 16.5x for agencies with EBITDA over £12.5M in the UK market, but US small-to-mid-market platform exits trade significantly lower. Using 9.3x as the US mid-market platform exit comp.

pricing_structure.value: ~75% fixed-fee/retainer (labor-hour-scoped), ~15% project/fixed-price, ~10% performance/commission; retainers audited by client procurement
pricing_structure.source: 4A's/ANA 'Decoding Compensation Models and Implementing the Right Model' report, March 2024; ANA survey on fee dominance
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: Fixed Fee emerged as the most utilized compensation model; seventy-five percent of all compensation plans using the fee-based model; retainers are scoped against staff-hour plans and rate cards

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: uplift_pp = 21.0pp which substantially exceeds the 3pp threshold. A = 4.0 which is below 5.0, so the tension check is not triggered. Consistent: high AI value potential (V=8.4) with moderate adoption speed (A=4.0) reflects that agencies are adopting meaningfully but the technology has a 2-3 year window to reach majority adoption. No inconsistency.

terminal_value.justification: Demand for advertising agency services survives mature AI but shrinks in scope. AI handles creative production, copy generation, media planning models and campaign reporting — the volume-based execution work that justified large retainers. What persists is brand stewardship, client relationship management, strategic judgment and cross-platform orchestration. The ANA in-housing trend (82% of brands with in-house agencies by 2023, handling 61% of work) is a structural demand headwind independent of AI. Google PMax and Meta Advantage+ automate media buying natively. The agency product is being restructured: not replaced entirely (executive trust in brand crises, complex multi-market campaigns, creative concepts still need human judgment) but compressed (fewer billable hours per outcome). This is PRESSURED rather than MELTING because a senior-counsel model survives, but PRESSURED rather than DURABLE because the volume underpinning today's retainer economics erodes. Evidence: headcount-per-revenue trends in the industry show declining ratios; account in-housing rates rising; programmatic disintermediation of media buying is already complete at scale.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V was derived from role-by-role assessment of which specific tasks current AI can substantially perform (copy drafting, asset generation, media analytics, admin automation) — no reference to M&A activity or deal count. A was derived from a named survey of current agency AI integration rates (StackAdapt/Ascend2 39% materially integrated) and historical programmatic adoption analog — no reference to consolidation trends.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: ~75% of agency revenue is fixed-fee retainer scoped against labor-hour plans (4A's/ANA 2024). When AI cuts the hours behind a scope, the mechanism is: (1) in the near term, agencies may recover chronic over-service (Gould+Partners data for PR shows this pattern holds in adjacent code 541820); (2) at retainer renewal, client procurement departments — which already audit staffing plans and benchmark rate cards — renegotiate the scope down. This is historically documented: procurement-driven fee compression in the 2010s directly extracted efficiency gains from agencies. The deflation mechanism is reflected in pi_dist = 0.30: the firm captures roughly 30% of client-relationship economics, with the remainder captured by clients (via fee renegotiation) or competed away (in pitch cycles). pi_moat = 0.30 captures the additional risk that clients bypass agencies entirely via AI tools plus in-house junior coordinators — a realistic substitution at the SMB tier.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A = 10/t50: at t50=1.5 A=6.67 vs t50=4.0 A=2.50. S shifts from ~3.9 to ~3.1 across this range — approximately 0.8 S-point swing, potentially affecting verdict bucket.

C = 10 × pi_dist × pi_moat. At pi_dist=0.20 C=0.60; at pi_dist=0.40 C=1.20. Combined with pi_moat range, C could range from 0.40 to 1.60 — the C factor is the largest uncertainty in absolute S impact, spanning roughly 0.5 S-point.
V_raw = 0.4814 × ARS: at ARS=0.35 V_raw=0.1685 V=6.74; at ARS=0.52 V_raw=0.250 V=10.0. S shifts approximately 0.3 points across this range.

evidence.reviewer_note: Role-mix contamination handled per prompt guidance: the 4-digit 5418 OEWS proxy includes Demonstrators and Product Promoters (41-9011, 1.6% wage share) and Merchandise Displayers (27-1026, 1.2% wage share) which are NOT advertising agency occupations (they originate from sibling codes 541850/541890). Similarly, 27-3031 PR Specialists (8.3% wage share) is substantially contaminated from 541820. The breakdown_by_role uses an adjusted agency structure with seven function buckets summing to 100% of wage bill, as specified in prompt guidance. Raw OEWS occupations were not used directly as role fractions. EBITDA normalization: buy_mult and exit_mult are sourced from actual transaction data at agency.co/FirstPageSage which report EV/EBITDA on adjusted EBITDA (after market-rate owner comp) basis per their methodology. The prompt notes the Damodaran margin (15.65%) is used for n_band derivation and was not recalculated. Boundary note: the roll-up-relevant population is the 977-firm 20-99-employee class plus part of the 100-499-employee class (n_band=1,175 per dataset); holding-company (WPP, Omnicom/IPG) data was not used for B or M inputs.

labor_share.method: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541820 ($9,020,215,177) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541820 ($16,903,608,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).
sources: BLS QCEW 2024 annual averages (US000, private)
BLS ECEC news release (raw/ecec.nr0.htm)
Census SUSB 2022 (receipts by NAICS)

occupations.title: Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists
Sales Representatives of Services, Except Advertising, Insurance, Financial Services, and Travel

n_band.derivation: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $271,021 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541820); class <5: 6,209 firms, avg revenue/firm $468,889 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $73,381 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 927 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,829,861 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $286,373 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 551 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,315,470 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $518,871 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 348 firms, avg revenue/firm $10,294,121 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,611,030 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 73 firms, avg revenue/firm $38,745,397 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,063,655 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 49 firms, avg revenue/firm $82,824,510 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $12,962,036 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 421
n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%

breakdown_by_role.role: PR practitioners (account staff, media relations, content)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Press releases, pitch drafts, media lists, coverage monitoring and reporting, bylines and social content are heavily AI-exposed — PR Pros survey (PRovoke 2025) shows 58% of AI users already use it for press release generation, 54% for pitching. Journalist relationships, crisis counsel and executive trust do not automate. The 4-digit OEWS proxy shows 27-3031 PR Specialists at 8.3% wage share, but within true PR agencies this function including all account tiers is estimated at ~30-40% of wage bill (prompt guidance). Using 35% central estimate. Fraction 0.45 reflects that production tasks (releases, pitches, content) are automatable but client-facing judgment and media relationships are not.

6,209 of 8,157 PR firms (76%) have <5 employees, meaning substantial owner-operator comp in this category. Client counsel, new business pitches and crisis response are not automatable. Internal management (resourcing, reporting, scheduling) automates. Fraction 0.18 is low because management's primary value — and the primary asset in founder-run PR firms — is client relationships and senior counsel, which is relationship-dependent and non-automatable.

Media analysis, sentiment tracking, share-of-voice measurement and coverage reporting are already tool-heavy in PR and automate rapidly with AI. Strategic framing and campaign architecting persist. Fraction 0.50 reflects that analytical/measurement execution is more automatable than top-level strategic counsel.

Visual content for PR (infographics, social graphics) automates; brand-level creative direction persists. Note: OEWS shows 27-1024 Graphic Designers (3.3%) and 27-1011 Art Directors (4.1%) in the proxy but these are partly contamination from 541810; within true PR agencies, creative/design support is a smaller function estimated at 4-6% of wage bill (prompt guidance). Using 5%. Fraction 0.40 reflects high design-automation potential but persistent need for brand judgment.

RFP responses and capability deck production automate; relationship-driven business development does not. The OEWS proxy shows 41-3011 Advertising Sales Agents (8.2% wage share) and 41-3091 Sales Reps (3.0%) but these are largely contamination from sibling codes; true PR biz-dev is estimated at 4-5% of wage bill (prompt guidance). Using 4.5%. Fraction 0.28 reflects that business development is predominantly relationship-dependent.

breakdown_by_role.role: Back office, admin and remaining tail
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Billing, timesheet administration, clip report compilation, media list maintenance and scheduling are directly automatable. Clip reporting (a core PR deliverable historically requiring manual assembly) is already largely automated by media monitoring tools; AI takes this further. The OEWS top-10 covers ~51% of the 4-digit wage bill including contaminant occupations (41-9011 Demonstrators 1.6%, 27-1026 Merchandise Displayers 1.2%) not present in PR agencies. Remaining ~49% of wage bill is predominantly admin, finance, project management and operations. Fraction 0.60 reflects high task routinization for this segment.

pi_dist.rationale: PR billing is ~70% monthly retainer scoped against hour plans (Gould+Partners benchmark; PR industry survey data). The retainer model has a structural deflation mechanism: when AI cuts the hours behind a scope, clients rescope the retainer down at renewal — Gould+Partners utilization data (only 42% of firms saw utilization improvement in 2024) suggests the efficiency already operates partially through recovering chronic over-service rather than expanding margin. Earn-out-heavy deal structures in PR M&A (documented by Gould+Partners) explicitly signal that client books are founder-portable, not firm-owned — meaning pi_dist must reflect that the firm's control over client relationships is weaker than the reported retainer base suggests. The industry's documented over-service habit (agencies delivering more hours than invoiced) means AI savings first close the over-service gap before reaching margin, further delaying capture. Slightly lower than 541810's 0.30 because PR relationships are even more founder-dependent and smaller firms dominate the code (76% of firms under 5 employees).
sources: Gould+Partners 2024 Financial Trend Report — 57% of PR firms reported profitability improvement in 2024, billing rates up at 51% of agencies; utilization improved at only 42%
Gould+Partners M&A commentary — earn-out structures dominate PR firm sales due to client-book portability risk
PR agency pricing surveys — ~70% retainer model, hour-scoped

pi_moat.rationale: No licensure protects PR. Journalist relationships (the traditional PR moat) are weakening as newsrooms contract; the earned media product depends on a shrinking journalism sector independent of AI. AI-generated pitch floods are already devaluing mass outreach (a core PR service), which means the moat is eroding even before AI automates the agency's own production. The mass-market PR product (releases, pitches, media lists, coverage reports) is directly substitutable: clients can combine an AI writing tool, a media monitoring SaaS, and a junior in-house coordinator. What persists as a genuine moat is senior crisis counsel and executive trust — valuable but supporting far fewer billable hours than today's retainer books. Vertical specialization (healthcare PR, financial PR, crisis PR) provides some defensibility. Slightly lower than 541810's 0.30 because PR's core earned-media product faces direct structural erosion from both AI and newsroom contraction.
sources: PRovoke Media 2025 — 'AI-generated pitch floods are already devaluing mass outreach'
USC Annenberg 2025 Global Communication Report — AI adoption in PR is accelerating; 73% use AI tools

t50_years.derivation: Current material adoption: ~18-25% of PR firms have integrated AI into core delivery workflows (PRovoke 2025: 18% have integrated AI into workflows 'in a way that measurably improves client outcomes'; USC Annenberg 2025: two-thirds use AI but material integration is lower). Central estimate: 25% currently at material integration. Historical analogs: media monitoring software adoption in PR took 6-8 years to reach majority; social media content tools reached 50%+ adoption in PR firms within 5-6 years of mainstream availability. AI tools for PR (writing, media lists, coverage analysis) became viable in 2023-2024. From 25% today (mid-2026) to 50% material adoption: at the current pace, requiring 3 additional years (by mid-2029). Slower than 541810's 2.5 years because PR firms are smaller on average (76% under 5 employees), have less technology budget, and AI replaces the firm's core production output more directly — creating some adoption resistance as adoption implies admitting AI can do the core work.

current_adoption_pct.source: PRovoke Media 2025 Global PR Industry Survey; USC Annenberg 2025 Global Communication Report
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: PRovoke 2025: 73% of agencies report using AI tools, but only 18% have integrated AI into core workflows in a way that measurably improves client outcomes. USC Annenberg 2025: two-thirds of communicators say they use AI frequently. 25% used as central estimate for material integration (between 18% measurable improvement and broader frequent-use figures).

historical_analogs.value: 5-8 years to reach 50% on comparable prior workflow technologies in PR
historical_analogs.source: PRovoke Media historical coverage; industry technology adoption patterns for media monitoring (Cision, Meltwater), CRM and social scheduling tools in PR
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: Social media management tools reached majority adoption in PR agencies within approximately 5-6 years of mainstream availability (c. 2010-2016); media monitoring SaaS tools reached majority within 6-8 years. AI tools became viable for PR in 2023-2024.

owners_60plus_pct.source: US Census Bureau Business Owner Characteristics (2025 release); SBA small business profiles 2024; Exit Planning Institute State of Owner Readiness 2025
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: Baby Boomers (55-73) form 37% of small business owners; self-employed age 65+ is 16.3% (up from 13.0% in 2013). Age 60+ estimated at ~25% by interpolation. The PR industry is notably founder-heavy (76% of firms under 5 employees per SUSB 2022) but no PR-industry-specific demographic survey was found.

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: O'Dwyer's (Oct 2025) identifies succession planning as a significant concern for PR firm owners but does not document a formal industry-wide shortage with a quantified gap. No PR trade association (PRSA, PRCA) has published a specific succession shortage report for 541820. Marking false: no qualifying trade-association citation specific to this code despite the qualitative concern being documented.

active_consolidators.source: PRovoke Media 2024 M&A Review; CommPRO; MastersInCommunications; PE Hub; Tracxn
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: 79 total PR agency deals in 2024, of which 35% (approximately 28) were PE-backed. Named active PE-backed platforms with documented add-on acquisitions in PR 2024-2026: Sodali & Co (TPG-backed), FGS Global (KKR-backed), Finn Partners (Stellus-backed), plus additional unnamed PE-backed platforms. Six active PE-backed consolidator platforms with documented add-on activity in the 24-month window is an ESTIMATE based on PRovoke/CommPRO coverage of deal flow.

buy_mult.source: Gould+Partners PR M&A commentary; ExitValue.ai PR firm valuation analysis 2026; ExitValue.ai database of 25,592 verified M&A transactions
buy_mult.figure_quoted: PR firms generally trade at 4.0-7.0x EBITDA, with the median around 5.5x for healthy independents. Gould+Partners notes 'averaging around 5x times recasted EBITDA' at the small end. After EBITDA normalization for owner comp (PR deal structures often quote SDE or pre-comp profit), the normalized buy-side multiple is approximately 5.0x for small firms under $5M EBITDA.

exit_mult.source: ExitValue.ai PR firm valuation; agencies.co marketing agency valuation 2026; PRovoke M&A deal flow data
exit_mult.figure_quoted: Above $3M EBITDA, strategic buyers show up and multiples can stretch to 8-9x; agencies with three-plus years of strong growth and low client concentration saw EBITDA multiples of 8-12x for EBITDA over $5M. Using 9.0x for a $10M+ EBITDA PR platform exit — slightly below the advertising equivalent (9.3x) to reflect higher earn-out risk and lower multiple certainty in PR transactions.

pricing_structure.value: ~70% monthly retainer (hour-scoped against staffing plan), ~20% project fees, ~10% performance/value-based; retainers calculated as estimated hours x billing rate
pricing_structure.source: Gould+Partners Annual Billing Rates & Utilization Report; PR agency pricing guides; Gould+Partners '6 Types of Billing' analysis
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: Approximately 70% of PR agencies prefer retainer arrangements; retainers are calculated as estimated hours x hourly rate (e.g., 40 hours/month x $250/hr = $10,000 monthly retainer); 'Retainer Against Hours' described as best billing method; average total labor costs 62.7% of net revenue in 2024 (Gould+Partners 2024 Financial Trend Report).

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: uplift_pp = 25.4pp which substantially exceeds the 3pp threshold. A = 3.33 which is below 5.0, so the tension check is not triggered. Consistent: very high AI value potential (V=10.0, V_raw just clears the 0.25 anchor) combined with moderate adoption speed (A=3.33) reflects that while PR labor is highly substitutable by AI, the industry is moving somewhat slower toward material adoption than advertising. No inconsistency.

terminal_value.justification: Demand for PR agency services survives mature AI but structurally contracts. The mass-market PR product — press releases, pitch letters, media lists, coverage reports, social content — is being directly automated. AI-generated pitch floods are already devaluing earned-media outreach (documented by PRovoke 2025). What persists is senior crisis counsel, journalist access (though newsroom contraction is an independent structural headwind), and executive trust for sensitive communications. The newsroom contraction headwind (declining from ~50,000 US newsroom employees in 2008 to under 35,000 by 2023 per Pew Research) reduces the value of journalist relationships independent of AI. The paid product is likely to consolidate into a smaller, senior-counsel model with far fewer hours per account than today's retainer structures imply. This is PRESSURED rather than MELTING because crisis PR and specialized vertical communications (healthcare, financial, public affairs) have durable demand; but PRESSURED rather than DURABLE because both AI and media-environment factors are simultaneously compressing the retainer hour base that sustains the current business model. Evidence: retainer hour audits, over-service levels, and the structural deflation of mass-outreach PR.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V was derived from role-by-role assessment of which specific PR tasks AI can substantially perform (press releases, pitch drafts, media lists, coverage monitoring, social content, billing/admin) — no reference to PE deal activity or consolidation trends. A was derived from PRovoke 2025 survey (18-25% material integration) and historical technology adoption analogs in PR (social media management, media monitoring SaaS timelines) — no reference to M&A deal flow.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: ~70% of PR agency revenue is monthly retainer scoped against hour plans (Gould+Partners benchmark). The deflation mechanism operates in two stages: (1) AI first reduces chronic over-service — Gould+Partners data shows utilization improved at only 42% of firms in 2024, meaning many agencies are already delivering more hours than billed; AI savings initially close this over-service gap without reaching margin; (2) at retainer renewal, clients rescope the hour plan down when AI has demonstrably reduced delivery hours. PR deals are earn-out-heavy (Gould+Partners documents this explicitly) precisely because client books are founder-portable — confirming that the firm's pi_dist is constrained even before AI deflation. The combined effect: AI savings in PR flow to clients via scope renegotiation faster than in industries with higher switching costs or proprietary assets. Reflected in pi_dist = 0.28 (below 0.30 for advertising): the firm captures less of the AI productivity gain than even advertising agencies, due to founder-portfolio dynamics and more direct product substitutability.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A = 10/t50: at t50=2.0 A=5.0 vs t50=5.0 A=2.0. S shifts from approximately 3.6 to 3.1 across this range — approximately 0.5 S-point swing. At t50=2.0, A reaches 5.0 triggering the uplift_vs_A cross-check (uplift_pp 25.4pp > 3pp so consistent, no issue).

C = 10 × pi_dist × pi_moat. At pi_dist=0.18, pi_moat=0.18: C=0.324. At pi_dist=0.38, pi_moat=0.38: C=1.444. Combined C range is 0.324-1.444, representing approximately a 0.5-0.6 S-point swing — the dominant uncertainty after t50.

V_raw = 0.5689 × ARS. At ARS=0.36 V_raw=0.205 V=8.20; at ARS=0.44 V_raw=0.250 V=10.0 (already at cap). The score is at the V cap (10.0), so upward revisions to ARS have no impact on S; downward revisions below ARS=0.439 reduce V and S by approximately 0.2-0.4 S-points.

evidence.reviewer_note: Role-mix contamination handled per prompt guidance: the 4-digit 5418 OEWS proxy includes Advertising Sales Agents (41-3011, 8.2% wage share), Demonstrators (41-9011, 1.6%), and Merchandise Displayers (27-1026, 1.2%) which are not PR agency occupations. Additionally, 27-1024 Graphic Designers (3.3%) and 27-1011 Art Directors (4.1%) reflect contamination from sibling code 541810. The breakdown_by_role uses a six-bucket adjusted structure summing to 100% of wage bill as specified in prompt guidance, ignoring the contaminated OEWS shares directly. EBITDA normalization note: PR deal structures frequently report SDE or structured earn-out maximums rather than clean EBITDA multiples (documented by Gould+Partners); buy_mult=5.0x and exit_mult=9.0x represent EBITDA-basis estimates after adjusting for owner comp normalization, recognizing that quoted headline multiples in earn-out deals often overstate at-close values. Labor_share note: the 0.5689 value is notably high (vs 0.4814 for 541810) — the MED quality reflects a 2024/2022 vintage mismatch and harmonization adjustment; if true labor share is lower (e.g., closer to 0.45), V_raw would fall below 0.25 and V would drop below 10 — flagged as a data uncertainty. Media environment dependency: the PR industry's earned-media product depends on shrinking newsrooms — this secular headwind is independent of AI and was factored into the terminal value classification (PRESSURED) and pi_moat (0.28) but not modeled separately in the formula.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Plan building, audience research, RFP generation, flighting, pacing, optimization and reporting are heavily AI-exposed — native platform automation (Google DV360, The Trade Desk AI) already does most of this; cross-platform complexity and vendor negotiation persist. Proxy OEWS 41-3011 (8.18%) + 13-1161 (6.55%) = 14.73% of wage bill, but actual media-buying firms are dominated by planner/buyer/investment staff — reconstructed to 35% per prompt guidance. Within-role fraction 0.50 (midpoint of prompt's 0.40-0.60 range).

Trafficking, tag management, make-goods, discrepancy reconciliation and media billing are the most directly automatable cost lines. AI and workflow tools (campaign manager APIs, automated reconciliation) already handle the bulk of this. Not in OEWS top-10; estimated at 10-15% per prompt guidance; using 12% midpoint.

Client relationships, media-vendor rate leverage (scale-based, survives acquisition) and principal deal-making persist; internal management and reporting automate. Proxy OEWS 11-1021 (9.26%) + 11-2021 (5.20%) = 14.46%; reconstructed to 16% for actual firms. Many firms are founder-led given n_total=663.

Attribution, MMM-lite reporting and dashboarding automate heavily via AI tools (Rockerbox, Northbeam, etc.); measurement design judgment and client-specific analytics interpretation persist. Not in OEWS top-10; estimated 6-10% per prompt; using 8% midpoint.

Status reporting, plan presentations, and performance decks automate; relationship ownership and strategic counsel persist. Proxy OEWS 41-3091 (3.02%) underestimates actual client-facing roles in buying shops; reconstructed to 9%.

breakdown_by_role.role: Back office and remaining tail (incl. proxy contaminants)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Finance/billing admin in a business that reconciles high-volume media invoices — strongly automatable. The 4-digit 5418 proxy contaminates with PR specialists (8.29%), Graphic Designers (3.30%), Demonstrators (1.62%), Merchandise Displayers (1.21%) — none present in media buying shops. These contaminating roles plus true finance/admin sum to ~20% of the reconstructed wage bill. AI-driven accounts-payable/receivable, media invoice reconciliation tools address most of this.

pi_dist.rationale: Dominant pricing model is commission on media spend (typically 3-15% of billings) and fixed-fee retainers benchmarked to spend. This is pricing-model-favorable: AI cost savings flow to the operator as margin because the billing basis is spend (not hours). However, the client-facing deflation channel is commission-rate pressure via fee auditors (Ebiquity, Accenture Media) and client procurement departments — ANA documented that 1/3 of advertisers revised media agency contracts in a recent year. Additionally, platform self-serve (Google PMax, Meta Advantage+) directly threatens the commission base itself for SMB/mid-market clients, the core market for these 663 firms. Client relationships are partially firm-level (buying clout and vendor relationships) and partially individual-level (account director relationships). Principal-based buying margin — the highest-capture channel — is structurally inaccessible to band-sized independents lacking holdco-scale inventory commitments. Net: moderate firm control, with ongoing erosion channel through rate compression and direct bypass.
sources: ANA Media Agency Compensation Practices 2019 (commission structure data)
ANA Programmatic Media Supply Chain Transparency Study 2023-2024
Marketing Dive: ANA one-third of advertisers revised contracts
AdExchanger: The Rise of Principal Media 2024

pi_moat.rationale: No licensure requirement. Switching costs are moderate: established media vendor rate leverage (reach-scale dependent), platform fee structures, workflow integration (DSPs, ad servers), and client onboarding friction. However, the moat is under active compression from (1) Google PMax and Meta Advantage+ targeting exactly the SMB/mid-market clients of these 663 firms; (2) retail media self-serve consoles reducing the cross-channel complexity that justified agency involvement; (3) absence of proprietary data moats at band-scale. The realistic threat is direct platform bypass, not just commodity competition. Score reflects meaningful but eroding switching costs with no hard barrier.
sources: eMarketer: FAQ on AI media buying 2026
MediaPost: Hey Agencies, Meta and Google Are Taking Your Business 2024
IAB State of Data 2025
JP Morgan: How Advertising Agencies Compete in 2026

t50_years.derivation: Current adoption: IAB State of Data 2025 (March 2025) found 30% of agencies/brands/publishers have fully integrated AI across media campaign lifecycle. Historical analog: programmatic advertising went from ~10% adoption (2012) to crossing ~50% adoption in approximately 2015-2016, a span of ~4 years from early adoption; eMarketer data shows US programmatic hit ~45% of digital display spend in 2014 and exceeded 60% by 2018, placing the 50% crossing at 2015-2016 (4-5 years from 2011 baseline). AI adoption in agencies is accelerating faster: IAB's 2025 study found 'half of those who haven't fully integrated AI expect to do it by 2026,' implying ~65% fully integrated by end of 2026. On this trajectory 50% is crossed approximately 2025-2026. Taking conservative view (smaller firms lag, 'fully integrated' overstates 'materially adopting'), t50 = 3.0 years (mid-2029). Plausible range: 1.5-5 years.

current_adoption_pct.source: IAB State of Data 2025
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: Only 30% of agencies, brands, and publishers have fully integrated AI across the media campaign lifecycle

historical_analogs.value: 4-5 years
historical_analogs.source: eMarketer / MarTech (programmatic advertising adoption 2012-2016)
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: Programmatic ad spend hit $10B in 2014, approximately 45% of digital display; reached 61%+ by 2018 — crossing 50% adoption circa 2015-2016, approximately 4 years from 2011-2012 baseline

owners_60plus_pct.source: Oberlo / SBA Advocacy (general small business owner age data)
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: 37% of small business owners are between 55-73 years old in 2024; share of self-employed age 65+ rose from 13.0% (2013) to 16.3% (2023) per Census/SBA

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: No succession shortage specifically documented for NAICS 541830 media buying agencies. General small business data cited; agency-industry-specific owner age surveys (Ad Age Agency Report) do not publish 60+ breakdowns. Estimate of 35% over 60 based on general small business demographics (37% aged 55-73) adjusted for the fact that many media buying agencies were founded in the 1990s-2000s digital-advertising expansion era by owners now in their 50s-60s.

active_consolidators.source: FE International / Campaign US / LinkedIn PE agency landscape (2025)
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: PE-backed performance and digital platforms including Tinuiti, Wpromote, Power Digital, Bounteous, and New Engen roll up specialty agencies; marketing services M&A rose 14% YTD 2025. Specific media-buying (541830) PE platforms are fewer — performance media roll-ups (Tinuiti, Power Digital, New Engen) overlap with this code.

buy_mult.source: Axial.net / First Page Sage marketing agency valuation multiples 2025
buy_mult.figure_quoted: Small agencies $1-2M EBITDA: 3.5x-5x; average ~4.5x for agencies around $1-3M EBITDA. TobinLeff data: ~$500K EBITDA averages 3.33x. Using 4.5x as representative of band-sized (10-99 employee class) media buying agency acquisitions.

exit_mult.source: First Page Sage / Axial / JEGI Clarity M&A data 2025
exit_mult.figure_quoted: Agencies $5-10M EBITDA: 8.1x-10.6x; larger agencies (10M+ EBITDA platform exits) typically 8x-10x for generalist media services. Using 8.5x for performance media platforms given AI/disintermediation risk discount vs. higher-growth digital specialties.

pricing_structure.value: Commission on media spend (3-15% of billings); fixed-fee retainers benchmarked to spend; some principal-based trading margin at holdco scale. Commission dominant for independents.
pricing_structure.source: ANA Media Agency Compensation Practices; ANA Programmatic Transparency studies 2023-2024
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: 40% of respondents use commissions for programmatic media; 24% for digital media buying; 12% for general agency services (ANA 2024 programmatic benchmark). Commission and % of spend models dominate independent media agency billing.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: V_raw = 14.18pp of revenue freed. A = 3.33 (< 5). The cross-check tension condition (uplift < 3pp AND A >= 5) does not apply: uplift is 14.18pp which far exceeds 3pp. Consistent — high V with moderate-to-slow adoption speed.

terminal_value.justification: The terminal state is not full disintermediation but meaningful demand compression. Two distinct mechanisms: (1) AI cuts operator costs — commission/% of spend model means AI efficiency on internal labor (planning, trafficking, analytics) flows to operator as margin, not to clients. This is the favorable case. (2) AI replaces the operator's product — Google PMax and Meta Advantage+ automate the buy-side for the SMB/mid-market clients these 663 firms serve, shrinking the spend that flows through independent media buyers. The terminal scenario is a smaller number of firms managing complex cross-channel campaigns and principal-deal inventory for mid-market advertisers that exceed self-serve capability thresholds, while the long tail of SMB spend routes directly through platform AI. This is PRESSURED not MELTING: some demand for independent, cross-platform, vendor-neutral media expertise survives mature AI (especially for clients with $1M+ media budgets across 5+ channels), but the market shrinks significantly from today's base. The n_total=663 universe likely contracts to ~200-300 firms over 10 years.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V was derived from the reconstructed role structure (prompt-guided) applied to the labor_share dataset input — no reference to M&A deal activity or PE consolidation trends. A was derived from IAB State of Data 2025 (current adoption survey) and eMarketer programmatic adoption historical analog — no reference to deal activity. Confirmed: V and A are grounded in AI capability and adoption evidence only.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Billing mix: commission on media spend (~40% of transactions per ANA 2024) and fixed fees benchmarked to spend (~35%); principal-based trading margin (~15%, mostly at holdco scale, not available to band-sized independents). The commission/% of spend model means AI efficiency flows TO THE OPERATOR as margin (the billing basis is spend, not hours — AI reducing planning hours does not mechanically reduce the commission). The deflation channel is instead: (a) fee auditors (Ebiquity, MediaLink) compressing commission rates via contract renegotiation — ANA found 1/3 of advertisers revised contracts recently; (b) platform self-serve reducing the spend that flows through agencies entirely. This is reflected in pi_dist=0.55 (moderate: operator keeps AI savings as margin but faces commission-rate pressure and base erosion) and pi_moat=0.35 (meaningful but eroding switching costs; platform bypass is a real mechanism). The principal-based margin trap for small independents is noted: they cannot access the highest-capture model, so their margin structure is more exposed.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: At t50=1.5 years: A=6.67 → S≈4.08; at t50=5.0 years: A=2.0 → S≈3.33. Range of approximately ±0.37 around base S=3.73.

top_uncertain_inputs.plausible_range: 0.45-0.58 (driven by media planner/buyer and back-office fraction uncertainty)
top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: At 0.45: V_raw=0.1242, V=4.97, S≈3.64; at 0.58: V_raw=0.1600, V=6.40, S≈3.80. Range of approximately ±0.08 around base S=3.73.
At 0.25: C=1.375, S≈3.57; at 0.45: C=2.475, S≈3.91. Range of approximately ±0.17 around base S=3.73.

evidence.reviewer_note: Labor_share caveat: the 0.2759 figure is computed on SUSB gross billings receipts (not net commission income). If net revenue is ~25-35% of gross billings (a typical gross-to-net ratio for commission-based media buying), the true labor_share on a net-revenue basis would be 3-4x higher (0.7-1.0+), implying V_raw would be capped at 1.0 and V=10. However, the dataset note already addresses this: the SUSB receipts figure for 541830 ($9B) and revenue/employee ($435K) suggest the SUSB is capturing mostly commission income (net), not gross billings pass-through — otherwise revenue/employee would be $2M-4M, which is holdco-scale. This creates ambiguity: if SUSB receipts approximate net commission revenue, then V=5.67 is reasonable; if there is substantial billings pass-through contamination, V could be higher. The score is reported at face value per dataset rules; this conflict is flagged here. Role-mix contamination: the 4-digit 5418 proxy (OEWS) was explicitly reconstructed per prompt guidance to remove PR specialists, graphic designers, demonstrators, and merchandise displayers — not present in actual media buying shops. The ai_replaceable_share breakdown uses reconstructed roles, not raw OEWS wage shares. Active consolidators (3) is an estimate reflecting performance-media-adjacent PE platforms; no dedicated 541830-only roll-up platform was identified in research — the true active count may be 1-4.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Relationship selling to agencies and advertisers on behalf of station/publisher clients is the product itself. AI assists prospecting, avails packaging and proposal generation but the negotiated sale — where it persists against programmatic displacement — remains human. Proxy OEWS 41-3011 (8.18%) + 41-3091 (3.02%) = 11.2% of proxy wage bill, but media rep firms are dominated by sales agents — reconstructed to 40% per prompt guidance. Within-role automatable fraction set at low 0.15: the non-automatable core (relationship, negotiation, rate leverage) is the primary value driver, though prospecting and avails proposals are AI-assisted.

Ratings/audience research decks, avails packaging, one-sheets and proposal materials are directly automatable. AI tools already handle audience segment analysis, CPM benchmarking, and proposal generation. Proxy OEWS 13-1161 (6.55%) covers part; reconstructed to 10% including research/planner tail. Within-role fraction 0.85: only output requiring judgment (narrative synthesis for complex sponsorship proposals) remains human.

Relationships with represented media owners (the rep contracts) and key agency accounts persist; these are the firm's core asset. Internal management and reporting automate. Proxy OEWS 11-1021 (9.26%) + 11-2021 (5.20%) = 14.46%; reconstructed to 16%. The exclusive rep contract relationship is the non-automatable core.

breakdown_by_role.role: Order management, traffic and back office
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Insertion orders, make-goods, invoicing and reconciliation are directly automatable — and already being displaced by programmatic pipes (programmatic TV, programmatic radio via Triton/AdsWizz). Traffic order entry and billing reconciliation have been the first functions automated in media companies. Not in OEWS top-10; estimated 15-20% per prompt; using 17% midpoint.

The 4-digit 5418 proxy imports PR specialists (8.29%), Graphic Designers (3.30%), Art Directors (4.07%), Demonstrators (1.62%), and Merchandise Displayers (1.21%) — none of which exist in media rep firms. These phantom occupations represent ~18% of the proxy wage bill. The true tail in rep firms is finance/IT/admin (~10%) and miscellaneous support. Finance/admin automates readily; the phantom proxy contamination is scored conservatively at 0.40 since we cannot separate real from phantom within this bucket. Automatable fraction 0.40 is lower than 541830 back office (0.70) because the contaminating creative roles would be near-zero if real, dragging the average down.

pi_dist.rationale: Rep firms earn commission on ad sales made for represented media owners (typically 10-15% of gross billings placed) — not hours-based. AI hour-savings flow to the operator by construction because the commission is per sale, not per labor hour. However, the commission base itself is the deflation channel: media owners are (1) cutting rep commissions or terminating contracts to in-house sales; (2) routing inventory to programmatic SSPs (FreeWheel for TV, Triton/AdsWizz for radio) that pay reps nothing; (3) consolidating to fewer rep relationships. The pi_dist question for this code is which party controls the client relationship — for rep firms the 'client' is the media owner (who grants the exclusive rep contract) AND the agency/advertiser (who buys via the rep). Rep contracts are typically exclusive but terminable (60-90 day cancellation clauses). Individual sales agents carry agency relationships (key account management is person-dependent). Score 0.45 reflects: commission model is operator-favorable for retaining AI margin, but media owner bypass is a real and accelerating mechanism (programmatic displacement), and individual account control creates dependency risk on senior salespeople.
sources: ANA Media Agency Compensation Practices 2019 (commission structure)
AdExchanger: The Rise of Principal Media 2024
IAB State of Data 2025 (programmatic adoption rates)

pi_moat.rationale: Very low moat for independent media rep firms. No licensure required. The sole barriers are: (1) exclusive rep contracts — legally meaningful but terminable with 60-90 day notice, and media owners are demonstrably exercising that termination option as programmatic SSP infrastructure matures; (2) established agency/advertiser buyer relationships — valuable but person-dependent and portable when salespeople change jobs. The national broadcast rep business has consolidated to near-monopoly (Katz Media Group, owned by iHeartMedia, dominates national TV/radio rep) — remaining independents serve niche segments (specialty publishers, local/regional, OOH, political broadcast) where programmatic pipes are less developed. The realistic threat is direct programmatic bypass: SSP platforms (Magnite, FreeWheel) let media owners sell inventory directly to DSPs without a rep firm in the chain. Political broadcast spend is the one durable niche (requires local market expertise and incumbent relationships that programmatic doesn't replicate). Score 0.25: some residual moat from niche specialization and established buyer relationships, but rapidly eroding.
sources: eMarketer: Programmatic advertising FAQ 2026
IAB State of Data 2025
Prompt guidance: Katz Media/iHeart consolidation history

t50_years.derivation: Current adoption: IAB State of Data 2025 found 30% of agencies/brands/publishers have fully integrated AI across media campaign lifecycle. For media rep firms specifically, sales support and order management AI tools (CRM automation, proposal generators, traffic systems) were adopted earlier than general AI — estimating current material adoption at ~30%. Historical analog: programmatic advertising went from ~10% (2012) to ~50% adoption (~2015-2016), approximately 4 years. For rep firms, AI adoption in core functions (sales support, trafficking) is faster than for buying agencies because the functions are more rote/templated — but the shrinking industry reduces investment incentive, and the subset of rep firm functions that are truly AI-automatable (sales support, back office) may already be at high adoption while the core (relationship sales) is inherently resistant. Estimate: t50 = 4.0 years (2030) for 50% of remaining firms adopting AI across automatable functions — slower than 541830 because the declining industry creates lower investment pressure.
